## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Briefly explain W. Edwards Deming's contribution to Total Quality Management. Mention any two of his 14 points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) is regarded as the **father of statistical quality control**. His major contributions to TQM include:

- The **14 Points for Management** — a philosophy for transforming organizational leadership
- The **System of Profound Knowledge** — comprising four lenses: System, Variation, Knowledge, Psychology
- The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** — a method for continuous improvement
- The **Deming Prize** — awarded in Japan since 1951

**Two representative 14 Points (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

- **Point 1:** Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.
- **Point 3:** Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality — build quality *into* the process.
- **Point 8:** Drive out fear so everyone can work effectively for the organization.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Deming Wheel? Explain its four stages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Deming Wheel, also known as the **PDCA cycle**, is a four-stage iterative model for continuous improvement:

| Stage | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | Identify the problem and plan a change for improvement |
| **Do** | Implement the change on a small scale and collect data |
| **Check** | Analyze the data and compare results with expectations |
| **Act** | If the change works, standardize it; otherwise, revise the plan |

The cycle repeats continuously, fostering **Kaizen** (continuous improvement).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points for Management in detail. How do they transform an organization's quality culture? (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the Deming Chain Reaction with a suitable engineering industry example. Show mathematically how prevention investment yields higher returns than failure-cost recovery. (7 marks)**

##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

The **14 Points for Management**, published in Deming's landmark book *Out of the Crisis* (1982), constitute a comprehensive philosophy for organizational transformation.

**(Marks distribution: 0.5 mark per point × 14 = 7 marks. Examiner expects a *table or grouped explanation*, not 14 one-line bullets.)**

**Cluster-wise presentation:**

- **Purpose & Philosophy:** Points 1 and 2 — emphasize long-term vision and rejection of mass inspection.
- **Systems Thinking:** Points 3, 4, 5 — build quality in, choose suppliers by total cost, continuous improvement.
- **People & Leadership:** Points 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 — training, leadership, no fear, cross-functional teams, no slogans, no quotas, no merit ratings.
- **Transformation Ownership:** Points 13 and 14 — self-improvement and top-management commitment.

**Cultural Impact:**
The 14 Points shift an organization from a **blame culture** (worker-fault) to a **learning culture** (system-fault). Deming argued that **94% of problems are systemic** — they vanish only when leadership transforms practices, not when workers try harder.

##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**The Deming Chain Reaction** captures the economic logic of quality improvement:

$$\text{Improvement in Quality} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lower Costs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Higher Productivity} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Market Capture} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Survival and Jobs}$$

**Engineering Industry Example — Automotive Assembly Line:**

Suppose a car manufacturer produces $N = 100{,}000$ cars/year. Current defect rate $p = 2\%$, average failure cost per defective car $C_f = \text{₹}50{,}000$, prevention cost per prevented defect $C_p = \text{₹}500$.

**Step 1 — Current failure cost:**

$$\text{FC}_1 = 100{,}000 \times 0.02 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}10 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 2 — After reducing defect rate by half** ($p' = 1\%$):

$$\text{FC}_2 = 100{,}000 \times 0.01 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹}5 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 3 — Savings:**

$$\Delta\text{FC} = 10 - 5 = \text{₹}5 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 4 — Prevention investment needed:**

$$\text{PI} = 100{,}000 \times 0.01 \times 500 = \text{₹}50 \text{ Lakh} = \text{₹}0.5 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 5 — Net Benefit and ROI:**

$$\text{NB} = 5 - 0.5 = \text{₹}4.5 \text{ Crore} \quad ; \quad \text{ROI} = \frac{4.5}{0.5} \times 100\% = 900\%$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Prevention investment yields a **900% return** — far higher than investing in inspection or warranty recovery. This is the mathematical foundation of Deming's chain reaction.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK). Describe its four components with engineering examples. (7 marks)**

**(b) A CNC turning process has specification 25.00 mm ± 0.04 mm. Sample data from 5 subgroups of size 4 yield grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.992$ mm and $\sigma = 0.008$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the 3-sigma control limits for $\bar{X}$-chart. Comment on process capability. (7 marks)**

##### **Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

The **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** is Deming's intellectual framework, stating that to transform any organization, leaders must master **four interrelated domains** simultaneously.

**(Marks split: 1.75 marks per component.)**

**1. Appreciation for a System (1.75 marks):**
An organization is a network of **interdependent components**. Optimizing one department at the expense of others degrades total performance.

*Engineering Example:* In a power plant, optimizing the boiler efficiency alone may overload the turbine. The *system optimum* matters, not local optima.

**2. Knowledge of Variation (1.75 marks):**
All processes exhibit **common-cause variation** (inherent) and **special-cause variation** (assignable). Deming insisted **94% of variation is common** — to be reduced by management.

*Engineering Example:* In an injection molding process, the daily fluctuation in part weight is mostly due to material humidity (common cause) — fixing humidity (system change) helps; punishing the operator does not.

**3. Theory of Knowledge (1.75 marks):**
Knowledge comes from **theory, prediction, and experimentation** (PDCA). Without a theory, data is meaningless.

*Engineering Example:* Predicting weld strength from a regression model and testing it with experiments.

**4. Psychology (1.75 marks):**
People differ. Fear destroys performance. Intrinsic motivation outperforms extrinsic rewards.

*Engineering Example:* Toyota's "Stop-the-Line" (Andon) system works because workers feel psychologically safe to halt production.

**Integration:** SoPK is not four separate ideas — it is **one lens with four facets**. A manager lacking any facet will fail.

##### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\text{USL} = 25.04$ mm, $\text{LSL} = 24.96$ mm
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 24.992$ mm
- $\sigma = 0.008$ mm
- Subgroup size $n = 4$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$ (1.5 marks):**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 24.96}{6 \times 0.008} = \frac{0.08}{0.048} \approx 1.667$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ — Upper (1.5 marks):**

$$C_{pkU} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 24.992}{3 \times 0.008} = \frac{0.048}{0.024} = 2.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$ — Lower (1.5 marks):**

$$C_{pkL} = \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.992 - 24.96}{0.024} = \frac{0.032}{0.024} \approx 1.333$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (0.5 mark):**

$$C_{pk} = \min(2.0,\, 1.333) = 1.333$$

**Step 5 — Compute 3-sigma control limits for $\bar{X}$-chart (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{x}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = 24.992 + \frac{3 \times 0.008}{\sqrt{4}} = 24.992 + 0.012 = 25.004 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{x}} = 24.992 - 0.012 = 24.980 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL} = 24.992 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Comment (0.5 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Since } C_p = 1.667 > 1.33 \text{ and } C_{pk} = 1.333 = 1.33, \\
&\text{the process is capable but BARELY meets the lower spec.}\\
&\text{Recommend re-centering } \bar{\bar{X}} \text{ at } 25.00 \text{ mm for safety margin.}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — How students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ assumes centered process; $C_{pk}$ accounts for shift. Always report BOTH.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sqrt{n}$** in $\bar{X}$-chart limits — only $\bar{X}$-chart uses $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$; the $R$-chart and $X$-chart use plain $\sigma$.
> 3. **Mis-stating the 94/6 rule:** It applies to *quality problems* in *typical processes*, not to all variance.
> 4. **Listing 14 Points without grouping** — examiners want conceptual clustering (Purpose, Systems, People, Transformation), not 14 random bullets.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the OESFT615 exam, tick every box below.**

- ☐ Deming = **Father of Statistical Quality Control**, awarded the **Deming Prize (1951)** in Japan.
- ☐ Deming's **14 Points** can be grouped into **4 clusters**: Purpose/Philosophy (1–2), Systems (3–5), People/Leadership (6–12), Transformation (13–14).
- ☐ Deming's **7 Deadly Diseases** include lack of constancy of purpose, short-term profits focus, merit ratings, and management mobility.
- ☐ **PDCA Cycle**: Plan → Do → Check → Act (iterative).
- ☐ **System of Profound Knowledge**: 4 lenses — System, Variation, Knowledge, Psychology.
- ☐ **94 / 6 Rule**: 94% system causes (management) vs. 6% special causes (workers).
- ☐ **Cost of Quality Ratio**: Prevention : Appraisal : Failure = **1 : 10 : 100**.
- ☐ **Control Chart Limits**: $\text{UCL}_{\bar{x}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.
- ☐ **Process Capability**: $C_p$ measures spread; $C_{pk}$ measures centering; require $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for capable process.
- ☐ **Red Bead Experiment**: Demonstrated that workers in a poor system cannot improve quality regardless of effort — workers (the "4 red bead workers") became an analogy for blaming employees.
- ☐ **Funnel Experiment**: Showed the four wrong ways to adjust a process, leading to *increased* variation.
- ☐ Deming transformed **post-war Japan** through lectures to top engineers in 1950 — foundation of the Japanese quality miracle.
- ☐ Difference between Deming (statistical, systemic) vs. Juran (planning, customer) vs. Crosby (zero defects, cost) vs. Ishikawa (company-wide, fishbone).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Juran's Contribution to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Juran's Contribution to TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008)** was an American engineer, management consultant, and one of the principal founding fathers of Total Quality Management (TQM). Juran's contribution to TQM is centered on his definition of quality as **"Fitness for Use"**, his universally adopted **Quality Trilogy**, and his pioneering application of the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** to quality problems. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, Juran's body of work provides a *managerial roadmap* for translating customer needs into engineered product characteristics, and for driving continuous quality improvement through a structured, project-based methodology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Juran's contribution is examined under three core pillars:
> 1. **Definition of Quality** – Fitness for Use and its sub-components.
> 2. **The Juran Trilogy** – Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement.
> 3. **The Breakthrough Sequence & Project-by-Project Approach** – Driving measurable quality gains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the **head chef of a large restaurant chain** opening 50 new outlets across Kerala. You have thousands of suppliers, hundreds of recipes, and lakhs of customers.

- **Without a quality system**, you simply *react* when a customer complains about a cold biriyani. You keep "inspecting" finished plates, but the problem keeps recurring.
- **With Juran's mindset**, you do three things *systematically*:
  1. **PLAN the recipe and process** *before* opening the kitchen — define what "perfect biriyani" means, design a process to cook it, and transfer the design to the cooks.
  2. **CONTROL the cooking process** during operations — measure actual taste/temperature against the planned standard, and act when there is a deviation.
  3. **IMPROVE** — when the current biriyani is "good enough," set a *breakthrough* goal: reduce cooking time by 20% while improving taste.

That is **Juran's Trilogy in a kitchen** — Plan, Control, Improve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Juran's Quality Definition — "Fitness for Use"**
> Quality is determined by the **customer's experience of the product** across **five sub-dimensions**: Quality of Design, Quality of Conformance, Availability, Safety, and Field Use.

### 1.3 Core Terms and Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Standard Metric |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| **Fitness for Use** | Product meets customer needs across its lifecycle | Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) |
| **Quality Trilogy** | Planning, Control, Improvement | Coverage of managerial processes |
| **Vital Few / Trivial Many** | Pareto classification of defects | **80% of defects** arise from **20% of causes** |
| **Breakthrough** | A planned, structured improvement | Reduction in Chronic Waste |
| **Sponsor (Juran)** | Top-management champion of a quality project | Charters and resources approved |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Juran's Contribution

#### Pillar 1 — Juran's Definition of Quality: "Fitness for Use"

Juran rejected the older "conformance to specifications" view. He argued that quality must be defined from the **customer's perspective**, not the producer's. He decomposed "Fitness for Use" into **five distinct sub-categories**:

1. **Quality of Design** — The intentional design of a product/service features (e.g., a smartphone with a 5000 mAh battery is a *design* choice).
2. **Quality of Conformance** — The degree to which the actual product adheres to its design (e.g., the manufactured battery actually delivers 5000 mAh).
3. **Availability** — The product is operational when needed (uptime / (uptime + downtime)).
4. **Safety** — Absence of unacceptable risk of harm to the user.
5. **Field Use** — Real-world customer service, reliability, and maintainability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in TQM:** All TQM activities (planning, control, improvement) must trace back to one of these five elements, otherwise the effort is "quality by inspection," not quality management.

#### Pillar 2 — The Juran Trilogy (Juran's Universal Trilogy)

Juran's most influential framework. It is **universal**, meaning it applies to *any* organization, process, or industry.

| Step | Name | What Managers Do | Key Output |
|------|------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Quality Planning** | Identify customers, determine their needs, develop product/process features to meet those needs, transfer the design to operations. | Product/Service Design + Process Capability |
| 2 | **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual operating performance, compare it to goals, act on the difference. | Process Stability + Conformance |
| 3 | **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, establish project teams, provide resources, train, review progress. | Breakthrough Projects, Chronic Waste Reduction |

**Why the Trilogy matters:** It converts the vague phrase "quality management" into **three discrete, executable managerial processes** that any CEO can delegate to specific executives.

#### Pillar 3 — Breakthrough and the Project-by-Project Approach

Juran observed that every organization has a "**chronic waste**" level — a stable, persistent level of defects, rework, and waste that nobody challenges. He called improvement projects that break this chronic level **"Breakthroughs."** A breakthrough is *not* a continuous, gradual activity — it is a **structured, finite, project-by-project** effort.

**Juran's Six-Step Breakthrough Sequence:**

1. **Establish the need for improvement** — Build awareness of the chronic-waste gap.
2. **Identify specific projects** — Apply the **Pareto Principle** to find the Vital Few problems.
3. **Organize for breakthrough** — Appoint sponsors, project leaders, and teams.
4. **Provide training and resources** — Make sure teams have the methodology (root cause analysis, SPC, FMEA).
5. **Diagnose the root causes** — Use data and statistical tools, not opinions.
6. **Implement remedies, establish controls, hand over to operations** — Standardize the new, improved process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pareto Principle in Quality (Juran's Adaptation):**
> Roughly **80% of the quality problems** (defects, costs, complaints) are caused by **20% of the causes**. The role of the quality manager is to identify that 20% (the "Vital Few") and attack them first.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \frac{\text{Met Expectations}}{\text{Total Expectations}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage (0–100%)** |
| 2 | Quality Cost Ratio (Pareto-style) | $Q_{cost} = \sum (P_i \times F_i)$ | $P_i$ = per-unit cost of defect $i$, $F_i$ = frequency |
| 3 | Conformance Ratio | $CR = \frac{\text{Number of Conforming Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Also known as **First Pass Yield (FPY)** |
| 4 | Availability (Juran's sub-dimension) | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair |
| 5 | Pareto Cumulative % | $P_{cum}(k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i} \times 100$ | Used to identify the **Vital Few** cutoff (usually at **80%**) |
| 6 | Chronic Waste Reduction | $\Delta W = W_{before} - W_{after}$ | Positive value indicates breakthrough success |
| 7 | Project Selection Priority | $Priority = \frac{\text{Impact} \times \text{Feasibility}}{\text{Cost of Delay}}$ | Higher score → earlier execution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Markdown Table Syntax Safeguard:** The vertical bar $\vert$ has been written as `\vert` inside equations above to avoid breaking the table grid. KTU valuation scripts and most markdown renderers will treat `|` as a column separator, so always use `\vert` or `\mid` in math expressions.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Juran's Framework

- **Manufacturing:** Used by Toyota (in the form of continuous improvement — *kaizen* was a parallel Japanese concept) to drive breakthrough quality projects.
- **Software Engineering:** The "breakthrough sequence" maps directly to Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) and the DMAIC cycle in Six Sigma.
- **Healthcare:** The Trilogy drives accreditation under NABH (National Accreditation Board for Hospitals) standards in India.
- **Service Industry (Banking, Hospitality):** "Vital Few / Trivial Many" is used to triage customer complaints and identify the top 3–4 root causes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Application: Pareto Analysis for Quality Defects

**KTU Frequently Asked Scenario:** *A KTU examiner frequently provides a defect-frequency table and asks you to (a) compute the cumulative percentage, (b) draw a Pareto chart, and (c) identify the Vital Few.* Let us solve a complete KTU-style problem.

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing unit records the following types of defects in one quarter:

| Defect Type | Frequency ($f_i$) |
|---|---|
| Surface Scratch | 140 |
| Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance | 95 |
| Paint Defect | 60 |
| Assembly Misalignment | 25 |
| Packaging Damage | 15 |
| Loose Wiring | 10 |
| Others | 5 |

**Total Defects** $= 140 + 95 + 60 + 25 + 15 + 10 + 5 = 350$.

#### Step 1 — Sort in Descending Order of Frequency

Juran stressed that Pareto analysis is meaningless without this step.

| Rank | Defect Type | Frequency $f_i$ | Individual % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Scratch | 140 | 40.00% | 40.00% |
| 2 | Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance | 95 | 27.14% | 67.14% |
| 3 | Paint Defect | 60 | 17.14% | 84.29% |
| 4 | Assembly Misalignment | 25 | 7.14% | 91.43% |
| 5 | Packaging Damage | 15 | 4.29% | 95.71% |
| 6 | Loose Wiring | 10 | 2.86% | 98.57% |
| 7 | Others | 5 | 1.43% | 100.00% |

#### Step 2 — Derive the Individual Percentage

For Surface Scratch:

$$
P_1 = \frac{f_1}{\sum f_i} \times 100 = \frac{140}{350} \times 100 = 40.00\%
$$

For Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance:

$$
P_2 = \frac{95}{350} \times 100 = 27.14\%
$$

For Paint Defect:

$$
P_3 = \frac{60}{350} \times 100 = 17.14\%
$$

#### Step 3 — Derive the Cumulative Percentage (General Formula)

$$
P_{cum}(k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i} \times 100
$$

Applying for $k=3$ (top 3 defects):

$$
P_{cum}(3) = \frac{140 + 95 + 60}{350} \times 100 = \frac{295}{350} \times 100 = 84.29\%
$$

#### Step 4 — Identify the Vital Few (Juran's 80% Cutoff)

The Vital Few are those defects whose cumulative percentage reaches the **80% mark**. From the table, the **top 3 defects (Surface Scratch, Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance, Paint Defect)** account for **84.29%** of all defects, which crosses the 80% threshold.

**Vital Few (Juran's 20%)**: Surface Scratch, Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance, Paint Defect  
**Trivial Many (Juran's 80% of causes)**: All remaining defects.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Conformance Ratio (Juran's Quality of Conformance)

Assuming 10,000 units were produced in the quarter:

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Conforming Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100 = \frac{10000 - 350}{10000} \times 100 = \frac{9650}{10000} \times 100 = 96.50\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Valuation:**
> A conformance ratio of **96.50%** is *not* a "good" benchmark in TQM — Juran's improvement philosophy argues that the chronic level (3.5% defect rate) must itself be subjected to a breakthrough project, not merely accepted.

#### Step 3.6 — Availability Calculation (Juran's Sub-Dimension)

For a CNC machine, suppose $MTBF = 480$ hours and $MTTR = 20$ hours.

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{480}{480 + 20} = \frac{480}{500} = 0.96 = 96.00\%
$$

In percentage terms: $A = 96.00\%$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Check:** Availability $A$ is bounded between 0 and 1 (i.e., 0% to 100%). The above result of 0.96 is physically valid, and reflects high reliability of the CNC machine, which is one of Juran's five "Fitness for Use" sub-dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Juran's Quality Trilogy (The Universal Trilogy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Identify Customers] --> A2[Determine Customer Needs]
    A2 --> A3[Develop Product Features to Meet Needs]
    A3 --> A4[Develop Process Features to Produce the Product]
    A4 --> A5[Transfer Design to Operations - Produce Trial Lots]
    A5 --> A6[Establish Process Controls - Field Validation]
    A6 --> A7[QUALITY PLANNING OUTPUT - Approved Design and Process]
    
    B1[Evaluate Actual Performance] --> B2[Compare Actual to Goals]
    B2 -->|Difference Exists| B3[Act on the Difference]
    B2 -->|No Difference| B4[Maintain Current Process]
    B3 --> B5[QUALITY CONTROL OUTPUT - Stable Conforming Process]
    B4 --> B5
    A7 --> B1
    
    C1[Build Awareness of Chronic Waste] --> C2[Identify Vital Few Projects via Pareto]
    C2 --> C3[Organize Project Teams and Sponsors]
    C3 --> C4[Train Teams in Diagnostic Methods]
    C4 --> C5[Diagnose Root Causes Using Data]
    C5 --> C6[Implement Remedies and Controls]
    C6 --> C7[Standardize and Hand Over to Operations]
    C7 --> C8[QUALITY IMPROVEMENT OUTPUT - Breakthrough Achieved]
    B5 --> C1
    C8 -->|New Baseline Established| A1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Juran's Six-Step Breakthrough Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 - Build Awareness of Need for Improvement] --> S2[Step 2 - Identify Specific Vital Few Projects]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 - Organize for Breakthrough - Sponsor and Team]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 - Provide Training and Resources]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 - Diagnose Root Causes with Data]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 - Implement Remedies and Establish Controls]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 - Hand Over to Operations - New Baseline]
    S7 -->|Continuous Vigilance| S1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Five Sub-Dimensions of "Fitness for Use"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FFU[FITNESS FOR USE - Juran's Definition of Quality]
    FFU --> QD[Quality of Design - Intentional Engineering Features]
    FFU --> QC[Quality of Conformance - Adherence to Design]
    FFU --> AV[Availability - Operational When Needed]
    FFU --> SF[Safety - Absence of Unacceptable Risk]
    FFU --> FU[Field Use - Reliability and Maintainability]
    
    QD --> EX1[Example - Battery Capacity in mAh]
    QC --> EX2[Example - Actual vs Designed Capacity]
    AV --> EX3[Example - Uptime Ratio]
    SF --> EX4[Example - BIS Certification]
    FU --> EX5[Example - Service Centre Response]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pareto Classification of Quality Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ALL[All Quality Defects 100%]
    ALL --> VITAL[Vital Few - Approximately 20% of Causes]
    ALL --> TRIVIAL[Trivial Many - Approximately 80% of Causes]
    
    VITAL --> V1[Surface Scratch]
    VITAL --> V2[Dimensional Out-of-Tolerance]
    VITAL --> V3[Paint Defect]
    
    TRIVIAL --> T1[Assembly Misalignment]
    TRIVIAL --> T2[Packaging Damage]
    TRIVIAL --> T3[Loose Wiring]
    TRIVIAL --> T4[Others]
    
    V1 -.->|Contribute 84% of Problems| RES[80% of Defect Impact]
    T1 -.->|Contribute 16% of Problems| RES
    T2 -.->|Contribute 16% of Problems| RES
    T3 -.->|Contribute 16% of Problems| RES
    T4 -.->|Contribute 16% of Problems| RES
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define quality according to Joseph Juran. List any two sub-dimensions of "Fitness for Use."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
According to Joseph M. Juran, quality is defined as **"Fitness for Use"** — that is, the product or service must be capable of meeting the customer's needs throughout its lifecycle.

Two sub-dimensions of Fitness for Use:

1. **Quality of Design** — The degree to which the product's engineered features are designed to meet customer expectations.
2. **Quality of Conformance** — The degree to which the actual product conforms to its design specifications during manufacturing.

*(Alternative: Availability, Safety, Field Use)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. State Juran's Universal Trilogy. Why is it called "Universal"?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Juran's Universal Trilogy consists of three managerial processes:

1. **Quality Planning** — Designing a product/process that meets customer needs.
2. **Quality Control** — Evaluating performance and acting on deviations.
3. **Quality Improvement** — Establishing infrastructure to break the chronic-waste level.

It is called **"Universal"** because these three processes apply to *any* organization, *any* industry, and *any* level of management — from manufacturing to service to healthcare.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2 ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three components of Juran's Quality Trilogy. Discuss the key activities performed under each component. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A process has recorded 600 defects over 30,000 units produced. Apply the Pareto Principle to identify the Vital Few using the following defect data:

| Defect Type | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Burr | 240 |
| Cracks | 180 |
| Surface Roughness | 90 |
| Color Mismatch | 50 |
| Dimensional Error | 25 |
| Missing Label | 15 |

Determine (i) the conformance ratio, (ii) the cumulative percentage of the top three defects, and (iii) the Vital Few. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution to Question A (a):**

Juran's Quality Trilogy consists of three interlinked managerial processes that together enable an organization to achieve "Fitness for Use":

**1. Quality Planning (QP):**
The purpose is to design a product/process that can meet customer needs. It follows the **Juran Road Map**:
- Identify the **customers** (internal and external).
- Determine the **customer needs** (voice of the customer).
- **Translate** those needs into engineered product features.
- **Develop the process** capable of producing those features.
- **Transfer the design** to operations through pilot runs and field trials.

**Key Output:** A documented, validated product and process design.

**2. Quality Control (QC):**
The purpose is to **maintain** the achieved level of quality during operations. It is a closed-loop feedback process:
- **Evaluate** actual operating performance using statistical measures.
- **Compare** actual performance to the established quality goals.
- **Act on the difference** — eliminate the cause of any deviation.

**Key Output:** A stable, conforming process (chronic-waste level is held, not improved).

**3. Quality Improvement (QI):**
The purpose is to **break** the chronic-waste level through a structured, project-based approach (Juran's Six-Step Breakthrough Sequence). Steps:
- Build awareness, identify Vital Few projects, organize teams, train, diagnose root causes, implement remedies, and hand over to operations.

**Key Output:** A new, lower baseline of waste (the breakthrough).

**How they interlink:** Quality Planning creates the design; Quality Control holds the design; Quality Improvement breaks through to a new design — and the cycle repeats.

---

**Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- Total defects = 600
- Total units produced = 30,000

**Step (i): Conformance Ratio**

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Total Units} - \text{Defects}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100 = \frac{30000 - 600}{30000} \times 100 = \frac{29400}{30000} \times 100 = 98.00\%
$$

**Step (ii): Pareto Table (sorted in descending order):**

| Rank | Defect Type | Frequency | Individual % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Burr | 240 | 40.00% | 40.00% |
| 2 | Cracks | 180 | 30.00% | 70.00% |
| 3 | Surface Roughness | 90 | 15.00% | 85.00% |
| 4 | Color Mismatch | 50 | 8.33% | 93.33% |
| 5 | Dimensional Error | 25 | 4.17% | 97.50% |
| 6 | Missing Label | 15 | 2.50% | 100.00% |
| | **Total** | **600** | | |

**Cumulative % of top 3 defects:**

$$
P_{cum}(3) = \frac{240 + 180 + 90}{600} \times 100 = \frac{510}{600} \times 100 = 85.00\%
$$

**Step (iii): Vital Few Identification**

Since the cumulative percentage of the **top 3 defects is 85.00%**, which exceeds the Juran 80% threshold, the **Vital Few** are:

- Burr (40.00%)
- Cracks (30.00%)
- Surface Roughness (15.00%)

The **Trivial Many** are: Color Mismatch, Dimensional Error, Missing Label.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A (b):**
> 1. **Do not skip the sorting step.** Juran's Pareto analysis is *invalid* if the defects are not arranged in descending order. KTU evaluators deduct up to 2 marks for unsorted data.
> 2. **Use the correct denominator.** The cumulative percentage denominator is the *sum of all defect frequencies (600)*, not the *total units produced (30,000)*. Mixing these two is the most common mistake.
> 3. **The 80% cutoff is a guideline, not an absolute rule.** State the cumulative percentage explicitly and then conclude which defects form the Vital Few. Writing "first three" without showing the calculation is incomplete and loses marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** What is meant by "Breakthrough" in Juran's framework? List and briefly explain the six steps of Juran's Breakthrough Sequence. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A CNC machine has a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 600 hours and a Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) of 50 hours.
- (i) Calculate the **availability** of the machine.
- (ii) If the design intent was 99% availability, identify the gap as per Juran's "Availability" sub-dimension and suggest two operational improvements.
- (iii) Explain how Juran's concept of "chronic waste" applies to this case. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Solution to Question B (a):**

**Breakthrough Definition:**
A **Breakthrough** in Juran's framework is a *structured, project-based, finite effort* to break the existing **chronic waste** level of an organization. It is **not** a continuous, gradual improvement — it is a discrete, planned, and resourced activity that produces a new, lower baseline of waste.

**Juran's Six-Step Breakthrough Sequence:**

1. **Build awareness of the need for improvement:** Top management and workforce must recognize that the current level of defects and waste is *unacceptable* and is *improvable*. Training and communication are key.

2. **Identify the specific Vital Few projects:** Apply the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** to quality data — find the 20% of causes that produce 80% of the defects. This is where quality projects should focus first.

3. **Organize for breakthrough — appoint sponsors and project leaders:** Each project needs a *sponsor* (usually a senior manager who provides resources and removes obstacles) and a *team* (cross-functional, with clear authority and a defined scope).

4. **Provide training and resources:** The project teams must be trained in diagnostic tools (statistical process control, root-cause analysis, FMEA, design of experiments). Without training, the project is reduced to opinion-based firefighting.

5. **Diagnose the root causes using data:** Use data-driven tools — not blame individuals, but identify the *systemic* causes of the chronic waste. Juran emphasized: **"Diagnosis must precede remedy."**

6. **Implement remedies, establish controls, and hand over to operations:** Once remedies are in place, **standardize the new process** through documented SOPs and control plans. Then hand the improved process back to line management. The new baseline becomes the next "chronic level" — and the cycle begins again.

---

**Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $MTBF = 600$ hours
- $MTTR = 50$ hours
- Design intent = 99% availability

**Step (i): Calculate Availability**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{600}{600 + 50} = \frac{600}{650} = 0.9231 = 92.31\%
$$

**Step (ii): Identify the Gap and Suggest Improvements**

$$
\text{Gap} = 99.00\% - 92.31\% = 6.69\%
$$

The machine is operating **6.69 percentage points below** the design intent. Juran's "Availability" sub-dimension of Fitness for Use is therefore *not satisfied*, and a breakthrough project is warranted.

**Two Operational Improvements:**

1. **Reduce MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Implement preventive maintenance, keep critical spares on the shop floor, and train technicians in rapid fault diagnosis. A reduction in $MTTR$ from 50 to 10 hours would lift availability to:

$$
A_{new} = \frac{600}{600 + 10} = \frac{600}{610} = 98.36\%
$$

2. **Increase MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Use root-cause analysis on the recurring failure modes, upgrade wear-prone components, and apply Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) practices.

**Step (iii): "Chronic Waste" Application**

The **chronic waste** in this case is the persistent 7.69% downtime (1 − 0.9231). It has become *accepted* as the normal state of operation — which is exactly what Juran warned against. He argued that such chronic waste is **improvable** through structured breakthrough projects, not simply controlled. The current 92.31% availability is the *baseline*; the breakthrough target is the design intent of 99%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B (b):**
> 1. **Always report availability as a decimal (0 to 1) AND as a percentage (0% to 100%).** Writing only "0.92" or only "92%" without the unit loses a mark in KTU valuations.
> 2. **The denominator of $A$ is $MTBF + MTTR$, not $MTBF$ alone.** This is the second most common error in availability problems.
> 3. **In the "suggest improvements" sub-part, examiners expect a *quantified* re-calculation** showing the impact of the proposed improvement. Simply listing "do preventive maintenance" without showing the new availability is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Juran's Quality Definition:** *Fitness for Use* — defined from the customer's perspective, not the producer's.
- [x] **Five Sub-Dimensions of Fitness for Use:** Quality of Design, Quality of Conformance, Availability, Safety, Field Use.
- [x] **Juran's Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement (Universal — applies to any industry).
- [x] **Quality Planning Output:** Validated product and process design.
- [x] **Quality Control Output:** Stable, conforming process (closed-loop feedback).
- [x] **Quality Improvement Output:** Breakthrough to a new, lower baseline.
- [x] **Pareto Principle (80/20):** ~80% of defects are caused by ~20% of causes — focus on the **Vital Few**.
- [x] **Breakthrough Sequence (6 Steps):** Awareness → Identify Vital Few → Organize → Train → Diagnose → Implement & Standardize.
- [x] **Chronic Waste:** The persistent, accepted defect level that Juran insists must itself be the target of improvement.
- [x] **Key Formulas to Memorize:** Conformance Ratio $CR$, Availability $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF+MTTR}$, Cumulative Pareto %.
- [x] **Examiner's Tip:** Always sort defects in descending order before Pareto analysis. Always use $\vert$ or `\vert` in LaTeX instead of `|` inside markdown tables.
- [x] **Distinguish from Deming:** Deming focused on *system-wide* statistical thinking and the 14 Points; Juran focused on the *managerial processes* and project-based improvement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crosby's Contribution to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crosby's Contribution to TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Philip Bayard Crosby — The Quality Guru

**Philip B. Crosby** (1924–2001) was an American quality management thought leader, author, and management consultant who revolutionized corporate thinking about quality during the late 20th century. He founded the **Philip Crosby Associates (PCA)** in 1979 and later the **Career IV Institute**. His landmark publications include **"Quality is Free"** (1979) and **"Quality Without Tears"** (1984).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Crosby defines quality as *"conformance to requirements, not elegance."* He introduced the world to the **Zero Defects** philosophy, asserting that quality is achieved through *prevention*, not inspection.

---

### 1.2 Crosby's Defining Principle: "Quality is Free"

Crosby's central thesis was revolutionary for its time:

$$ \text{Cost of Quality (COQ)} = \text{Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)} $$

His argument: **The money currently wasted on rework, scrap, warranty claims, and rework-labor should be reinvested into prevention. Therefore, doing things right the first time actually saves money — quality is FREE.**

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Driving Test" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine learning to drive. You have two choices:
> - **Approach A (Inspection):** Learn however you want, then take a strict driving test. Fail often, retake, retake… each retake costs time and money.
> - **Approach B (Prevention):** Train properly, follow traffic rules, attend a good school, and you pass on the first attempt.
>
> Crosby's **Quality is Free** says: Approach B costs less overall because you never pay for accidents, retests, or repairs. **Prevention is cheaper than inspection.**

---

### 1.4 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality (The Foundation)

These are **non-negotiable, absolute truths** Crosby insisted every manager must accept:

| # | Absolute | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** | Not "goodness" — meeting specifications exactly. |
| 2 | **Prevention, not Inspection** | Build it right the first time. |
| 3 | **Zero Defects Performance Standard** | The only acceptable target is ZERO defects. |
| 4 | **Measure Quality by Price of Non-Conformance** | The cost of NOT doing it right the first time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU exams:** The "Four Absolutes" is the **most frequently asked 3-mark question** in Module 2. Memorize them in the exact order above.

---

### 1.5 Glossary of Crosby's Key Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Conformance** | Meeting the documented requirements/specifications exactly. |
| **Non-Conformance** | Any failure to meet specified requirements (a defect). |
| **Zero Defects (ZD)** | A performance standard stating that defects are not acceptable; the target is to do it right the first time, every time. |
| **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** | All money wasted because work was not done correctly the first time. |
| **Cost of Quality (COQ)** | Sum of Prevention + Appraisal + Failure costs (P–A–F model). |
| **Quality Vaccine** | Crosby's 5-step inoculation management program (also called QVP). |
| **Maturity Grid** | 5-stage ladder measuring an organization's quality management maturity. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Absolutes — Expanded Logic

Crosby constructed his philosophy on the premise that quality is a **management responsibility**, not a worker problem. Each Absolute attacks a common industrial-age misconception.

#### Absolute 1: Quality = Conformance to Requirements

$$\text{Quality} = f(\text{Requirements being precisely met})$$

- Requirements must be **documented, measurable, and clearly communicated**.
- Crosby rejected subjective measures like "goodness," "excellence," or "luxury."

#### Absolute 2: Prevention is Preferable to Inspection

$$\text{Prevention Cost} \ll \text{Inspection Cost} + \text{Failure Cost}$$

- **Inspection** finds defects *after* they occur — too late, expensive to fix.
- **Prevention** removes the *cause* of defects before they occur — eliminates them entirely.

#### Absolute 3: Zero Defects is the Performance Standard

$$\text{Defect Rate Target} = 0$$

- Not a quota, not a goal — a **standard**.
- Inspired by the original **Martin Company** (Orlando) missile program where Crosby launched ZD in 1961.

#### Absolute 4: Measure Quality by Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)

$$\text{Quality Metric} = \text{PONC}$$

- The only meaningful KPI for top management.
- PONC = scrap + rework + warranty + customer complaints + lost goodwill.

---

### 2.2 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The P-A-F Model

Crosby classified all quality-related expenditures into **three (technically four) categories**. His COQ model is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

| Category | Meaning | Examples | Trigger |
|----------|---------|----------|---------|
| **Prevention (P)** | Costs to *prevent* defects | Training, process design, supplier capability audits, quality planning | BEFORE defect |
| **Appraisal (A)** | Costs to *detect* defects | Inspection, testing, calibration, audits | DURING/AFTER |
| **Internal Failure (IF)** | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime | BEFORE delivery |
| **External Failure (EF)** | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost customers | AFTER delivery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Insight:** As quality improves, **Prevention costs rise slightly, but Failure costs plummet dramatically**. The *total* COQ curve has a minimum — that minimum is the *economic* point. Crosby argued that economic point is essentially **zero defects**.

---

### 2.3 Crosby's Quality Vaccine (QVP) — 5-Step Inoculation

Crosby built the "**Quality Vaccine**" (also called the **QVP Process**) as a management framework to inoculate organizations against quality problems.

**The Five Steps:**

1. **Determination** — Top management commits *in writing* to a quality policy and roadmap.
2. **Skill** — All managers are formally trained in quality concepts and the Four Absolutes.
3. **Communication** — A monthly Quality Awareness program keeps the message alive organization-wide.
4. **Implementation** — A formal **Zero Defects program** is launched with clear goals, reward-recognition, and corrective-action loops.
5. **Verification** — A **Quality Management Maturity Grid** is used annually to benchmark progress.

---

### 2.4 Crosby's 14 Steps to Quality Improvement

Crosby's detailed action plan (introduced in *Quality is Free*) for any organization seeking transformation:

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Make it clear that management is committed to quality. |
| 2 | Form a Quality Council with representatives from each department. |
| 3 | Measure quality — identify the current cost of non-conformance. |
| 4 | Calculate the cost of quality (P-A-F) and communicate it. |
| 5 | Raise the **quality awareness** of all employees. |
| 6 | Take **corrective action** systematically to remove problems. |
| 7 | Establish a **Zero Defects program** with a formal launching ceremony. |
| 8 | Train **supervisors** in their role in quality improvement. |
| 9 | Hold a **Zero Defects Day** to launch the program. |
| 10 | Encourage individuals to set **improvement goals**. |
| 11 | Encourage **personal communication** to identify obstacles. |
| 12 | Recognize and **reward** those who meet quality goals. |
| 13 | Establish **Quality Councils** to communicate progress regularly. |
| 14 | **Repeat — do it all over again** to reinforce the culture. |

---

### 2.5 Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG)

Crosby's 5-stage diagnostic tool to assess where an organization stands on its quality journey.

| Stage | Description | Characteristic |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Stage 1: Uncertainty** | Management has no awareness; assumes quality problems are normal. | "We don't know why we have problems." |
| **Stage 2: Awakening** | Management recognizes quality as a problem but no formal systems. | Compliance-driven, firefighting. |
| **Stage 3: Enlightenment** | Management commits to quality and begins structured approach. | Quality policy, training programs. |
| **Stage 4: Wisdom** | Quality is fully integrated; prevention is the norm. | Cost reductions visible, ZD adopted. |
| **Stage 5: Certainty** | Quality is embedded in culture; continuous improvement is instinctive. | Lowest PONC, highest profitability. |

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables | Use |
|-------------------|----------|-----------|-----|
| **COQ (Total Quality Cost)** | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | P, A, IF, EF in monetary units | Compute total quality expenditure |
| **PONC (Price of Non-Conformance)** | $PONC = IF + EF + \text{hidden costs}$ | Sum of failure + opportunity costs | Quantify waste |
| **Conformance Cost** | $C_{conf} = P + A$ | P + A | Cost of doing things right |
| **Non-Conformance Cost** | $C_{non-conf} = IF + EF$ | IF + EF | Cost of doing things wrong |
| **Net Quality Savings (after ZD)** | $S = COQ_{\text{before}} - COQ_{\text{after}}$ | Cost difference | Justify quality investment |
| **Cost Avoidance (Crosby)** | $CA = (Rework\ hrs \times Rate) + (Scrap\ value) + Warranty$ | Components | KTU 14-mark numerical |
| **Per-Unit Quality Cost** | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{COQ}{N_{units}}$ | COQ / total units | Allocate quality cost to product |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Crosby's COQ model is widely applied in:

- **Manufacturing KPIs:** Scrap rate, rework hours, warranty budgets.
- **Software Engineering:** Defect density, MTBF, cost per bug fix.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital-acquired infection costs, readmission penalties.
- **Service Sectors:** Customer churn cost, SLA breach penalties.
- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford):** Supplier PONC reduction programs.
- **Aerospace (NASA, Boeing):** Zero-defect culture for safety-critical parts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Quality Cost Point

Crosby argued that the optimal economic point of operation is at **zero defects**. Mathematically:

Let $P$ be prevention cost, $A$ be appraisal cost, and $F(d)$ be the failure cost as a function of defect rate $d$.

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ(d) &= P(d) + A(d) + F(d)
\end{aligned}$$

Crosby's empirical observation:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{As } d \rightarrow 0: \\
&\quad P(d) \uparrow \text{ slightly (prevention must be maximum)} \\
&\quad A(d) \downarrow \text{ (fewer inspections needed once process is stable)} \\
&\quad F(d) \rightarrow 0 \text{ (no failures when } d=0\text{)} \\
&\therefore COQ(0) \approx P_{\max} + A_{\min} + 0 \\
&\text{This is the GLOBAL MINIMUM of COQ.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum COQ occurs at $d = 0$, contradicting the classical "economic optimum" view (Deming/Juran) which placed optimum at a small positive defect rate. This is Crosby's most controversial contribution.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Crosby's Cost of Quality Calculation

**Problem (Model Question):** A manufacturing plant reports the following annual quality data:

| Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|----------|------------------|
| Quality training & planning | 5 |
| Incoming material inspection | 3 |
| In-process testing | 2 |
| Rework | 6 |
| Scrap | 4 |
| Warranty claims | 8 |
| Customer returns | 3 |
| Lost customer goodwill (estimated) | 5 |

**Find:**
1. Total Prevention cost (P)
2. Total Appraisal cost (A)
3. Total Internal Failure cost (IF)
4. Total External Failure cost (EF)
5. Total Cost of Quality (COQ)
6. Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)
7. Conformance cost

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Prevention Cost (P)**
$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \text{Quality training \& planning} \\
  &= 5 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Appraisal Cost (A)**
$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Incoming inspection} + \text{In-process testing} \\
  &= 3 + 2 = 5 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Internal Failure Cost (IF)**
$$\begin{aligned}
IF &= \text{Rework} + \text{Scrap} \\
   &= 6 + 4 = 10 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: External Failure Cost (EF)**
$$\begin{aligned}
EF &= \text{Warranty} + \text{Returns} + \text{Goodwill loss} \\
   &= 8 + 3 + 5 = 16 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Total COQ**
$$\begin{aligned}
COQ &= P + A + IF + EF \\
    &= 5 + 5 + 10 + 16 \\
    &= 36 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**
$$\begin{aligned}
PONC &= IF + EF \\
      &= 10 + 16 = 26 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Conformance Cost**
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{conf} &= P + A \\
          &= 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Insight:** Failure costs (26L) are **2.6× higher** than Conformance costs (10L). According to Crosby, shifting even 5L from failure to prevention would yield a net saving of ~15L.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Crosby vs. Other Gurus

| Aspect | **Crosby** | **Deming** | **Juran** |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Quality Definition** | Conformance to requirements | A predictable degree of uniformity | Fitness for use |
| **Focus** | Prevention, Zero Defects | Statistical Process Control | Quality planning, Trilogy |
| **Cost View** | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) | Cost of poor quality reduction | Cost of poor quality |
| **Optimal Defect Level** | Zero | Small but acceptable | Zero is impractical, reduce chronic waste |
| **Training** | 5-step Vaccine | 14 Points, System of Profound Knowledge | Quality Trilogy (Q-P-C) |
| **Origin** | American (1970s) | American/Japanese | American |
| **Key Book** | *Quality is Free* (1979) | *Out of the Crisis* (1982) | *Juran's Quality Handbook* |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Net Savings from Zero Defects Implementation

Suppose a company spends $COQ_{\text{before}}$ today. After ZD, suppose:
- Prevention rises by $\Delta P$ (positive).
- Failure drops by $\Delta F$ (negative, i.e., a reduction).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings } (S) &= COQ_{\text{before}} - COQ_{\text{after}} \\
&= (P + A + F)_{\text{before}} - (P + A + F)_{\text{after}} \\
&= (P_{\text{before}} - P_{\text{after}}) + (A_{\text{before}} - A_{\text{after}}) + (F_{\text{before}} - F_{\text{after}}) \\
&= -\Delta P - \Delta A + \Delta F
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\Delta F \gg \Delta P + \Delta A$ in Crosby's experience, $S > 0$ — quality pays back.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
