| **Performed by** | Operators / shop-floor staff | Dedicated maintenance crew |
| **Frequency** | Daily / shift-wise | Weekly, monthly, yearly schedules |
| **Type of task** | Cleaning, lubrication, inspection | Overhaul, calibration, breakdown prevention |
| **Pillar number** | Pillar 1 | Pillar 2 |
| **Skill required** | Basic operator skill | Engineering / technical expertise |
| **Goal** | Preserve basic condition of machine | Prevent breakdown, extend equipment life |
| **Tool used** | 5S, one-point lessons | TPM card, condition-monitoring devices |
| **Loss addressed** | Deterioration, contamination | Wear-out, component failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write *“autonomous = done by operator, planned = done by maintenance”* and stop. That gets only **2 of 7 marks**. To score full marks, you must add: (i) the **pillar number**, (ii) **frequency**, (iii) the **type of task**, and (iv) the **specific loss it addresses**. Missing the **loss mapping** loses another 2 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the concept of **OEE** and the **Six Big Losses** in TPM. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is the gold standard for measuring manufacturing productivity. It is the product of three factors:

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

- **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Loading Time}}$
- **Performance** = $\dfrac{\text{Standard Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
- **Quality** = $\dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$

**World-class benchmark = 85 %**, average Indian plant = 60 %.

**The Six Big Losses** are the diagnostic map for OEE improvement:

| OEE Factor | Loss 1 | Loss 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Availability | Equipment Failure | Setup & Adjustments |
| Performance | Idling & Minor Stops | Reduced Speed |
| Quality | Defects in Process | Startup / Yield Losses |

Each loss is tackled by specific pillars — Availability losses by Pillars 1 & 2, Performance losses by Pillar 4, and Quality losses by Pillar 3.

#### (b) The following data refers to a CNC turning machine. Calculate **OEE**. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Planned production time (per week) | 100 hours |
| Planned downtime (preventive maintenance) | 10 hours |
| Unplanned downtime (breakdowns + setup) | 14 hours |
| Standard cycle time | 50 seconds / piece |
| Total pieces produced | 5,000 pieces |
| Defective pieces | 250 pieces |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loading Time:**

$$
T_L = 100 - 10 = 90 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_O = 90 - 14 = 76 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{76}{90} \times 100 = 84.44\%
$$

**Step 4 — Performance Rate:**

$$
P = \frac{50 \times 5000}{76 \times 3600} \times 100 = \frac{250000}{273600} \times 100 = 91.37\%
$$

**Step 5 — Quality Rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{5000 - 250}{5000} \times 100 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 6 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = \frac{84.44 \times 91.37 \times 95.00}{10000} = \mathbf{73.31\%}
$$

> **Conclusion:** OEE = **73.31 %**, which is below the world-class benchmark of 85 %. The plant must focus on reducing **breakdown downtime** (Availability) and **defect rate** (Quality).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Unit Conversion:** Many students forget to multiply by **3600** when converting hours to seconds. Standard cycle time is in **seconds/piece** but Operating Time is in **hours**. Forgetting this conversion leads to a final OEE that is off by a factor of 3600 and results in **0 marks** for the calculation sub-part.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- TPM was developed by **Seiichi Nakajima** in **1971** under **JIPM**.
- The word **"Total"** implies **5 dimensions** — Productivity, Quality, Maintenance, Effectiveness, Participation.
- **Three Zeros:** Zero breakdowns, Zero defects, Zero accidents.
- **Eight Pillars:** Autonomous, Planned, Quality Maintenance, Focused Improvement, Early Equipment Management, Training, SHE, TPM in Administration.
- **Five S (5S)** is the foundation on which Pillar 1 is built.
- **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**, target **≥ 85 %**.
- **Six Big Losses** map directly to the three OEE components.
- **MTBF** should be high; **MTTR** should be low.
- World-class plant benchmarks: Availability ≥ 90 %, Performance ≥ 95 %, Quality ≥ 99 %.
- **5-Whys, Pareto, PDCA, Poka-Yoke, MUST matrix, 4M analysis** are essential TPM tools.
- TPM is **not just for engineers** — it is a **management philosophy** requiring top-management commitment (often cited as the #1 reason for TPM failure).
- For OEE numerical problems, **always verify units** (hours vs. seconds) and **do not round intermediate steps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** If you write nothing else in a TPM answer, write *"Nakajima (1971), JIPM, Eight Pillars, OEE ≥ 85 %, MTBF, MTTR, Six Big Losses"* — these seven keywords alone earn partial credit and frame a strong answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Products and Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Products and Services # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Defining a Product (KTU 2024 Perspective) In the context of **Quality Engineering and Management**, a **Product** is formally defined as the output of any process or activity that satisfies a customer need or requirement. According to the **ISO 9001:2015** standard (a foundational reference in KTU syllabus), a product is the *"result of a process"* that can be tangible (a physical good) or intangible (a service, software, or processed information). > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Kotler-aligned):** A product is anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need. It includes physical objects, services, persons, places, organizations, and ideas. ### Intuitive Analogy 🍎 Think of a product like a **cooked meal served on a plate**. You can *see* it, *touch* it, *measure* its weight, count its calories, and store it. If the quality is bad, you can *return it* or *complain* about its physical defect (e.g., undercooked rice, spilled curry). The meal is a **Product** because it has **physical substance and form**. --- ## 1.2 Defining a Service A **Service** is an intangible output of a process, characterized by a simultaneous production and consumption activity. It is essentially **work performed for another** that creates value without transferring ownership of any physical material. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (ISO 9000:2015):** A service is a *type of product* in the form of an **intangible activity** or a *process* that results from the interaction between the **service provider**, the **customer**, and the **physical/information resources** involved. ### Intuitive Analogy 🚖 Consider an **Uber ride from your home to the college**. After the trip, what do you *own*? Nothing. You cannot touch the ride, store it, or resell it. The driver, the route, the car, and your presence all happened **simultaneously**. Once the ride ends, the service is *gone forever*. That is a **Service**. --- ## 1.3 The Goods–Services Continuum (KTU High-Yield Concept) In KTU 2024, students MUST understand that very few offerings are *pure* goods or *pure* services. Most real-world offerings lie on a **continuum**. > [!NOTE] > **Pure Goods ←—————— Mixed Offerings ——————→ Pure Services** > Example Left: Salt, Sugar, Cement > Example Middle: Restaurant Meal, Air Travel, Hospital Treatment > Example Right: Consulting, Teaching, Financial Advice, Meditation The **degree of tangibility** and **degree of customer involvement** are the two axes along which a product-service position is plotted. --- ## 1.4 The IHIP Characteristics of Services (MUST-MEMORIZE for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Pillars of Services (IHIP):** > 1. **I**ntangibility — Cannot be seen, touched, or stored > 2. **H**eterogeneity (Variability) — Quality varies provider-to-provider > 3. **I**nseparability — Production and consumption occur together > 4. **P**erishability — Unused capacity cannot be inventoried These four **IHIP** features distinguish services from products and form the backbone of all service quality questions in KTU examinations. --- ## 1.5 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the following are standardized reference parameters for this module: | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Description | |---|---|---| | **SERVQUAL Gap Score** | $SQ$ | Calculated as Perception − Expectation | | **5 Dimensions of Service Quality** | $D_1$ to $D_5$ | Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy | | **Goods-Services Continuum Position** | $P_{gs}$ | Ranges $0$ (pure good) to $1$ (pure service) | | **Product Life Cycle Stages** | $N$ | 4 stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline | | **Service Quality Gap** | $G_i$ | 5 gaps identified by Parasuraman et al. | | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI$ | Expressed in **percentage (%)** between **0% to 100%** | All quantitative performance metrics in quality management are **dimensionless ratios or percentages**, except time-based metrics which use **hours, days, or months**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Product Classification (Taxonomy) Products are classified into **three major categories** according to the KTU quality management framework: ### A. Tangible Products (Physical Goods) - **Consumer Goods:** Bought by end-users for personal use (rice, soap, phone) - **Industrial Goods:** Bought for business operations (machinery, raw materials) ### B. Intangible Products - **Services:** Banking, healthcare, education - **Digital Products:** Software, e-books, music downloads ### C. Augmented Products A product is not just its physical form. It includes the **core product**, the **actual product**, and the **augmented product** (warranty, service, brand). > [!NOTE] > **Three Levels of a Product (Levitt's Model — High Yield for KTU):** > - **Core Product:** The fundamental benefit (e.g., a phone gives *"connectivity"*) > - **Actual Product:** The physical features and design > - **Augmented Product:** After-sales service, warranty, brand reputation --- ## 2.2 Service Classification (Based on Customer Involvement & Customization) | Service Type | Customization | Customer Contact | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Professional Service** | High | High | Lawyer, Doctor | | **Mass Service** | Low | Low | Public Transport, Cinema | | **Service Shop** | Medium | Medium | Bank Branch, Salon | | **Factory Service** | Low | Low | Laundry, Car Service | --- ## 2.3 The 5 Gaps Model of Service Quality (Parasuraman, Berry & Zeithaml — 1985) This is a **TOP-RATED KTU Exam Topic**. The model identifies **5 critical gaps** that cause service failure: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gap 1:** Difference between **customer expectations** and **management's perception** of those expectations. > > **Gap 2:** Difference between management's perception and the **actual service specification** (quality standards set). > > **Gap 3:** Difference between service specification and **actual service delivery** (performance gap). > > **Gap 4:** Difference between service delivery and **external communication** about the service (promise gap). > > **Gap 5 (KEY GAP):** Difference between **customer expectations** and **perceived service** — the resultant service quality experienced. --- ## 2.4 The SERVQUAL Model (Service Quality Formula) The **SERVQUAL** instrument measures service quality across 5 dimensions using a **22-item questionnaire** based on the formula: $$SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$ Where: - $SQ$ = Overall **Service Quality** score - $P_i$ = **Perception** of customer about service dimension $i$ - $E_i$ = **Expectation** of customer about service dimension $i$ - $i$ = Item number, ranging from $1$ to $22$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **negative $SQ$** value indicates **poor service quality** (expectations exceeded by actual delivery). A **positive $SQ$** value indicates **exceeding expectations** (exceptional service quality). For KTU exams, $SQ \leq 0$ is generally required for customer satisfaction. ### The 5 SERVQUAL Dimensions ($D_1$ to $D_5$): | Dimension | Code | What It Measures | |---|---|---| | **Tangibles** | $D_1$ | Physical facilities, equipment, staff appearance | | **Reliability** | $D_2$ | Ability to perform the promised service dependably | | **Responsiveness** | $D_3$ | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | | **Assurance** | $D_4$ | Knowledge and courtesy of staff, and their ability to inspire trust | | **Empathy** | $D_5$ | Caring, individualized attention given to customers | The total SERVQUAL score for one dimension is: $$SQ_{D_j} = \frac{1}{n_j} \sum_{k=1}^{n_j} (P_{jk} - E_{jk})$$ Where $n_j$ is the number of items in dimension $j$, $j \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$. --- ## 2.5 Product Life Cycle (PLC) A KTU-favorite concept used to plan quality strategies across time: $$S(t) = S_{\max} \cdot f(t)$$ Where $S(t)$ is the **sales** at time $t$ and $f(t)$ is the **S-curve function** of the product's life. ### The 4 PLC Stages: 1. **Introduction Stage** — Low sales, high costs, low profit, **quality focus on conformance** 2. **Growth Stage** — Rapid sales increase, competitors enter, **quality focus on reliability** 3. **Maturity Stage** — Sales peak, intense competition, **quality focus on differentiation** 4. **Decline Stage** — Sales fall, product withdrawal, **quality focus on cost reduction** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pearl:** Quality strategies differ at each PLC stage. Companies cannot use a *"one-size-fits-all"* quality approach throughout the product life cycle. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Equation | Application | |---|---|---| | **Service Quality Score** | $SQ = P - E$ | SERVQUAL measurement | | **Dimension-wise SERVQUAL** | $SQ_{D_j} = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum (P_{jk} - E_{jk})$ | Dimension analysis | | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \frac{N_s}{N_t} \times 100\%$ | Total survey percentage | | **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U}$ | Six Sigma metric | | **Yield Rate** | $Y = \frac{U - D}{U} \times 100\%$ | Process effectiveness | | **Process Capability** | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Quality control | | **First Pass Yield** | $FPY = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | Manufacturing quality | | **Goods-Service Position** | $P_{gs} = \frac{\text{Tangibility Score}}{\text{Max Tangibility}}$ | Continuum placement | | **Service Gap 5 (Resultant)** | $G_5 = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)$ | Gap closure effectiveness | > [!WARNING] > Always express $CSI$ as a **percentage (%)**. For SERVQUAL, remember the direction: **$SQ = P - E$**, NOT $E - P$. This is the most common error in KTU answer scripts. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The Products and Services framework is the foundation for: - **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — Designing quality into both goods and services - **Six Sigma ($6\sigma$)** — Reducing defects in both manufacturing and service processes - **ISO 9001 QMS Certification** — Applicable to both product manufacturers and service providers - **Lean Manufacturing** — Eliminating waste in physical and intangible processes - **Service Engineering** — Designing reliable, repeatable service delivery systems - **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** — Translating customer needs (VOICE OF CUSTOMER) into product/service specifications # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Service Quality Gap Equation The **resultant service quality gap** ($G_5$) experienced by the customer is *not* a standalone variable. It is the **cumulative function** of all upstream gaps in the service design and delivery chain. Let us derive this step by step. ### Step 1: Define the Four Provider-Side Gaps We begin with the formal definition of each internal gap as proposed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985): **Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap:** $$G_1 = E_{cust} - P_{mgmt}$$ **Textual Logic:** This is the difference between what the *customer expects* ($E_{cust}$) and what the *management perceives* the customer to expect ($P_{mgmt}$). If management underestimates expectations, $G_1$ is positive (a bad sign). **Gap 2 — Standards Gap:** $$G_2 = P_{mgmt} - S_{spec}$$ **Textual Logic:** The difference between management's perception of customer expectations and the **service quality specifications** ($S_{spec}$) actually set by the firm. A positive value means the standards are too low. **Gap 3 — Performance Gap:** $$G_3 = S_{spec} - D_{actual}$$ **Textual Logic:** The difference between the standards set and the **actual service delivered** ($D_{actual}$) by the front-line employees. Even good standards fail if not delivered. **Gap 4 — Communication Gap:** $$G_4 = D_{actual} - C_{promised}$$ **Textual Logic:** The difference between the service actually delivered and what was **promised through external communication** ($C_{promised}$) such as advertising, brochures, and sales pitches. ### Step 2: Define Gap 5 (The Customer-Experienced Gap) The customer experiences the gap between their **expected service** and the **perceived service** they received: $$G_5 = E_{cust} - S_{perceived}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Chain of Gaps Substituting the upstream gap definitions into the downstream perceived service: $$\begin{aligned} S_{perceived} &= C_{promised} - G_4 \\ &= C_{promised} - (D_{actual} - C_{promised}) \\ &= 2 \cdot C_{promised} - D_{actual} \end{aligned}$$ **Textual Logic:** The customer perceives the service based on what was *promised* minus any *delivery shortfall* from that promise. Continuing the substitution: $$\begin{aligned} G_5 &= E_{cust} - S_{perceived} \\ &= E_{cust} - (C_{promised} - G_4) \\ &= E_{cust} - C_{promised} + G_4 \end{aligned}$$ **Textual Logic:** The customer's experienced gap equals the **expectation–promise mismatch** plus the **communication-induced delivery gap**. ### Step 4: Final Functional Form In its general functional form, $G_5$ is expressed as a function of all four upstream gaps: $$\boxed{G_5 = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** To close $G_5$ (improve service quality), management MUST simultaneously close $G_1, G_2, G_3$, and $G_4$. Closing one gap while others remain open is **ineffective**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Products vs Services The following **comparison matrix** is a KTU exam favourite: | Attribute | Product (Good) | Service | |---|---|---| | **Tangibility** | Tangible, physical form | Intangible, abstract | | **Storage** | Can be inventoried | Cannot be stored | | **Transport** | Can be shipped | Cannot be transported (provider moves) | | **Ownership Transfer** | Yes (goods change hands) | No (only access/use rights) | | **Customer Contact** | Low to medium | High (often co-created) | | **Production-Consumption** | Separated in time | Simultaneous | | **Variability** | Low (standardized) | High (human-dependent) | | **Quality Measurement** | Objective, easy | Subjective, complex | | **Return Possible?** | Yes (defective return) | Difficult or impossible | | **Quality Dimensions** | Conformance, Reliability, Performance | SERVQUAL 5 dimensions | | **Regulation** | BIS, ISO product standards | ISO 9001 service standards | | **Example** | Smartphone, Pen | Hospital treatment, Tuition | --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A hospital surveyed 100 patients. Patients rated expectations and perceptions on a 1–5 scale across 5 SERVQUAL dimensions. The aggregated results are: | Dimension | $E$ (Expectation) | $P$ (Perception) | $n_j$ (items) | |---|---|---|---| | Tangibles | 4.5 | 3.8 | 4 | | Reliability | 4.7 | 4.0 | 5 | | Responsiveness | 4.6 | 3.5 | 4 | | Assurance | 4.8 | 4.2 | 4 | | Empathy | 4.4 | 3.6 | 5 | **Step 1: Calculate Gap per Item per Dimension** For Tangibles: $$SQ_{D_1} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=1}^{4} (P_{1k} - E_{1k})$$ Using the aggregate values: $$SQ_{D_1} = P_1 - E_1 = 3.8 - 4.5 = -0.7$$ **Step 2: Repeat for All Dimensions** $$\begin{aligned} SQ_{D_2} &= 4.0 - 4.7 = -0.7 \\ SQ_{D_3} &= 3.5 - 4.6 = -1.1 \\ SQ_{D_4} &= 4.2 - 4.8 = -0.6 \\ SQ_{D_5} &= 3.6 - 4.4 = -0.8 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the Overall SERVQUAL Score** $$SQ_{overall} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} SQ_{D_j}$$ $$SQ_{overall} = \frac{1}{5} \times (-0.7 - 0.7 - 1.1 - 0.6 - 0.8)$$ $$SQ_{overall} = \frac{-3.9}{5} = -0.78$$ **Step 4: Interpretation** > [!IMPORTANT] > The overall $SQ = -0.78$ is **negative**, meaning the hospital is **failing to meet customer expectations** across all dimensions. The **largest gap is in Responsiveness** ($-1.1$), indicating that patients are most dissatisfied with **prompt service delivery**. The hospital should prioritize training staff to respond faster to patient needs. --- ## 3.4 The Goods-Services Continuum — Quantitative Position For a given offering, its position on the continuum can be quantified as: $$P_{gs} = 1 - T_{score}$$ Where $T_{score}$ is the **tangibility score** normalized to the range $[0, 1]$: $$T_{score} = \frac{\text{Number of tangible features}}{\text{Total number of features}}$$ - If $P_{gs} = 0$ → **Pure Good** (e.g., a bar of gold) - If $P_{gs} = 1$ → **Pure Service** (e.g., a lecture) - If $0 < P_{gs} < 1$ → **Hybrid Offering** (e.g., restaurant meal) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Goods–Services Continuum (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pure Good
Salt, Sugar] --> B[Core Good with
Minor Service
Appliance + Warranty] B --> C[Hybrid Offering
Restaurant, Airline] C --> D[Core Service with
Minor Goods
Hospital + Medicines] D --> E[Pure Service
Consulting, Teaching] style A fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:3px style D fill:#e1e8ff,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#f3e1ff,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Move from left to right, the offering becomes *less tangible* and *more service-dominated*. The middle (red zone) is where most real businesses operate. --- ## 4.2 The 5 Gaps Model of Service Quality ```mermaid flowchart TB CUST[Customer
Expectations]:::customer MGT[Management
Perception]:::management SPEC[Service Quality
Specifications]:::spec DELIV[Service Delivery
by Frontline Staff]:::delivery COMM[External
Communication
and Promises]:::comm PERC[Customer
Perceived Service]:::perceived CUST -->|Gap 1: Knowledge Gap| MGT MGT -->|Gap 2: Standards Gap| SPEC SPEC -->|Gap 3: Performance Gap| DELIV DELIV -->|Gap 4: Communication Gap| COMM CUST -->|Gap 5: Resultant Gap| PERC COMM --> PERC classDef customer fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef management fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef spec fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef delivery fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef comm fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef perceived fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Gap 5 is the *sum effect* of all 4 provider-side gaps. The customer only ever *sees* Gap 5. They do not see the internal operational gaps. --- ## 4.3 The IHIP Characteristics of Services (Conceptual Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB SVC[Service
Characteristics]:::service I[Intangibility
Cannot be touched or seen]:::int H[Heterogeneity
Quality varies provider to provider]:::het IS[Inseparability
Production equals consumption]:::ins P[Perishability
Cannot be stored or inventoried]:::per SVC --> I SVC --> H SVC --> IS SVC --> P I --> I1[Example: Cannot test-drive a haircut before buying]:::example H --> H1[Example: Same doctor, different outcomes]:::example IS --> IS1[Example: Teacher teaches only when students are present]:::example P --> P1[Example: Empty hotel room is lost revenue forever]:::example classDef service fill:#1976d2,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef int fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef het fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ins fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef per fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef example fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 The Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Sales vs Time ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 1: Introduction
Low sales, high investment]:::intro B[Stage 2: Growth
Rapid sales rise]:::growth C[Stage 3: Maturity
Peak sales, saturation]:::maturity D[Stage 4: Decline
Sales fall, withdrawal]:::decline A --> B --> C --> D A -.-> Q1[Quality Focus:
Conformance and basic reliability]:::qf B -.-> Q2[Quality Focus:
Performance improvement]:::qf C -.-> Q3[Quality Focus:
Differentiation from competitors]:::qf D -.-> Q4[Quality Focus:
Cost reduction]:::qf classDef intro fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef growth fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef maturity fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decline fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef qf fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.5 SERVQUAL Model — The 5 Dimensions Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SQ[SERVQUAL
Service Quality Score]:::sq D1[Tangibles
Physical evidence]:::dim D2[Reliability
Performs dependably]:::dim D3[Responsiveness
Prompt service]:::dim D4[Assurance
Trust and confidence]:::dim D5[Empathy
Caring attention]:::dim SQ --> D1 SQ --> D2 SQ --> D3 SQ --> D4 SQ --> D5 D1 --> F1[Equipment, Staff appearance, Communication materials]:::feat D2 --> F2[Accurate billing, On-time service, Correct records]:::feat D3 --> F3[Quick response, Willingness to help, No waiting]:::feat D4 --> F4[Competent staff, Polite behaviour, Safe transactions]:::feat D5 --> F5[Personalized attention, Understanding needs, Convenience]:::feat classDef sq fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef dim fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef feat fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.6 Three Levels of a Product (Levitt's Model) ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Product]:::product C[Core Product
The fundamental benefit
e.g., Connectivity]:::core A[Actual Product
Physical features and design
e.g., Smartphone with 6GB RAM]:::actual AG[Augmented Product
Warranty, Service, Brand
e.g., Free cloud storage, 1-year warranty]:::aug P --> C C --> A A --> AG classDef product fill:#311b92,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef core fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actual fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef aug fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#1a237e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pearl:** In modern marketing, the **augmented product** is what creates competitive advantage. Two smartphones may have identical core and actual levels, but the *augmented level* (warranty, software updates, customer care) differentiates them. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Service. List its four key characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **service** is an intangible output of a process that results from the interaction between a service provider and a customer, creating value without transferring ownership of any physical good. It is work performed for another that satisfies a need. The **four key characteristics of services (IHIP)** are: 1. **Intangibility** — Services cannot be seen, touched, tasted, or stored before purchase. The customer buys a *promise*, not a physical product. 2. **Heterogeneity (Variability)** — Service quality varies from provider to provider, and even from one occasion to another for the same provider, due to human involvement. 3. **Inseparability** — Services are produced and consumed **simultaneously**. The customer is often *present* during production (e.g., a haircut, a classroom lecture). 4. **Perishability** — Services cannot be inventoried. An unsold service (e.g., an empty airline seat at takeoff) is *lost forever* and cannot be recovered. > [!NOTE] > **Word Count Guidance (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + 4 characteristics with one-line explanation each (2 marks). Keep it crisp. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between a Product and a Service with at least four points. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | S.No. | Aspect | Product | Service | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Tangibility** | Tangible, physical form | Intangible, abstract | | 2 | **Storage** | Can be stored and inventoried | Cannot be stored (perishable) | | 3 | **Ownership** | Ownership is transferred to buyer | Ownership is NOT transferred | | 4 | **Production** | Produced first, then consumed | Produced and consumed simultaneously | | 5 | **Customer Involvement** | Low (passive) | High (often co-creator) | | 6 | **Quality Assessment** | Objective, measurable | Subjective, perception-based | | 7 | **Return** | Can be returned if defective | Cannot be returned | | 8 | **Example** | A laptop | A software training session | > [!NOTE] > **3-Mark Strategy:** Use a **comparison table** with at least 4 rows. A table scores higher than a paragraph in KTU valuation. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1): 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **five-gap model of service quality** by Parasuraman, Berry, and Zeithaml. With a suitable diagram, describe each gap and its impact on customer-perceived service quality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A restaurant conducted a SERVQUAL survey with 200 customers. Compute the overall service quality score and identify the dimension requiring the most urgent improvement. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a): The **Five-Gap Model**, developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985)**, is a framework used to identify and analyze the sources of service quality problems. The model identifies **five critical gaps** — four on the *provider's side* and one on the *customer's side*. **Gap 1 — The Knowledge Gap:** $$\text{Gap 1} = E_{cust} - P_{mgmt}$$ This gap arises when **management does not accurately understand customer expectations**. Causes include inadequate market research, poor communication between management and frontline staff, and too many layers of hierarchy. Impact: Wrong service design from the start. **Gap 2 — The Standards Gap:** $$\text{Gap 2} = P_{mgmt} - S_{spec}$$ Even when management knows what customers want, they may **fail to set the right service standards**. Causes include insufficient resources, poor planning, and lack of clear service goals. Impact: The service is designed for a lower quality than the customer expects. **Gap 3 — The Performance Gap:** $$\text{Gap 3} = S_{spec} - D_{actual}$$ The service standards are set correctly, but **the actual delivery falls short**. Causes include employee incompetence, lack of training, poor technology, or low motivation. Impact: The promised service is not delivered. **Gap 4 — The Communication Gap:** $$\text{Gap 4} = D_{actual} - C_{promised}$$ The service delivered is acceptable, but the **promises made through advertisements, brochures, or sales pitches** create unrealistic expectations. Causes include over-promising in marketing. Impact: Customer feels cheated even though actual service is good. **Gap 5 — The Resultant Gap (Customer-Experienced):** $$\text{Gap 5} = E_{cust} - S_{perceived}$$ This is the **cumulative effect** of all four upstream gaps. It is the gap between what the customer expected and what they actually perceived they received. This is the **only gap visible to the customer** and the only gap the customer can evaluate. $$\boxed{G_5 = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)}$$ **Impact on Quality:** The wider the five gaps, the poorer the service quality. Quality improvement programs in service organizations must focus on **simultaneously closing all four provider-side gaps** to close Gap 5. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Given Data:** | Dimension | $E$ (Expectation) | $P$ (Perception) | $n_j$ | |---|---|---|---| | Tangibles | 4.4 | 3.9 | 4 | | Reliability | 4.6 | 4.0 | 5 | | Responsiveness | 4.5 | 3.2 | 4 | | Assurance | 4.7 | 4.3 | 4 | | Empathy | 4.3 | 3.7 | 5 | **Step 1: Calculate Per-Item Gap for Each Dimension** $$\begin{aligned} SQ_{D_1} &= 3.9 - 4.4 = -0.5 \quad \text{(Tangibles)} \\ SQ_{D_2} &= 4.0 - 4.6 = -0.6 \quad \text{(Reliability)} \\ SQ_{D_3} &= 3.2 - 4.5 = -1.3 \quad \text{(Responsiveness)} \\ SQ_{D_4} &= 4.3 - 4.7 = -0.4 \quad \text{(Assurance)} \\ SQ_{D_5} &= 3.7 - 4.3 = -0.6 \quad \text{(Empathy)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Overall SERVQUAL Score** $$SQ_{overall} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} SQ_{D_j} = \frac{1}{5} \times (-0.5 - 0.6 - 1.3 - 0.4 - 0.6)$$ $$SQ_{overall} = \frac{-3.4}{5} = -0.68$$ **Step 3: Identify the Weakest Dimension** The **largest negative gap** is in **Responsiveness** with $SQ_{D_3} = -1.3$, which means customers are most dissatisfied with the **promptness of service** at the restaurant. **Step 4: Recommendation** > [!IMPORTANT] > The restaurant should **immediately focus on Responsiveness improvement** — reduce waiting time, train staff for faster service, and improve order-to-delivery time. The overall $SQ = -0.68$ indicates **service quality is below customer expectations** and needs structured improvement. --- ### Question B (Choice 2): 14 Marks — Internal Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **goods–services continuum** with examples. Why is it important for a quality engineer to understand the position of an offering on this continuum? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **four stages of the Product Life Cycle (PLC)** with specific quality strategies appropriate for each stage. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a): The **Goods–Services Continuum** is a conceptual framework proposed by **Christopher Lovelock and Lauren Wright** that recognizes that most market offerings are *not* purely goods or purely services, but lie somewhere along a spectrum based on the degree of **tangibility** and the nature of the exchange. **The Continuum Structure:** 1. **Pure Tangible Good (Far Left):** No service component at all. Examples: Salt, sugar, raw cement, a bar of gold. The customer buys *only* the physical item. 2. **Tangible Good with Accompanying Services:** A physical product is accompanied by some service. Examples: A car with a warranty, a washing machine with installation service, a smartphone with cloud support. 3. **Hybrid Offering (Middle):** Roughly equal mix of goods and services. Examples: A restaurant meal (food + service), air travel (transport + in-flight service), a hospital treatment (consultation + medication + room). 4. **Service with Accompanying Minor Goods:** Primarily a service supported by some physical elements. Examples: A flight (service) with a meal and headphones (goods), a salon service with shampoo products. 5. **Pure Service (Far Right):** No physical component. Examples: A consulting session, financial advice, a therapy session, a lecture, meditation training. **Importance for Quality Engineers:** > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **Quality Measurement Differs:** Tangible goods can be measured with **objective metrics** (dimensions, weight, defect count). Services require **subjective metrics** (SERVQUAL, customer satisfaction scores). > > 2. **Quality Standards Differ:** Goods use **BIS, ISO product standards**. Services use **ISO 9001 service standards** and SLAs (Service Level Agreements). > > 3. **Quality Control Techniques Differ:** Goods use **statistical process control (SPC)**, acceptance sampling, and Six Sigma DMAIC. Services use **service blueprinting, poka-yoke in service design**, and employee training programs. > > 4. **Customer Involvement Differs:** In pure goods, customer is *passive*. In pure services, customer is *co-producer*. Quality engineers must design **self-service error-proofing** for hybrid offerings. > > 5. **Storage and Inventory:** Goods can be inventoried; services cannot. This affects **capacity planning, demand forecasting, and yield management**. #### Model Solution to Part (b): The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** describes the stages a product goes through from introduction to withdrawal from the market. Quality strategies must evolve with each stage. **Stage 1: Introduction** - **Characteristics:** Low sales, high marketing costs, low or negative profit, few or no competitors. - **Quality Focus:** **Conformance to design specifications** and **basic reliability**. The product must work as promised. - **Engineering Strategy:** Extensive testing, prototype validation, beta testing, ISO 9001 process compliance. - **Example:** A new electric vehicle model — quality focus is on safety and reliability. **Stage 2: Growth** - **Characteristics:** Rapid sales growth, increasing profit, competitors entering the market. - **Quality Focus:** **Performance improvement** and **feature enhancement** to differentiate from competitors. - **Engineering Strategy:** Continuous improvement (Kaizen), Six Sigma DMAIC projects, voice-of-customer analysis. - **Example:** Smartphones adding better cameras and processors. **Stage 3: Maturity** - **Characteristics:** Peak sales, intense competition, market saturation, profit begins to decline. - **Quality Focus:** **Differentiation and brand positioning** through superior quality and service. Reliability and durability become critical. - **Engineering Strategy:** QFD (Quality Function Deployment), benchmarking against competitors, customer loyalty programs. - **Example:** Premium brands offering extended warranty and superior after-sales service. **Stage 4: Decline** - **Characteristics:** Falling sales, dropping profit, product being phased out. - **Quality Focus:** **Cost reduction** while maintaining acceptable quality. Eliminate non-value-adding features. - **Engineering Strategy:** Value engineering, lean manufacturing, design simplification, and standardization. - **Example:** Older car models being sold with minimal variants. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pearl:** The *quality strategy* is **NOT the same** across PLC stages. A common mistake is to apply *growth-stage quality tactics* (heavy R&D) to a *mature product* (which needs cost reduction and differentiation). This mismatch is a frequent 14-mark KTU question trap. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Products & Services Questions:** > > 1. **Reversing the SERVQUAL Formula:** Many students write $SQ = E - P$ instead of $SQ = P - E$. This is an **automatic 0** for that sub-question. **Remember: Perception minus Expectation.** > > 2. **Confusing IHIP:** Writing IHIP as *Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability* but explaining *Inseparability* as *Heterogeneity*. Markers deduct 1 mark per wrong definition. > > 3. **Mixing Goods and Services Continuum:** Calling the continuum the *product life cycle*. These are **two different concepts**. The continuum classifies offerings; the PLC describes a single product's journey. > > 4. **Forgetting the 5th Gap:** When listing gaps, students often stop at 4 gaps. Gap 5 (*the resultant, customer-experienced gap*) is the **most important** gap and the one most frequently asked. > > 5. **No Diagrams:** A 7-mark question without a labelled diagram loses at least **1 to 2 marks**. Always include a **labelled SERVQUAL gap diagram** or a **PLC sales curve**. > > 6. **PLC Quality Strategy Mismatch:** Saying *"focus on innovation"* in the **decline** stage. Innovation is for the *introduction* stage. Decline requires **cost reduction**, not innovation. > > 7. **Generic Answers Without Examples:** KTU examiners reward **industry-specific examples** (e.g., "In hospitals, Gap 3 occurs when..."). Generic textbook phrasing is penalized. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Products and Services (Module 3)** > > ✅ **Product Definition** — Tangible output of a process; satisfies customer need. Three levels: Core, Actual, Augmented. > > ✅ **Service Definition** — Intangible, simultaneous production-consumption, no ownership transfer. > > ✅ **IHIP** — Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability. **MUST memorize the order.** > > ✅ **Goods-Services Continuum** — 5 positions: Pure Good → Tangible + Service → Hybrid → Service + Goods → Pure Service. > > ✅ **SERVQUAL Formula** — $SQ = P - E$. Five dimensions: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy. > > ✅ **Gap Model** — 4 provider-side gaps + 1 customer-experienced gap (Gap 5). $G_5 = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)$. > > ✅ **Product Life Cycle** — 4 stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline. Each with **distinct quality strategies**. > > ✅ **Quality Differences** — Goods → objective metrics; Services → subjective metrics (SERVQUAL). > > ✅ **Customer Role** — Passive in goods, **co-producer** in services. > > ✅ **Kotler's Definition** — A product is anything that can be offered to satisfy a want or need. > > ✅ **ISO 9001** — Applies to both product manufacturers and service providers. > > ✅ **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — $FPY = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$. > > ✅ **Three Levels of Product (Levitt)** — Core (benefit), Actual (form), Augmented (extra value). > > ✅ **Stereotypical KTU Words to Use in Answers** — *Tangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability, SERVQUAL, Gap 5, Voice of Customer, Conformance, Reliability, Augmentation, Co-production*.

topics.title: Classification of Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Quality Engineering and Management**, **Classification of Services** refers to the systematic categorization of intangible economic activities based on their operational characteristics, customer interaction intensity, degree of customization, and the nature of the delivery mechanism. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this classification serves as the foundational framework for designing appropriate **Service Quality (SERVQUAL)** models, benchmarking tools, and quality control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Services are economic activities offered by one party to another, characterized by **intangibility**, **inseparability**, **variability**, and **perishability** (the four cardinal characteristics of services, originally coined by **Valarie A. Zeithaml** and later formalized in services marketing literature). Classification is the prerequisite step before applying any Total Quality Management (TQM) or Six-Sigma framework to a service organization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **restaurant** versus ordering a **swiggy food delivery**. Both deliver food (a product), but the *service* wrapping around it is fundamentally different.

- In the **restaurant**, you sit, a waiter attends to you, the chef cooks fresh, and the ambience is part of the offering → **high-contact, people-based, customized service**.
- In **swiggy delivery**, the food travels in a standardized box via a courier; there is **low interpersonal contact**, but **high process dependency**.

If a Quality Engineer treats both with the same quality tool (e.g., a statistical control chart on weight of food alone), the *restaurant* complaints about ambience or behaviour of staff will go undetected. This is exactly why **classification of services** is taught first — different service classes demand **different quality measurement instruments**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *Classify the service first, then choose the quality tool. Never the reverse.*

## 1.3 The Four Cardinal Characteristics of Services (Foundation Block)

Before classification, every KTU student must memorize the **four I's of services** that distinguish them from manufactured goods:

| Characteristic | Definition | Engineering/Quality Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Intangibility** | Services cannot be touched, seen, tasted, or stored | Quality must be measured via **surrogate indicators** (waiting time, ambience score, NPS rating) |
| **Inseparability** | Production and consumption happen simultaneously | Quality defects cannot be recalled; **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** at the point of delivery is critical |
| **Variability (Heterogeneity)** | Service quality varies by provider, time, and customer | Requires **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **standardized SOPs** |
| **Perishability** | Unused service capacity (e.g., empty airline seat) is lost forever | Demands **Yield Management** and demand forecasting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Frameworks Used in KTU:** The most widely referenced classification systems are:
> 1. **Lovelock's Five-Category Classification** (1983) — based on the *type of service action* and *who/what is the direct recipient*.
> 2. **Chase's Service Process Matrix** (1978) — based on *degree of customer contact* and *customization*.
> 3. **Kotler's Classification** — based on *consumer vs. industrial user*.
> 4. **Rust's Customer Relationship Matrix** — based on *duration and intensity of relationships*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lovelock's Five-Category Classification of Services (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

Christopher **Lovelock** (1983) proposed classifying services based on two axes:
- **Axis 1 (Type of Action):** Tangible actions vs. Intangible actions
- **Axis 2 (Direct Recipient):** People vs. Possessions

This produces a **2 × 2 matrix with five categories** (because the "People–Tangible Actions" quadrant is split into two sub-categories).

### 2.1.1 Category Breakdown (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Category 1: People Processing (Tangible Action → People)**
- The customer must **physically present** themselves to receive the service.
- Examples: Hospitals, Hair salons, Fitness centers, Airlines (passenger transport), Hotels.
- **Quality Challenge:** Variability due to individual customer cooperation (e.g., patient may not follow doctor's advice).

**Category 2: Possession Processing (Tangible Action → Possessions)**
- The customer's *physical possession* is the recipient, not the customer.
- Examples: Car repair, Laundry, Lawn care, Appliance maintenance, Pest control.
- **Quality Challenge:** Quality is judged after the service is delivered, requiring **inspection windows**.

**Category 3: Mental Stimulus Processing (Intangible Action → People)**
- The service acts on the *mind* of the customer; no physical transformation.
- Examples: Education, Entertainment, Broadcasting, Museums, Psychotherapy.
- **Quality Challenge:** Difficult to measure; relies on **subjective feedback scales** (e.g., Likert-scale course evaluations).

**Category 4: Information Processing (Intangible Action → Possessions)**
- The service acts on the customer's *assets, information, or data*.
- Examples: Banking, Insurance, Consulting, Accounting, Legal services.
- **Quality Challenge:** Confidentiality (data security) and **accuracy of information** become paramount.

**Category 5: Hybrid / Multi-Category Services**
- Most real-world services span multiple categories (e.g., a hospital provides People Processing + Information Processing for billing).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When asked "Classify the following service," always apply *both* the action type and recipient type explicitly to score full marks.

## 2.2 Chase's Service Process Matrix (1978)

**Richard Chase** classified services along a continuum based on:
- **Degree of Customer Contact** (X-axis: Low → High)
- **Customization / Flexibility** (Y-axis: Low → High)

This yields four quadrants:

| Quadrant | Customer Contact | Customization | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1 – Mass Service** | Low | Low | Airlines (economy), ATMs, Toll booths, Fast food chains |
| **Q2 – Service Factory** | Low | High | Custom software firms, Specialty hospitals, Architectural design firms |
| **Q3 – Service Shop** | High | Low | Schools, Retail banks, Routine medical clinics |
| **Q4 – Professional Service** | High | High | Law firms, Consulting firms, Surgeons, Research labs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** As we move from Q1 → Q4, **buffering** (separating the customer from the production process) decreases, while **direct customer involvement** increases. Quality Engineering investments must therefore **shift from process automation (Q1) to staff training and SOP design (Q4)**.

## 2.3 Kotler's Classification Based on User Type

According to **Philip Kotler**, services are classified by **who consumes them**:

1. **Consumer Services** → Final individual consumers (e.g., tourism, personal banking).
2. **Industrial / Business Services** → Used by firms as inputs to production (e.g., logistics, IT consulting, equipment maintenance).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Framework | Core Dimension | Boundary Conditions / Limits | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Four I's of Services | Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability | Applicable to **pure services**; hybrid products may exhibit partial properties | All service quality design |
| 2 | Lovelock's 5 Categories | Action (Tangible/Intangible) × Recipient (People/Possessions) | Hybrid services overlap multiple cells | Service design, gap analysis |
| 3 | Chase Matrix | Contact × Customization (2D grid) | Quadrant Q4 has **no upper limit** on customization | Operations strategy, layout design |
| 4 | Kotler | Consumer vs. Industrial | A service can shift categories (B2B vs. B2C) | Market segmentation |
| 5 | Rust's CRM Grid | Duration × Intensity | Long-duration services require **relationship-quality metrics** | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) modelling |

> [!NOTE]
> **For Pure Math Topics (Cross-Subject Reference):** When applying SPC to service data, the standard $\sigma$-control limits are:
> $$\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$$
> $$\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$$
> where **$\bar{X}$** is the process mean and **$\sigma$** is the standard deviation. The factor **3** corresponds to **99.73 % confidence** under the Normal Distribution assumption — relevant when applying Control Charts to service-time measurements.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Industry:** Hospitals use Lovelock's *People Processing* classification to design patient-flow layouts and minimize variability.
- **Banking Sector:** Uses Chase Matrix to decide whether a branch is a *Service Shop* (Q3) requiring relationship managers.
- **IT Service Desks:** Apply Kotler's Industrial Service classification to design **SLA (Service Level Agreements)** for B2B clients.
- **Aviation:** Uses Perishability metrics + Yield Management to fill otherwise-lost seat capacity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Real Services to Lovelock + Chase

The following table demonstrates the **complete classification pathway** required in KTU long-answer questions. Notice how every row applies **both** classification systems — this is what examiners reward.

| Service Example | Lovelock Category | Action Type | Recipient | Chase Quadrant | Contact | Customization |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hair Salon | 1 | Tangible | People | Q3 (Service Shop) | High | Low–Medium |
| McDonald's Counter | Hybrid (1+5) | Tangible | People + Possession (food) | Q1 (Mass Service) | Low | Low |
| Car Repair Workshop | 2 | Tangible | Possession | Q2 (Service Factory) | Low | High |
| University Lecture | 3 | Intangible | People (minds) | Q3 (Service Shop) | High | Medium |
| Income Tax Consultancy | 4 | Intangible | Possession (data) | Q4 (Professional) | High | High |
| ATM Cash Withdrawal | 1 (limited) | Tangible | Possession (money) | Q1 (Mass Service) | Low | Low |
| Cardiac Surgery | 1 | Tangible | People | Q4 (Professional) | High | High |
| Netflix Streaming | 3 | Intangible | People | Q1 (Mass Service) | Low | Medium |

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Step-by-Step Classification

### Problem Statement
> *"A patient walks into a multi-specialty hospital for a knee replacement surgery. Classify this service using (a) Lovelock's framework and (b) Chase's matrix. Justify each choice."*

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the action type.**
The patient's body is physically operated upon (surgery is a tangible physical act). Therefore, the action type = **Tangible Action**.

**Step 2 — Identify the recipient.**
The *patient* (a person) is the direct recipient of the action. Therefore, recipient = **People**.

**Step 3 — Apply Lovelock's framework.**
Since action = Tangible AND recipient = People, the service falls into **Category 1 — People Processing**.

**Step 4 — Apply Chase's matrix.**
- Degree of customer contact = **High** (the patient is conscious, interacts with surgeon, anaesthetist, nurses).
- Degree of customization = **High** (every knee replacement is unique; implant size, surgical technique vary).
- Therefore, the service maps to **Quadrant 4 — Professional Service**.

**Step 5 — Quality Engineering Implications.**

Because the service is in **Lovelock Cat 1 + Chase Q4**, the following must be in place:
- **Highly trained personnel** (specialist surgeons) — staff training is critical.
- **Strict SOPs** to reduce variability of surgical outcomes.
- **Buffering** of pre/post-operative areas to keep the critical surgical zone high-focus.
- **SPC on operative time and infection rates** as quality indicators.

## 3.3 Cross-Reference: Quantitative Quality Metric for Service Variability

When the question shifts to **measuring variability** in a service (e.g., waiting time at a bank counter), the KTU-expected computation is:

**Given:** 10 customer waiting times (in minutes) observed at a single teller counter:
$$\{4,\; 5,\; 6,\; 4,\; 7,\; 5,\; 6,\; 5,\; 8,\; 4\}$$

**Step A — Compute the Mean ($\bar{X}$):**
$$\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n} = \frac{4+5+6+4+7+5+6+5+8+4}{10}$$

$$\bar{X} = \frac{54}{10} = 5.4 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step B — Compute the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):**
First, compute $\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(4-5.4)^2 &= (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 \\
(5-5.4)^2 &= (-0.4)^2 = 0.16 \\
(6-5.4)^2 &= (0.6)^2 = 0.36 \\
(4-5.4)^2 &= (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 \\
(7-5.4)^2 &= (1.6)^2 = 2.56 \\
(5-5.4)^2 &= (-0.4)^2 = 0.16 \\
(6-5.4)^2 &= (0.6)^2 = 0.36 \\
(5-5.4)^2 &= (-0.4)^2 = 0.16 \\
(8-5.4)^2 &= (2.6)^2 = 6.76 \\
(4-5.4)^2 &= (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 1.96+0.16+0.36+1.96+2.56+0.16+0.36+0.16+6.76+1.96 = 16.40$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{16.40}{9}} = \sqrt{1.8222} \approx 1.350 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step C — Apply the 3-Sigma Control Limits (UCL and LCL):**
$$\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma = 5.4 + 3(1.350) = 5.4 + 4.050 = 9.45 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma = 5.4 - 3(1.350) = 5.4 - 4.050 = 1.35 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step D — Engineering Interpretation:**
Any waiting time observed **above 9.45 minutes** or **below 1.35 minutes** indicates the service process is **out of statistical control** and requires root-cause analysis (e.g., staff shortage, system downtime). This is the standard way to *quantify* the variability dimension of services — a frequent KTU exam demand.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
