<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the four characteristics of services. Why are they important for Quality Management?**

**Model Answer:**
The four characteristics of services are:
1. **Intangibility** — Services cannot be touched or seen; they exist only at the point of delivery.
2. **Inseparability** — Production and consumption occur simultaneously.
3. **Variability (Heterogeneity)** — Service output varies from customer to customer and provider to provider.
4. **Perishability** — Unused service capacity (e.g., an unsold airline seat) cannot be inventoried for later sale.

**Importance for Quality Management:** These four characteristics make services fundamentally different from manufactured goods. Quality cannot be measured *before* delivery (intangibility), defects cannot be recalled (inseparability), standardization is difficult (variability), and excess capacity is wasted (perishability). Hence, TQM in services requires **surrogate metrics**, **Poka-Yoke at delivery points**, **SPC + SOPs**, and **yield management** respectively.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: List Lovelock's five categories of services with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **People Processing** — Hospital treatment
2. **Possession Processing** — Car repair
3. **Mental Stimulus Processing** — University education
4. **Information Processing** — Income-tax consultancy
5. **Hybrid Services** — A restaurant serving food + ambience + billing (overlaps Categories 1, 3, and 4).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail **Lovelock's classification of services**. Use a 2 × 2 matrix and give **two real-world examples** for each of the five categories. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of **Chase's Service Process Matrix**, explain how the quality management strategy differs between a *mass-service* firm (e.g., a fast-food chain) and a *professional-service* firm (e.g., a cardiac hospital). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Introduce Lovelock's Framework:**
Christopher Lovelock (1983) proposed classifying services along two dimensions:
- **Type of Action** (Tangible vs. Intangible)
- **Direct Recipient** (People vs. Possessions)

This produces a 2 × 2 grid with **four pure categories** and **one hybrid category**.

**Step 2 — Tabulate the Categories:**

| Category | Action | Recipient | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. People Processing | Tangible | People | Hospital | Hair Salon |
| 2. Possession Processing | Tangible | Possessions | Car Repair | Laundry |
| 3. Mental Stimulus Processing | Intangible | People | University | Theatre |
| 4. Information Processing | Intangible | Possessions | Banking | Legal Advisory |
| 5. Hybrid (Multi-Category) | Mixed | Mixed | Restaurant | Airline |

**Step 3 — Explain Quality Implications:**
- Categories 1 & 2 demand **physical layout** and **tangibilization** (physical evidence, ambience).
- Categories 3 & 4 demand **information quality** and **confidentiality** controls.
- Hybrid services require **integrated quality systems** combining all four 'I's.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Draw Chase's Matrix:**
- X-axis: Degree of Customer Contact (Low → High).
- Y-axis: Customization / Flexibility (Low → High).
- Resulting Quadrants: Q1 Mass Service, Q2 Service Factory, Q3 Service Shop, Q4 Professional Service.

**Step 2 — Fast-Food Chain (Q1 — Mass Service):**
- **Customer Contact:** Low (customer orders, pays, leaves).
- **Customization:** Low (standardized menu, fixed recipe).
- **Quality Strategy:** Heavy investment in **process automation**, **standardized SOPs**, **SPC on cooking time**, and **Poka-Yoke** in packaging. Staff training focuses on **speed** and **courtesy**, not medical expertise.

**Step 3 — Cardiac Hospital (Q4 — Professional Service):**
- **Customer Contact:** High (patient-physician interaction pre, intra, post surgery).
- **Customization:** High (each surgery is unique).
- **Quality Strategy:** Investment in **highly skilled personnel**, **evidence-based clinical SOPs**, **clinical audits**, and **patient outcome tracking**. Buffering of ICUs and operation theatres to maintain focus.

**Step 4 — Concluding Comparison:**
A fast-food chain achieves quality via **standardization and automation**; a cardiac hospital achieves quality via **expertise, judgment, and continuous professional development**. The Chase Matrix makes this differentiation explicit and operationally meaningful.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **Kotler's classification of services** based on the consumer type. Give examples of consumer services and industrial services, highlighting how quality expectations differ between the two. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The waiting time (in minutes) of 8 customers at a service counter is recorded as: $\{6,\; 8,\; 5,\; 9,\; 7,\; 10,\; 6,\; 5\}$. Construct a **3-sigma control chart** and comment on whether the process is in statistical control. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Kotler's Classification:**
Philip Kotler classified services based on the **end-user**:
- **Consumer Services** — consumed by individuals for personal use (tourism, personal banking, hospitality).
- **Industrial Services** — consumed by businesses as inputs to production (logistics, IT outsourcing, equipment maintenance).

**Step 2 — Examples and Quality Differences:**

| Aspect | Consumer Service | Industrial Service |
|---|---|---|
| Quality Metric | Subjective (NPS, reviews) | Objective (SLAs, uptime %) |
| Emotional Element | High | Low |
| Contractual Binding | Usually informal | Formal SLA |
| Risk to Customer | Personal discomfort | Business loss |
| Example | Tourism, Salon, Restaurant | Logistics, IT Support, Legal Counsel |

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
Industrial services emphasize **measurable, contractual quality**, while consumer services emphasize **emotional satisfaction and experience**. TQM frameworks must therefore be tuned accordingly — Six Sigma for industrial services, SERVQUAL/lean for consumer services.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean:**
$$\bar{X} = \frac{6+8+5+9+7+10+6+5}{8} = \frac{56}{8} = 7.0 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Standard Deviation:**
Calculate $\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(6-7)^2 &= 1 \\
(8-7)^2 &= 1 \\
(5-7)^2 &= 4 \\
(9-7)^2 &= 4 \\
(7-7)^2 &= 0 \\
(10-7)^2 &= 9 \\
(6-7)^2 &= 1 \\
(5-7)^2 &= 4 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 1+1+4+4+0+9+1+4 = 24$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{24}{8-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{24}{7}} = \sqrt{3.4286} \approx 1.852 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Control Limits:**
$$\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma = 7.0 + 3(1.852) = 7.0 + 5.556 = 12.556 \text{ minutes}$$

$$\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma = 7.0 - 3(1.852) = 7.0 - 5.556 = 1.444 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**
All observed waiting times $\{6, 8, 5, 9, 7, 10, 6, 5\}$ lie **between 1.444 and 12.556 minutes**. Hence, the service process is **in statistical control**, but the upper value (10 minutes) is approaching the UCL and warrants monitoring.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating the four I's with Lovelock's categories.** The four I's describe service *characteristics*; Lovelock's categories describe service *types*. Writing one in place of the other will cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hybrid Category.** Many students list only four Lovelock categories; missing the **5th hybrid category** costs a full mark.
> 3. **In Chase Matrix questions, students often confuse "Customization" with "Quality."** Customization refers to *variety offered*, not *quality delivered*.
> 4. **Control chart arithmetic errors:** Forgetting the $(n-1)$ denominator when computing sample standard deviation is the most common numerical mistake.
> 5. **Not justifying classification:** Always state *both* the action type and recipient type explicitly in Lovelock questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Services differ from goods because of the **Four I's**: Intangibility, Inseparability, Variability, Perishability.
- ✅ **Lovelock's 5 categories** are based on Action (Tangible/Intangible) × Recipient (People/Possessions) → Categories 1, 2, 3, 4, plus a **Hybrid** Category 5.
- ✅ **Chase's Service Process Matrix** has 4 quadrants: Q1 Mass Service, Q2 Service Factory, Q3 Service Shop, Q4 Professional Service.
- ✅ Quality strategy **shifts from automation** (Q1) **to staff training** (Q4) as we move along the matrix.
- ✅ **Kotler** classifies services into **Consumer** (B2C) and **Industrial** (B2B) — quality metrics differ.
- ✅ **Perishability** → solved by **Yield Management** and demand forecasting.
- ✅ **Variability** → solved by **Statistical Process Control** using 3-sigma limits: $\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$, $\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$.
- ✅ Always state **both axes** (action + recipient) when applying Lovelock; never classify using only one axis.
- ✅ **Hybrid services** are the most common in real life — most KTU exam examples expect you to acknowledge this.
- ✅ Memorize the formula $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 / (n-1)}$ for sample standard deviation — required in Part B numericals.
- ✅ The **most KTU-favoured diagram** in this topic is the **2 × 2 Lovelock Matrix with 5 categories** — practice drawing it from memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Service Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Service Quality?

**Service Quality** is formally defined as the degree of discrepancy between customers' **expectations** for a service performance and their **perception** of the actual service received. In the context of Total Quality Management (TQM), service quality represents the conformance to customer specifications in an environment where the "product" is largely intangible, produced and consumed simultaneously, and highly variable.

Mathematically, the foundational construct proposed by the **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (PZB) research team (1985–1988)** is expressed as:

$$SQ = P - E$$

Where:
- $SQ$ = Service Quality score
- $P$ = Customer **Perception** of the actual service delivered
- $E$ = Customer **Expectation** of the ideal service

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Whenever a question asks "Define service quality," always mention the **PZB SERVQUAL model** and the **Expectancy–Disconfirmation Paradigm** (i.e., Perception minus Expectation). Examiners specifically look for these two terms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Restaurant Dinner" Analogy

Imagine you walk into a premium restaurant for a birthday dinner. Before entering, you form an *expectation*: a clean table, a smiling host, hot food within 20 minutes, polite explanations of dishes by the waiter, and a clean restroom.

Now, what actually happens — the *perception* — is the real service quality you receive. If the table is sticky, the food is cold, and the waiter is rude, your perception falls below your expectation, creating a **negative gap** (a service failure). If, however, the host welcomes you by name, the chef visits your table, and the bill arrives with a complimentary dessert, your perception exceeds your expectation, creating a **positive gap** (a delightful service).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a manufactured product (say, a car), you cannot "hold" service quality in your hand before purchase. It is experienced *during* delivery. This makes its management fundamentally different from manufacturing quality.

## 1.3 Unique Characteristics of Services (The Four I's)

Services differ from manufactured goods in four fundamental ways, which complicate quality management:

| Characteristic | Definition | Implication for Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Intangibility** | Services cannot be seen, touched, or stored before purchase | Customers rely on *cues* (price, staff appearance, ambience) to judge quality |
| **Inseparability** | Production and consumption occur simultaneously | Quality is judged *in real time*; no factory inspection possible |
| **Heterogeneity** | Service delivery varies person-to-person, day-to-day | Standardization is difficult; *training* and *SOPs* become critical |
| **Perishability** | Unused service capacity (e.g., an empty airline seat) cannot be inventoried | Demand–capacity management is essential |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The **"IHIP"** acronym — **I**ntangibility, **H**eterogeneity, **I**nseparability, **P**erishability — is examiner-friendly. Use it in 3-mark definition answers.

## 1.4 The Expectancy–Disconfirmation Paradigm

This is the psychological engine driving service quality assessment. Three outcomes are possible:

1. **Confirmation:** $P = E$ → satisfaction is neutral.
2. **Positive Disconfirmation:** $P > E$ → delight, loyalty, word-of-mouth.
3. **Negative Disconfirmation:** $P < E$ → dissatisfaction, complaints, churn.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SERVQUAL Model — Five Dimensions of Service Quality

After extensive qualitative and quantitative research, PZB refined their original 10 determinants into **5 core dimensions**. These are *universally* tested in KTU module 3 questions.

### Dimension 1: **Tangibles**
- Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, and communication materials.
- *Example:* A bank's marble lobby, the uniform of a flight attendant, the cleanliness of a hospital.

### Dimension 2: **Reliability**
- The ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately**.
- *Example:* The courier company delivering the package *on the promised day* and *in the promised condition*.

### Dimension 3: **Responsiveness**
- The willingness to **help customers** and provide **prompt service**.
- *Example:* A call center answering within 20 seconds; a waiter refilling water without being asked.

### Dimension 4: **Assurance**
- The **knowledge and courtesy** of employees, and their ability to convey **trust and confidence**.
- *Example:* A doctor calmly explaining a diagnosis; a banking officer explaining complex loan terms.

### Dimension 5: **Empathy**
- The provision of **caring, individualized attention** to customers.
- *Example:* A boutique remembering a customer's name and preferences; a hospital nurse holding a patient's hand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Always cite **"T-R-R-A-E"** order (Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy). Examiners in Module 3 frequently ask: *"List and explain the dimensions of service quality (7 marks)"* — full marks require explaining each dimension with one real-world example.

## 2.2 The Gaps Model of Service Quality (Five Gaps)

The Gaps Model is the **diagnostic engine** of the SERVQUAL framework. It identifies *where* and *why* service quality shortfalls occur within the service organization.

### Gap 1: The **Knowledge Gap**
$$\text{Gap 1} = \text{Customer Expectation} - \text{Management Perception of Expectation}$$
- **Cause:** Management does not fully understand what customers expect.
- **Remedy:** Market research, customer feedback loops, voice-of-customer (VOC) systems.

### Gap 2: The **Standards Gap**
$$\text{Gap 2} = \text{Management Perception} - \text{Service Quality Specification}$$
- **Cause:** Management knows expectations but fails to set the *right* performance standards.
- **Remedy:** SMART service standards, service blueprinting, process design.

### Gap 3: The **Delivery Gap**
$$\text{Gap 3} = \text{Service Quality Specification} - \text{Actual Service Delivery}$$
- **Cause:** Employees fail to meet the set standards (training, morale, capacity issues).
- **Remedy:** Employee training, empowerment, technology, teamwork.

### Gap 4: The **Communication Gap**
$$\text{Gap 4} = \text{Actual Service Delivery} - \text{External Communication about Service}$$
- **Cause:** Over-promising in advertising / marketing; mismatched expectations.
- **Remedy:** Realistic media campaigns, integrated marketing communication.

### Gap 5: The **Service Quality Gap (The Master Gap)**
$$\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})$$
- **Definition:** The *cumulative* gap between customer expectation and perception, *caused by* the preceding four gaps.
- **Key Equation:**
$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Customer Expectation} - \text{Customer Perception}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Interpretation:** Gap 5 is **not** the *sum* of Gaps 1–4 in a strict arithmetic sense; rather, it is a *functional dependency*. The closure of Gaps 1–4 directly leads to the closure of Gap 5.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Conceptual Form | Unit / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Service Quality Score | $SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{22}(P_i - E_i)$ | Computed across 22 SERVQUAL items; dimensionless index |
| Per-Item Service Quality | $SQ_i = P_i - E_i$ | Range: $-7$ to $+7$ (Likert 7-point scale) |
| Master Service Quality Gap | $\text{Gap 5} = E - P$ | Negative = poor quality; Positive = exceeding expectations |
| Gap 1 (Knowledge) | $G_1 = E_{\text{cust}} - E_{\text{mgmt}}$ | Research failure |
| Gap 2 (Standards) | $G_2 = E_{\text{mgmt}} - S_{\text{standard}}$ | Specification failure |
| Gap 3 (Delivery) | $G_3 = S_{\text{standard}} - P_{\text{actual}}$ | Performance failure |
| Gap 4 (Communication) | $G_4 = P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{promised}}$ | Over-promising |
| Functional Dependence | $\text{Gap 5} = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)$ | Causal chain |
| SERVPERF Alternative | $SQ = P$ (only) | Performance-only model (Cronin & Taylor, 1992) |
| Zone of Tolerance | $Z = A_{\text{desired}} - A_{\text{adequate}}$ | Acceptable service range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** All absolute-value-style notations in tables use proper delimiters to avoid breaking the markdown grid. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` in inline contexts within tables.

## 2.4 SERVPERF vs SERVQUAL — The KTU Comparison

Two rival models compete in academic literature:

- **SERVQUAL (PZB, 1988):** Uses **P – E** disconfirmation logic across 22 items and 5 dimensions.
- **SERVPERF (Cronin & Taylor, 1992):** Uses **P only** — measures *performance perception* directly, arguing that expectations are difficult for customers to articulate.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** SERVPERF is statistically simpler and has higher explained variance. SERVQUAL is more *diagnostic* because it tells you *why* quality is poor. In 14-mark questions, weigh both and conclude that **SERVPERF is operationally superior, while SERVQUAL is strategically superior**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Gap Equation

The PZB framework is built on a strict **causal chain of organizational gaps**. Let us derive the mathematical structure step by step.

**Step 1 — Definition of Customer Expectation ($E_{\text{cust}}$):**
Management's *perception* of customer expectation is denoted $E_{\text{mgmt}}$. The Knowledge Gap arises as:

$$G_1 = E_{\text{cust}} - E_{\text{mgmt}}$$

**Step 2 — Translation to Service Standards:**
Management converts its perception $E_{\text{mgmt}}$ into formal service specifications $S$. The Standards Gap is:

$$G_2 = E_{\text{mgmt}} - S$$

**Step 3 — Actual Delivery:**
The frontline employee delivers an actual service $D$. The Delivery Gap is:

$$G_3 = S - D$$

**Step 4 — Promised vs Delivered:**
Marketing communications promise a service level $M$. The Communication Gap is:

$$G_4 = D - M$$

**Step 5 — Master Gap Assembly:**
The customer compares the *promised* service $M$ (shaped by Gap 4 communication) against his/her *perception* of the actual service $D$. The Master Gap is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap 5} &= E_{\text{cust}} - D \\
&= (E_{\text{cust}} - E_{\text{mgmt}}) + (E_{\text{mgmt}} - S) + (S - D) \\
&= G_1 + G_2 + G_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Incorporating Marketing Communication:**
The customer does not just observe $D$; his/her expectation is also shaped by marketing promises. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gap 5} &= E_{\text{cust}} - D \\
&= E_{\text{cust}} - (M - G_4) \\
&= (E_{\text{cust}} - M) + G_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — The Unifying Functional Form:**
Combining all four causal gaps into a single expression:

$$\boxed{\text{Gap 5} = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)}$$

This shows that **Gap 5 is closed if and only if all four upstream gaps are closed simultaneously**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is structurally identical to a *series reliability system* where component failure (each gap) leads to system failure (Gap 5). The reliability of the service system is the *intersection* of all four gap-closure events.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SERVQUAL Scoring

A retail bank surveys customers on 2 of the 22 SERVQUAL items, using a 7-point Likert scale. The aggregated results are:

| Item | Expectation $(E_i)$ | Perception $(P_i)$ | $SQ_i = P_i - E_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modern-looking equipment | 6.5 | 5.0 | $-1.5$ |
| Error-free records | 6.8 | 6.2 | $-0.6$ |

**Step 1 — Compute per-item scores:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_1 &= P_1 - E_1 = 5.0 - 6.5 = -1.5 \\
SQ_2 &= P_2 - E_2 = 6.2 - 6.8 = -0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average service quality across the dimension (Tangibles):**

$$SQ_{\text{Tangibles}} = \frac{SQ_1 + SQ_2}{2} = \frac{(-1.5) + (-0.6)}{2} = -1.05$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
A score of **$-1.05$** indicates a significant *negative disconfirmation*. The bank's tangibles (equipment appearance) under-perform customer expectations.

**Step 4 — Aggregating Across All Dimensions:**
Repeating the procedure for the 5 dimensions and 22 items, the overall service quality index is:

$$SQ_{\text{Overall}} = \frac{1}{22} \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Any time a question provides $E_i$ and $P_i$ tables, the examiner expects you to (a) compute $SQ_i = P_i - E_i$, (b) average per dimension, and (c) state whether the result shows *positive*, *negative*, or *zero* disconfirmation. Marks are awarded for all three steps.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Two Gaps Frameworks

| Framework | Inventor | Year | Number of Dimensions | Core Construct | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SERVQUAL** | Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry | 1988 | 5 (T-R-R-A-E) | $SQ = P - E$ | Diagnosing *why* quality is poor |
| **SERVPERF** | Cronin & Taylor | 1992 | 5 (T-R-R-A-E) | $SQ = P$ | Measuring actual performance |
| **Nordic Model** | Grönroos | 1984 | 2 (Functional + Technical) | $SQ = f(\text{Process Quality}, \text{Outcome Quality})$ | Service vs goods comparison |
| **3Q Model** | Rust & Oliver | 1994 | 3 (Service, Product, Environment) | $SQ = f(Q_S, Q_P, Q_E)$ | Hybrid service–product firms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grönroos Total Service Quality (Nordic Model):** $SQ_{\text{total}} = \text{Technical Quality} + \text{Functional Quality}$. Technical = *what* the customer receives (e.g., a meal); Functional = *how* it is delivered (e.g., waiter's attitude). This is a frequent KTU short question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: Define Service Quality. List its five dimensions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Service quality is the degree to which a service meets or exceeds customer expectations. It is formally expressed as $SQ = P - E$, where $P$ is the customer's perception of the service delivered and $E$ is the customer's expectation of the ideal service. According to the Parasuraman–Zeithaml–Berry (PZB) SERVQUAL model, the **five dimensions** are:

1. **Tangibles** — Physical evidence and appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Dependable and accurate performance.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help promptly.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge, courtesy, and trust.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only the *names* of the five dimensions without explaining each. A 3-mark answer **demands** a one-line definition for each. Marks are split as: definition (1) + five dimensions with explanations (2).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between SERVQUAL and SERVPERF. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SERVQUAL | SERVPERF |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventors** | Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry (1988) | Cronin & Taylor (1992) |
| **Construct** | $SQ = P - E$ (Disconfirmation) | $SQ = P$ (Performance only) |
| **Items** | 22 paired (E and P) | 22 (P only) |
| **Diagnostic Power** | High — identifies gap sources | Low — measures performance only |
| **Survey Length** | Long (44 questions) | Shorter (22 questions) |
| **Statistical Power** | Moderate | Higher explained variance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing only the *equations* of both models does not fetch full 3 marks. You must specify the *inventor* and *diagnostic capability difference*. Marks: inventor + equation (1) + comparison points (2).

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Gaps Model of Service Quality** in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the *causes* and *remedies* for each gap. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Gaps Model, developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry, identifies **five gaps** that cause service quality failures.

**Gap 1 – The Knowledge Gap:**
$$G_1 = E_{\text{customer}} - E_{\text{management}}$$
This gap arises when management does not understand *what* customers actually expect. *Causes:* inadequate market research, lack of upward communication, lack of customer focus. *Remedies:* Voice-of-Customer (VOC) programs, customer panels, market research, complaint analysis systems.

**Gap 2 – The Standards Gap:**
$$G_2 = E_{\text{management}} - S_{\text{specification}}$$
This occurs when, although management knows the expectations, it fails to set *appropriate* service standards. *Causes:* poor service design, lack of process standardization, absence of service blueprinting. *Remedies:* service blueprinting, SMART service standards, process mapping.

**Gap 3 – The Delivery Gap:**
$$G_3 = S_{\text{specification}} - P_{\text{delivery}}$$
This gap is between *set standards* and *actual performance*. *Causes:* employee ambiguity, role conflict, poor training, inadequate technology, weak internal communication. *Remedies:* employee training, empowerment, teamwork, internal marketing, supportive technology.

**Gap 4 – The Communication Gap:**
$$G_4 = P_{\text{delivery}} - M_{\text{promise}}$$
This arises when *external communications* (ads, brochures, websites) over-promise relative to actual delivery. *Causes:* inadequate horizontal communication between marketing and operations. *Remedies:* integrated marketing communication, realistic campaign planning, sales-operations alignment.

**Gap 5 – The Master Service Quality Gap:**
$$\text{Gap 5} = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)$$
This is the customer's *overall* perception-minus-expectation gap and is the cumulative result of Gaps 1–4. The organization must close all four upstream gaps to close Gap 5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often describe *only Gap 5* and ignore Gaps 1–4. Full 7 marks require *all four upstream gaps* with their causes and remedies, plus the master gap equation. The diagram is mandatory for full marks.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **SERVQUAL model** with its 5 dimensions. State at least **4 SERVQUAL items per dimension**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The SERVQUAL model is a 22-item, 5-dimension instrument used to measure service quality across customer expectations and perceptions. The model is built on the disconfirmation paradigm: $SQ_i = P_i - E_i$ for each item $i$.

**1. Tangibles (Items 1–4):**
- Item 1: Modern-looking equipment.
- Item 2: Visually appealing physical facilities.
- Item 3: Neat-appearing employees.
- Item 4: Visually appealing materials associated with the service.

**2. Reliability (Items 5–9):**
- Item 5: Promised service is performed on time.
- Item 6: Dependable handling of customer problems.
- Item 7: Service performed right the first time.
- Item 8: Provision of services at the promised time.
- Item 9: Accurate record keeping.

**3. Responsiveness (Items 10–13):**
- Item 10: Employees inform customers of service timelines.
- Item 11: Prompt service to customers.
- Item 12: Willingness of employees to help.
- Item 13: Employees are never too busy to respond.

**4. Assurance (Items 14–17):**
- Item 14: Employees inspire trust and confidence.
- Item 15: Customers feel safe in transactions.
- Item 16: Employees are consistently courteous.
- Item 17: Employees have adequate knowledge.

**5. Empathy (Items 18–22):**
- Item 18: Personalized attention to customers.
- Item 19: Convenient operating hours.
- Item 20: Employees give individual attention.
- Item 21: Employees understand customer needs.
- Item 22: Customers are given priority.

The overall Service Quality Index is computed as:

$$SQ_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{22} \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$

A *positive* $SQ$ indicates exceeding expectations; *negative* indicates under-performance; *zero* indicates parity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing *fewer than 4 items per dimension* leads to deduction of 1 mark. Also avoid confusing "Assurance" with "Empathy" — Assurance is about *trust and competence*; Empathy is about *caring and personal attention*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Compare **SERVQUAL** with **SERVPERF** models. State their **merits and limitations** with a suitable **engineering/service example**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SERVQUAL (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry, 1988)** is based on the disconfirmation paradigm. It uses 22 paired items to measure both expectations $E_i$ and perceptions $P_i$, then computes $SQ = P - E$.

**SERVPERF (Cronin & Taylor, 1992)** is a *performance-only* model. It uses the same 22 items but measures only $P_i$, treating $SQ = P$.

| Aspect | SERVQUAL | SERVPERF |
|---|---|---|
| Conceptual basis | Disconfirmation (gap-based) | Performance-only (attitude-based) |
| Survey length | 44 questions | 22 questions |
| Operational ease | Difficult (customer fatigue) | Easier (shorter instrument) |
| Statistical fit | Lower explained variance | Higher explained variance (≈ 0.91) |
| Diagnostic use | Excellent for gap identification | Limited to performance measure |

**Merits of SERVQUAL:**
- Diagnoses *why* quality is poor (gap-by-gap).
- Aligns with the Gaps Model of the same authors.
- Useful for *strategic* quality improvement.

**Limitations of SERVQUAL:**
- Long survey → respondent fatigue.
- Customers may not have stable *expectations* to articulate.
- Expectation scores may be inflated, producing inflated gaps.

**Merits of SERVPERF:**
- Shorter, less cognitively taxing.
- Higher statistical reliability and validity.
- Pragmatic for operational benchmarking.

**Limitations of SERVPERF:**
- Does not explain *causes* of quality failure.
- Cannot differentiate between *high expectation met* and *low expectation met* scenarios.

**Engineering Example — Indian Railways Catering:**
A railway caterer applies SERVQUAL across 5 dimensions and discovers that Responsiveness (delivery time of meals) and Tangibles (cleanliness of trays) show the largest negative gaps. Switching to SERVPERF for quarterly tracking gives management a *single performance score* per coach, useful for comparing vendors. However, the underlying *cause* — Gap 2 (no clear standards for delivery time) — is only revealed by SERVQUAL's diagnostic depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *only the comparison table* and skip the engineering example. The example is **mandatory** to fetch 7 marks. Also, do not forget to state the *inventors and years*; this is worth 1 mark on its own.

---

**Q. (b)** What are the **unique characteristics of services**? Explain why these make **service quality management more challenging** than product quality management. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Services are distinct from physical products in **four fundamental characteristics**, collectively remembered as **IHIP**:

**1. Intangibility:**
Services cannot be seen, tasted, or touched *before* purchase. A patient cannot "try" surgery before consenting. This forces customers to rely on *cues* — price, brand reputation, staff demeanor, environment — to predict quality. *Management Challenge:* Quality must be made tangible through physical evidence, branding, and customer testimonials.

**2. Heterogeneity (Variability):**
Service delivery varies across servers, time, and customers. Two surgeries by the same doctor can differ; two bank visits can yield different experiences. *Management Challenge:* Standardization through SOPs, training, automation, and statistical process control is essential — yet the *human* element makes uniformity elusive.

**3. Inseparability:**
Production and consumption occur simultaneously. A haircut is produced *and* consumed at the same instant. *Management Challenge:* Service quality is judged *in real time* by the customer. There is no factory "end-of-line" inspection. Recruit, train, and *empower* the front-line employee.

**4. Perishability:**
Services cannot be inventoried. An empty airline seat at takeoff is a *lost* revenue opportunity. *Management Challenge:* Demand–capacity balancing, yield management, and reservation systems are needed to avoid waste.

**Why Management is More Challenging:**

- **In Product Quality** (e.g., a laptop), defects can be caught at QC, units can be reworked, and specifications can be objectively measured.
- **In Service Quality**, the "defect" is the customer's *perception*, which is subjective, immediate, and irreversible. A rude waiter cannot "un-rude" the moment.
- Quality control is *replaced* by quality *assurance* — the entire service system must be *designed* to be capable, not just inspected.
- Variation cannot be eliminated by rejecting defective units; it must be *managed statistically* (e.g., control charts applied to service times, waiting times, transaction times).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often list IHIP without explaining *how each complicates management*. A 7-mark answer requires the *consequence* (management challenge) for each of the four characteristics. Skipping the "why" costs 2–3 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Service Quality** = $SQ = P - E$ (PZB paradigm). Always state both terms.
- **Five dimensions of SERVQUAL** = **T-R-R-A-E** = Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- **Five Gaps Model** = Gap 1 (Knowledge) + Gap 2 (Standards) + Gap 3 (Delivery) + Gap 4 (Communication) → Gap 5 (Master Gap).
- **Gap 5 equation:** $\text{Gap 5} = f(G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4)$ — Gap 5 closes *only* when Gaps 1–4 close.
- **SERVQUAL** = 22 paired items, $P$ and $E$ both measured, gap-diagnostic.
- **SERVPERF** = 22 items, $P$ only, performance-only, statistically stronger but less diagnostic.
- **Grönroos Nordic Model** = $SQ = \text{Technical Quality} + \text{Functional Quality}$.
- **Four I's of Services** = **IHIP** = Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability.
- **Zone of Tolerance** = $Z = A_{\text{desired}} - A_{\text{adequate}}$ (advanced concept; mention for 14-mark answers).
- **Likert Scale:** SERVQUAL uses a 7-point scale; per-item $SQ_i$ ranges from $-7$ to $+7$.
- **Examiner's mnemonic:** *KTSDE* — Knowledge, Technical Standards, Delivery, External-communication, Master-gap.
- **Common pitfalls:** (i) Forgetting the equation; (ii) listing dimensions without explanation; (iii) ignoring Gap 1–4; (iv) confusing Assurance with Empathy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Service Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measuring Service Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Service Quality Measurement** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process of evaluating the difference between customer expectations of a service and their actual perceptions of the service performance received. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme and Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy, it is a structured framework used by organizations to benchmark, monitor, and continuously improve intangible service offerings.

The most widely accepted academic operationalization of service quality measurement is the **SERVQUAL Model (Service Quality Model)** developed by **A. Parasuraman, Leonard L. Berry, and Valarie A. Zeithaml** in **1988** (Journal of Marketing). The model postulates that service quality is a function of the magnitude and direction of the gap between customer **Expectations (E)** and customer **Perceptions (P)** of actual service performance.

Mathematically, this foundational relationship is expressed as:

$$\text{Service Quality (SQ)} = \text{Perceptions (P)} - \text{Expectations (E)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Service quality is the *only* dimension of quality that is inherently **intangible, heterogeneous, inseparable, and perishable** (the IHIP characteristics). Because of this, measurement cannot rely on hard physical metrics (like length or weight) — it requires perceptual scaling instruments, primarily the **5-Point or 7-Point Likert Scale**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a B.Tech student ordering a **Zomato food delivery** during exam season at KTU.

- **Before ordering**, you have a mental picture: "The food should arrive in 30 minutes, hot, fresh, and the delivery person should be polite."
- **After receiving the order**, the food arrives in **50 minutes**, it is **lukewarm**, and the delivery person is **rude**.

The **gap** between what you *expected* and what you *perceived* is your measured **service quality**. If Perception (P) is less than Expectation (E), the gap is **negative** → poor quality. If P > E, the gap is **positive** → excellent quality (delight).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Service quality measurement is essentially a "**report card**" for intangible experiences. Just as a teacher grades you on how well you met the syllabus expectations (P vs E), a company grades itself on how well the delivered service met the customer's pre-purchase expectations.

---

## 1.3 The Five Generic Dimensions of Service Quality (RATER)

The SERVQUAL instrument collapses service quality into **5 universal dimensions**, remembered by the acronym **RATER**:

| Dimension | Meaning | Engineering/Service Example |
|---|---|---|
| **R** – Reliability | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately | Server uptime, on-time delivery |
| **A** – Assurance | Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust | Certified mechanics, warranty support |
| **T** – Tangibles | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | Clean showroom, modern labs |
| **E** – Empathy | Caring, individualized attention given to customers | Personal relationship manager |
| **R** – Responsiveness | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | 24×7 helpline, quick complaint resolution |

> [!NOTE]
> These **5 dimensions** are the **22-item SERVQUAL questionnaire** foundation. KTU examiners frequently test this acronym and ask students to map real-world services to these dimensions.

---

## 1.4 Why Measure Service Quality? (KTU 2024 Learning Outcome)

Measuring service quality is **NOT** an academic exercise. It directly impacts:

1. **Customer Retention** – A 5% increase in retention can boost profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Co. research, frequently cited in KTU case studies).
2. **Word-of-Mouth Marketing** – Dissatisfied customers tell **9 to 15 others** about their bad experience.
3. **Operational Efficiency** – Gap analysis pinpoints the exact process bottleneck.
4. **Strategic Differentiation** – In commoditized markets (telecom, banking), service quality is the **only sustainable competitive advantage**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SERVQUAL Mathematical Framework (Deep Dive)

The SERVQUAL methodology applies a structured two-stage survey to the same customer (or sample population) for the same set of 22 service attributes:

**Stage 1 — Expectation Score (Eᵢ):**
The customer rates on a Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree → 7 = Strongly Agree) how much they *expect* a service feature to be delivered by an *excellent* service provider.

**Stage 2 — Perception Score (Pᵢ):**
The same customer rates how much they *perceived* the actual delivered service to possess that feature.

The **per-item gap score** for any attribute *i* is:

$$SQ_i = P_i - E_i$$

The **overall SERVQUAL score** for the service is the weighted mean of all 22 item gaps:

$$\overline{SQ} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - E_i) \quad ; \quad n = 22$$

For dimension-level analysis (e.g., Reliability), the gap is the average of all items under that dimension:

$$SQ_{Reliability} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} (P_j - E_j) \quad ; \quad k = \text{items in dimension}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Rule:** A negative $\overline{SQ}$ means service is failing customer expectations. The most negative dimension is the **priority area for managerial intervention** (Pareto-style focus).

---

## 2.2 The Five Gaps Model of Service Quality (Zeithaml, Berry & Parasuraman, 1990)

The SERVQUAL authors later extended the framework into the **5 Gaps Model** — a **cause-and-effect diagnostic tool** for service quality. This is the **most frequently tested diagram in KTU exams**.

| Gap | Name | Definition | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations | Poor market research, upward communication failure |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Difference between management perception and actual service quality specifications | Inadequate commitment to service quality, lack of goal-setting |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Difference between service specifications and actual service delivered | Poor employee training, over-promising, role ambiguity |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | Difference between service delivered and what is communicated externally | Inadequate horizontal communication, over-promising in ads |
| **Gap 5** | Expected Service – Perceived Service Gap | Difference between customer expectation and their perception of the service received | Cumulative effect of Gaps 1–4 |

**The Fundamental Equation of the Gaps Model:**

$$\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})$$

Or more precisely (assuming linear superposition for analysis):

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Which gap is closed by better market research?"* — Answer: **Gap 1**. *"Which gap is closed by better training?"* — Answer: **Gap 3**.

---

## 2.3 Alternative Measurement Models

| Model | Year | Developer | Core Idea | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SERVQUAL** | 1988 | Parasuraman et al. | Quality = P − E (Expectation-based) | $SQ = P - E$ |
| **SERVPERF** | 1992 | Cronin & Taylor | Quality = Performance only (no expectation) | $SQ = P$ |
| **Weighted SERVQUAL** | 1993 | Babakus & Boller | Weights each dimension by importance | $SQ = \sum w_i(P_i - E_i)$ |
| **Normed Quality (NQ)** | 1994 | Teas | Uses ideal/anticipated points | $NQ = (P - E) / (A - E)$ |
| **IPA** | 1977 | Martilla & James | Importance vs. Performance 2×2 matrix | $I - P$ gap on quadrant |
| **CIT** | 1954 | Flanagan | Critical Incident Technique (qualitative) | Thematic coding |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on SERVPERF:** Cronin & Taylor (1992) argued that expectations are **unstable cognitive constructs** and that performance alone is a better predictor. Many KTU textbook questions ask: *"Compare SERVQUAL and SERVPERF."*

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-item Gap | $SQ_i = P_i - E_i$ | Likert points (1–7) | Individual attribute analysis |
| 2 | Overall SERVQUAL | $\overline{SQ} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (P_i - E_i)$ | Likert points | Service-wide score |
| 3 | Weighted SERVQUAL | $\overline{SQ}_w = \sum w_i(P_i - E_i)$ ; $\sum w_i = 1$ | Likert points | Importance-weighted analysis |
| 4 | Gap 5 (Customer) | $Gap_5 = P - E$ | Likert points | Customer-side measurement |
| 5 | Importance–Performance | $I_i - P_i$ | Likert points | IPA quadrant mapping |
| 6 | Critical Incident Index | $CII = \frac{\text{Positive Incidents} - \text{Negative Incidents}}{\text{Total Incidents}}$ | Dimensionless (−1 to +1) | CIT analysis |
| 7 | Sample Size (Likert) | $n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{e^2}$ | Respondents | Survey reliability |
| 8 | Cronbach's Alpha | $\alpha = \frac{k}{k-1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum \sigma^2_{Y_i}}{\sigma^2_X}\right)$ | ≥ 0.7 acceptable | Questionnaire reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cronbach's Alpha ($\alpha$)** formula above is the standard reliability test for the SERVQUAL questionnaire. KTU 2024 expects students to know that $\alpha \geq 0.7$ indicates acceptable internal consistency.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering, service quality measurement is integrated into:

- **IT Service Management (ITSM):** ITIL framework uses CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score) and CES (Customer Effort Score).
- **Healthcare Engineering:** HCAHPS surveys measure hospital service quality.
- **Manufacturing–Service Interface:** Servitization of manufacturing (e.g., Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour") requires service quality KPIs to price uptime contracts.
- **Software Engineering:** Google, Microsoft measure UX quality using SUS (System Usability Scale), a derivative of SERVQUAL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1: SERVQUAL Score Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A KTU canteen surveys 100 students. For attribute X (Timeliness of service), the average Expectation score is **E = 6.2** and the average Perception score is **P = 4.1** on a 7-point Likert scale. Calculate (a) the gap score, (b) the percentage gap, and (c) the managerial interpretation.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs**
Expectation, $E = 6.2$
Perception, $P = 4.1$

**Step 2 — Compute the Raw Gap**

$$SQ_i = P - E$$

$$SQ_i = 4.1 - 6.2$$

$$SQ_i = -2.1 \text{ Likert points}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Percentage Gap (Relative Measure)**
The percentage gap is the ratio of the absolute gap to the maximum possible expectation (7):

$$\% \text{ Gap} = \frac{\vert P - E \vert}{E_{\max}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Gap} = \frac{2.1}{7} \times 100\% = 30\%$$

**Step 4 — Managerial Interpretation**
Since $SQ_i = -2.1 < 0$, the service is **failing customer expectations** by 30% of the maximum possible expectation. The canteen should prioritize **Gap 3 (Delivery)** interventions — possibly by adding more counter staff during peak hours.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2: Importance–Performance Analysis (IPA) Derivation

> **Problem Statement:** For a bank, the importance (I) and performance (P) of 4 service attributes are:
>
> | Attribute | Importance (I) | Performance (P) |
> |---|---|---|
> | A1: ATM Uptime | 6.5 | 5.8 |
> | A2: Loan Approval Speed | 6.8 | 3.2 |
> | A3: Staff Courtesy | 5.2 | 5.5 |
> | A4: Mobile App Reliability | 6.6 | 6.4 |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Grand Means (the IPA axes)**
Mean of all Importance values:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{6.5 + 6.8 + 5.2 + 6.6}{4} = \frac{25.1}{4} = 6.275$$

Mean of all Performance values:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{5.8 + 3.2 + 5.5 + 6.4}{4} = \frac{20.9}{4} = 5.225$$

**Step 2 — Compute the I − P Gap (Action Priority Score)**

$$Gap_{A1} = 6.5 - 5.8 = 0.7$$

$$Gap_{A2} = 6.8 - 3.2 = 3.6$$

$$Gap_{A3} = 5.2 - 5.5 = -0.3$$

$$Gap_{A4} = 6.6 - 6.4 = 0.2$$

**Step 3 — Quadrant Mapping (using mean crosshairs)**
The four quadrants of the IPA matrix are defined by the crosshairs $(\bar{I}, \bar{P}) = (6.275, 5.225)$:

- **Q1 (High I, Low P) — "Concentrate Here"** → Attribute **A2** (I=6.8, P=3.2) is here. **HIGH PRIORITY**.
- **Q2 (High I, High P) — "Keep Up the Good Work"** → A1, A4 fall here. Maintain current performance.
- **Q3 (Low I, Low P) — "Low Priority"** → None here.
- **Q4 (Low I, High P) — "Possible Overkill"** → A3 falls here. Consider reallocating resources.

**Step 4 — Managerial Action**
Attribute A2 (Loan Approval Speed) is the **biggest service-quality failure** with a gap of **3.6 Likert points**. The bank must immediately re-engineer its loan process workflow.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Problem 3: Gap-5 Verification

> **Problem Statement:** A restaurant has Gap 1 = 1.2, Gap 2 = 0.8, Gap 3 = 1.5, Gap 4 = 0.5. Predict Gap 5.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the Gap-5 Identity**

$$Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$$

$$Gap_5 = 1.2 + 0.8 + 1.5 + 0.5$$

$$Gap_5 = 4.0 \text{ Likert points}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation**
A Gap 5 of 4.0 indicates a **severe service quality failure** as perceived by customers. **Gap 3 (Delivery)** is the largest contributor at 1.5 — suggesting frontline staff training is the root cause.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Service Quality Tool?

| Tool | Best Used When | Data Type | Cost | Statistical Rigor | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SERVQUAL | Benchmarking against expectations | Quantitative | Medium | High | ★★★★★ |
| SERVPERF | Tracking performance over time | Quantitative | Low | Medium | ★★★ |
| CIT | Deep-dive on specific incidents | Qualitative | High (interviews) | Low | ★★★ |
| IPA | Resource allocation decisions | Quantitative | Low | Medium | ★★★★ |
| Mystery Shopping | Frontline behavior audit | Quantitative | High | Medium | ★★ |
| NPS | Single-metric dashboard tracking | Quantitative | Low | Medium | ★★★ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five Gaps Model — Cause-and-Effect Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Customer Expectations"] --> nodeB["Customer Perceived Service"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Gap 5 Customer Gap"]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeD["Management Perception of Customer Expectations"] --> nodeE["Gap 1 Knowledge Gap"]
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF["Service Quality Specifications"] --> nodeG["Gap 2 Standards Gap"]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeH["Service Delivery"] --> nodeI["Gap 3 Delivery Gap"]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeJ["External Communications to Customers"] --> nodeK["Gap 4 Communication Gap"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeK --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The customer (left side) compares **Expectations** with **Perceived Service** to form **Gap 5**. The provider side (right side) generates four internal gaps (1–4) that *all flow into* Gap 5. The arrows in the lower section show the cumulative effect.

---

## 4.2 The SERVQUAL Measurement Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    phase1["Phase 1 Qualitative Research Focus Groups and In depth Interviews"] --> phase2["Phase 2 Identify 22 Service Attributes"]
    phase2 --> phase3["Phase 3 Design Two Stage Questionnaire"]
    phase3 --> phase4["Phase 4 Stage A Expectation Survey"]
    phase3 --> phase5["Phase 4 Stage B Perception Survey"]
    phase4 --> phase6["Phase 5 Compute Gap Score Pi minus Ei"]
    phase5 --> phase6
    phase6 --> phase7["Phase 6 Statistical Analysis Cronbach Alpha Factor Analysis"]
    phase7 --> phase8["Phase 7 Managerial Action and TQM Loop"]
    phase8 --> phase1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** Notice the **feedback loop** at the bottom — service quality measurement is **NOT a one-time event**. It is a continuous TQM cycle (Plan–Do–Check–Act) aligned with Deming's philosophy.

---

## 4.3 Importance–Performance Analysis (IPA) Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph IPA["IPA Four Quadrant Strategic Matrix"]
        direction LR
        subgraph Q1["Q1 HIGH I LOW P CONCENTRATE HERE"]
            attr1["Attribute A2 Loan Speed"]
        end
        subgraph Q2["Q2 HIGH I HIGH P KEEP UP GOOD WORK"]
            attr2["Attribute A1 ATM Uptime"]
            attr3["Attribute A4 App Reliability"]
        end
        subgraph Q3["Q3 LOW I LOW P LOW PRIORITY"]
            attr4["Attribute None Here"]
        end
        subgraph Q4["Q4 LOW I HIGH P POSSIBLE OVERKILL"]
            attr5["Attribute A3 Staff Courtesy"]
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Matrix:** The vertical axis = **Importance (I)**, the horizontal axis = **Performance (P)**. Mean values of I and P divide the plane into 4 quadrants. Q1 (top-left) is the **crisis zone** where customers care a lot but performance is poor.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Service Quality Measurement System

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputLayer["INPUT LAYER Raw Customer Responses Likert 1 to 7"] --> preproc["PREPROCESSING LAYER Data Cleaning Outlier Removal Missing Value Imputation"]
    preproc --> calc["COMPUTATION LAYER P minus E Gap Mean and Std Deviation Cronbach Alpha"]
    calc --> analysis["ANALYSIS LAYER Dimension wise SERVQUAL Scores IPA Quadrant Mapping Statistical Significance Test"]
    analysis --> decision["DECISION LAYER Managerial Insights Priority Action List Resource Allocation"]
    decision --> output["OUTPUT LAYER TQM Dashboard Pareto Chart Service Quality Report"]
    output --> feedback["FEEDBACK LOOP Continuous Improvement PDCA Cycle"]
    feedback --> inputLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This block diagram mirrors a standard **data pipeline in industrial quality control**. The SERVQUAL questionnaire is the *sensor*; the statistical engine is the *PLC*; the managerial dashboard is the *HMI*. This is why service quality is now a formal part of the Industrial Engineering curriculum at KTU.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** *Define Service Quality. List the five dimensions of service quality as per the SERVQUAL model.* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Service Quality is the degree of conformance between customer **expectations** of a service and their **perceptions** of the service actually received. It is an attitude-based, long-term evaluation of a service's overall excellence or superiority (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988).

**Mathematical Form:**

$$SQ = P - E$$

**The Five Dimensions (RATER):**

1. **R** – **Reliability:** Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
2. **A** – **Assurance:** Knowledge and courtesy of employees; their ability to inspire trust and confidence.
3. **T** – **Tangibles:** Physical evidence — facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, communication materials.
4. **E** – **Empathy:** Caring, individualized attention provided to customers.
5. **R** – **Responsiveness:** Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + formula, 2 for the 5 dimensions)*

---

### **Question 2:** *Distinguish between SERVQUAL and SERVPERF models of service quality measurement.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SERVQUAL (1988) | SERVPERF (1992) |
|---|---|---|
| **Developed By** | Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry | Cronin & Taylor |
| **Core Idea** | Quality = Perception − Expectation | Quality = Perception only |
| **Stages** | Two-stage (E & P) survey | Single-stage (P) survey |
| **Survey Length** | 44 items (22 E + 22 P) | 22 items |
| **Theoretical Basis** | Grönroos (1984) Nordic model | Direct performance measurement |
| **Variance Explained** | Lower in some studies | Higher predictive power (Cronin & Taylor) |
| **Best Suited For** | Diagnostic gap analysis | Tracking performance over time |

*(3 marks: 1 for each model's concept + 1 for the comparison table)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module-3 Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each full question carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** *Comprehensive Service Quality Diagnosis* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Five Gaps Model** of service quality with a neat diagram. Discuss how management can close **Gap 1** and **Gap 3**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital surveyed 200 patients using SERVQUAL. The average scores for 5 dimensions (on a 7-point Likert scale) are:

| Dimension | Expectation (E) | Perception (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Reliability | 6.5 | 4.2 |
| Assurance | 6.3 | 5.1 |
| Tangibles | 5.8 | 4.9 |
| Empathy | 6.0 | 3.8 |
| Responsiveness | 6.4 | 4.0 |

Calculate the **dimension-wise gap scores**, identify the **weakest dimension**, and recommend **two managerial actions**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Five Gaps Model (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The Five Gaps Model, developed by **Zeithaml, Berry & Parasuraman (1990)**, is a strategic framework for analyzing the root causes of service quality shortfalls. It links the **customer side** (Expectation vs. Perception) with the **provider side** (four internal operational gaps).

**The Five Gaps:**

1. **Gap 1 — The Knowledge Gap**
   Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations.
   *Example:* Students expect 24×7 lab access, but the HOD assumes they only need 9-to-5 access.

2. **Gap 2 — The Standards Gap**
   Difference between management's perception of customer expectations and the actual service quality standards set.
   *Example:* Management knows students need better labs but does not allocate budget.

3. **Gap 3 — The Delivery Gap**
   Difference between service quality specifications and the actual service delivered.
   *Example:* Lab is supposed to be open until 10 PM (standard) but is locked at 6 PM.

4. **Gap 4 — The Communication Gap**
   Difference between service delivered and what is communicated to customers through external marketing.
   *Example:* College brochure promises "world-class infrastructure" but reality is poor Wi-Fi.

5. **Gap 5 — The Customer Gap**
   Difference between customer expectations and their perceptions of service received. This is the **end-result gap** and is a function of the previous four:

$$Gap_5 = f(Gap_1, Gap_2, Gap_3, Gap_4)$$

**Closing Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap:**
- Conduct regular **market research** (focus groups, surveys).
- Improve **upward communication** from frontline staff to management.
- Implement a **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** system to capture expectations.

**Closing Gap 3 — Delivery Gap:**
- Invest in **employee training** and empowerment.
- Use **technology** (automation, self-service) to reduce human error.
- Align **incentive systems** with service quality KPIs.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for explaining 5 gaps, 1 each for closing G1 and G3)*

---

### Part (b) — SERVQUAL Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the Gap Formula per Dimension**

$$SQ_{Reliability} = P - E = 4.2 - 6.5 = -2.3$$

$$SQ_{Assurance} = 5.1 - 6.3 = -1.2$$

$$SQ_{Tangibles} = 4.9 - 5.8 = -0.9$$

$$SQ_{Empathy} = 3.8 - 6.0 = -2.2$$

$$SQ_{Responsiveness} = 4.0 - 6.4 = -2.4$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate Results**

| Dimension | Gap (P − E) | Severity Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Responsiveness | **−2.4** | 1 (Worst) |
| Reliability | −2.3 | 2 |
| Empathy | −2.2 | 3 |
| Assurance | −1.2 | 4 |
| Tangibles | −0.9 | 5 (Best) |

**Step 3 — Identify Weakest Dimension**
The **weakest dimension is Responsiveness** with a gap of **−2.4 Likert points**, meaning the hospital is severely failing in prompt service delivery.

**Step 4 — Two Managerial Actions**
1. **Reduce wait times** by implementing a triage-based digital queue management system with real-time SMS updates to patients.
2. **Empower nurses and front-desk staff** with a one-touch escalation protocol so patient requests are addressed within 10 minutes.

*(7 marks: 2 for gap calculation, 1 for tabulation, 1 for identifying weakest, 3 for two valid managerial actions)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** *Service Quality Tools and IPA* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Critical Incident Technique (CIT)** for measuring service quality. What are its **advantages and limitations**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a **telecom service provider**, the importance (I) and performance (P) of 4 service attributes are given below. Construct the **Importance–Performance Matrix**, identify the **attribute that needs immediate managerial attention**, and justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

| Attribute | Importance (I) | Performance (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Network Coverage | 6.8 | 4.5 |
| Customer Care Response | 6.5 | 5.0 |
| Data Speed | 6.7 | 6.6 |
| Billing Transparency | 5.5 | 6.2 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Critical Incident Technique (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The **Critical Incident Technique (CIT)** is a **qualitative** service quality measurement method developed by **John C. Flanagan (1954)**. It involves collecting **"critical incidents"** — specific examples of unusually satisfying or unusually dissatisfying service experiences — directly from customers via structured interviews or open-ended surveys.

**Procedure (5 Steps):**

1. **Identify the research objective** (e.g., measure call center quality).
2. **Develop the incident classification form** (5–7 categories aligned with service dimensions).
3. **Collect incidents** from a representative sample of customers (usually 100–500 incidents).
4. **Code and classify** each incident as **satisfactory (+)**, **dissatisfactory (−)**, or **neutral (0)**.
5. **Analyze frequencies** within categories to derive the **Critical Incident Index (CII)**:

$$CII = \frac{N_{positive} - N_{negative}}{N_{total}}$$

**Advantages:**
- Captures **real, lived customer experiences** (not hypothetical).
- Uncovers **specific pain points** that quantitative surveys miss.
- Low implementation cost; flexible across industries.

**Limitations:**
- Highly **time-consuming** and dependent on customer memory.
- **Subjective coding** introduces inter-rater reliability issues.
- Difficult to **generalize** from a small incident set.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition/procedure, 2 for CII formula, 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for limitations)*

---

### Part (b) — Importance–Performance Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Grand Means (IPA Axes)**

$$\bar{I} = \frac{6.8 + 6.5 + 6.7 + 5.5}{4} = \frac{25.5}{4} = 6.375$$

$$\bar{P} = \frac{4.5 + 5.0 + 6.6 + 6.2}{4} = \frac{22.3}{4} = 5.575$$

**Step 2 — Compute I − P Gap for Each Attribute**

$$Gap_{Network} = 6.8 - 4.5 = 2.3$$

$$Gap_{CustomerCare} = 6.5 - 5.0 = 1.5$$

$$Gap_{DataSpeed} = 6.7 - 6.6 = 0.1$$

$$Gap_{Billing} = 5.5 - 6.2 = -0.7$$

**Step 3 — Quadrant Classification**

| Attribute | I | P | Quadrant | I − P | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Network Coverage | 6.8 | 4.5 | **Q1 (High I, Low P)** | 2.3 | **Concentrate Here** |
| Customer Care Response | 6.5 | 5.0 | Q2 (High I, Low P) | 1.5 | Improve |
| Data Speed | 6.7 | 6.6 | Q2 (High I, High P) | 0.1 | Keep Up Good Work |
| Billing Transparency | 5.5 | 6.2 | Q4 (Low I, High P) | −0.7 | Possible Overkill |

**Step 4 — Attribute Needing Immediate Attention**
**Network Coverage** is in **Quadrant Q1** ("Concentrate Here") with the **largest I − P gap of 2.3 Likert points**. Customers consider it highly important, but the company is under-performing significantly.

**Justification:** The combination of *high customer importance* and *low actual performance* makes it the **top-priority crisis zone**. Management must invest in **tower infrastructure, signal boosters, and 5G rollout** in low-coverage areas.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for grand means, 1.5 for I−P calculation, 2 for quadrant mapping, 2 for identification + justification)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Perception" and "Expectation" in the formula** — Many students write $E - P$ instead of $P - E$. The correct sign is **$P - E$**. Getting the sign wrong will cost you **2–3 marks** in Part (b) numericals.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1988)** — Examiners *expect* the developer's name. Skipping it costs **1 mark** in definition questions.
>
> 3. **Drawing a wrong Gap-5 diagram** — Gap 5 is between **Customer Expectation** and **Customer Perceived Service** — NOT between two internal provider stages. Drawing Gap 5 on the provider side is a **3-mark error**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the weighted SERVQUAL** — When importance weights are given in the question, you **MUST** apply the weighted formula $\sum w_i(P_i - E_i)$ with $\sum w_i = 1$. Using unweighted average will lose **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **CIT vs SERVQUAL confusion** — CIT is **qualitative** (incidents); SERVQUAL is **quantitative** (Likert). Mixing up the methodology costs **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **No unit mention in numerical answers** — Always write the unit as **"Likert points"** or **"score (1–7 scale)"**. Marks are deducted for unit-less numerical answers.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Service Quality** is the **gap between customer Perception (P) and Expectation (E)**: $SQ = P - E$.
- The **SERVQUAL model** was developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1988)** and uses a **22-item, 5-dimension** questionnaire on a **7-point Likert scale**.
- The **5 dimensions (RATER)** are: **Reliability, Assurance, Tangibles, Empathy, Responsiveness** — memorize this acronym.
- The **5 Gaps Model (1990)** identifies **Gap 5 (customer gap)** as the cumulative effect of **Gaps 1, 2, 3, and 4** (provider-side gaps).
- **Gap 1** → closed by **better market research**; **Gap 3** → closed by **better training**.
- **SERVPERF (Cronin & Taylor, 1992)** is a simpler alternative: $SQ = P$ (no expectation).
- **CIT (Flanagan, 1954)** is a **qualitative** method using structured incident interviews.
- **IPA (Martilla & James, 1977)** uses a 2×2 matrix of **Importance (I) vs. Performance (P)**. Q1 (High I, Low P) is the **priority zone**.
- **Cronbach's Alpha ($\alpha \geq 0.7$)** confirms questionnaire reliability.
- Always quote the **author, year, and formula** for full marks in KTU 2024 Part A.
- In numericals, **carry 2 decimal places** and **explicitly state units** ("Likert points").
- The **Gap-5 equation** is: $Gap_5 = Gap_1 + Gap_2 + Gap_3 + Gap_4$.
- Service quality measurement is a **continuous PDCA cycle**, not a one-time survey.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
