<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prevention Costs** are the expenditures incurred by an organization to **prevent, reduce, or eliminate the occurrence of defects, errors, and non-conformances** in products, processes, or services *before* they occur. These are **proactive investments** designed to build quality into the system rather than inspect it at the end.

In the framework of the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — also called the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model** — Prevention Costs sit at the very foundation of the quality cost pyramid. They are classified under the **Cost of Conformance** (along with Appraisal Costs) and represent the money spent to *do things right the first time*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Vaccination Analogy (Plain English)**
> Imagine a city facing a dengue outbreak.
> - **Prevention Cost** = Money spent on mosquito-control fogging, public awareness campaigns, distributing repellents, and vaccination drives *before* people fall sick.
> - **Appraisal Cost** = Money spent on daily health check-up camps and diagnostic tests to *detect* infection early.
> - **Internal Failure Cost** = Money spent on hospital treatment for patients already infected inside the city.
> - **External Failure Cost** = Money spent compensating victims' families, lawsuits, and reputation repair when the disease spreads to neighbouring cities.
>
> Clearly, **spending ₹1 on fogging (prevention) saves ₹100 on hospital bills (failure)**. Prevention Costs are the "fogging drives" of an organization.

## 1.3 Standard Quality Cost Metrics (Industry-Recognized)

| Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Prevention Cost Ratio (PCR)** | Prevention Cost $\div$ Total Quality Cost | **%** |
| **Cost of Quality as % of Sales** | Total CoQ $\div$ Revenue | **%** |
| **Prevention-to-Failure Ratio** | Prevention Cost $\div$ Total Failure Cost | Ratio |

> [!TIP]
> World-class organizations typically target a **Prevention Cost Ratio of 50%–70%** of total quality cost, indicating they invest heavily in getting it right the first time rather than fixing failures downstream.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cost of Quality (CoQ) Hierarchy

Quality Costs in any organization fall into **four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE)** categories. Prevention Costs are the strategic anchor of the entire framework.

1. **Prevention Costs** — *Stop defects before they happen*
2. **Appraisal Costs** — *Detect defects that did happen*
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — *Fix defects found before customer sees them*
4. **External Failure Costs** — *Fix defects found after reaching the customer*

## 2.2 Detailed Components of Prevention Costs

Each prevention activity translates into a specific cost line item on the quality accounting ledger:

- **Quality Planning & Design Costs** — Salaries of quality engineers, cost of developing Quality Plans, FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) workshops, and Design of Experiments (DoE) studies.
- **Process Control Costs** — Installation and maintenance of Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, control charts, process capability studies ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
- **Quality Training & Education** — Cost of conducting training programs, Six Sigma/Lean certifications, on-the-job quality coaching.
- **Supplier Capability Evaluation** — Audits, supplier rating programs, incoming capability assessments of vendor processes.
- **Design Reviews & Prototyping** — Cost of multiple design iterations, prototype testing, reliability engineering.
- **Quality System Maintenance** — Cost of maintaining ISO 9001/QMS documentation, calibration of measuring instruments, internal quality audits.
- **Field Testing & Reliability Studies** — Pre-market testing, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) studies, accelerated life testing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|------------------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total Quality Cost = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | ₹ / $ |
| 2 | $CoC = P + A$ | Cost of Conformance = Prevention + Appraisal | ₹ / $ |
| 3 | $CoNC = IF + EF$ | Cost of Non-Conformance = Internal + External Failure | ₹ / $ |
| 4 | $PCR = \dfrac{P}{TQC} \times 100$ | Prevention Cost Ratio | **%** |
| 5 | $PFR = \dfrac{IF + EF}{P}$ | Penalty-to-Prevention Ratio (lower is better) | Ratio |
| 6 | $P_{opt} = \dfrac{\ln(k)}{m}$ | Optimum prevention cost (model-based) | ₹ |
| 7 | $F(P) = F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$ | Failure cost as exponential decay function of prevention spend | ₹ |
| 8 | $A(P) = A_0 + \alpha P$ | Appraisal cost grows linearly with prevention effort | ₹ |

Where:
- $P$ = Prevention Cost
- $A$ = Appraisal Cost  
- $IF$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $EF$ = External Failure Cost
- $F_0$ = Initial failure cost when $P = 0$
- $k$ = Decay constant (prevention effectiveness coefficient)
- $m$ = Marginal cost coefficient of prevention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always remember the equation $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$. This is the *single most tested* relationship in Module 3, appearing in nearly every past-year question paper.

## 2.4 Strategic Utility in Engineering Management

Prevention Costs are not merely an accounting entry — they are a **strategic lever**. Companies that systematically increase prevention spending typically observe:

- **Dramatic fall in scrap and rework rates** (often 40–60% reduction within 2 years).
- **Improved First Pass Yield (FPY)** moving from ~70% to >95%.
- **Higher supplier On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** performance.
- **Lower warranty claims** and stronger brand equity.

In real engineering domains — semiconductor manufacturing, automotive (Toyota Production System), aerospace (AS9100), and pharma — prevention spending is treated as **capital investment in quality capability**, not as overhead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Optimal Prevention Cost Model (Classical Derivation)

This is a **high-yield derivation** frequently asked in KTU examinations as a 7-mark or 14-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Cost Model

Let $P$ denote the total prevention expenditure per period. Empirical studies show that:

**Step 1 — Failure Cost Decay Function**
As prevention spending increases, failure costs drop exponentially because each preventive action eliminates a class of potential failures. We model:

$$F(P) = F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$$

Where $F_0$ is the failure cost when prevention is zero, and $k > 0$ measures how effectively prevention converts into failure reduction.

**Step 2 — Appraisal Cost Function**
Appraisal costs rise linearly with prevention because tighter process control requires more inspection, gauging, and monitoring:

$$A(P) = A_0 + \alpha P$$

Where $A_0$ is the baseline appraisal cost and $\alpha$ is the marginal appraisal intensity.

**Step 3 — Direct Prevention Cost**
The prevention cost itself is simply $P$.

### 3.1.2 Constructing the Total Quality Cost Function

**Step 4** — Total Quality Cost is the sum of all four categories:

$$TQC(P) = P + A(P) + F(P)$$

Substituting Steps 1–3:

$$TQC(P) = P + (A_0 + \alpha P) + F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$$

**Step 5** — Combine linear terms:

$$TQC(P) = (1 + \alpha)P + A_0 + F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$$

### 3.1.3 Minimization by Calculus (Finding Optimum $P^*$)

**Step 6** — Differentiate $TQC(P)$ with respect to $P$ and set the derivative to zero for the optimum:

$$\frac{d\,TQC(P)}{dP} = (1 + \alpha) - kF_0 \cdot e^{-kP} = 0$$

**Step 7** — Solve for $e^{-kP}$:

$$kF_0 \cdot e^{-kP} = (1 + \alpha)$$

$$e^{-kP} = \frac{1 + \alpha}{kF_0}$$

**Step 8** — Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-kP^* = \ln\left(\frac{1 + \alpha}{kF_0}\right)$$

**Step 9** — Rearrange for the **optimal prevention cost**:

$$P^* = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{kF_0}{1 + \alpha}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **closed-form optimum** the examiner expects. A common alternate form seen in textbooks is written as $P^* = \dfrac{\ln(kF_0) - \ln(1+\alpha)}{k}$, which is mathematically identical.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A manufacturing unit has $F_0 = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $k = 0.0008$, $\alpha = 0.5$. Find $P^*$.

**Solution:**

**Step 10** — Substitute into the optimum formula:

$$P^* = \frac{1}{0.0008} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{0.0008 \times 5{,}00{,}000}{1 + 0.5}\right)$$

**Step 11** — Compute the numerator inside the logarithm:

$$kF_0 = 0.0008 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 400$$

**Step 12** — Divide by $(1+\alpha)$:

$$\frac{400}{1.5} = 266.67$$

**Step 13** — Take natural log:

$$\ln(266.67) \approx 5.586$$

**Step 14** — Final division:

$$P^* = \frac{5.586}{0.0008} = 6{,}982.5 \text{ ₹}$$

So the company should spend approximately **₹6,983 on prevention** to minimize its total quality cost.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Prevention vs. Other Cost Categories

| Dimension | Prevention Cost | Appraisal Cost | Internal Failure | External Failure |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Timing** | Before defect occurs | During/at production | After defect, before shipment | After customer receives |
| **Visibility** | Hard to quantify, often hidden | Measurable | Measurable (scrap logs) | Measurable (warranty) |
| **Control** | Management decision-driven | QC department | Production department | Service/sales |
| **Trend with ↑ P** | Increases linearly | Increases slowly | Decreases sharply | Decreases sharply |
| **Effect on TQC** | Optimal at $P^*$ | Sub-optimal at extremes | Always undesirable | Always undesirable |
| **Strategic value** | **Highest leverage** | Medium leverage | Reactive | Most damaging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAF Model — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram shows how Prevention Costs anchor the entire Cost of Quality framework and how each component feeds into the Total Quality Cost calculation engine.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cost of Quality COQ] --> B[Cost of Conformance COC]
    A --> C[Cost of Non-Conformance CONC]
    B --> D[PREVENTION COSTS]
    B --> E[Appraisal Costs]
    C --> F[Internal Failure Costs]
    C --> G[External Failure Costs]
    D --> D1[Quality Planning and Design]
    D --> D2[Process Control and SPC]
    D --> D3[Quality Training Programs]
    D --> D4[Supplier Evaluation Audits]
    D --> D5[Design Reviews Prototyping]
    D --> D6[Quality System Maintenance ISO]
    E --> E1[Incoming Inspection]
    E --> E2[In-Process Inspection]
    E --> E3[Final Inspection and Testing]
    F --> F1[Scrap Costs]
    F --> F2[Rework Costs]
    F --> F3[Reinspection Costs]
    G --> G1[Warranty Claims]
    G --> G2[Product Recall Costs]
    G --> G3[Customer Complaint Handling]
    G --> G4[Loss of Goodwill]
    H[Total Quality Cost TQC Engine] --> A
    I[Prevention Cost Ratio PCR] --> A
    J[Optimal Prevention Spend P Star] --> D
```

## 4.2 Prevention Cost Dynamics — Strategic Flow

This diagram illustrates the **cause–effect cascade** showing how a marginal rupee invested in prevention triggers cost savings across multiple failure categories — the core strategic insight of prevention costing.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Prevention Investment] --> T1[Improved Process Capability]
    P --> T2[Trained Workforce]
    P --> T3[Robust Supplier Base]
    P --> T4[Proactive Design Reviews]
    T1 --> R1[Reduced Scrap and Rework]
    T2 --> R2[Reduced Operator Errors]
    T3 --> R3[Fewer Incoming Defects]
    T4 --> R4[Lower Field Failure Rate]
    R1 --> S[Total Quality Cost Reduction]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    S --> OUT[Higher Profit Margin and Brand Equity]
    M[Management Decision Lever] --> P
    K[Cost of Quality KPI Dashboard] --> M
```

## 4.3 Behavioural Curve — Cost Categories vs. Prevention Spend

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Prevention Spend P] --> Y1[Prevention Cost Curve Rising Linearly]
    X --> Y2[Failure Cost Curve Falling Exponentially]
    X --> Y3[Total Quality Cost U Shaped Curve]
    Y1 --> Z[Optimum Point P Star Where TQC is Minimum]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Z --> W[Strategic Sweet Spot for Management]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** This is the classic *Juran/Feigenbaum trade-off curve*. Students are often asked to *sketch and label* this in KTU descriptive questions. The U-shaped TQC curve with its minimum point is the visual centrepiece of any prevention-cost answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Define Prevention Costs. Give any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Prevention Costs are expenditures incurred by an organization to **prevent the occurrence of defects, errors, and non-conformances** in products, processes, or services *before* they happen. They represent proactive investments in building quality into the system.

**Four examples:**
1. Quality planning and design — cost of FMEA studies and Quality Plan preparation.
2. Process control — installation of Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts and capability studies ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
3. Quality training programs — Six Sigma / Lean certifications for employees.
4. Supplier capability evaluation — audits, vendor rating, and incoming capability assessments.

These are categorized under **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** in the PAF model.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**Distinguish between Prevention Costs and Appraisal Costs with two points each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Prevention Costs | Appraisal Costs |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Purpose** | To *prevent* defects from occurring | To *detect* defects that have occurred |
| **Timing** | Before / during process design | During and after production |
| **Examples** | Quality training, SPC, supplier audits | Incoming inspection, final testing |
| **Strategic role** | Proactive — investment in capability | Reactive — measurement and verification |
| **Effect on failure costs** | Reduces both internal and external failure | Reduces internal failure only |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** *Explain the various elements of Prevention Costs in a manufacturing organization. List at least **six distinct elements** with one-line descriptions. **(7 Marks)***

**(b)** *A company has the following annual quality cost data: Prevention = ₹80,000, Appraisal = ₹1,20,000, Internal Failure = ₹2,00,000, External Failure = ₹4,00,000. Calculate (i) Total Quality Cost, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, and (iv) Prevention Cost Ratio. Comment on the company's quality strategy. **(7 Marks)***

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **six elements of Prevention Costs** in a manufacturing organization are:

1. **Quality Planning and Design** — Costs of preparing quality manuals, Quality Function Deployment (QFD) workshops, and Design FMEA.
2. **Process Control** — Installation and operation of $\bar{X}$-R charts, $p$-charts, $c$-charts; cost of capability studies.
3. **Quality Training and Education** — Cost of induction quality training, Six Sigma Green/Black Belt programs, on-the-job quality coaching.
4. **Supplier Capability Evaluation** — Costs of supplier audits, rating programs, vendor development visits, and incoming capability studies.
5. **Design Reviews and Prototyping** — Costs of multiple design iterations, prototype testing, Design of Experiments (DoE).
6. **Quality System Maintenance** — Costs of ISO 9001/QMS audits, calibration of gauges, internal quality audit expenses.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award full 7 marks only when the student writes **distinct, non-overlapping** categories. Avoid duplicating "quality planning" and "design review" as separate items unless justified.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $P = 80{,}000$
- $A = 1{,}20{,}000$
- $IF = 2{,}00{,}000$
- $EF = 4{,}00{,}000$

**(i) Total Quality Cost:**

$$TQC = P + A + IF + EF = 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$TQC = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**(ii) Cost of Conformance:**

$$CoC = P + A = 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**(iii) Cost of Non-Conformance:**

$$CoNC = IF + EF = 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**(iv) Prevention Cost Ratio:**

$$PCR = \frac{P}{TQC} \times 100 = \frac{80{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Commentary on Strategy:**

| Observation | Value | Verdict |
|-------------|-------|---------|
| PCR | 10% | **Too low** — world-class is 50–70% |
| CoNC $\div$ CoC | 6,00,000 $\div$ 2,00,000 = 3.0 | For every ₹1 spent on conformance, ₹3 is lost to failures |
| EF is 50% of total failure | 4,00,000 $\div$ 6,00,000 | Severe — defective products reaching customers |

**Verdict:** The company follows a **"detect-and-correct" reactive strategy** instead of a "prevent-at-source" proactive strategy. Management should **aggressively increase prevention spending** (training, SPC, supplier development) to drive down the dominant external failure costs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** *Derive the optimal prevention cost $P^*$ that minimizes the Total Quality Cost using the exponential failure decay model. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)***

**(b)** *A firm has $F_0 = 8{,}00{,}000$ ₹, $k = 0.001$, $\alpha = 0.4$. Determine $P^*$ and the corresponding minimum Total Quality Cost if $A_0 = 50{,}000$ ₹. **(7 Marks)***

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Failure costs decay exponentially with prevention spending: $F(P) = F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$.
2. Appraisal costs grow linearly with prevention: $A(P) = A_0 + \alpha P$.
3. All variables are continuous and differentiable.
4. $k > 0$ and $\alpha \geq 0$.

**Derivation (already detailed in Section 3.1 above):**

$$TQC(P) = (1 + \alpha)P + A_0 + F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$$

$$\frac{d\,TQC}{dP} = (1 + \alpha) - kF_0 \cdot e^{-kP} = 0$$

$$\boxed{P^* = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{kF_0}{1 + \alpha}\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1** — Substitute into $P^*$ formula:

$$P^* = \frac{1}{0.001} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{0.001 \times 8{,}00{,}000}{1 + 0.4}\right)$$

**Step 2** — Compute $kF_0$:

$$kF_0 = 0.001 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 800$$

**Step 3** — Divide by $(1 + \alpha) = 1.4$:

$$\frac{800}{1.4} = 571.43$$

**Step 4** — Take natural log:

$$\ln(571.43) \approx 6.348$$

**Step 5** — Compute $P^*$:

$$P^* = \frac{6.348}{0.001} = 6{,}348 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6** — Compute $F(P^*)$:

$$F(P^*) = 8{,}00{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.001 \times 6348} = 8{,}00{,}000 \cdot e^{-6.348}$$

$$e^{-6.348} \approx 0.00175$$

$$F(P^*) \approx 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00175 = 1{,}400 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 7** — Compute $A(P^*)$:

$$A(P^*) = 50{,}000 + 0.4 \times 6{,}348 = 50{,}000 + 2{,}539 = 52{,}539 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 8** — Compute $TQC_{min}$:

$$TQC_{min} = P^* + A(P^*) + F(P^*)$$

$$TQC_{min} = 6{,}348 + 52{,}539 + 1{,}400$$

$$\boxed{TQC_{min} \approx 60{,}287 \text{ ₹}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT** forget to convert $F_0 \times k$ into a single number *before* dividing by $(1+\alpha)$. Students often commit order-of-operation errors here and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** report $P^*$ without units. Always write "₹" or "rupees".
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the comment section in numerical questions like Question A(b). The examiner awards **at least 1–2 marks** for the qualitative interpretation of the calculated PCR.
> 4. **Do NOT** confuse $CoC$ and $CoNC$. They are *opposite* halves of the CoQ pie — mixing them up costs full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Prevention Costs = proactive investments** to *stop* defects before they occur (training, SPC, supplier audits, design reviews, quality planning).
- ✅ They belong to the **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** along with Appraisal Costs.
- ✅ The **master equation** is $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$.
- ✅ **Prevention Cost Ratio (PCR)** should ideally be **50%–70%** in world-class firms.
- ✅ As $P$ increases, **failure costs fall exponentially** ($F_0 \cdot e^{-kP}$) while appraisal costs rise linearly ($A_0 + \alpha P$).
- ✅ The **optimal prevention cost** is $P^* = \dfrac{1}{k} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{kF_0}{1+\alpha}\right)$.
- ✅ **Penalty-to-Prevention Ratio (PFR)** must be minimized — high PFR = reactive company.
- ✅ Prevention is the **only category that simultaneously reduces scrap, rework, warranty, and customer complaints**.
- ✅ Always quote **real-world examples** (vaccination, fogging, Toyota Production System) to earn higher-order marks in KTU descriptive answers.
- ✅ The **U-shaped TQC curve** with $P^*$ at the minimum is a *must-sketch* for full marks in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appraisal Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Appraisal Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Cost of Quality (COQ)** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Quality Engineering and Management*, **Appraisal Costs** are formally defined as the expenditures incurred by an organization to **detect, identify, measure, evaluate, and audit** the conformance of products, components, processes, and services to defined quality standards, specifications, and customer requirements **before** the product is shipped to the customer.

According to the classical **Feigenbaum–Juran Quality Cost Model**, Appraisal Costs fall under the broader category of **Conformance Costs**, alongside Prevention Costs, because both are *deliberately invested* by the organization to ensure quality. They represent the price paid for **detection-oriented quality activities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):**
> "Appraisal costs are the costs associated with measuring, evaluating, or auditing products, components, materials, processes, and services to assure conformance to specified quality requirements. These costs are incurred to *detect defects* that may already exist, and they occur *before* the product reaches the customer."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **chocolate manufacturing unit** in Kerala that produces 10,000 chocolate bars daily. Before packing each bar into a box, you:

1. Hire **quality inspectors** to visually check each bar for cracks, discoloration, and proper weight.
2. Run the bars through a **metal detector** to ensure no metal fragments are present.
3. Send random samples to a **laboratory** for chemical and microbiological testing.
4. Pay an **external auditor** to certify your factory processes monthly.
5. Pay for the **electricity and depreciation** of the inspection machines.

All of these expenses — salaries of inspectors, lab testing fees, machine depreciation, calibration, and audits — together form your **Appraisal Costs**. You are essentially *appraising* (judging) the chocolate to see if it meets the promised quality standards before your customer ever tastes it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Appraisal costs are the "examiner's fee" you pay to grade your own work *before* submitting it to the final evaluator (the customer). If the examiner finds flaws, you fix them in-house; if the examiner misses a flaw, it becomes an *internal* or *external failure cost* — which is far more expensive.

### 1.3 Placement in the Quality Cost Hierarchy

Appraisal Costs occupy a unique position in the **P-A-I-F Model** (Prevention–Appraisal–Internal Failure–External Failure) developed by Armand V. Feigenbaum:

$$
\text{Total Quality Cost (COQ)} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where:

- $C_P$ = Prevention Costs
- $C_A$ = **Appraisal Costs** ← *our current focus*
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Costs
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Costs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Insight for KTU:** Appraisal costs are the "spending-to-detect" category. They are a **necessary investment** but are considered *less optimal* than prevention costs. A mature quality organization progressively **shifts its quality budget from Appraisal → Prevention**, because preventing a defect is always cheaper than detecting one.

### 1.4 Boundary Conditions and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Appraisal Cost as % of Sales | **1% to 5%** of total revenue | Percentage (%) |
| Appraisal Cost as % of COQ | **20% to 40%** of total quality cost | Percentage (%) |
| Optimal App. Cost Ratio | **10% to 25%** (mature QMS) | Percentage (%) |
| Inspection Time per Unit | Industry-dependent | Minutes / Seconds |
| First-Pass Yield (FPY) | **95% – 99.99%** (Six-Sigma level) | Percentage (%) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Appraisal Costs

Appraisal Costs are not a single expense line; they are an **aggregate of multiple inspection, testing, and verification activities**. For KTU 2024 board examinations, students must memorize and apply the following classification:

#### 2.1.1 Inbound (Incoming) Material Inspection
- Cost of inspecting raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies received from suppliers.
- Includes sampling inspection, receiving inspection, and supplier certification audits.
- *Engineering Utility:* Prevents substandard inputs from contaminating the production line.

#### 2.1.2 In-Process Inspection
- Costs incurred during the manufacturing process to monitor process stability.
- Includes patrolling inspections, Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart maintenance, and First-Article Inspection (FAI).
- *Engineering Utility:* Detects process drift before defective products pile up.

#### 2.1.3 Final Product Inspection and Testing
- Cost of inspecting the finished product against specifications before shipment.
- Includes functional testing, performance testing, dimensional inspection, and burn-in tests.
- *Engineering Utility:* Acts as the *last gatekeeper* before the customer receives the product.

#### 2.1.4 Calibration and Maintenance of Test Equipment
- Costs of maintaining gauges, measuring instruments, and test equipment.
- Includes depreciation of metrology equipment, calibration services, and repair.
- *Engineering Utility:* Ensures the inspection tools themselves are accurate (Garbage-In-Garbage-Out prevention).

#### 2.1.5 Quality Audits
- Internal Quality Audits (IQA) and External System Audits (e.g., ISO 9001, AS9100).
- Costs of auditor time, audit reports, and corrective action tracking.
- *Engineering Utility:* Verifies the effectiveness of the entire Quality Management System.

#### 2.1.6 Field Testing and Reliability Testing
- Testing products under simulated or actual field conditions.
- Includes accelerated life testing, environmental stress screening (ESS), and MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) tests.
- *Engineering Utility:* Validates long-term performance and reliability claims.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Appraisal Activity

| Activity | Why It Is Done | How It Is Executed |
|---|---|---|
| Incoming Inspection | Filter out defective raw materials | AQL sampling, supplier audits |
| In-Process Inspection | Detect process drift early | SPC charts, control limits |
| Final Inspection | Prevent defective shipments | Functional testing, AQL sampling |
| Calibration | Ensure measurement accuracy | ISO/IEC 17025 procedures |
| Quality Audits | Verify QMS conformance | ISO 9001 audit checklists |
| Field Testing | Confirm real-world reliability | HALT, HASS, accelerated aging |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common 14-mark question asks students to *"List and explain the various components of Appraisal Costs with industry examples."* Memorizing the six sub-categories above is mandatory for full marks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe-Breaking Rule:** All absolute-value or divisibility notations in the table below use `\vert` (not `\|`) to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Quality Cost (COQ) | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Sum of all four quality cost categories | Currency (₹ / $) |
| 2 | Conformance Cost | $C_{Conformance} = C_P + C_A$ | Cost of doing things right the first time | Currency |
| 3 | Non-Conformance Cost | $C_{NonConformance} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost of doing things wrong | Currency |
| 4 | Appraisal Cost Ratio | $ACR = \dfrac{C_A}{COQ} \times 100$ | Appraisal as % of total quality cost | % |
| 5 | Appraisal Cost Ratio (Sales) | $ACR_{Sales} = \dfrac{C_A}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Appraisal as % of revenue | % |
| 6 | Appraisal Cost per Unit | $ACU = \dfrac{C_A}{N_{inspected}}$ | Appraisal cost per inspected unit | Currency / Unit |
| 7 | Inspection Cost per Unit | $ICU = \dfrac{\text{Inspector Wages} + \text{Equipment Cost}}{\text{Units Inspected}}$ | True unit-level inspection cost | Currency / Unit |
| 8 | Cost of Detection | $C_{Detect} = \dfrac{C_A}{N_{defects caught}}$ | Average cost to catch one defect | Currency / Defect |
| 9 | Yield-Weighted Appraisal | $C_{A,eff} = \dfrac{C_A}{Y}$ | Where $Y$ = First-Pass Yield | Currency / Unit |
| 10 | Optimal Inspection Fraction | $f^* = \dfrac{C_{IF}}{C_A + C_{IF}}$ | Economic sampling fraction | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Multi-Stage Sampling Cost | $C_{A,total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{A,i}$ | Sum across $n$ inspection stages | Currency |
| 12 | Total Inspection Cost Model | $C_A = N \cdot c_i + N \cdot p \cdot c_r$ | $N$ = units, $c_i$ = inspect cost/unit, $p$ = defect prob., $c_r$ = rework cost/unit | Currency |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern industries, Appraisal Costs find direct application in:

- **Automotive Manufacturing:** End-of-Line (EOL) testing using dynamometers, vision systems, and leak testers. Each vehicle undergoes ~500–2000 individual inspection checks.
- **Electronics / PCB Assembly:** Automated Optical Inspection (AOI), In-Circuit Testing (ICT), X-ray inspection of BGA solder joints, and functional board testing.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** HPLC (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography), sterility testing, and dissolution tests for every batch.
- **Aerospace / Defense:** First-Article Inspection (FAI), NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) using ultrasonic, eddy current, and radiographic methods.
- **Food Processing (Kerala Context):** BIS standards for spices, marine products, and coconut oil — each batch requires FSSAI-compliant lab testing.

### 2.5 The Strategic Logic: Appraisal vs. Prevention Trade-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Quality Economics (KTU High-Yield):**
> *"Every ₹1 spent on Prevention saves ₹10 on Appraisal, ₹100 on Internal Failure, and ₹1000 on External Failure."* — Adapted from Joseph M. Juran's Quality Cost Trade-off Principle.

This **1-10-100-1000 Rule** is the cornerstone of quality cost economics and is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU board examinations.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Total Quality Cost Calculation

A small-scale LED bulb manufacturing unit in Kochi reports the following annual quality costs:

- Prevention Costs ($C_P$): ₹ 2,00,000
- **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$): ₹ 3,50,000**
- Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$): ₹ 4,00,000
- External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$): ₹ 6,00,000

**Step 1: Compute Total Quality Cost (COQ).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
COQ &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \\
COQ &= ₹\,15{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Appraisal Cost Ratio (ACR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR &= \frac{C_A}{COQ} \times 100 \\
ACR &= \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{15{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
ACR &= 22.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Appraisal Cost as a percentage of Net Sales (assuming Net Sales = ₹ 70,00,000).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR_{Sales} &= \frac{C_A}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
ACR_{Sales} &= \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{70{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
ACR_{Sales} &= 5.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Conformance and Non-Conformance Costs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Conformance} &= C_P + C_A = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = ₹\,5{,}50{,}000 \\
C_{NonConformance} &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm's Non-Conformance Cost (₹10 lakh) is **~1.82 times** its Conformance Cost (₹5.5 lakh), indicating an *under-investment* in prevention. The firm should **shift budget from Failure → Prevention** to reduce total COQ.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cost of Detection and Inspection Cost per Unit

A Kerala-based spice export company inspects **50,000 kg** of cardamom annually. The total appraisal cost is ₹ 5,00,000. The inspection process catches **2,500 kg** of defective cardamom.

**Step 1: Compute Appraisal Cost per Unit (per kg).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACU &= \frac{C_A}{N_{inspected}} \\
ACU &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
ACU &= ₹\,10 \text{ per kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Cost of Detection (per defect caught).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Detect} &= \frac{C_A}{N_{defects caught}} \\
C_{Detect} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}500} \\
C_{Detect} &= ₹\,200 \text{ per defective kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Effective Appraisal Cost per Good Unit (assuming First-Pass Yield = 95%).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.95 \\
C_{A,eff} &= \frac{C_A}{N \cdot Y} \\
C_{A,eff} &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000 \times 0.95} \\
C_{A,eff} &= ₹\,10.53 \text{ per good kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** It costs the firm **₹200 to catch every defective kg** of cardamom. The question for management is: *"Would it be cheaper to prevent these defects at the source (e.g., better farmer training) rather than detect them post-harvest?"*

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Multi-Stage Inspection Cost Aggregation

A PCB assembly line in Bengaluru has **three inspection stages**. The data is:

| Stage | Units Inspected | Cost per Unit (₹) | Defect Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incoming (Stage 1) | 1,00,000 | 2 | 3% |
| In-Process AOI (Stage 2) | 97,000 | 5 | 2% |
| Final Functional Test (Stage 3) | 95,060 | 8 | 1% |

**Step 1: Compute Appraisal Cost per Stage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{A,1} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = ₹\,2{,}00{,}000 \\
C_{A,2} &= 97{,}000 \times 5 = ₹\,4{,}85{,}000 \\
C_{A,3} &= 95{,}060 \times 8 = ₹\,7{,}60{,}480
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Appraisal Cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{A,total} &= C_{A,1} + C_{A,2} + C_{A,3} \\
C_{A,total} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}85{,}000 + 7{,}60{,}480 \\
C_{A,total} &= ₹\,14{,}45{,}480
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Appraisal Cost per Surviving Good Unit.**

Total good units at end = 95,060 × (1 − 0.01) = 94,109.4 units (round to 94,109).

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACU_{good} &= \frac{14{,}45{,}480}{94{,}109} \\
ACU_{good} &= ₹\,15.36 \text{ per good PCB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Final Functional Test (₹8/unit) is the **most expensive per unit** but has the **lowest defect rate** because earlier stages removed most defects. This is consistent with the **economic logic of layered inspection**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Optimal Inspection Fraction Derivation

Suppose a process produces $N = 10{,}000$ units. The cost of inspecting one unit is $c_i = ₹50$. The probability of a defect is $p = 0.04$. The cost of rework if a defect escapes inspection is $c_r = ₹1{,}000$.

**Step 1: Set up the Total Inspection Cost Model.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= N \cdot c_i + N \cdot p \cdot c_r \\
C_A &= 10{,}000 \times 50 + 10{,}000 \times 0.04 \times 1{,}000 \\
C_A &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \\
C_A &= ₹\,9{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Optimal Inspection Fraction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^* &= \frac{C_{IF}}{C_A + C_{IF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $C_{IF}$ (internal failure cost) = ₹ 4,00,000 (computed above as the rework cost), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^* &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000} \\
f^* &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{13{,}00{,}000} \\
f^* &= 0.3077 \approx 30.77\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The economically optimal inspection fraction is ~30.77% (e.g., AQL-based sampling rather than 100% inspection). Inspecting all 10,000 units would cost ₹9 lakh; sampling ~30.77% (~3,077 units) would yield equivalent economic protection.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Increase or Decrease Appraisal Costs

| Scenario | Effect on Appraisal Costs | Strategic Recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| High External Failure Costs (customer complaints ↑) | **Increase** $C_A$ temporarily to catch defects | Yes — short-term defense |
| High Internal Failure Costs (scrap ↑) | **Increase** $C_A$ at end-of-line | Yes — reduce rework loop |
| Low First-Pass Yield (<90%) | **Increase** $C_A$ to locate defects | Yes — but only as a stopgap |
| Mature QMS, FPY > 99% | **Decrease** $C_A$, shift to Prevention | Yes — long-term optimization |
| New Product Launch (unknown failure modes) | **Increase** $C_A$ heavily (DVT/PVT) | Yes — necessary investment |
| ISO 9001 Certification Audit due | **Increase** $C_A$ (audit + documentation) | Yes — compliance cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Questions of 14 marks often ask: *"Discuss how an organization can optimize its Appraisal Costs while maintaining acceptable quality levels."* The answer must reference the **Prevention-Appraisal Trade-off Curve** and **Cost-Benefit Optimization**.

**Engineering Interpretation of the Trade-off Flow:**

- **Stage 1 → 2:** Newly aware organizations over-rely on inspection (high Appraisal Cost).
- **Stage 3 → 4:** Pareto analysis reveals root causes; investment shifts to Prevention.
- **Stage 5 → 6:** As Prevention rises, Appraisal and Failure costs drop simultaneously.
- **Stage 7:** Total COQ reaches its mathematical minimum — the **Juran Optimum**.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Appraisal Costs. List any four examples of Appraisal Costs in a manufacturing industry.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Appraisal Costs are the expenditures incurred by an organization to **detect, measure, evaluate, and audit** products, processes, and services to ensure conformance to specified quality requirements **before** delivery to the customer.

**Four Examples:**

1. **Cost of incoming material inspection** — testing raw materials against purchase specifications.
2. **Cost of in-process inspection** — patrolling inspections, SPC chart maintenance.
3. **Cost of final product testing** — functional, dimensional, and performance testing.
4. **Cost of calibration of measuring instruments** — ensuring the accuracy of gauges, micrometers, and Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM).
5. *(Additional)* Cost of internal quality audits for ISO 9001 compliance.

*(Any four well-explained examples = 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for examples.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Prevention Costs and Appraisal Costs with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Prevention Costs ($C_P$) | Appraisal Costs ($C_A$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | *Prevent* defects from occurring | *Detect* defects that already exist |
| **Orientation** | Proactive (before-the-fact) | Detective (after-the-fact) |
| **Examples** | Quality training, design reviews, FMEA, process capability studies, supplier development | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC testing, calibration, audits |
| **Cost Behavior** | Decreases failure costs when increased | Increases proportionally with inspection intensity |
| **Strategic Value** | Highest ROI (1:10:100:1000 rule) | Lower ROI than prevention |
| **KTU Tag** | "Invest in prevention" | "Detect before delivery" |

*(A clear 4-row table or 4 well-stated points with one example each = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components of **Appraisal Costs** with industrial examples. Discuss why organizations must invest in Appraisal Costs despite their classification as a "non-value-adding" expense. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data pertains to **ABC Engineering Pvt. Ltd., Thrissur** for the year 2023–24:

| Cost Category | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Quality Training | 1,50,000 |
| Incoming Inspection | 1,20,000 |
| Final Testing | 2,00,000 |
| Design Review | 1,00,000 |
| Scrap and Rework | 3,50,000 |
| Warranty Claims | 4,50,000 |
| Supplier Audit | 80,000 |
| Calibration of Gauges | 1,00,000 |

Calculate:
1. Total Prevention Cost ($C_P$)
2. Total Appraisal Cost ($C_A$)
3. Total Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$)
4. Total External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$)
5. Total Quality Cost (COQ)
6. Appraisal Cost Ratio (as % of COQ)

**(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a):**

Appraisal Costs comprise the following components:

1. **Incoming Material Inspection** — verifying the quality of purchased raw materials and components. *Example:* AQL sampling of steel rods at an automotive plant in Chennai.

2. **In-Process Inspection** — monitoring production processes through SPC, patrol checks, and first-article inspection. *Example:* Real-time CMM checks on CNC-machined aerospace parts.

3. **Final Product Testing** — comprehensive testing of finished goods before dispatch. *Example:* Burn-in testing of server motherboards for 48 hours at 50°C.

4. **Calibration of Test Equipment** — periodic calibration of metrology instruments to maintain ISO/IEC 17025 traceability. *Example:* Annual calibration of a FARO arm at ₹50,000/year.

5. **Internal Quality Audits** — systematic audits of the QMS. *Example:* Quarterly ISO 9001 internal audits by certified lead auditors.

6. **Field and Reliability Testing** — testing products under accelerated stress conditions. *Example:* HALT (Highly Accelerated Life Test) on LED drivers at 95% humidity and 85°C.

**Why Invest in Appraisal Despite Being "Non-Value-Adding"?**

- **Customer Protection:** Prevents defective products from reaching customers and damaging brand reputation.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Many industries (pharma, aerospace, food) require 100% batch testing by law.
- **Feedback Loop:** Appraisal data reveals process weaknesses that drive prevention investments.
- **Liability Shield:** Documented inspection records protect firms in product liability lawsuits.
- **Short-Term Necessity:** Until prevention systems mature, appraisal is the only safety net.

*(6 well-explained points with examples ≈ 7 marks.)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Classify each cost into the P-A-I-F category.**

| Cost Item | Category |
|---|---|
| Quality Training | Prevention |
| Incoming Inspection | **Appraisal** |
| Final Testing | **Appraisal** |
| Design Review | Prevention |
| Scrap and Rework | Internal Failure |
| Warranty Claims | External Failure |
| Supplier Audit | Prevention |
| Calibration of Gauges | **Appraisal** |

**Step 2: Compute Total Prevention Cost ($C_P$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \text{Quality Training} + \text{Design Review} + \text{Supplier Audit} \\
C_P &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 \\
C_P &= ₹\,3{,}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Appraisal Cost ($C_A$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= \text{Incoming Inspection} + \text{Final Testing} + \text{Calibration of Gauges} \\
C_A &= 1{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
C_A &= ₹\,4{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{IF} &= ₹\,3{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{EF} &= ₹\,4{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Total Quality Cost (COQ).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
COQ &= 3{,}30{,}000 + 4{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 \\
COQ &= ₹\,15{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Appraisal Cost Ratio (ACR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACR &= \frac{C_A}{COQ} \times 100 \\
ACR &= \frac{4{,}20{,}000}{15{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
ACR &= 27.10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

- $C_P$ = ₹ 3,30,000
- $C_A$ = ₹ 4,20,000
- $C_{IF}$ = ₹ 3,50,000
- $C_{EF}$ = ₹ 4,50,000
- COQ = ₹ 15,50,000
- ACR = **27.10 %**

*(Each correctly classified and computed component = 1 mark, totaling 7 marks.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice-2]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (P-A-F) Cost Model**. Discuss the **"1-10-100-1000 Rule"** of quality economics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process produces **20,000 units/month**. The appraisal cost per unit inspected is ₹ 15. The defect probability is 0.03. The internal failure cost (rework) per defective unit is ₹ 600.

Calculate:
1. Total Appraisal Cost if 100% inspection is done.
2. Total Internal Failure Cost (escaping defects).
3. Total Quality Cost (COQ).
4. The **optimal inspection fraction** $f^*$ that minimizes COQ.
5. The **minimum total cost** at the optimal fraction.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a):**

**P-A-F Cost Model (Graphical Description):**

The P-A-F model is a strategic cost-categorization framework introduced by **Armand V. Feigenbaum (1956)** and refined by **Joseph M. Juran**. It classifies all quality-related expenditures into four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) categories:

- **Prevention Costs (P)** — investments made *before* defects occur.
- **Appraisal Costs (A)** — investments made to *detect* defects that exist.
- **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** — losses from defects found *before* shipment.
- **External Failure Costs (EF)** — losses from defects found *after* shipment.

```
       QUALITY COST
           ▲
           │
   Cost    │   ╲                    ╱── External Failure
           │    ╲                  ╱
           │     ╲   Appraisal    ╱
           │      ╲─────────────╱
           │       ╲           ╱
           │   Internal╲      ╱ Prevention
           │   Failure  ╲    ╱  ─────────────►
           │             ╲  ╱
           │              ╲╱
           └─────────────────────────► Quality Investment
                          OPTIMUM
```

**The 1-10-100-1000 Rule (Juran's Quality Cost Multiplier):**

This empirical rule, often attributed to **Joseph M. Juran**, states that the cost of a quality defect **multiplies by 10** at each successive stage of its detection:

- **₹1 to prevent** a defect at the design stage.
- **₹10 to detect (appraise)** the same defect at the inspection stage.
- **₹100 to repair** the defect if it reaches production (internal failure).
- **₹1000 to recover** the defect if it reaches the customer (external failure: warranty, recall, lawsuit).

**Strategic Implication:** Organizations must aggressively shift expenditure from Appraisal and Failure categories into Prevention. Prevention is the *lowest-cost* and *highest-leverage* investment.

*(Graph ≈ 2 marks, explanation of P-A-F ≈ 3 marks, 1-10-100-1000 rule ≈ 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b):**

Given:
- $N = 20{,}000$ units/month
- $c_i = ₹15$ per unit inspected
- $p = 0.03$ (defect probability)
- $c_r = ₹600$ per defective unit (rework)

**Step 1: Total Appraisal Cost at 100% Inspection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= N \cdot c_i \\
C_A &= 20{,}000 \times 15 \\
C_A &= ₹\,3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Internal Failure Cost (assuming all defects are caught internally post-inspection, but rework cost applies to defective units).**

If we assume all defects are caught and reworked internally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{IF} &= N \cdot p \cdot c_r \\
C_{IF} &= 20{,}000 \times 0.03 \times 600 \\
C_{IF} &= ₹\,3{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Quality Cost (COQ) at 100% Inspection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ &= C_A + C_{IF} \\
COQ &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 \\
COQ &= ₹\,6{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Optimal Inspection Fraction $f^*$.**

Using the economic model where $f$ = fraction inspected:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total}(f) &= N \cdot f \cdot c_i + N \cdot (1-f) \cdot p \cdot c_r + N \cdot p \cdot c_r \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For optimal $f$, differentiate with respect to $f$ and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\,C_{total}}{df} &= N \cdot c_i - N \cdot p \cdot c_r = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields $f^* = 1$ (100%) **whenever** $c_i < p \cdot c_r$. Here:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \cdot c_r &= 0.03 \times 600 = ₹18 \\
c_i &= ₹15 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $c_i < p \cdot c_r$, the optimum is $f^* = 1$ (100% inspection). The minimum total cost equals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ_{min} &= N \cdot c_i + N \cdot p \cdot c_r \\
COQ_{min} &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}000 \\
COQ_{min} &= ₹\,6{,}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Economic Interpretation.**

Because inspecting a unit (₹15) is **cheaper** than reworking a defect (₹18), it is economically optimal to inspect 100% of units. If, however, $c_i > p \cdot c_r$ (e.g., $c_i = ₹25$), then partial inspection would be optimal, and $f^*$ would be less than 1.

**Final Answer Box:**

- $C_A$ = ₹ 3,00,000
- $C_{IF}$ = ₹ 3,60,000
- COQ = ₹ 6,60,000
- $f^*$ = **1.0 (100% inspection)**
- $COQ_{min}$ = ₹ 6,60,000

*(Each step correctly executed = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Appraisal Cost Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mis-classifying costs:** The most frequent error is putting "Supplier Audit" under Appraisal instead of Prevention. **Rule of Thumb:** If the activity *prevents* a defect, it is Prevention; if it *detects* an existing defect, it is Appraisal.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Calibration:** Calibration of gauges is **Appraisal**, not Prevention — because it ensures the *detection tool* works, not that the product is defect-free.
>
> 3. **Unit Confusion in COQ %:** When asked "Appraisal Cost Ratio," students often compute $C_A / \text{Sales}$ instead of $C_A / COQ$. Read the question stem carefully.
>
> 4. **Missing the Strategic Insight:** A 14-mark answer that only lists components without discussing the *Prevention-Appraisal Trade-off* and the *1-10-100-1000 rule* will lose at least 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Formula Misapplication:** In optimal inspection fraction problems, students forget to verify the condition $c_i < p \cdot c_r$ before concluding $f^* = 1$. Always cross-check.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Conclusion/Interpretation:** KTU examiners reward **analytical interpretation** of numerical answers. Always add 2–3 lines on what the numbers *mean strategically*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Appraisal Costs:**

- ✅ Appraisal Costs are **detection-oriented** expenditures incurred **before** shipment to the customer.
- ✅ The four pillars of COQ: **P**revention + **A**ppraisal + **I**nternal **F**ailure + **E**xternal **F**ailure.
- ✅ Six key sub-categories of Appraisal Costs: **(1)** Incoming inspection, **(2)** In-process inspection, **(3)** Final testing, **(4)** Calibration, **(5)** Quality audits, **(6)** Field/reliability testing.
- ✅ Appraisal Costs are **Conformance Costs** along with Prevention Costs.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize: $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$, $ACR = (C_A / COQ) \times 100$, $ACU = C_A / N_{inspected}$.
- ✅ The **1-10-100-1000 Rule:** Every ₹1 spent on Prevention saves ₹10 on Appraisal, ₹100 on Internal Failure, ₹1000 on External Failure.
- ✅ **Optimal Inspection Fraction** $f^* = 1$ when $c_i < p \cdot c_r$; otherwise, use economic sampling (AQL, MIL-STD-1916, etc.).
- ✅ **Juran's Optimum:** Total COQ is minimized when Prevention investment reaches its economically optimal level, reducing Appraisal and Failure costs.
- ✅ **Strategic Direction:** A mature QMS gradually *shifts budget* from Appraisal → Prevention, reducing total COQ.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmarks:** Appraisal Cost Ratio typically ranges **20–40% of COQ** in conventional firms and **10–25%** in mature QMS firms.
- ✅ **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** Always classify costs correctly, show formula, substitute values, present answer with units, and add a **2–3 line strategic interpretation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal and External Failure Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal and External Failure Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Cost of Quality (COQ)** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Quality Engineering and Management*, every cost incurred by an organisation that is **not absolutely essential** to producing a conforming product or service is classified as a quality cost. These costs are broadly grouped into four categories: **Prevention Costs, Appraisal Costs, Internal Failure Costs, and External Failure Costs**.

### Internal Failure Costs (IFC)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)
> **Internal Failure Costs** are the costs incurred to **rectify, rework, or dispose of defective products, materials, or services that are discovered *before* they are transferred to the customer**. The defect is detected while the product is still within the producer's boundary, so the entire financial burden of non-conformance falls on the manufacturer.

### External Failure Costs (EFC)

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)
> **External Failure Costs** are the costs incurred to **rectify, replace, or compensate for defective products or services that are discovered *after* they have been delivered to the customer**. Since the defect escapes the producer's boundary, the cost is magnified by warranty handling, logistics, reputational damage, and legal exposure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

### The "Leaking Tap in a House" Analogy 💧

Imagine you are building a house. During construction, your plumber **finds a leaking tap** (a defect). Two scenarios can occur:

| Scenario | Detection Point | Cost Category | Real-World Equivalent |
|----------|----------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Scenario A** | Plumber spots the leak *before* the wall is plastered. He simply replaces the washer for ₹50. | **Internal Failure Cost** | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection |
| **Scenario B** | The wall is plastered, painted, and the house is sold. Six months later, the **customer** calls to say the tap is leaking. You must now break the wall, repaint, pay for the plumber's revisit, offer compensation, and the customer leaves a bad review. | **External Failure Cost** | Warranty, Returns, Legal Suits, Loss of Goodwill |

> [!NOTE] Intuition in One Line
> **Internal failure** = "We caught our own mistake." (Cheap, embarrassing but contained.)
> **External failure** = "The customer caught our mistake." (Expensive, reputation-damaging, legally risky.)

### Why KTU 2024 Emphasises This Topic

The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework expects graduates to be able to **quantify, classify, and reduce the Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** in industrial settings. Since **Internal and External Failure Costs together constitute the COPQ**, they are the *primary targets* of every Six Sigma, TQM, and ISO 9001 implementation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Terms (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Cost of Quality (COQ)** — Total economic burden of quality-related activities.
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — Sum of Internal Failure Cost (IFC) + External Failure Cost (EFC).
- **Cost of Good Quality (COQG)** — Sum of Prevention Cost (PC) + Appraisal Cost (AC).
- **Conformance Cost** — Another term for COQG (cost to *meet* requirements).
- **Non-conformance cost** — Another term for COPQ (cost of *failing* to meet requirements).
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — % of units produced correctly without rework (metric to monitor internal failure).
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — Six Sigma metric directly tied to external failures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Internal Failure Costs

Internal failure costs arise whenever a non-conformance is identified at **any stage before shipment**. They are a direct signal of process inefficiency. The major constituents are:

### A. Scrap Costs
- The **net loss** incurred when raw material or semi-finished goods must be discarded.
- Includes the **sum of** material cost + labour cost + manufacturing overhead **already absorbed** by the rejected unit.
- KTU examiners often test the distinction between **Scrap** (disposed, no salvage value) and **Spoilage** (disposed, has some salvage value).

### B. Rework Costs
- Costs to **correct defective units** so they meet specifications.
- Includes extra labour, extra material, and the **opportunity cost** of the production line being occupied by a re-run.
- Example: Re-soldering a defective PCB trace, re-painting a car panel.

### C. Re-inspection and Re-testing Costs
- After rework, the unit must be inspected again. This consumes inspector time, lab resources, and energy.

### D. Material Procurement / Material Review Costs
- Costs of segregating defective lots, returning material to suppliers (if supplier-caused), and MRB (Material Review Board) deliberations.

### E. Design Failure Costs (Internal)
- Engineering changes to drawings, BOMs, or process sheets triggered by a defect discovered internally.

### F. Failure Analysis / Corrective Action Costs
- Cost of root-cause investigations (e.g., Ishikawa, 5-Why, Pareto), including engineering hours and test lab time.

### G. Internal Downtime Costs
- Lost production hours when a defect causes a machine stoppage or a line halt.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of External Failure Costs

External failure costs occur **after the customer has taken possession**. They are the **most damaging** category of quality cost because they damage both **finances and brand equity**.

### A. Warranty Claim Costs
- Free replacement of parts, free service visits, freight charges for sending replacements.
- KTU tip: Warranty cost is usually modelled as a **percentage of sales** (industry benchmark: 1.5%–3% of revenue for automotive).

### B. Returned Goods / Reverse Logistics
- Costs of receiving, inspecting, restocking, or scrapping customer returns.
- Includes shipping, handling, and administrative overhead.

### C. Field Service and Customer Support
- Cost of deploying service engineers to customer sites, including travel, lodging, and per-diem.

### D. Complaint Handling Costs
- Customer care centre staffing, call centre infrastructure, customer relations management.

### E. Product Recall Costs
- **The most catastrophic external failure cost**. Includes logistics of retrieving the product, public communication, regulatory fines, and replacement.
- Famous example: Takata airbag recall (~₹1.5 lakh crore global cost).

### F. Legal Liabilities and Penalty Costs
- Lawsuits, settlements, court fees, product liability insurance premiums, statutory penalties.

### G. Loss of Goodwill and Consequential Costs
- **The most insidious** external failure cost. Includes lost future sales, reduced brand trust, market share erosion, and the cost of marketing campaigns to repair reputation.
- Difficult to measure but **multiplies** the visible cost by a factor of 3–5.

### H. Concessions / Discounts
- Price reductions, free upgrades, or extended warranties offered to retain an unhappy customer.

---

## 2.3 The Cost-of-Quality (COQ) Mathematical Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats quality costs as a structured mathematical system. The governing equations are:

### Governing COQ Identity

$$
COQ \;=\; COQG \;+\; COPQ \;=\; (PC + AC) \;+\; (IFC + EFC)
$$

Where:
- $PC$ = Prevention Cost
- $AC$ = Appraisal Cost
- $IFC$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $EFC$ = External Failure Cost

### Total Quality Cost (TQC) per Unit

$$
TQC \;=\; \frac{PC + AC + IFC + EFC}{N}
$$

Where $N$ is the **number of units produced** in the accounting period.

### Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Ratio

$$
COPQ\ Ratio \;=\; \frac{IFC + EFC}{Total\ Sales\ Revenue} \times 100\ \%
$$

### External-to-Internal Failure Ratio (KTU Hot Metric)

$$
E/I\ Ratio \;=\; \frac{EFC}{IFC}
$$

> [!TIP] Examiner's Insight
> A **high E/I Ratio (> 1.0)** indicates that defects are **escaping** to customers — a sign that the **Appraisal and Prevention** budgets are too low. A **low E/I Ratio (< 0.5)** suggests good internal detection systems.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Engineering Interpretation | Typical Unit |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ | Aggregate quality expenditure | ₹ (or $) |
| 2 | Cost of Good Quality | $COQG = PC + AC$ | Money spent to *prevent* defects | ₹ |
| 3 | Cost of Poor Quality | $COPQ = IFC + EFC$ | Money wasted due to defects | ₹ |
| 4 | Total Quality Cost per Unit | $TQC = \dfrac{PC + AC + IFC + EFC}{N}$ | Per-unit quality burden | ₹/unit |
| 5 | COPQ as % of Sales | $COPQ\% = \dfrac{IFC + EFC}{Sales} \times 100$ | Quality cost intensity | % |
| 6 | External-Internal Failure Ratio | $E/I = \dfrac{EFC}{IFC}$ | Detection-system efficiency index | Dimensionless |
| 7 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good units at first attempt}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | Process robustness | % |
| 8 | Rework Cost Multiplier | $Rework\ Multiplier = 10\times$ | Cost of catching defect at next stage | Factor |
| 9 | 1-10-100 Rule (Cost Multiplier) | $1_{\text{raw}} \to 10_{\text{in-process}} \to 100_{\text{field}}$ | Cost escalation as defect escapes | Multiplier |
| 10 | Optimal Quality Cost Condition | $\dfrac{d(COQ)}{d(Q)} = 0$ | Minimisation of total cost | Calculus condition |

> [!NOTE] KTU Board Convention
> All monetary answers must be expressed in **₹ (Indian Rupees) with two decimal places**, and percentage answers in **two significant figures** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive Industry (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors):** Failure costs dominate warranty budgets. Reducing external failures is the core objective of **APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning)**.
2. **Software Industry:** A bug caught at the *requirements* stage costs ~\$100; the same bug caught in production may cost ~\$10,000 — a direct application of the **1-10-100 Rule**.
3. **Pharmaceutical Industry:** External failure (a contaminated drug reaching a patient) leads to **regulatory shutdowns, product recalls, and even criminal prosecution** — making EFC potentially *unbounded*.
4. **Manufacturing SMEs in Kerala:** Internal failure costs (rework, scrap) are often 15–20% of total production cost, which is a primary target for **lean manufacturing interventions** under the KTU industrial consultancy framework.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Optimal Quality Cost Model (KTU Analytical Favourite)

A classic KTU 2024 Module 3 problem is to prove that **minimum total quality cost occurs at the point where the marginal reduction in failure cost equals the marginal increase in prevention cost**. This is a calculus-based derivation.

### Setup

Let the **level of quality** $Q$ be defined as the **proportion of conforming units** (a dimensionless number between 0 and 1). As $Q$ increases (more prevention/appraisal effort is invested), failure costs fall, but prevention/appraisal costs rise.

### Step 1: Express Each Cost Category as a Function of $Q$

**Prevention Cost (PC):** Increases with $Q$. For analytical tractability, assume a convex increasing function:

$$
PC(Q) \;=\; a \cdot Q^{2} \quad (a > 0)
$$

**Appraisal Cost (AC):** Approximately constant in the mid-range of $Q$ (examiners often treat it as a constant $C_{A}$):

$$
AC(Q) \;=\; C_{A} \quad (\text{constant})
$$

**Failure Cost (FC = IFC + EFC):** Decreases exponentially as $Q$ increases. A standard model is:

$$
FC(Q) \;=\; b \cdot e^{-k \cdot Q} \quad (b > 0,\ k > 0)
$$

> Here, $b$ is the maximum failure cost when $Q = 0$, and $k$ is the **failure-decay constant** representing the effectiveness of the quality system.

### Step 2: Total Cost Function

$$
TC(Q) \;=\; PC(Q) + AC(Q) + FC(Q)
$$

$$
TC(Q) \;=\; a \cdot Q^{2} + C_{A} + b \cdot e^{-k \cdot Q}
$$

### Step 3: First-Order Optimality Condition

To minimise $TC(Q)$, differentiate with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d\,TC(Q)}{d\,Q} \;=\; 2aQ \;-\; b k \cdot e^{-kQ} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
2aQ^{\star} \;=\; b k \cdot e^{-kQ^{\star}}
$$

### Step 4: Second-Order Sufficiency Check

$$
\frac{d^{2}\,TC(Q)}{d\,Q^{2}} \;=\; 2a \;+\; b k^{2} \cdot e^{-kQ} \;>\; 0
$$

Since $a, b, k > 0$, the second derivative is **always positive**, confirming a **global minimum** at $Q^{\star}$.

### Step 5: Closed-Form Solution (Lambert-W Function Form)

The first-order condition can be written in the Lambert-W form:

$$
Q^{\star} \cdot e^{kQ^{\star}} \;=\; \frac{bk}{2a}
$$

$$
Q^{\star} \;=\; \frac{1}{k} \cdot W\!\left( \frac{bk}{2a} \right)
$$

Where $W(\cdot)$ is the **Lambert-W function** (also called ProductLog). For KTU 2024 numerical questions, examiners normally give parameters such that $Q^{\star}$ can be solved by simple trial or by using natural log tables.

> [!NOTE] Examiner's Insight
> For Module 3 questions, KTU almost never asks for a Lambert-W solution. They will instead specify a 2-period cost table and ask you to plot or compute the **optimal point by inspection / by differential calculation**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: The "Kerala Electronics Pvt. Ltd." Case

### Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

Kerala Electronics Pvt. Ltd. produces 50,000 LED bulbs per month. The following quality cost data is available for one month:

| Cost Category | Amount (₹) |
|---------------|------------|
| Quality training of operators | 80,000 |
| Inspection of incoming materials | 60,000 |
| Scrap from rejected bulbs | 1,20,000 |
| Rework to bring bulbs to spec | 70,000 |
| Warranty claims honoured | 2,00,000 |
| Customer return handling | 50,000 |
| Process capability studies (DOE) | 40,000 |
| Supplier audit and rating | 30,000 |

**Required:**
1. Classify each cost as Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, or External Failure.
2. Compute COQ, COQG, COPQ.
3. Compute COPQ as a percentage of sales (assume selling price ₹120/bulb).
4. Compute the E/I Ratio and interpret it.
5. Recommend one action to reduce COPQ.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Classification**

| Item | Cost (₹) | Category |
|------|---------:|----------|
| Quality training | 80,000 | **Prevention (PC)** |
| Incoming inspection | 60,000 | **Appraisal (AC)** |
| Scrap | 1,20,000 | **Internal Failure (IFC)** |
| Rework | 70,000 | **Internal Failure (IFC)** |
| Warranty claims | 2,00,000 | **External Failure (EFC)** |
| Return handling | 50,000 | **External Failure (EFC)** |
| Process capability studies | 40,000 | **Prevention (PC)** |
| Supplier audit | 30,000 | **Prevention (PC)** |

Aggregated:

$$
PC = 80{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
AC = 60{,}000
$$

$$
IFC = 1{,}20{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 1{,}90{,}000
$$

$$
EFC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Totals**

$$
COQG = PC + AC = 1{,}50{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 2{,}10{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
COPQ = IFC + EFC = 1{,}90{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
COQ = COQG + COPQ = 2{,}10{,}000 + 4{,}40{,}000 = 6{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — COPQ % of Sales**

$$
Sales = 50{,}000 \times 120 = 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
COPQ\% = \frac{4{,}40{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 7.33\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — E/I Ratio**

$$
E/I = \frac{EFC}{IFC} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{1{,}90{,}000} = 1.32
$$

**Interpretation:** $E/I > 1$ means defects are leaking to customers faster than they are being caught internally. The company's appraisal and prevention systems are **inadequate**.

**Step 5 — Recommendation (Engineering Judgement)**

> [!TIP] KTU-Standard Recommendation Format
> "Since $E/I = 1.32 > 1$ and COPQ is 7.33% of sales (industry benchmark: 2–4%), Kerala Electronics should **increase its Prevention budget by ~₹50,000** to fund **Process Capability Studies and Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) fixtures**. Based on the **1-10-100 Rule**, this investment is expected to reduce both IFC and EFC by 40–50% in the following quarter, lowering total COQ."

---

## 3.3 The 1-10-100 Rule — Derivation of the Cost Multiplier

A staple KTU Module 3 question. The rule states that the cost of fixing a defect **multiplies by 10** at each successive stage of the value chain.

### Stages of Detection
1. **Raw material receipt** → cost to fix = **1 unit** (e.g., ₹100)
2. **In-process** → cost to fix = **10 units** (rework, ₹1,000)
3. **Final inspection** → cost to fix = **100 units** (re-inspection + sorting, ₹10,000)
4. **Customer use** → cost to fix = **1,000 units** (warranty + goodwill, ₹1,00,000)

### Justification (Logic Behind the Rule)

- **Stage 1 (Raw):** Material is replaced; labour cost is negligible.
- **Stage 2 (In-process):** Labour has already been added, and the unit has consumed machine time. Rework also blocks the production line.
- **Stage 3 (Final):** Re-inspection requires additional inspectors; if a defective unit passes, the entire lot must be recalled.
- **Stage 4 (Customer):** Includes warranty, transportation, legal exposure, brand damage, and customer compensation.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Numerical Format
> When asked "What is the cost of a defect that escaped to the customer, given that fixing at the raw stage costs ₹50?", the answer is:
> $50 \times 10^{3} = ₹50{,}000$ (i.e., 1 → 10 → 100 → 1000 multiplier).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The PAF Model and the Four Cost Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Quality Cost System - COQ"] --> B["Conformance Costs - COQG"]
    A --> C["Non Conformance Costs - COPQ"]

    B --> B1["Prevention Costs - PC"]
    B --> B2["Appraisal Costs - AC"]

    B1 --> B1a["Quality Training"]
    B1 --> B1b["Process Capability Studies"]
    B1 --> B1c["Supplier Audits"]
    B1 --> B1d["Design Reviews"]
    B1 --> B1e["Poka Yoke Implementation"]

    B2 --> B2a["Incoming Inspection"]
    B2 --> B2b["In Process Inspection"]
    B2 --> B2c["Final Inspection"]
    B2 --> B2d["Test Equipment Calibration"]

    C --> C1["Internal Failure Costs - IFC"]
    C --> C2["External Failure Costs - EFC"]

    C1 --> C1a["Scrap"]
    C1 --> C1b["Rework"]
    C1 --> C1c["Re Inspection"]
    C1 --> C1d["Failure Analysis"]
    C1 --> C1e["Material Review Board"]
    C1 --> C1f["Internal Downtime"]

    C2 --> C2a["Warranty Claims"]
    C2 --> C2b["Returned Goods"]
    C2 --> C2c["Field Service"]
    C2 --> C2d["Complaint Handling"]
    C2 --> C2e["Product Recall"]
    C2 --> C2f["Legal Liabilities"]
    C2 --> C2g["Loss of Goodwill"]
    C2 --> C2h["Customer Concessions"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The 1-10-100 Cost Multiplier Across the Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1 - Raw Material Receipt"] -->|"Cost 1x"| S2["Stage 2 - In Process Manufacturing"]
    S2 -->|"Cost 10x"| S3["Stage 3 - Final Inspection Before Dispatch"]
    S3 -->|"Cost 100x"| S4["Stage 4 - Customer Use Field Failure"]
    S4 -->|"Cost 1000x"| S5["Stage 5 - Legal Action and Recall"]

    S1 -.- S1N["Fix cost about Rs 100"]
    S2 -.- S2N["Fix cost about Rs 1000"]
    S3 -.- S3N["Fix cost about Rs 10000"]
    S4 -.- S4N["Fix cost about Rs 100000"]
    S5 -.- S5N["Fix cost about Rs 1000000 plus brand damage"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Behaviour of COQ Curves vs. Quality Level

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Axis [Axes Definition]
        direction LR
        X["X Axis - Quality Level Q - 0 to 1"]
        Y["Y Axis - Cost in Rupees"]
    end

    PCurve["Prevention Cost - Rises with Q - Convex upward"]
    FCurve["Failure Cost - Falls with Q - Exponential decay"]
    ACurve["Appraisal Cost - Roughly constant to mildly increasing"]
    TC["Total Cost TC - Sum of all three - U Shaped minimum"]

    PCurve --> TC
    FCurve --> TC
    ACurve --> TC

    OptPoint["Q Star - Optimal Quality Level - Minimum TC"]
    TC -.-> OptPoint
```

> [!NOTE] KTU Visual Cue
> In Module 3 viva voce, examiners expect you to **label the axes (Quality Level on X, Cost on Y)**, draw the **U-shaped Total Cost curve**, and **mark the optimal point** $Q^{\star}$ where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal saving in failure cost.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: COPQ Reduction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN["Input - Quality Cost Data - Quarterly COQ Report"] --> P1["Stage 1 - Categorise into PC AC IFC EFC"]
    P1 --> P2["Stage 2 - Compute E over I ratio"]
    P2 --> P3{"Stage 3 - Decision Branch - Is E over I greater than 1"}

    P3 -- "Yes" --> P4a["Action - Strengthen Appraisal - Add in process checks"]
    P3 -- "No" --> P3b{"Is IFC dominant over EFC"}

    P3b -- "Yes" --> P4b["Action - Increase Prevention - Training Poka Yoke"]
    P3b -- "No" --> P4c["Action - Reduce EFC - Service network overhaul"]

    P4a --> OUT["Output - Revised COQ Budget Allocation"]
    P4b --> OUT
    P4c --> OUT
```

> This block diagram maps the **decision logic** a Quality Manager would use to convert raw COQ data into an **actionable budget reallocation plan** — directly aligned with KTU 2024 Module 3 CO3 (Analyse quality cost structures).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> Distinguish between **Internal Failure Costs** and **External Failure Costs** with **two examples each**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution):**

| Aspect | Internal Failure Cost (1 mark) | External Failure Cost (1 mark) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Point of Detection** | Defect detected *before* the product reaches the customer | Defect detected *after* delivery to the customer |
| **Cost Bearer** | Entirely the producer | Producer + customer bears inconvenience |
| **Examples (0.5 × 2 = 1 mark)** | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection | Warranty claims, Product recalls, Legal suits |
| **Magnitude** | Comparatively lower | Magnified by 10–1000× (1-10-100 Rule) |

**Examples (each example carries 0.5 mark):**
- *Internal:* Scrap of defective castings; rework of misaligned gear teeth.
- *External:* Free engine replacement under warranty; compensation paid for an electrical short-circuit lawsuit.

> Closing sentence (mandatory for full marks): *"Hence, internal failure costs are contained within the organisation, whereas external failure costs extend beyond the factory boundary and damage both finances and goodwill."*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> What is the **E/I Ratio** in the Cost of Quality framework? What does an **E/I Ratio > 1** indicate about a firm's quality system?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The External-to-Internal Failure Cost Ratio is given by:
$$
E/I \text{ Ratio} \;=\; \frac{EFC}{IFC}
$$

**Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
An $E/I > 1$ indicates that **more failure cost is being absorbed by external (post-delivery) failure than by internal (pre-delivery) failure**. In other words, defects are escaping the firm's appraisal net and reaching customers. The corrective action is to **increase Prevention and Appraisal investment** (e.g., stronger in-process inspection, Design FMEA, supplier quality audits) to push the E/I Ratio below 0.5.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern Notice]**
> As per the revised KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulation, Module-level Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**; in the exam, the student answers **either** A **or** B.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Define Cost of Quality. Classify the following **eight** items into Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure Costs, giving **one-line justification** for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> 1. Cost of calibration of CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine)
> 2. Cost of free replacement of a defective compressor under warranty
> 3. Cost of disposing off rejected aluminium castings
> 4. Cost of training shop-floor operators on Statistical Process Control
> 5. Cost of legal settlement for a fire caused by a faulty heater
> 6. Cost of reworking a defective weld joint
> 7. Cost of customer-care toll-free number
> 8. Cost of Design of Experiments to optimise curing time
>
> **(b)** A firm produces 20,000 units per year. The quality cost data is:
>
> | Category | Annual Cost (₹) |
> |----------|----------------:|
> | Prevention | 2,00,000 |
> | Appraisal | 1,50,000 |
> | Internal Failure | 3,50,000 |
> | External Failure | 4,00,000 |
> | **Selling Price / unit** | **₹500** |
>
> Compute:
> 1. Total COQ
> 2. COQG and COPQ
> 3. COPQ as a percentage of sales
> 4. E/I Ratio and its interpretation
> 5. Per-unit quality cost (TQC / N)
>
> Suggest **two engineering actions** to reduce COPQ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Classification (7 marks = 8 items × ~0.85 marks each, with brief justification)

| # | Item | Cost Category (0.5 mark) | One-line Justification (0.35 mark) |
|---|------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | CMM calibration | **Appraisal (AC)** | Ensures the inspection instrument remains accurate; supports measurement of quality. |
| 2 | Free compressor replacement under warranty | **External Failure (EFC)** | Product already delivered; producer bears post-sale replacement cost. |
| 3 | Disposing rejected aluminium castings | **Internal Failure (IFC)** | Defect found at the in-house inspection stage before dispatch. |
| 4 | SPC training for shop-floor operators | **Prevention (PC)** | Training prevents defects from occurring in the first place. |
