| 5 | Legal settlement for fire | **External Failure (EFC)** | Customer-incurred damage leading to litigation, settled post-sale. |
| 6 | Rework of defective weld | **Internal Failure (IFC)** | Defective unit is corrected *before* it leaves the plant. |
| 7 | Customer-care toll-free number | **External Failure (EFC)** | Operational cost of handling customer complaints after sale. |
| 8 | Design of Experiments for curing time | **Prevention (PC)** | DOE optimises the process to *prevent* defects at the design stage. |

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks = 1 mark per sub-question + 2 marks for engineering actions)

**Step 1 — Total COQ:**

$$
COQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC
$$

$$
COQ = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — COQG and COPQ:**

$$
COQG = PC + AC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
COPQ = IFC + EFC = 3{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — COPQ % of Sales:**

$$
Sales = 20{,}000 \times 500 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
COPQ\% = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 7.50\ \%
$$

**Step 4 — E/I Ratio and Interpretation:**

$$
E/I = \frac{EFC}{IFC} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 1.143
$$

> *Since $E/I > 1$, defects are escaping the firm's internal detection systems and reaching customers. The current Prevention and Appraisal budget is inadequate.*

**Step 5 — Per-Unit Quality Cost:**

$$
TQC_{\text{per unit}} = \frac{COQ}{N} = \frac{11{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹55 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Engineering Actions to Reduce COPQ (2 marks):**

1. **Increase Prevention Spend (₹50,000):** Invest in **Design of Experiments and Poka-Yoke fixtures** to eliminate the root cause of weld defects and casting rejections. This will directly cut IFC.
2. **Strengthen Appraisal (₹30,000):** Add **end-of-line automated vision inspection** to catch compressor defects *before* dispatch, reducing EFC (warranty replacements). This will lower the E/I Ratio below 0.5 over two quarters.

> **Closing line (for full 14 marks):** *"By reallocating ₹80,000 from the COPQ pool toward Prevention + Appraisal, the firm is expected to reduce the E/I Ratio to ~0.6, lower COPQ% to ~4%, and save an estimated ₹3,00,000 annually — a 3.75× return on the prevention investment."*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

> **(a)** Explain the **1-10-100 Rule** in the context of Cost of Quality. A defect caught at the **raw material stage** costs ₹75 to fix. Estimate the cost of the same defect if it is detected at **(i) in-process stage, (ii) final inspection stage, (iii) customer-use stage**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **optimal quality cost model** showing the relationship between Prevention Cost, Appraisal Cost, Failure Cost, and Total Cost. Mark and justify the **optimal quality level** $Q^{\star}$. A company finds that for every **5% increase in prevention investment**, failure cost reduces by ₹1,20,000 but prevention cost itself rises by ₹90,000. Determine the **economically optimal point** of prevention investment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — The 1-10-100 Rule (7 marks)

**Explanation (3 marks):**

The 1-10-100 Rule, also called the **Rule of Ten** or **Cost-of-Error Multiplier**, states that the cost of correcting a defect **multiplies by a factor of 10** as the defect progresses to the next stage of the value chain. The cost escalation arises from the **cumulative addition of labour, materials, inspection effort, logistics, and reputational damage**.

| Stage | Multiplier | Nature of Cost |
|-------|-----------:|----------------|
| 1. Raw material receipt | **1×** | Material replacement only |
| 2. In-process manufacturing | **10×** | Labour + machine time + rework |
| 3. Final inspection / pre-dispatch | **100×** | Re-inspection + line hold + sortation |
| 4. Customer use (field failure) | **1000×** | Warranty + transport + goodwill + legal |

**Numerical Estimation (4 marks = 1 × 3 + 1 for conclusion):**

Given: Cost at Stage 1 = ₹75.

**(i) In-process (Stage 2):**

$$
C_{2} = 75 \times 10^{1} = ₹750
$$

**(ii) Final inspection (Stage 3):**

$$
C_{3} = 75 \times 10^{2} = ₹7{,}500
$$

**(iii) Customer-use (Stage 4):**

$$
C_{4} = 75 \times 10^{3} = ₹75{,}000
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *A defect that costs only ₹75 to fix at the raw stage becomes ₹75,000 (a thousand-fold increase) if it reaches the customer. This empirically justifies investment in upstream Prevention activities.*

### Part (b) — Optimal Quality Cost Model (7 marks)

**Sketch / Description (3 marks):**

A graph with:
- **X-axis:** Quality Level $Q$ (from 0 to 1, i.e., 0% to 100% conformance).
- **Y-axis:** Cost in ₹.
- **Prevention Cost (PC):** Convex upward-rising curve.
- **Appraisal Cost (AC):** Approximately horizontal (mildly rising).
- **Failure Cost (FC):** Exponentially decaying curve.
- **Total Cost (TC):** U-shaped curve, sum of all three.
- **$Q^{\star}$:** The point at which the **TC curve reaches its minimum**, located where the **tangent to TC is horizontal**, i.e., $\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0$.

**Economic Justification of $Q^{\star}$ (2 marks):**

- To the **left** of $Q^{\star}$ → failure cost dominates → increasing $Q$ reduces total cost.
- To the **right** of $Q^{\star}$ → prevention cost dominates → further increase in $Q$ inflates total cost.
- At $Q^{\star}$ → **marginal cost of prevention = marginal saving in failure cost**.

**Numerical Determination of Optimal Prevention Investment (2 marks):**

Net benefit per 5% step = reduction in failure cost − increase in prevention cost:

$$
\Delta \text{Net Benefit} = 1{,}20{,}000 - 90{,}000 = ₹30{,}000\ \text{per 5\% step}
$$

Since the **net benefit is positive (₹30,000)**, the firm should **continue increasing prevention investment** in 5% steps **as long as the marginal benefit remains positive**. The optimal point is reached when:

$$
\Delta FC \leq \Delta PC \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Marginal Benefit} \to 0
$$

> **Conclusion (for full marks):** *Under the given data, prevention investment should be increased incrementally until the next 5% step would deliver a net benefit ≤ ₹0. At that step, the firm has reached $Q^{\star}$, and the E/I Ratio will have dropped below 0.5, indicating a healthy, customer-centric quality system.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Before Writing the Exam)
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Appraisal" with "Internal Failure".** A finished-goods inspection that *catches* a defect is **Appraisal**; the **rework** of that defect is **Internal Failure**. Many students wrongly attribute the entire inspection+rework cost to IFC.
> 2. **Do NOT classify "warranty administration cost" as a Prevention cost.** It is strictly **External Failure Cost**.
> 3. **Do NOT express E/I Ratio as a percentage.** It is a **dimensionless ratio** (e.g., 1.32, not 132%).
> 4. **Always state units** in numerical answers (₹, ₹/unit, %).
> 5. **In 14-mark answers, always close with an "engineering recommendation" line** — the KTU 2024 OBE rubric awards up to **2 marks** for actionable judgement beyond pure calculation.
> 6. **The 1-10-100 Rule is 1 → 10 → 100 → 1000**, not 1 → 2 → 4 → 8. Writing a wrong multiplier series costs the entire 4-mark sub-part.
> 7. **Scrap vs. Spoilage distinction:** Scrap has **zero salvage value**; Spoilage has **some salvage value**. Examiners award 0.5 marks for this distinction in classification questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] High-Density Revision Sheet — Pin This Before Every KTU ESE Attempt

- ⭐ **COQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC** — the master identity.
- ⭐ **COQG (Conformance) = PC + AC** | **COPQ (Non-conformance) = IFC + EFC**.
- ⭐ **IFC** = Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection, Failure Analysis, MRB, Internal Downtime.
- ⭐ **EFC** = Warranty, Returns, Field Service, Complaints, Recall, Legal, Goodwill Loss, Concessions.
- ⭐ **E/I Ratio = EFC / IFC**. **Target: E/I < 0.5** for a healthy system.
- ⭐ **1-10-100 Rule:** 1× → 10× → 100× → 1000× as defect moves from raw → in-process → final → customer.
- ⭐ **Optimal Quality Level $Q^{\star}$** is at the **minimum of the U-shaped Total Cost curve**, where marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost saving.
- ⭐ **COPQ % of Sales** is the headline metric reported to top management; industry benchmark is **2–4%**; > 6% is a red flag.
- ⭐ **Loss of Goodwill** is the **most insidious** external failure cost — difficult to quantify but multiplies visible cost by 3–5×.
- ⭐ **Increase Prevention + Appraisal to reduce IFC + EFC** — this is the **strategic insight** behind every TQM, Six Sigma, and ISO 9001 cost-reduction programme.
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 Skill Outcome:** Be able to **classify, compute, and recommend** — the question will always have these three legs. A pure calculation answer without recommendation loses ~2 marks.
- ⭐ **In every numerical answer**, carry units in **every intermediate step** and round to **two decimal places** for ₹ and **two significant figures** for %.
- ⭐ **Remember the master trade-off:** Prevention is paid **once**; Failure is paid **many times** — hence the *Pareto shift* of budget from COPQ to COQG is the single most important strategic move in quality management.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Cost of Quality (COQ)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that its products and services conform to the specified quality standards. It is a comprehensive measure of all costs associated with the **prevention, detection, and rectification (or consequences) of poor quality**, encompassing both the price of conformance and the price of non-conformance.

In simple terms, COQ answers one of the most important questions in industrial engineering:
> *"How much money does our organization spend — directly or indirectly — because of quality?"*

The Cost of Quality is **NOT** the cost of producing a high-quality product. Rather, it is the sum of all the costs that **would disappear** if the product or service were perfect from the very first attempt.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight — KTU Board Favourite:** Quality is "free" in the sense that any money spent on doing the job right the first time is always cheaper than the money spent repairing failures later. Quality cost accounting is the financial mirror of an organization's quality maturity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Service Station"

Imagine you own a car. Two extreme strategies exist for keeping it running:

**Strategy A — "Cheap Now, Expensive Later":**
You ignore the oil change, skip tire rotation, and avoid servicing the brakes. You save ₹5,000 per year in "prevention costs." But the engine seizes after two years, costing ₹2,00,000 in replacement — a massive **failure cost**.

**Strategy B — "Invest in Prevention":**
You follow a strict service schedule, spending ₹15,000 per year on maintenance. The car runs smoothly for 15 years with negligible breakdowns.

**The Lesson:**
- Money spent on **prevention and appraisal** is an investment.
- Money spent on **failures** (internal + external) is a loss.
- The optimal total cost lies in balancing these two — this is exactly what the **COQ models** try to quantify.

In manufacturing, the "car" is your production line, the "oil change" is your quality planning, and the "engine seizure" is customer returns, warranty claims, and reputational damage.

---

## 1.3 Standard COQ Metrics in Industry

| Metric | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Cost of Conformance | **CoC / PONC** | ₹ / ₹-per-unit / % of Sales |
| Cost of Non-Conformance | **CoNC / POC** | ₹ / ₹-per-unit / % of Sales |
| Total Cost of Quality | **TCQ** | ₹ / % of Sales |
| Quality Loss Function Coefficient | **k** | ₹ / unit² |
| Optimal Quality Cost | **TCQ*** | ₹ |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently quote COQ as a **percentage of sales turnover** (typically **2.5% to 15%** in service industries and **5% to 25%** in manufacturing — a strong indicative benchmark for board answers).

---

## 1.4 Historical Context of COQ Models

| Year | Contributor | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| 1956 | **A.V. Feigenbaum** | Introduced the **PAF Model** (Prevention, Appraisal, Failure) in *Total Quality Control* |
| 1951 | **Joseph M. Juran** | Developed the **"Gold Mine"** concept of internal/external failure costs |
| 1979 | **Philip B. Crosby** | Coined **Price of Conformance (POC)** vs **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** |
| 1985 | **Genichi Taguchi** | Introduced the **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** linking deviation to societal loss |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure Model)

The PAF model, pioneered by **A.V. Feigenbaum** and refined by **Juran**, classifies all quality costs into **four** hierarchical categories:

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (PC)
Costs incurred to **prevent defects from occurring** in the first place.
- Quality planning and administration
- Training of workforce in quality
- Process capability studies
- Supplier capability audits
- Design review meetings
- Quality improvement projects

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (AC)
Costs incurred to **detect defects** that have already occurred.
- Incoming inspection of raw materials
- In-process quality checks
- Final product testing
- Calibration of measuring equipment
- Maintenance of test laboratories

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IFC)
Costs incurred when defective products are detected **before** reaching the customer.
- Scrap and re-work
- Re-inspection and re-testing
- Failure mode analysis
- Downtime due to defects
- Material wastage during production

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EFC)
Costs incurred when defective products are detected **after** reaching the customer.
- Warranty claims and settlements
- Returned goods handling
- Field service repairs
- Product recall expenses
- Loss of goodwill and brand damage

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Structure of PAF:**
> $$TCQ_{PAF} = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$$
> $$TCQ_{PAF} = \underbrace{(PC + AC)}_{\text{Cost of Conformance}} + \underbrace{(IFC + EFC)}_{\text{Cost of Non-Conformance}}$$

### 2.1.5 Behavioral Insight of the PAF Model
As an organization increases its investment in **Prevention (PC)**, the failure costs (IFC + EFC) fall **disproportionately faster**. This produces a U-shaped total cost curve with a clearly identifiable optimum.

---

## 2.2 Crosby's Price Model (POC vs PONC)

**Philip Crosby**, in his landmark book *"Quality is Free" (1979)*, restructured the cost hierarchy into a **binary framework** that is philosophically sharper than the PAF model:

| Crosby's Term | Equivalent PAF Component | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Price of Conformance (POC)** | Prevention + Appraisal | Cost of doing things right |
| **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** | Internal + External Failure | Cost of doing things wrong |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Core Argument:** In most organizations, the **PONC is 3 to 4 times the POC**. Hence, investing additional rupees in conformance actually *reduces* the total quality cost, because each additional rupee of prevention saves multiple rupees in failures. This is the foundation of the *"Zero Defects"* philosophy.

$$TCQ_{Crosby} = POC + PONC$$

---

## 2.3 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)

**Genichi Taguchi** revolutionized COQ thinking by arguing that quality loss is **not binary** (good/bad) but **continuous** — even a product within specification limits produces a loss to society.

### 2.3.1 The Core Premise
Any deviation of a quality characteristic (say, a dimension) from its **target value (m)** produces a loss to:
1. The manufacturer (rework, warranty, scrap)
2. The customer (reduced functionality, higher operating cost)
3. The society (environmental impact, disposal cost)

### 2.3.2 The Quadratic Loss Function
For a quality characteristic **y** with target **m** and tolerance **Δ**:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$

At the specification limit (y = m ± Δ), the loss equals **A** (the cost of replacing a defective product):

$$A = k \cdot \Delta^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often write $k = A / \Delta$. The correct denominator is $\Delta^2$ because the loss is **quadratic** in deviation. Board examiners deduct full marks for this single error.

### 2.3.3 Average Loss Across a Production Batch
If **n** units are produced with measured deviations $d_i = (y_i - m)$:

$$\bar{L} = k \cdot \left[ \sigma^2 + (\bar{y} - m)^2 \right]$$

where $\sigma^2$ is the process variance and $\bar{y}$ is the process mean.

### 2.3.4 Cost-Benefit Optimization Using Taguchi's QLF
The **optimal process setting** (target value) is found by minimizing total expected loss, which equals quality cost per unit:

$$TCQ_{Taguchi}(m) = C_{manufacture}(m) + k \cdot \sigma^2 + k \cdot (m - m_{ideal})^2$$

The first term is the manufacturing cost at target m, the second is the variance loss, and the third is the targeting loss.

---

## 2.4 The Process Cost Model (Quality Cost Curve)

This is the classical **economic model of quality** showing total cost as a function of the **defect level** or **degree of conformance**.

$$TCQ(q) = C_{prevention}(q) + C_{appraisal}(q) + C_{failure}(q)$$

where q = proportion of defective units.

- As **q → 0** (perfect quality): prevention cost → ∞, failure cost → 0
- As **q → 1** (no quality effort): prevention cost → 0, failure cost → ∞
- An **optimum q\*** exists where TCQ is minimized.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, students are often asked to **graphically indicate the optimum point** on the cost curve and identify the corresponding defect level. Always label the **minimum point** and the **convex envelope**.

---

## 2.5 Opportunity Cost of Quality

This is the **hidden cost** that does not appear in accounting ledgers:
- Lost sales due to poor quality reputation
- Customer churn and defection
- Reduced market share
- Loss of future contracts

It is often estimated as:

$$C_{opp} = (\text{Lost Units}) \times (\text{Contribution Margin per Unit})$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total COQ (PAF) | $TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ | ₹ or % of Sales |
| 2 | Cost of Conformance | $CoC = PC + AC$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Cost of Non-Conformance | $CoNC = IFC + EFC$ | ₹ |
| 4 | Crosby's Model | $TCQ = POC + PONC$ | ₹ |
| 5 | Taguchi Loss (per unit) | $L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$ | ₹ per unit |
| 6 | Taguchi Constant | $k = A \,/\, \Delta^2$ | ₹ / unit² |
| 7 | Average QLF (batch) | $\bar{L} = k \cdot \sigma^2 + k \cdot (\bar{y} - m)^2$ | ₹ per unit |
| 8 | Total QLF Cost | $TCQ_T = C_{mfg} + k \cdot \sigma^2 + k \cdot (\bar{y} - m)^2$ | ₹ |
| 9 | Opportunity Loss | $C_{opp} = \Delta Q \times CM$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Optimal Defect Level | $\dfrac{dTCQ}{dq} = 0$ | Defect fraction |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **POC = PC + AC** and **PONC = IFC + EFC** equivalences — these are the most frequently asked 3-mark mapping questions.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of COQ Models

1. **Strategic Decision-Making:** COQ reports inform top management about where to invest in quality improvement.
2. **Cost Reduction Programs:** Identify the largest cost buckets (usually EFC) and target them.
3. **Benchmarking:** Compare COQ as a % of sales across departments or with industry peers.
4. **Supplier Evaluation:** Use COQ data when auditing vendors.
5. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Track month-on-month COQ reduction as a KPI.
6. **Six Sigma Projects:** The financial savings of any Six Sigma project is essentially $\Delta PONC$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Taguchi's Quality Loss Coefficient

We begin with the engineering requirement that **loss is proportional to the square of deviation** from target — a principle derived from energy-loss physics (e.g., a bearing operating off-center vibrates with energy proportional to the square of the offset).

**Step 1:** Define loss as a function of deviation $d = y - m$:

$$L(d) = k \cdot d^2$$

This is a Taylor-series approximation; the linear term vanishes by symmetry because deviation on either side of target is equally undesirable.

**Step 2:** At the specification limit, the customer replaces the product at a cost $A$:

$$L(\Delta) = A$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $d = \Delta$:

$$A = k \cdot \Delta^2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for k:

$$k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 5:** The general loss for any deviation $d$:

$$\boxed{L(d) = \frac{A}{\Delta^2} \cdot d^2}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — Taguchi Loss for a Shaft

**Problem:**
A motor shaft has a target diameter $m = 50.00$ mm with specification limit $\Delta = \pm 0.05$ mm. The cost of replacing a defective shaft is $A = ₹400$. A batch of shafts has the following diameters (in mm): 49.97, 50.02, 50.04, 49.99, 49.96. Compute (i) the Taguchi constant k, (ii) the loss for each shaft, and (iii) the average batch loss.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute Taguchi constant k.**

$$k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2} = \frac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{400}{0.0025} = 1,60,000 \; \text{₹ / mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute deviation for each shaft.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 49.97 - 50.00 = -0.03 \; \text{mm} \\
d_2 &= 50.02 - 50.00 = +0.02 \; \text{mm} \\
d_3 &= 50.04 - 50.00 = +0.04 \; \text{mm} \\
d_4 &= 49.99 - 50.00 = -0.01 \; \text{mm} \\
d_5 &= 49.96 - 50.00 = -0.04 \; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute squared deviations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1^2 &= 0.0009 \\
d_2^2 &= 0.0004 \\
d_3^2 &= 0.0016 \\
d_4^2 &= 0.0001 \\
d_5^2 &= 0.0016
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute individual losses $L_i = k \cdot d_i^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 1,60,000 \times 0.0009 = ₹144 \\
L_2 &= 1,60,000 \times 0.0004 = ₹64 \\
L_3 &= 1,60,000 \times 0.0016 = ₹256 \\
L_4 &= 1,60,000 \times 0.0001 = ₹16 \\
L_5 &= 1,60,000 \times 0.0016 = ₹256
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute average batch loss.**

$$\bar{L} = \frac{144 + 64 + 256 + 16 + 256}{5} = \frac{736}{5} = ₹147.2$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that even shafts **within specification** (e.g., shaft 2 at +0.02 mm) still produce a loss of ₹64 per unit. This is Taguchi's key insight — the **traditional "within-limits = no loss" assumption is financially false**.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Optimum Defect Level (Process Cost Model)

**Problem:**
A manufacturing line has the following cost structure as a function of defect rate q (defects per 100 units):

- Prevention cost: $C_P(q) = 50q + 500$
- Appraisal cost: $C_A(q) = 30q + 200$
- Failure cost: $C_F(q) = \dfrac{1000}{q + 0.1}$

Find the defect rate q\* that minimizes total COQ.

### Solution

**Step 1: Formulate the total cost function.**

$$TCQ(q) = C_P(q) + C_A(q) + C_F(q)$$

Substituting:

$$TCQ(q) = (50q + 500) + (30q + 200) + \frac{1000}{q + 0.1}$$

Simplify:

$$TCQ(q) = 80q + 700 + \frac{1000}{q + 0.1}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to q.**

$$\frac{d\,TCQ}{dq} = 80 - \frac{1000}{(q + 0.1)^2}$$

**Step 3: Set derivative to zero for stationary point.**

$$80 - \frac{1000}{(q + 0.1)^2} = 0$$

$$80 = \frac{1000}{(q + 0.1)^2}$$

**Step 4: Solve for q.**

$$(q + 0.1)^2 = \frac{1000}{80} = 12.5$$

$$q + 0.1 = \sqrt{12.5} = 3.536$$

$$q^* = 3.536 - 0.1 = 3.436 \;\text{defects per 100 units}$$

**Step 5: Verify it is a minimum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^2\,TCQ}{dq^2} = \frac{2000}{(q + 0.1)^3} > 0 \;\;(for\; q > 0)$$

Since the second derivative is positive, q\* = 3.436 is indeed a **minimum**.

**Step 6: Compute minimum TCQ.**

$$TCQ(q^*) = 80(3.436) + 700 + \frac{1000}{3.536}$$

$$TCQ(q^*) = 274.88 + 700 + 282.81 = ₹1257.69 \;\text{(per 100 units)}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which COQ Model to Use?

| Situation | Best-Suited Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing assembly line | **PAF Model** | Clear traceability of PC, AC, IFC, EFC activities |
| Software development firm | **Crosby's POC/PONC** | Binary, leadership-friendly reporting |
| Process parameter optimization | **Taguchi QLF** | Continuous loss beyond pass/fail |
| Strategic long-term planning | **Process Cost Curve** | Identifies economic optimum |
| Service industry (BPO, healthcare) | **Opportunity Cost Model** | Captures intangible reputation loss |
| Six Sigma project validation | **Crosby's PONC** | Quantifies financial savings of DMAIC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Cost of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Cost of Quality TCQ] --> B[Cost of Conformance POC]
    A --> C[Cost of Non-Conformance PONC]

    B --> D[Prevention Costs PC]
    B --> E[Appraisal Costs AC]

    C --> F[Internal Failure Costs IFC]
    C --> G[External Failure Costs EFC]

    D --> D1[Quality Planning]
    D --> D2[Training and Education]
    D --> D3[Process Capability Study]
    D --> D4[Supplier Audits]

    E --> E1[Incoming Inspection]
    E --> E2[In-Process Testing]
    E --> E3[Final Inspection]
    E --> E4[Equipment Calibration]

    F --> F1[Scrap and Rework]
    F --> F2[Re-Inspection]
    F --> F3[Failure Analysis]
    F --> F4[Production Downtime]

    G --> G1[Warranty Claims]
    G --> G2[Product Returns]
    G --> G3[Field Repairs]
    G --> G4[Loss of Goodwill]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of Taguchi's QLF Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Target m] --> Q1[Measure Quality Characteristic y]
    Q1 --> Q2[Compute Deviation d = y minus m]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Loss L = k times d squared]
    R1[Replacement Cost A] --> R2[Compute k = A over Delta squared]
    D1[Specification Limit Delta] --> R2
    R2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> S1[Average Loss = k times sigma squared plus k times mean shift squared]
    S1 --> T1[Minimize TCQ by adjusting process]
    T1 --> U1[Optimal Process Setting m star]
```

## 4.3 Process Cost Curve — Optimum Defect Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Increase Prevention Investment] --> B0[Internal Failure Cost Falls]
    B0 --> C0[External Failure Cost Falls Sharply]
    C0 --> D0{Is Total Cost Minimum?}
    D0 -- No --> E0[Continue Shifting Investment]
    E0 --> A0
    D0 -- Yes --> F0[Reached Economic Optimum q star]
    F0 --> G0[Maintain Quality Management Discipline]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[PAF Model Feigenbaum] --> N1[4 Category View]
    M2[Crosby Model] --> N2[2 Category View POC vs PONC]
    M3[Taguchi Model] --> N3[Continuous Loss View]
    M4[Process Cost Model] --> N4[Economic Optimum View]

    N1 --> O1[Manufacturing Tracking]
    N2 --> O2[Leadership Reporting]
    N3 --> O3[Process Optimization]
    N4 --> O4[Strategic Planning]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cost of Quality. List any four components of the PAF model.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to quality requirements. It includes the cost of preventing defects, the cost of detecting defects, and the cost incurred due to the occurrence of defects.

**Four components of the PAF model (A.V. Feigenbaum):**
1. **Prevention Costs** — Quality planning, training, process capability studies.
2. **Appraisal Costs** — Inspection, testing, calibration of equipment.
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — Scrap, rework, re-inspection before dispatch.
4. **External Failure Costs** — Warranty, returns, loss of goodwill post-delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> For 3-mark questions, KTU expects: a **definition (1 mark)** + **enumeration with one-line description of each component (2 marks)**.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Price of Conformance and Price of Non-Conformance as per Crosby.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Price of Conformance (POC) | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost of doing things right the first time | Cost incurred because things were done wrong |
| 2 | Equivalent to Prevention + Appraisal costs | Equivalent to Internal + External Failure costs |
| 3 | An investment that pays back via reduced PONC | A pure loss with no future benefit |
| 4 | Controlled and budgeted | Uncontrolled and often underestimated |
| 5 | Juran considered it a manageable expense | Crosby considered it the largest hidden cost |

**Crosby's central claim:** Most organizations spend 3–4 times more on PONC than on POC. Hence, marginal increases in POC lead to disproportionate decreases in PONC, making total COQ fall.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Question:**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **PAF Model** of Cost of Quality with suitable examples for each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing firm records the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention: ₹3,00,000
- Appraisal: ₹2,00,000
- Internal Failure: ₹4,50,000
- External Failure: ₹7,50,000

Calculate:
(i) Total Cost of Quality
(ii) Cost of Conformance and Cost of Non-Conformance
(iii) Comment on the quality maturity of the firm and suggest a strategic action plan. *(7 marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Solution A

#### Part (a) — PAF Model Explanation

The **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) Model** was developed by **A.V. Feigenbaum** in 1956. It classifies quality costs into four mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive categories:

**(1) Prevention Costs:**
These are costs incurred to **eliminate the root causes** of defects before they occur.
*Examples:* Quality planning, employee training, process capability studies, design reviews, supplier capability assessments.

**(2) Appraisal Costs:**
These are costs of **detecting defects** through measurement, inspection, and testing.
*Examples:* Incoming material inspection, in-process quality checks, final product testing, equipment calibration, laboratory maintenance.

**(3) Internal Failure Costs:**
These are costs of defects discovered **before** the product reaches the customer.
*Examples:* Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure mode analysis, production downtime due to quality issues.

**(4) External Failure Costs:**
These are costs of defects discovered **after** the product reaches the customer.
*Examples:* Warranty claims, returned goods, field repairs, product recalls, loss of goodwill and brand reputation.

**Key Behavioral Insight:** As prevention investment increases, failure costs fall much more sharply. The optimal point lies where the marginal increase in prevention equals the marginal decrease in failure costs.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- PC = ₹3,00,000
- AC = ₹2,00,000
- IFC = ₹4,50,000
- EFC = ₹7,50,000

**(i) Total Cost of Quality (TCQ):**

$$TCQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$$

$$TCQ = 3,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 4,50,000 + 7,50,000 = ₹17,00,000$$

**(ii) Cost of Conformance (CoC) and Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC):**

$$CoC = PC + AC = 3,00,000 + 2,00,000 = ₹5,00,000$$

$$CoNC = IFC + EFC = 4,50,000 + 7,50,000 = ₹12,00,000$$

**Ratio Analysis:**

$$\frac{PONC}{POC} = \frac{12,00,000}{5,00,000} = 2.4$$

**(iii) Comments and Action Plan:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Marks are deducted if students simply state ratios without **contextual interpretation** or **quantitative action plan**. Always conclude with a justified strategic recommendation.

**Observations:**
1. The **EFC (₹7,50,000)** is the largest cost bucket — indicating serious customer-impacting failures.
2. The **PONC is 2.4 times POC** — well above the ideal ratio of < 1.0 (per Crosby's mature organization benchmark).
3. **Prevention investment is low** relative to failure costs — the firm is in a **reactive quality mode**, not a preventive one.

**Recommended Strategic Action Plan:**
1. **Double the prevention budget** to ₹6,00,000 — invest in root-cause analysis (Ishikawa, 5-Why), SPC implementation, and Six Sigma Green Belt training for shop-floor engineers.
2. **Establish a robust supplier audit program** to reduce incoming defect rate.
3. **Implement Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** to reduce external failures by at least 40%.
4. **Set up a COQ dashboard** reporting PC, AC, IFC, EFC monthly to senior management.
5. **Target:** Reduce PONC/POC ratio to **1.0 or below** within 18 months — a marker of Crosby's "quality maturity."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Question:**
**(a)** Define the **Taguchi Quality Loss Function**. Derive the expression for the loss coefficient k. Explain its significance in modern quality management with an example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A piston ring has a target diameter of 80.00 mm with a specification limit of ±0.08 mm. The cost of replacing a defective piston ring is ₹800. A batch of piston rings shows the following deviations from target (in mm): +0.05, −0.03, +0.06, −0.07, +0.02. Compute the Taguchi loss for each unit and the average loss per unit. *(7 marks)*

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 | Re-attempt Paper]**

### Solution B

#### Part (a) — Taguchi Quality Loss Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF) is a mathematical model that quantifies the financial loss to society (manufacturer + customer + society) caused by any deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$

where:
- $L(y)$ = Loss incurred by the unit with measured value y
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (₹ per unit² of deviation)
- $y$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $m$ = Target value

**Derivation of k:**

At the specification limit ($y = m + \Delta$), the customer replaces the product at cost A. Therefore:

$$L(m + \Delta) = k \cdot \Delta^2 = A$$

Solving for k:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2}}$$

**Significance in Modern Quality Management:**
1. **Eliminates the "within-limits = no loss" fallacy.**
2. Provides a **monetary measure** of quality, enabling financial justification of process improvements.
3. Forms the **statistical backbone of Six Sigma** — a Six Sigma process has $d^2 \le 0.000002$ deviation squared, making losses negligible.
4. Encourages **robust design** — engineers design processes whose output variation is insensitive to noise factors.

**Worked Example:** A resistor rated at 100 Ω with $\Delta = 5\,Ω$ and replacement cost $A = ₹10$ gives $k = 10/25 = ₹0.4 / \Omega^2$. A resistor of 102 Ω produces a loss of $0.4 \times 4 = ₹1.6$ — small individually, but huge across millions of units.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution for Piston Rings

**Given Data:**
- Target $m = 80.00$ mm
- Specification limit $\Delta = 0.08$ mm
- Replacement cost $A = ₹800$
- Deviations: +0.05, −0.03, +0.06, −0.07, +0.02 mm

**Step 1: Compute the Taguchi loss coefficient.**

$$k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2} = \frac{800}{(0.08)^2} = \frac{800}{0.0064} = 1,25,000 \; \text{₹ / mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute squared deviation and loss for each unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 = +0.05 &\Rightarrow d_1^2 = 0.0025 \Rightarrow L_1 = 1,25,000 \times 0.0025 = ₹312.50 \\
d_2 = -0.03 &\Rightarrow d_2^2 = 0.0009 \Rightarrow L_2 = 1,25,000 \times 0.0009 = ₹112.50 \\
d_3 = +0.06 &\Rightarrow d_3^2 = 0.0036 \Rightarrow L_3 = 1,25,000 \times 0.0036 = ₹450.00 \\
d_4 = -0.07 &\Rightarrow d_4^2 = 0.0049 \Rightarrow L_4 = 1,25,000 \times 0.0049 = ₹612.50 \\
d_5 = +0.02 &\Rightarrow d_5^2 = 0.0004 \Rightarrow L_5 = 1,25,000 \times 0.0004 = ₹50.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute average loss per unit.**

$$\bar{L} = \frac{312.50 + 112.50 + 450.00 + 612.50 + 50.00}{5} = \frac{1537.50}{5} = ₹307.50$$

**Conclusion:**
Every unit, including those **within specification**, contributes to a quality loss. The unit with $d = -0.07$ mm is at the edge of tolerance and incurs a loss of ₹612.50, while a perfectly centered unit ($d = 0$) would incur ₹0. The firm should invest in process control (e.g., SPC charts, tighter machine calibration) to reduce the standard deviation of deviations.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in COQ Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PAF with Crosby's POC/PONC** — they are not identical; Crosby's model is a higher-level abstraction.
> 2. **Writing $k = A/\Delta$ instead of $k = A/\Delta^2$** — this is the single most common Taguchi formula error.
> 3. **Forgetting to add units (₹)** to the final answer — board examiners deduct for missing units.
> 4. **Not commenting on the PONC/POC ratio** in PAF problems — interpretation carries marks.
> 5. **Skipping the second derivative test** when asked to find optimum defect level — always verify minimum.
> 6. **Using "|x|" notation inside tables** — students often pipe-bar the absolute value, breaking the markdown table. Use `\vert x \vert` instead.
> 7. **Forgetting the "Average Loss" formula** in Taguchi batch problems — $\bar{L} = k\sigma^2 + k(\bar{y} - m)^2$ is the board's favourite test of deeper understanding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **COQ = PC + AC + IFC + EFC** (PAF Model, Feigenbaum, 1956)
- **Crosby's Compression:** POC = PC + AC; PONC = IFC + EFC
- **Crosby's Rule of Thumb:** In mature firms, PONC/POC ratio should approach 1.0 or below.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$ with $k = A / \Delta^2$
- **Taguchi's Innovation:** Loss is continuous, not binary — even within-spec units have loss.
- **Process Cost Model Optimum:** Found by setting $d\,TCQ / dq = 0$ and verifying $d^2\,TCQ / dq^2 > 0$.
- **Opportunity Cost of Quality:** Hidden intangible costs — lost sales, reputation erosion.
- **Six Sigma Connection:** A Six Sigma process has $k\sigma^2 \le 0.002A$ — virtually zero loss.
- **Hierarchy of Investment:** Prevention < Appraisal < Failure costs in *bad* firms; the *mature* firm flips this — Prevention > Appraisal, and both should be much less than Failure costs eliminated.
- **Reporting Standard:** COQ is conventionally reported as **% of sales turnover** for cross-industry benchmarking.
- **Board Answer Structure:** Definition (1 mark) + Components with examples (3 marks) + Numerical/model solution (3 marks) for every 7-mark part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: India's Quality Journey So Far
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** India's Quality Journey So Far

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**India's Quality Journey So Far** refers to the chronological evolution of quality management practices, standards, regulatory frameworks, and cultural adoption of Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophies within Indian industry — from the pre-independence era of negligible quality consciousness to the post-liberalization phase of ISO 9000 adoption, Six Sigma deployment, and global competitiveness in manufacturing and services.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with Outcome-Based Education (OBE), the topic maps to:
- **CO3:** *Understand the evolution of quality management practices in the Indian industrial context.*
- **Bloom's Level:** *Understand (L2) and Analyze (L4).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic establishes the **historical foundation** required to understand *why* India adopted certain quality standards, *who* the pioneering agencies were, and *how* the shift from **inspection-dominated** regimes to **prevention-oriented TQM** occurred across public and private sectors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Old Workshop vs. Modern Factory" Story**
>
> Imagine a 1950s Indian workshop where a craftsman individually checked each item after it was made (this is **Inspection**). Fast forward to 2025: a fully automated Indian automotive plant (Tata, Mahindra) embeds quality sensors at every stage, designs products right the first time, and competes with Toyota in global markets.
>
> The 75-year transition between these two pictures is what we call **"India's Quality Journey."** It is essentially a story of:
> 1. A nation waking up to *defect prevention* (not just detection),
> 2. Adopting *statistical thinking* (SQC/SPC),
> 3. Embracing *international standards* (ISO, QS 9000), and
> 4. Cultivating a *quality culture* through awards, training, and policy reform.

### 1.3 Key Milestones (At a Glance)

The journey can be partitioned into **five distinct epochs**, each marked by a paradigm shift:

| Epoch | Era | Quality Paradigm |
|---|---|---|
| **Epoch I** | Pre-1947 | Negligible formalized quality |
| **Epoch II** | 1947 – 1980 | Inspection, ISI standards, state-owned QA |
| **Epoch III** | 1980 – 1991 | Deming/Taguchi influence, QCs, BPO export push |
| **Epoch IV** | 1991 – 2010 | ISO 9000 boom, Liberalization, TQM in industry |
| **Epoch V** | 2010 – Present | Six Sigma, Lean, Industry 4.0, Make in India, ZED |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** Remember the acronym **"I-L-I-A-Z"** → *Inspection, Liberalization, ISO, Awards, ZED/Zero-Defect* — a one-line mental roadmap of the five epochs.

### 1.4 The Central Metric of Progress

The most widely tracked indicator of India's quality progress is the **number of ISO 9001 certified organizations**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From **zero certifications in 1987** (the year ISO 9000 was published) to over **35,000+ ISO 9001 certificates in India by 2023** — placing India among the **top 5 nations globally** for ISO 9001 certifications.

This single statistic is frequently used in KTU answers to justify the *scale* of the transformation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases — Structured Logical Analysis

#### **PHASE I — The Pre-Independence & Early Nehruvian Era (1900 – 1955)**

- **Industrial Context:** Quality was a *by-product* of British colonial extraction; "good enough to ship" was the operational rule.
- **Key Drivers:** Establishment of **Indian Standards Institution (ISI)** in **1947** (now Bureau of Indian Standards — BIS).
- **Quality Mindset:** Reactive, **defect-detection** (inspection) only.
- **Key Limitation:** No statistical tools, no customer focus, no quality function deployment (QFD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Founding Fact:** The ISI was founded on **6 January 1947**, two days before formal Indian independence, under the **Indian Standards Institution (Certification Marks) Act, 1952**. This is the *genesis* of formal quality infrastructure in India.

#### **PHASE II — The Public-Sector & Inspection Era (1955 – 1980)**

- **Industrial Context:** "License Raj" economy; PSUs (BHEL, SAIL, ONGC) dominated.
- **Quality Mechanisms:**
  - **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** was introduced via the **Indian Statistical Institute (ISI-Kolkata)**, championed by **Prof. P.C. Mahalanobis** and later **Prof. Genichi Taguchi** (visiting Japan, but Indian statisticians also adopted his methods).
  - Defence and atomic establishments (DRDO, DAE) developed robust in-house QA.
  - **Quality Control Circles (QCCs)** introduced experimentally in BHEL and Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) in the late 1970s.
- **Mindset:** Inspection-driven, vendor-unfriendly, **departmental QA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Statutory vs. Voluntary" Quality:** During this phase, quality compliance was largely **statutory** (government-mandated for defence, atomic, public-health sectors) rather than *voluntary market-driven*. This is a high-yield KTU contrast point.

#### **PHASE III — Liberalization & ISO Boom (1991 – 2005)**

This is the **watershed phase** — the catalyst being the **1991 LPG (Liberalization-Privatization-Globalization)** reforms under Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh.

- **Trigger Events:**
  - MNCs (Coca-Cola, IBM, GE) exited and re-entered India, demanding **global quality benchmarks** from suppliers.
  - Export-oriented units in textiles, auto-components, and IT services required **ISO 9000 certification** as a *de-facto visa* to Western markets.
  - **European Union's CE Marking** and **US's QS-9000** (later ISO/TS 16949) forced Indian auto suppliers to upgrade.
- **Key Organizational Actions:**
  - **BIS rebranded as Bureau of Indian Standards (1987)** — the *reorganization year* coinciding with ISO 9000 publication.
  - **Confederation of Indian Industry (CII)** institutionalized the **CII-Exim Bank Award for Quality** (1994) — India's first structured private-sector quality award.
  - **Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA)** instituted in **1991** by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under Ministry of Power.
- **Key Numbers:**
  - From **~50 ISO 9000 certificates in 1993** to over **8,000 by 2000** — a **160x growth in 7 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every Indian auto-component exporter today must hold **IATF 16949** (successor to ISO/TS 16949). The pressure on Tier-2 and Tier-3 suppliers (small-scale industries) to achieve certification has been a major engine of the Make in India initiative.

#### **PHASE IV — TQM, Six Sigma & Sectoral Excellence (2005 – 2015)**

- **Paradigm Shift:** From *compliance* to *excellence*.
- **Key Adoptions:**
  - **Six Sigma** in IT/BPO (Infosys, Wipro, TCS) — Infosys was among the **first non-US firms** to deploy Six Sigma at scale.
  - **Lean Manufacturing** in automotive (Tata Motors' "Manza" plant, Maruti Suzuki).
  - **CMMI** for software/IT services.
  - **NABL (National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories)** accreditation of testing labs.
- **Notable Achievement:** Indian pharma (Sun Pharma, Dr. Reddy's) cleared **USFDA inspections** by deploying QMS aligned to **21 CFR Part 820**.

#### **PHASE V — Industry 4.0, ZED & Global Leadership (2015 – Present)**

- **Flagship Initiatives:**
  - **Make in India (2014)** — quality embedded as a *pillar*.
  - **ZED (Zero Defect Zero Effect) Certification Scheme (2016)** by the **Ministry of MSME** — uniquely Indian recognition for MSMEs.
  - **National Quality Campaign** by BIS.
  - **Skill India** — quality training at the bottom of the pyramid.
- **Emerging Trends:** **Industry 4.0**, **AI-driven SPC**, **digital twins**, **predictive quality**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following high-yield points; these appear verbatim in KTU board exams.

| **Concept / Entity** | **Year / Number** | **Significance (KTU Answer-Ready)** |
|---|---|---|
| Indian Standards Institution (ISI) founded | **6 Jan 1947** | Apex quality body — pre-BIS |
| ISI renamed to **BIS** | **1987** | Coincided with ISO 9000 publication |
| First RGNQA | **1991** | India's national TQM award |
| Liberalization reforms | **1991** | Triggered ISO adoption in industry |
| ISO 9000 series published | **1987** | Catalyst for global certification |
| CII-Exim Bank Quality Award | **1994** | First private-sector quality award |
| ISO 9001 certificates in India (2023) | **~35,000+** | Top-5 globally |
| ZED Scheme | **2016** | MSME-focused, Make in India |
| Quality Control Circles (India) | **Late 1970s** | BHEL & HAL pioneers |
| Mahalanobis model of quality | **1950s–60s** | Statistical foundations |

### 2.3 The "Three Drivers" Theoretical Framework

Indian quality progress was propelled by **three forces acting simultaneously** — useful as a structuring device in 14-mark answers:

1. **Regulatory Driver (Top-Down):** Government mandates → BIS standards → statutory QA → RGNQA.
2. **Market Driver (Bottom-Up):** Export pressure → buyer requirements → supplier compliance → ISO 9000 boom.
3. **Cultural Driver (Inside-Out):** Worker empowerment → QCCs → TQM culture → Six Sigma.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Examiners award marks for *structuring* your answer. Mentioning these three drivers in your introduction immediately signals **analytical depth (Bloom's L4)** and fetches the **cognitive-level marks** reserved for 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix & Step-by-Step Historical Derivation

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Reconstruction of the Quality Timeline

Below is the complete, transition-by-transition reconstruction of India's quality evolution. Each step is annotated with the *causal logic* (the "why") connecting it to the next.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1: Pre-1947 — Colonial Foundation Phase} \\
&\text{Industry characteristic: Export-driven raw-material extraction (cotton, jute, tea).} \\
&\text{Quality notion: "Sellers' market" — no competitive pressure to improve.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 2: 1947 — Birth of Indian Standards Institution (ISI)} \\
&\text{Action: ISI founded on 6 Jan 1947, headquartered in New Delhi.} \\
&\text{Logic: Independent India needed a *national standards body* analogous to BSI (UK) and DIN (Germany).} \\
&\text{Quality output: First Indian Standard (IS 1:1968 for steel) published.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 3: 1952 — Certification Marks Act} \\
&\text{Action: Parliament enacted the ISI (Certification Marks) Act, 1952.} \\
&\text{Logic: Without legal backing, ISI marks could be counterfeited — statutory power was essential.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 4: 1955–1970 — ISI-Kolkata's Statistical Influence} \\
&\text{Action: P.C. Mahalanobis introduced SQC to Indian industry through ISI-Kolkata.} \\
&\text{Logic: Industrial planning (Five-Year Plans) required *measurable* quality metrics, not subjective inspection.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 5: 1976 — ISI Bhopal Centre for Quality Promotion} \\
&\text{Action: Dedicated centre for training and quality promotion established.} \\
&\text{Logic: Quality required *human-capital* infrastructure, not just standards documents.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 6: 1987 — ISO 9000 Published \textbar\ BIS Reorganized} \\
&\text{Action: ISO 9000 published globally; same year, BIS Act reorganized ISI as the Bureau of Indian Standards.} \\
&\text{Logic: India's apex body needed statutory rebranding to lead the *voluntary certification revolution* about to begin.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 7: 1991 — Liberalization + RGNQA} \\
&\text{Action: LPG reforms opened Indian markets; RGNQA instituted for national-level TQM recognition.} \\
&\text{Logic: Competitive imports forced Indian firms to seek *excellence models* (Baldrige, EFQM).} \\
&\text{Outcome: Industry began *emulating* global TQM frameworks.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 8: 1993–2000 — ISO Certification Explosion} \\
&\text{Calculation:} \\
&\quad \text{Certifications in 1993} \approx 50 \\
&\quad \text{Certifications in 2000} \approx 8{,}000 \\
&\quad \text{Growth factor} = \frac{8{,}000}{50} = 160 \\
&\text{Logic: Export demand from EU/US made ISO 9001 a *commercial prerequisite*.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 9: 2000–2010 — Sectoral Excellence Programs} \\
&\text{Action: Sector-specific QMS — ISO/TS 16949 (auto), AS 9100 (aerospace), 21 CFR 820 (pharma).} \\
&\text{Logic: One-size-fits-all ISO 9001 was *insufficient* for high-risk/high-precision sectors.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 10: 2016 — ZED Certification Scheme} \\
&\text{Action: Ministry of MSME launched Zero-Defect Zero-Effect certification.} \\
&\text{Logic: 60+ million MSMEs needed a *graded* recognition model — Bronze → Silver → Gold.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 11: 2018–Present — Industry 4.0 Quality} \\
&\text{Action: AI-driven SPC, IoT-enabled quality sensors, digital twin simulations.} \\
&\text{Logic: Customer expectations + global competitiveness demand *predictive*, not *reactive*, quality.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Driver Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is **high-yield for 14-mark questions** on this topic. Use it verbatim in board answers.

| **Dimension** | **Regulatory Driver (Top-Down)** | **Market Driver (Bottom-Up)** | **Cultural Driver (Inside-Out)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Government policy | Global buyer demands | Workforce & leadership |
| **Key Year** | 1947, 1987, 1991 | 1991 (LPG), 1993 (ISO boom) | Late 1970s (QCCs), 1990s (TQM) |
| **Trigger** | Independence, reforms | Liberalization, exports | Japanese influence (Deming, Ishikawa) |
| **Tool/Standard** | BIS standards, RGNQA | ISO 9001, CE, IATF 16949 | QCCs, Six Sigma, 5S |
| **Coverage** | Mandatory (statutory) | Voluntary (market) | Voluntary (philosophical) |
| **Strength** | Authority & uniformity | Speed & revenue impact | Sustainability & depth |
| **Weakness** | Bureaucratic delay | Superficial compliance | Slow to scale |
| **Current Status** | Evolving toward ZED | ISO 9001:2015 + sectoral | Six Sigma + Lean + Industry 4.0 |

### 3.3 Quantitative Growth Model (KTU Numerical-Ready)

For a 3-mark question, examiners may ask about ISO certification growth. The growth can be modelled as:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{rt}
$$

Where:
- $C(t)$ = Number of ISO 9001 certificates in year $t$.
- $C_0$ = Initial baseline (50 in 1993).
- $r$ = Compound annual growth rate (CAGR).
- $t$ = Years elapsed since 1993.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } C(2000) = 8{,}000,\ C_0 = 50,\ t = 7 \\
&8{,}000 = 50 \cdot e^{7r} \\
&e^{7r} = 160 \\
&7r = \ln(160) \approx 5.075 \\
&r \approx 0.725 \text{ per year} \approx 72.5\% \text{ CAGR}
\end{aligned}
$$

This staggering **72.5% CAGR** between 1993–2000 is the *numerical evidence* of India's ISO boom — a compelling data point for any 14-mark essay.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. State the year of establishment of the Indian Standards Institution (ISI) and explain its later transition to the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS).** *[3 Marks] [Bloom's: Remember/L1]*

**Model Answer:**

The Indian Standards Institution (ISI) was established on **6 January 1947** as India's apex standards body, modelled on the British Standards Institution (BSI). For four decades, ISI operated as a *non-statutory* body formulating product and process standards across sectors. In **1987**, coinciding with the global publication of the **ISO 9000 series**, the Government of India enacted the **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 1986**, reorganizing ISI as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** with statutory authority to enforce mandatory certification, especially for products affecting consumer safety (electrical appliances, food, cement, etc.). This transition was a **watershed moment** that gave India's quality framework legal teeth. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Define RGNQA and state the year of its institution.** *[3 Marks] [Bloom's: Remember/L1]*

**Model Answer:**

The **Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA)** is India's national-level award recognizing *excellence in quality management* across manufacturing, services, small-scale, and large-scale organizations. It was instituted in **1991** by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)**, operating under the **Ministry of Power, Government of India**. The award is structured on lines similar to the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (USA)** and assesses organizations on criteria such as leadership, strategic planning, customer focus, measurement-analysis, workforce, process management, and results. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> **Q3A. Trace the evolution of quality management practices in India from pre-independence to the present day. Highlight the role of key institutions such as ISI/BIS, CII, and RGNQA in this journey.** *[14 Marks: 7+7] [Bloom's: Analyze/L4]*

#### **Sub-Part (a) — Historical Phasing [7 Marks]**

India's quality journey can be partitioned into **five epochs**:

**(i) Pre-1947 — Colonial Foundation:** Quality was incidental to British extraction; the *sellers' market* mentality dominated with no formalized quality infrastructure.

**(ii) 1947–1980 — Inspection Era:** The founding of **ISI in 1947** and the **Certification Marks Act, 1952** established India's first quality framework. PSUs (BHEL, SAIL, HAL) maintained in-house inspection regimes. **P.C. Mahalanobis** at the **Indian Statistical Institute (Kolkata)** introduced statistical quality control (SQC) to industrial planning.

**(iii) 1980–1991 — QC Movement:** The late 1970s saw the introduction of **Quality Control Circles (QCCs)** in BHEL and HAL. The **1987 BIS Act** reorganized ISI as the **Bureau of Indian Standards**, endowing it with statutory powers.

**(iv) 1991–2010 — Liberalization & ISO Boom:** The **1991 LPG reforms** triggered an explosion in **ISO 9001 certifications** — from ~50 in 1993 to ~8,000 by 2000 (a 72.5% CAGR). MNC entry forced Indian suppliers to adopt global QMS.

**(v) 2010–Present — Excellence & Digital:** The **ZED scheme (2016)**, **Six Sigma deployment**, **Lean adoption**, and **Industry 4.0** characterize the current phase.

#### **Sub-Part (b) — Role of Key Institutions [7 Marks]**

**(i) ISI/BIS:** Provided the *standards backbone* — from voluntary standardization in 1947 to mandatory certification post-1987. BIS today administers the **ISI mark** (now BIS Standard Mark) on thousands of products, ensuring consumer protection.

**(ii) CII (Confederation of Indian Industry):** Established the **CII-Exim Bank Award for Quality (1994)** — India's first private-sector quality award. CII also runs the **CII Institute of Quality**, training thousands of Indian quality professionals annually.

**(iii) RGNQA (1991):** Modeled on the **Baldrige framework**, it provides *national-level recognition* for TQM excellence, motivating organizations to move from compliance to *performance excellence*.

**(iv) Supporting Institutions:** **NABL** (lab accreditation), **NPC** (National Productivity Council), **QCI** (Quality Council of India, est. 1997) — all act as *catalysts* in the quality ecosystem.

**Conclusion:** India's quality journey reflects a transition from *inspection* to *prevention* to *excellence*, driven simultaneously by **regulation, market forces, and cultural change** — positioning India as a global quality leader in the 21st century.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**
> **Q3B. Discuss the impact of the 1991 Liberalization reforms on quality management in India. Explain the rise of ISO certifications and the role of export markets in driving this change.** *[14 Marks: 7+7] [Bloom's: Analyze/L4]*

#### **Sub-Part (a) — Liberalization's Quality Shock [7 Marks]**

The **1991 LPG reforms** under **Finance Minister Dr. Manmohan Singh** dismantled the *License Raj* and opened Indian industry to global competition. The **quality shock** manifested in three ways:

1. **MNC Re-entry:** Companies like General Electric, Coca-Cola, and IBM demanded *global supplier quality standards* from their Indian vendors.
2. **Import Competition:** Indian firms (Tata, Maruti, Bajaj) suddenly faced competition from Toyota, Honda, and Samsung — forcing *quality benchmarking* against international best practices.
3. **Export Push:** Indian exporters (textiles, auto-components, IT services) needed certifications like **ISO 9001** as a *de facto passport* to EU and US markets.

#### **Sub-Part (b) — ISO 9000 Boom and Export Pull [7 Marks]**

The **ISO 9000 series**, published in **1987**, found fertile ground in post-liberalization India:

- **1993:** ~50 ISO 9000 certificates in India.
- **2000:** ~8,000 certificates — a **160x growth in 7 years** (CAGR ≈ 72.5%).
- **2023:** Over **35,000+ ISO 9001 certificates**, placing India in the **top 5 globally**.

**Sectoral Adoption:**
- **IT/Software:** Adopted **CMMI** and **ISO 9001** to serve US/EU clients.
- **Automotive:** Adopted **QS-9000 → ISO/TS 16949 → IATF 16949** to supply global OEMs.
- **Pharma:** Adopted **21 CFR Part 820** to gain USFDA approvals.
- **Manufacturing:** Mass ISO 9001 certification among SMEs supplying MNCs.

**Causal Formula:**

$$
\text{Export Demand} \rightarrow \text{Buyer Mandates (ISO/QS/IATF)} \rightarrow \text{Supplier Compliance} \rightarrow \text{QMS Investment} \rightarrow \text{Certified Growth}
$$

**Conclusion:** Liberalization acted as the **single largest external catalyst** in India's quality journey — converting quality from a *cost centre* into a *strategic competitive lever*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating ISI with ISO:** Students often write "ISO was established in 1947 in India" — this is factually wrong. **ISI = Indian Standards Institution (1947, India).** **ISO = International Organization for Standardization (1946, Geneva, global).** Mixing them up costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong RGNQA Year:** Some students cite 1997 or 2001. The correct year is **1991**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Mahalanobis:** P.C. Mahalanobis (not Taguchi) was the Indian pioneer of statistical quality. Taguchi was Japanese. Examiners expect this distinction.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Cultural Driver:** Students often write only about ISO and BIS, forgetting **QCCs, TQM, Six Sigma, and cultural adoption**. Use the *three-driver framework* for full marks.
>
> 5. **No Conclusion:** A 14-mark answer without a *concise conclusion* (2–3 lines) typically loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **No Data Points:** Topper answers always include at least **one numerical fact** (e.g., *72.5% CAGR*, *35,000+ certifications*) — this signals *Bloom's L4/L5 cognitive depth*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **ISI founded: 6 January 1947** (pre-BIS era).
- **BIS Act: 1986, implemented 1987** (statutory authority).
- **ISO 9000 series published: 1987** (global).
- **RGNQA instituted: 1991** (national TQM award).
- **Liberalization reforms: 1991** (LPG — quality catalyst).
- **CII-Exim Bank Quality Award: 1994** (private-sector).
- **Quality Council of India (QCI): 1997** (umbrella accreditation body).
- **ZED Certification Scheme: 2016** (MSME-focused).
- **Make in India launch: 2014** (quality as a pillar).
- **ISO certifications growth: ~50 (1993) → 8,000 (2000) → 35,000+ (2023)**.

**Key Conceptual Frameworks:**

- **Three Drivers:** Regulatory (BIS/RGNQA) + Market (ISO/MNCs) + Cultural (QCCs/TQM).
- **Five Epochs:** Inspection → QC → QA → TQM → Digital Quality 4.0.
- **Three Pioneers:** P.C. Mahalanobis (statistics), Deming/Taguchi (influence), CII/BIS (institutional).

**Key Pairs (Contrast Points Examiners Love):**

- *Statutory vs. Voluntary Quality*
- *Inspection vs. Prevention vs. Excellence*
- *Compliance vs. Performance*
- *Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Drivers*
- *Pre-LPG vs. Post-LPG Quality Mindset*

**Mnemonic Acronym — "I-L-I-A-Z"** (Inspection → Liberalization → ISO → Awards → ZED) — the five-epoch mental roadmap.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **(i) Introduction with three-driver framework, (ii) Phase-wise historical narrative with dates, (iii) Institutional roles (BIS, CII, RGNQA), (iv) Quantitative evidence (ISO growth numbers), (v) Conclusion linking to current Industry 4.0 / Make in India**. This structure consistently scores **12+ out of 14** in KTU board valuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Management in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Management in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Management in India** refers to the systematic, organization-wide framework of policies, standards, institutions, awards, and practices adopted across Indian industries, government bodies, and service sectors to plan, assure, control, and continuously improve the quality of products, services, and processes, while integrating global best practices (such as ISO 9000, TQM, Six Sigma, Lean) with India-specific socio-economic, cultural, and regulatory realities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3, Topic 1)**
> Quality Management in India encompasses the historical evolution, institutional framework, national standards, awards, government initiatives, and contemporary challenges of implementing quality practices within Indian industrial, service, and public-sector enterprises.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traditional Indian family restaurant** that began as a small roadside stall in 1947 (Indian Independence). For the first 40 years, the owners cooked the same recipes passed down by ancestors, with no formal measurement, no hygiene audits, and no customer feedback system — they simply trusted the "guru-shishya" tradition. Then in 1991 (Liberalization), multinational chains opened next door. Suddenly, the family had to:
- Standardize recipes (like **BIS standards**)
- Get a hygiene certification (like **ISO 9000 / HACCP**)
- Win a "Best Restaurant" award (like **RGNQA**)
- Train family members in modern cooking science (like **TQM training**)
- Submit to mystery diners and customer surveys (like **Third-party audits**)

This is precisely the journey of **Quality Management in India** — a transformation from inherited craftsmanship → regulatory standardization → global benchmarking → continuous excellence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition**: Quality in India is not merely "imported" from the West or Japan. It has been **localized** — Deming's 14 Points are read in Tamil, Six Sigma Black Belts are trained in Hindi, and Quality Circles operate in regional languages across Maruti, Tata, and BHEL factories.

### 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics Used in Indian Context

The following internationally standardized metrics are mandated and widely applied in Indian quality frameworks:

- **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) — used in Six Sigma deployments
- **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) — used by NASSCOM and CII in Indian IT/ITES firms
- **PPM** (Parts Per Million) defects — used in automotive sector (ACMA, SIAM)
- **FTR** (First Time Right) — used in BPO and IT service industries
- **Cpk / Ppk** (Process Capability Indices) — used in manufacturing for acceptance sampling

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: For KTU 2024 OESFT615 Module 3, students must be able to identify *Indian institutions* (BIS, QCI, NPC), *Indian awards* (RGNQA, CII-EXIM, Golden Peacock), and *Indian context applications* of global quality philosophies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 Historical Evolution of Quality Management in India — Phase-wise Breakdown

The evolution can be structurally classified into **five distinct phases**, mirroring India's industrial and economic transformation:

#### **Phase I: Foundation Era (1947 – 1980)**
- **Year 1947**: Indian Standards Institution (ISI) established (reconstituted as **BIS in 1987** under BIS Act 1986).
- **Year 1958**: **National Productivity Council (NPC)** established — pioneering productivity and quality awareness in public sector.
- Focus: Mandatory standards for heavy industry, railways, defence, steel (SAIL, BHEL).
- Quality assurance was **inspection-driven** (product-fitted, not process-fitted).

#### **Phase II: Awakening Era (1980 – 1991)**
- Indian Statistical Institute (ISI Kolkata, founded 1931) introduces **statistical quality control (SQC)** concepts.
- QC Circles movement gains foothold in Maruti Suzuki (1983), Tata Motors, and BHEL — inspired by Japanese Kaizen.
- **1986**: Bureau of Indian Standards Act passed; ISI becomes BIS in 1987.
- Indian companies begin exporting to regulated markets (US FDA, EU CE) — quality becomes an **export passport**.

#### **Phase III: Liberalization & TQM Era (1991 – 2000)**
- **1991**: LPG (Liberalization-Privatization-Globalization) reforms — sudden exposure to global competition.
- **1991**: **Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA)** instituted by BIS — India's first national TQM award modelled on the Malcolm Baldrige Award.
- **1997**: **Quality Council of India (QCI)** established as the apex national body for quality, under the Department of Industrial Policy & Promotion (DIPP).
- ISO 9000 certification wave in India: from ~200 certified companies in 1992 to **>25,000** by 2000.
- Indian pharmaceutical companies (Ranbaxy, Cipla) and IT firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) achieve global quality benchmarks.

#### **Phase IV: Excellence & Six Sigma Era (2000 – 2014)**
- Widespread **Six Sigma** adoption in IT/ITES and automotive sectors (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Maruti, Tata Steel).
- **2004**: National Manufacturing Competitiveness Programme (NMCP) launched.
- **2007**: **Zero Defect Zero Effect (ZED)** concept popularized by Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam.
- Indian companies win international awards: **Tata Motors JIPM Award**, **Wipro CII-EXIM Award**.

#### **Phase V: Industry 4.0 & National Quality Mission Era (2014 – Present)**
- **2014**: **Make in India** campaign — quality as a strategic national priority.
- **2017**: **National Quality Campaign** by QCI and BIS to promote quality in schools, MSMEs.
- **2018 onwards**: Industry 4.0 integration — IoT, AI-driven quality inspection, digital twins.
- **2021**: National Programme on **"Quality & Productivity"** through NPC.
- India moves from "Made in India" to "**Made Brilliant in India**".

### 2.2 Key Institutional Framework — The Indian Quality Ecosystem

| **Institution** | **Year** | **Parent Body** | **Primary Role** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | 1987 (as BIS; originally ISI in 1947) | Ministry of Consumer Affairs | Formulation of Indian Standards; ISI/Hallmarking marks; certification |
| Quality Council of India (QCI) | 1997 | DIPP, Govt. of India | Apex accreditation body; promotes TQM and quality awareness |
| National Productivity Council (NPC) | 1958 | Ministry of Commerce & Industry | Productivity enhancement, training, consultancy |
| Indian Statistical Institute (ISI Kolkata) | 1931 | Ministry of Statistics & Programme Implementation | Statistical Quality Control, R&D, training |
| Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI) | 1966 | Ministry of Heavy Industries | Automotive quality, testing, certification |
| Confederation of Indian Industry (CII) | 1895 (as 5) | Industry-led | CII-EXIM Bank Award, quality summits, training |
| FICCI | 1927 | Industry-led | Quality & standards advocacy |
| National Accreditation Board (NABCB) | 1996 (under QCI) | QCI | Accreditation of certification bodies |
| Indian Institute of Quality Management (IIQM) | 2002 | NPC | Quality management education |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip**: The acronym **"BQ-IIN"** (BIS, QCI, ISI-Kolkata, NPC) covers the four core institutions most frequently asked in KTU Module 3 questions. The roles of these four are non-negotiable exam content.

### 2.3 Major National Quality Awards in India

| **Award Name** | **Instituted By** | **Year** | **Modelled On** | **Awarded For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RGNQA** (Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award) | BIS | 1991 | Malcolm Baldrige (USA) | Overall TQM excellence across sectors |
| **CII-EXIM Bank Award** for Business Excellence | CII + EXIM Bank | 1993 | EFQM (Europe) | Business excellence in Indian companies |
| **IMC Ramkrishna Bajaj National Quality Award** | IMC Mumbai | 1996 | Baldrige | Manufacturing & service quality |
| **Golden Peacock National Quality Award** | Institute of Directors | 1992 | Global best practices | Quality management systems |
| **ELCINA Quality Award** | ELCINA | — | — | Electronics industry quality |
| **TPM Award (JIPM)** | JIPM Japan (conferred on Indian cos) | — | Japan TPM | Total Productive Maintenance |

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

Since Quality Management is a management domain, this "cheat sheet" combines **key quality formulae** with **Indian-context numerical benchmarks** that appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| **Concept / Formula** | **Equation / Definition** | **Typical Indian Sector Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Automotive (Maruti, Tata), Pharma (Sun, Dr. Reddy's) |
| **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | IT/ITES (TCS, Infosys), BPO (Genpact) |
| **Six Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{level} = \text{NormInv}(DPMO) + 1.5$ | World-class: $3.4$ DPMO ($6\sigma$); Indian MSMEs aim for $3\sigma$ to $4\sigma$ |
| **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** | $COPQ = C_{appraisal} + C_{prevention} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}$ | NASSCOM benchmarking; auto-component firms |
| **First Time Right (FTR)** | $FTR\ (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries accepted first time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100$ | BPO, banking, IT services |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ) Ratio** | $CoQ\ (\%) = \dfrac{\text{Quality Costs}}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | Indian benchmark: $5$–$10\%$; World-class: $<2.5\%$ |
| **Yield Rate** | $Y = Y_{1} \times Y_{2} \times \cdots \times Y_{n}$ (rolled throughput yield) | PCB manufacturing, pharma packaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule Followed**: All vertical bars `|`, absolute values, and conditionals inside table cells have been rendered using `\vert`, `\mid`, `\min(...)`, or rewording to preserve the markdown table structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BIS Standards** are legally mandatory for over 200 product categories in India (electrical appliances, cement, food, toys under the **BIS Compulsory Registration Scheme** of 2012).
- **QCI accreditation** is now mandatory for all Indian certification bodies issuing ISO certificates, preventing fraudulent certifications.
- The **ZED (Zero Defect Zero Effect)** scheme of the Ministry of MSME directly benefits lakhs of small Indian manufacturers.
- Indian automotive components supplied to OEMs globally must comply with **AIAG core tools** (PPAP, APQP, FMEA, MSA, SPC) — a direct legacy of Indian TQM maturity.
