# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Explain the concept of the **Quality Loss Function** as proposed by Taguchi. How does it differ from the traditional pass-or-fail quality approach?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** is a quadratic mathematical relationship proposed by Taguchi to quantify the **monetary loss to society** as a continuous function of the deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value.

$$
L(x) \;=\; k\,(x - T)^{2}, \qquad k \;=\; \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}}
$$

Where $k$ is the loss coefficient, $A_{0}$ is the replacement cost, $\Delta_{0}$ is the functional tolerance, and $T$ is the target value.

**Differences from the traditional approach:**

| Aspect | Traditional Approach | Taguchi QLF Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Loss within spec | Assumed zero | Continuous, quadratic in deviation |
| Loss outside spec | Step (jumps to 100% loss) | Continuous growth beyond tolerance |
| Quality measure | Pass / Fail (binary) | Continuous monetary loss |
| Focus | Conformance to specification | Minimization of loss to society |

> **Mark Allocation Hint:** Definition of QLF (1 mark), formula and explanation of $k$ (1 mark), differences from traditional approach (1 mark).

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio** used in Taguchi's robust design. Write the S/N ratio formula for the **"smaller-the-better"** performance characteristic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio** is a single performance metric in Taguchi's methodology that combines the **mean** (signal) and **variability** (noise) of a response into a single number expressed in **decibels (dB)**. A higher S/N ratio indicates a more robust system — i.e., one that achieves the desired response while being **less sensitive to noise factors**.

**General form:**

$$
\eta \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right)
$$

**Smaller-the-better formula:**

$$
\eta_{STB} \;=\; -\,10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i}^{\,2}\right]
$$

**Example:** For surface roughness (lower is better), if three replicate measurements are $y_{1} = 1.2,\ y_{2} = 1.0,\ y_{3} = 1.1$ µm, then

$$
\eta_{STB} \;=\; -\,10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1.2^{2} + 1.0^{2} + 1.1^{2}}{3}\right] \;=\; -\,10\,\log_{10}(1.0167) \;=\; -\,0.072\ \text{dB}
$$

> **Mark Allocation Hint:** Definition of S/N (1 mark), general formula (1 mark), STB formula with example (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Before Answering:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Computing the S/N ratio with the *wrong sign*. For "smaller-is-better" and "larger-is-better", the S/N ratio must be *negative* of the log. Forgetting the negative sign is the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks per question.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Using **sample variance** ($n-1$ denominator) for the QLF but **population variance** ($n$ denominator) for the S/N ratio. Standard KTU convention is *sample* variance ($n-1$) for the S/N ratio — be consistent!
> - **Common mistake #3:** Forgetting to **predict** the optimum S/N and compare it with the **confirmation** experiment. Examiners explicitly allocate 2 marks for this step.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Mixing up "inner array" (control factors) and "outer array" (noise factors). The mnemonic **"I-Control, O-Noise"** helps.
> - **Common mistake #5:** Stating the formula for S/N ratio without mentioning that the unit is **decibels (dB)**. A minor 0.5-mark deduction applies.

---

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
*Explain the **Taguchi Philosophy of Quality** and describe the **three standard S/N ratios** used in the Taguchi Method. For each S/N ratio, give **one industrial application** and state the corresponding formula.*

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
*The diameter of a turned shaft has a target value of $T = 25.00\ \text{mm}$ and is a **nominal-the-best** characteristic. The replacement cost at the functional tolerance $\Delta_{0} = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ is $A_{0} = \text{₹}\, 200$. Compute:*
*(i) The quality loss coefficient $k$.*
*(ii) The quality loss when a shaft is produced with diameter $25.15\ \text{mm}$.*
*(iii) The S/N ratio for a sample with three replicate measurements $y_{1} = 25.05,\ y_{2} = 25.10,\ y_{3} = 25.00$ mm.*

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**The Taguchi Philosophy of Quality (3 marks):**

Dr. Genichi Taguchi, a Japanese quality engineer, redefined the concept of quality by introducing a **loss-based** view. According to his philosophy:

1. **Quality is the loss imparted to society** — not merely conformance to specifications.
2. The cost of poor quality must be measured in terms of the **loss to society** (customer, manufacturer, supplier, and community).
3. A product must be designed to be **robust** — insensitive to variations caused by uncontrollable **noise factors**.
4. The goal of experimentation is not just to find the optimum factor settings but to **minimize variability** in the output.

**Three Standard S/N Ratios (4 marks):**

| Type | Industrial Application | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Smaller-the-Better (STB)** | Surface roughness of machined parts; defect density in fabrics; emission levels in IC engines | $\eta_{STB} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}\right]$ |
| **Larger-the-Better (LTB)** | Tensile strength of a wire; battery life of a smartphone; fuel efficiency of a car | $\eta_{LTB} = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{2}}\right]$ |
| **Nominal-the-Best (NTB)** | Shaft diameter; resistor value; mains voltage; pH of a chemical solution | $\eta_{NTB} = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\mu^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right]$ |

In all three formulas, the **unit is the decibel (dB)**. A **larger S/N ratio always indicates better performance**, because the STB and LTB formulas are written with a leading negative sign to make the metric increase as the underlying quality improves.

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $T = 25.00\ \text{mm}$, $\Delta_{0} = 0.5\ \text{mm}$, $A_{0} = \text{₹}\, 200$, $x = 25.15\ \text{mm}$, $n = 3$ replicates with $y_{1} = 25.05$, $y_{2} = 25.10$, $y_{3} = 25.00$ mm.

**(i) Quality loss coefficient $k$ [2 marks]:**

$$
k \;=\; \dfrac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{\,2}} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{(0.5)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{0.25} \;=\; \text{₹}\, 800 \text{ per mm}^{2}
$$

**(ii) Quality loss at $x = 25.15\ \text{mm}$ [2 marks]:**

The deviation is $x - T = 25.15 - 25.00 = 0.15\ \text{mm}$.

$$
L(25.15) \;=\; k\,(x - T)^{2} \;=\; 800 \times (0.15)^{2} \;=\; 800 \times 0.0225 \;=\; \text{₹}\, 18.00
$$

So each shaft produced at 25.15 mm imposes a loss of **₹ 18** to society.

**(iii) S/N ratio for the three replicates [3 marks]:**

Step 1 — Sample mean:

$$
\mu \;=\; \dfrac{25.05 + 25.10 + 25.00}{3} \;=\; \dfrac{75.15}{3} \;=\; 25.05\ \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Sample variance (using $n - 1 = 2$ in the denominator):

$$
\sigma^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[(25.05 - 25.05)^{2} + (25.10 - 25.05)^{2} + (25.00 - 25.05)^{2}\right]
$$

$$
\sigma^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[0 + 0.0025 + 0.0025\right] \;=\; \dfrac{0.0050}{2} \;=\; 0.0025\ \text{mm}^{2}
$$

Step 3 — S/N ratio:

$$
\eta_{NTB} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{25.05^{2}}{0.0025}\right) \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{627.5025}{0.0025}\right) \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}(251\,001) \;\approx\; 10 \times 5.3997 \;\approx\; 54.00\ \text{dB}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $k = \text{₹}\, 800 / \text{mm}^{2}$
- (ii) $L(25.15) = \text{₹}\, 18.00$
- (iii) $\eta_{NTB} \approx 54.00\ \text{dB}$

> **Mark Allocation Hint:** 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii). The $\sigma^{2}$ computation is the trickiest — show it step by step to earn full marks.

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
*Define an **Orthogonal Array (OA)**. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **inner-array and outer-array concept** of Taguchi's crossed-array design. Why is the Taguchi crossed array more efficient than a full factorial design?*

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
*A grinding process has two **control factors**, each at **two levels**. The performance characteristic is **"smaller-the-better"** (surface roughness in $\mu m$). The following $L_{4}$ orthogonal array was used, with three replicates per run:*

| Run | A (Feed mm/min) | B (Depth of Cut mm) | $y_{1}$ | $y_{2}$ | $y_{3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 (50) | 1 (0.2) | 0.60 | 0.62 | 0.61 |
| 2 | 1 (50) | 2 (0.4) | 0.80 | 0.82 | 0.78 |
| 3 | 2 (80) | 1 (0.2) | 0.70 | 0.72 | 0.71 |
| 4 | 2 (80) | 2 (0.4) | 0.90 | 0.88 | 0.92 |

*Compute the **S/N ratio for each run** (smaller-the-better), determine the **optimal factor levels**, and **predict the optimum S/N ratio**.*

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Orthogonal Array (2 marks):**

An **Orthogonal Array (OA)** is a pre-defined experimental design matrix, denoted $L_{N}$, in which:
- $N$ = number of experimental runs
- Each column represents a **factor** or **interaction**
- The levels of any factor appear with **equal frequency** in every column
- The level **combinations** of any two columns appear with **equal frequency** for all level pairs

This property of **orthogonality** ensures that the main effect of each factor can be estimated **independently** of all other factors.

**Inner-Outer Array Concept (3 marks):**

Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.2** for a visual representation.

- The **Inner Array** is an orthogonal array in which the columns represent the **control factors** (parameters we can adjust).
- The **Outer Array** is a second orthogonal array in which the columns represent the **noise factors** (uncontrollable conditions).
- For each row of the inner array, *every* row of the outer array is run. The number of outer-array rows for a given inner-array row is the number of **noise replicates**.

**Why is the crossed array more efficient? (2 marks):**

For $k$ control factors at 2 levels and $m$ noise factors at 2 levels:
- **Full factorial:** $2^{k} \times 2^{m} = 2^{k+m}$ runs
- **Taguchi crossed array:** $2^{k} \times 2^{m}$ runs (formally the same, but Taguchi uses much smaller *fractional* arrays like $L_{4}, L_{8}, L_{9}$ that confounded only the higher-order interactions, achieving a 50–80% reduction in runs)

For example, with $k = 4$ control factors and $m = 2$ noise factors at 2 levels:
- Full factorial: $2^{6} = 64$ runs
- Taguchi $L_{8}$ inner $\times$ $L_{4}$ outer: $8 \times 4 = 32$ runs (**50% reduction**)

The savings grow dramatically as the number of factors increases. Taguchi's efficiency comes from the **orthogonality** of the arrays and the **fractional replication** of the noise factor combinations.

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given data:**

| Run | A | B | $y_{1}$ | $y_{2}$ | $y_{3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.60 | 0.62 | 0.61 |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | 0.80 | 0.82 | 0.78 |
| 3 | 2 | 1 | 0.70 | 0.72 | 0.71 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | 0.90 | 0.88 | 0.92 |

**Step 1 — S/N ratio for each run (Smaller-the-Better) [3 marks]:**

Formula:

$$
\eta_{STB} \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{y_{1}^{2} + y_{2}^{2} + y_{3}^{2}}{3}\right]
$$

**Run 1:**

$$
\eta_{1} \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.60^{2} + 0.62^{2} + 0.61^{2}}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.3600 + 0.3844 + 0.3721}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}(0.3722)
$$

$$
\eta_{1} \;=\; -10 \times (-0.4293) \;=\; +4.29\ \text{dB}
$$

**Run 2:**

$$
\eta_{2} \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.80^{2} + 0.82^{2} + 0.78^{2}}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.6400 + 0.6724 + 0.6084}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}(0.6403)
$$

$$
\eta_{2} \;=\; -10 \times (-0.1935) \;=\; +1.93\ \text{dB}
$$

**Run 3:**

$$
\eta_{3} \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.70^{2} + 0.72^{2} + 0.71^{2}}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.4900 + 0.5184 + 0.5041}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}(0.5042)
$$

$$
\eta_{3} \;=\; -10 \times (-0.2974) \;=\; +2.97\ \text{dB}
$$

**Run 4:**

$$
\eta_{4} \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.90^{2} + 0.88^{2} + 0.92^{2}}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{0.8100 + 0.7744 + 0.8464}{3}\right] \;=\; -10\,\log_{10}(0.8103)
$$

$$
\eta_{4} \;=\; -10 \times (-0.0914) \;=\; +0.91\ \text{dB}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Run | A | B | $\eta$ (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **+4.29** |
| 2 | 1 | 2 | **+1.93** |
| 3 | 2 | 1 | **+2.97** |
| 4 | 2 | 2 | **+0.91** |

**Step 2 — Average S/N per level [2 marks]:**

| Factor | Level 1 | Level 2 | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | $(4.29 + 1.93)/2 = 3.11$ dB | $(2.97 + 0.91)/2 = 1.94$ dB | **A1** |
| B | $(4.29 + 2.97)/2 = 3.63$ dB | $(1.93 + 0.91)/2 = 1.42$ dB | **B1** |

**Step 3 — Optimal factor levels [1 mark]:**

The optimal combination is **A1B1** (Feed = 50 mm/min, Depth of Cut = 0.2 mm) because both factors have their higher S/N ratio at Level 1.

**Step 4 — Predict the optimum S/N [1 mark]:**

Grand mean:

$$
\bar{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{4.29 + 1.93 + 2.97 + 0.91}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{10.10}{4} \;=\; 2.525\ \text{dB}
$$

Predicted optimum:

$$
\eta_{\text{opt}} \;=\; \bar{\eta}_{A1} \;+\; \bar{\eta}_{B1} \;-\; \bar{\eta} \;=\; 3.11 \;+\; 3.63 \;-\; 2.525 \;=\; 4.215\ \text{dB}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The predicted optimum S/N of **4.22 dB** means that the optimal process is **~ 2.5× more robust** (a 2.5 dB improvement) than the worst-performing combination (A2B2 = 0.91 dB). In manufacturing terms, the optimal settings will yield **less variable surface roughness** under typical operating noise.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

Use this **high-density rapid-revision checklist** during the last 30 minutes before your KTU ESE exam.

- [ ] **Taguchi Method** = a robust-design philosophy that minimizes **quality loss to society** by selecting control-factor levels that make the product/process insensitive to noise.
- [ ] **Quality Loss Function (QLF):** $L(x) = k(x - T)^{2}$, where $k = A_{0} / \Delta_{0}^{2}$. Continuous loss, not step loss at specification limits.
- [ ] **S/N Ratio Unit:** Always in **decibels (dB)**. Always state the unit to avoid a 0.5-mark deduction.
- [ ] **Three S/N Ratio Categories:**
  - STB: $\eta = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_{i}^{2}\right]$
  - LTB: $\eta = -10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_{i}^{2}}\right]$
  - NTB: $\eta = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{\mu^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right]$
- [ ] **Higher S/N ratio = Better performance**, regardless of STB/LTB/NTB category.
- [ ] **Orthogonal Array (OA):** $L_{N}$ where $N$ = number of runs. Choose the smallest $N$ satisfying $N \geq 1 + \sum(L_{i} - 1)$.
- [ ] **Inner Array = Control Factors** (mnemonic: *"I-Control"*). **Outer Array = Noise Factors** (mnemonic: *"O-Noise"*).
- [ ] **Total experiments** = (rows of inner array) $\times$ (rows of outer array).
- [ ] **Eight-Step Taguchi Algorithm:** Define response $\rightarrow$ Identify factors $\rightarrow$ Select OA $\rightarrow$ Run experiments $\rightarrow$ Compute S/N $\rightarrow$ Average S/N per level $\rightarrow$ Identify optimum $\rightarrow$ Confirm.
- [ ] **Optimum prediction formula:** $\eta_{\text{opt}} = \bar{\eta}_{\text{factor 1 opt}} + \bar{\eta}_{\text{factor 2 opt}} + \cdots - (k - 1)\,\bar{\eta}_{\text{grand}}$, where $k$ is the number of factors.
- [ ] **Confirmation experiment is mandatory** — predicted S/N must be experimentally verified (within $\pm 0.5$ dB).
- [ ] **Degrees of Freedom (DoF) Rule:** $N_{\min} \geq 1 + \sum (L_{i} - 1)$. **Memorize** this — it is a 1-mark question by itself.
- [ ] **Common Pitfall:** Sample variance uses $n - 1$ in the denominator; the S/N ratio for STB and LTB uses the **mean square** which has $n$ in the denominator. Be consistent.
- [ ] **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting the **negative sign** in the STB and LTB formulas. Without it, a "good" result (small sum of squares) would give a *negative* S/N — a sign error.
- [ ] **Engineering Tie-in:** Taguchi Method is widely used in **automotive (welding, paint), electronics (PCB etching, resistor trimming), pharmaceutical (tablet dissolution), and software (defect-density optimization)** — be ready with at least one real-world example for full marks.
- [ ] **Taguchi vs Full Factorial:** Taguchi uses **fractional** crossed arrays and a single S/N metric, achieving 50–80% experimental savings for the same information content (at the cost of confounding higher-order interactions).
- [ ] **AKTU 2024 Specific:** The Module 4 question paper typically tests **(i) definition of QLF (3 marks), (ii) S/N formulas with industrial example (3 marks), and (iii) a 14-mark numerical on a small $L_{4}$ or $L_{8}$ array with all three sub-steps (compute S/N, identify optimum, predict optimum S/N).** Practice all three sub-steps for the 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Six Sigma (Module 4 Anchor Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Six Sigma is a rigorous, data-driven methodology and statistical quality philosophy that seeks to eliminate defects in any process by understanding and managing process variation. The term "Six Sigma" refers to a process capability where the specification limits are set at ±**6 standard deviations (σ)** from the process mean, resulting in a defect rate of no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.*

### 1.2 Origin and Evolution

The concept was developed by **Motorola** in the mid-1980s under the leadership of **Bill Smith** and **Bob Galvin**. Later popularized by **General Electric (Jack Welch)** in the 1990s, it became a globally adopted framework. KTU 2024 emphasizes Six Sigma as a structured problem-solving methodology rather than just a statistical metric.

| Era | Milestone | Key Contributor |
|---|---|---|
| 1980s | Birth of Six Sigma at Motorola | Bill Smith, Bob Galvin |
| 1990s | GE adoption and "Six Sigma Quality" branding | Jack Welch |
| 2000s | Integration with **Lean** (Lean Six Sigma) | Mixed industry adoption |
| 2020s | Digital Six Sigma with AI/ML analytics | Industry 4.0 frameworks |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Shop" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Six Sigma Coffee Shop**
>
> Imagine a coffee shop that promises to deliver every coffee order at **exactly 60°C** (the perfect drinking temperature). The acceptable range is **55°C to 65°C** (specification limits).
>
> - **1 Sigma Shop:** The barista is so inconsistent that on most days, customers get coffee that is either too cold or too hot. Defects are common.
> - **3 Sigma Shop:** Better, but the temperature fluctuates outside the acceptable range frequently.
> - **6 Sigma Shop:** The process is so tightly controlled that out of **1 million cups served**, only **3.4 cups** are outside the perfect temperature range.
>
> **The Concept:** Six Sigma is the discipline of *controlling variation* so tightly that *defects become virtually impossible* — not by working harder, but by **redesigning and stabilizing the process**.

### 1.4 Why "Six Sigma" Specifically?

The Greek letter **σ (sigma)** represents **standard deviation**, the most fundamental measure of variation in statistics. The number **6** represents the number of standard deviations that fit between the process mean and the nearest specification limit. A higher sigma level means **lower variability** and **higher quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Quality Metrics (KTU Must-Know Terms):**
> - **DPU** — Defects Per Unit
> - **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities (**3.4 at Six Sigma**)
> - **RTY** — Rolled Throughput Yield
> - **COPQ** — Cost of Poor Quality
> - **σ Level** — Process capability index expressed in standard deviations
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Six Sigma

Six Sigma operates on **two complementary pillars**:

### Pillar 1: The Statistical (Metric) Pillar
This treats Six Sigma as a **measurement scale** for process quality. A process operating at Six Sigma has **zero-defect tolerance** within a 12σ spread (6σ on each side of the mean).

### Pillar 2: The Methodological (DMAIC) Pillar
This treats Six Sigma as a **project-driven problem-solving roadmap**. Every Six Sigma project follows the **DMAIC** structure.

## 2.2 The DMAIC Framework — The Heart of Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DMAIC** is the universally mandated project execution roadmap for KTU 2024 Module 4.

| Phase | Full Name | Core Activity | Key Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | **Define** | Identify the problem, customer (CTQ), and project scope | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| **M** | **Measure** | Quantify the current process performance | Data collection, MSA, Capability analysis |
| **A** | **Analyze** | Identify the root causes of variation/defects | Hypothesis testing, Regression, Ishikawa |
| **I** | **Improve** | Implement solutions to address root causes | DOE, Pilot runs, Kaizen |
| **C** | **Control** | Standardize and sustain the improvements | Control charts, FMEA, Poka-Yoke |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for ESE)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board Exam Favorites. Memorize the units and logical meaning of each.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DPU = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units}}$ | Defects Per Unit | Defects/Unit |
| 2 | $DPO = \dfrac{DPU}{\text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | Defects Per Opportunity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $DPMO = DPO \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Defects/Million |
| 4 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - DPO$ | First-Pass Yield (per stage) | Fraction or % |
| 5 | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (n stages) | Fraction or % |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Z-score (Standard Normal Distance) | σ-units |
| 7 | $\text{Process Capability} = 6\sigma$ | Total Process Spread (ideal) | Same as characteristic |
| 8 | $COPQ = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Cost of Poor Quality | ₹ / $ / € |

## 2.4 The Sigma Level Conversion (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The σ level is *not* simply Z. The σ level accounts for the **1.5σ shift** that Motorola engineers observed in long-term process behavior.

$$\text{Long-term } \sigma \text{ Level} = Z_{\text{short-term}} + 1.5$$

This is why **6σ corresponds to 4.5σ** in a 1-sided tail from the mean on a long-term basis, yielding **3.4 DPMO**.

### Sigma Level Reference Table (Memorize for KTU)

| σ Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2σ** | **308,537** | **69.1%** | Industry average (uncontrolled) |
| **3σ** | **66,807** | **93.3%** | Typical manufacturing baseline |
| **4σ** | **6,210** | **99.38%** | Good but not world-class |
| **5σ** | **233** | **99.977%** | Excellent (Lean Six Sigma target) |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | World-class (Six Sigma goal) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Reduces semiconductor defects, automotive recalls, and aerospace component failures.
- **Service Industry:** Banking (transaction errors), Healthcare (medication errors), IT (software bug reduction).
- **Software Engineering:** Adapted as **CMMI Level 5** equivalent in quality.
- **KTU 2024 Relevance:** B.Tech students encounter Six Sigma in **Industrial Engineering, Production, and Quality Management** courses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: DPMO and Process Yield

### Step 1: Understanding the "Opportunity"

> A single unit can have **multiple opportunities** for a defect.
>
> Example: A printed circuit board (PCB) has **100 solder joints**. Each solder joint is **one opportunity** for a defect. A PCB with **3 defective solder joints** out of **500 PCBs produced** must account for all 100 opportunities per unit.

### Step 2: Derive DPU

$$DPU = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects Observed}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$$

*Textual logic:* DPU is the average number of defects per single unit, regardless of how many "chances" for defects that unit had.

### Step 3: Derive DPO

$$DPO = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} = \dfrac{DPU}{\text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$

*Textual logic:* DPO normalizes the defects by the total opportunities, giving the probability of a defect on a single opportunity.

### Step 4: Derive DPMO

$$DPMO = DPO \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

*Textual logic:* Multiplying by 1,000,000 scales the probability to "per million opportunities" — the standard Six Sigma reporting metric.

### Step 5: Derive First-Pass Yield (per stage)

$$Y = 1 - DPO$$

*Textual logic:* If 2% of opportunities produce a defect, then 98% produce a "good" result at that stage.

### Step 6: Derive Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

$$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \dots \times Y_n$$

*Textual logic:* RTY is the probability of a unit being defect-free through *all* n sequential stages. This is the "true" yield accounting for the compounding effect of stage-level defects.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU ESE Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model Question):**
> *A PCB assembly line has **4 sequential stages**: Soldering, Cleaning, Testing, and Packaging. The number of opportunities for defects at each stage is **100, 50, 80, and 20** respectively. The defects recorded at each stage are: 1200, 400, 800, and 100 respectively. **Total units processed = 1000.** Calculate:*
> *(i) DPU, DPO, DPMO at each stage;*
> *(ii) First-Pass Yield at each stage;*
> *(iii) Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY);*
> *(iv) The σ level of the process.*

### Stage 1: Soldering (100 opps/unit, 1200 defects, 1000 units)

$$DPU_1 = \frac{1200}{1000} = 1.2 \text{ defects/unit}$$

$$DPO_1 = \frac{1200}{1000 \times 100} = \frac{1200}{100{,}000} = 0.012$$

$$DPMO_1 = 0.012 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 12{,}000$$

$$Y_1 = 1 - 0.012 = 0.988 \text{ (98.8\%)}$$

### Stage 2: Cleaning (50 opps/unit, 400 defects, 1000 units)

$$DPU_2 = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4 \text{ defects/unit}$$

$$DPO_2 = \frac{400}{1000 \times 50} = \frac{400}{50{,}000} = 0.008$$

$$DPMO_2 = 0.008 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 8{,}000$$

$$Y_2 = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992 \text{ (99.2\%)}$$

### Stage 3: Testing (80 opps/unit, 800 defects, 1000 units)

$$DPU_3 = \frac{800}{1000} = 0.8 \text{ defects/unit}$$

$$DPO_3 = \frac{800}{1000 \times 80} = \frac{800}{80{,}000} = 0.010$$

$$DPMO_3 = 0.010 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 10{,}000$$

$$Y_3 = 1 - 0.010 = 0.990 \text{ (99.0\%)}$$

### Stage 4: Packaging (20 opps/unit, 100 defects, 1000 units)

$$DPU_4 = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1 \text{ defects/unit}$$

$$DPO_4 = \frac{100}{1000 \times 20} = \frac{100}{20{,}000} = 0.005$$

$$DPMO_4 = 0.005 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 5{,}000$$

$$Y_4 = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 \text{ (99.5\%)}$$

### Final RTY and Overall DPMO

$$RTY = 0.988 \times 0.992 \times 0.990 \times 0.995$$

$$RTY = 0.988 \times 0.992 = 0.980096$$

$$RTY = 0.980096 \times 0.990 = 0.970295$$

$$RTY = 0.970295 \times 0.995 = 0.965443 \text{ (96.54\%)}$$

*Interpretation:* Out of 1000 PCBs, only **965.4 units** (on average) make it through all 4 stages defect-free.

### Overall DPMO (Aggregate Metric)

$$\text{Total Defects} = 1200 + 400 + 800 + 100 = 2500$$

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = 1000 \times (100 + 50 + 80 + 20) = 1000 \times 250 = 250{,}000$$

$$DPMO_{\text{overall}} = \frac{2500}{250{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 10{,}000$$

### Sigma Level Determination

Using the KTU reference table, a DPMO of **10,000** corresponds approximately to a **3.9σ** level — between 3σ and 4σ, indicating the process needs significant improvement (target: 5σ+).

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix (Stage-wise)

| Stage | Defects | Opps/Unit | Total Opps | DPO | DPMO | Yield | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soldering | 1200 | 100 | 100,000 | 0.012 | **12,000** | 98.8% | Worst stage — needs root cause analysis |
| Cleaning | 400 | 50 | 50,000 | 0.008 | **8,000** | 99.2% | Acceptable |
| Testing | 800 | 80 | 80,000 | 0.010 | **10,000** | 99.0% | High rework area |
| Packaging | 100 | 20 | 20,000 | 0.005 | **5,000** | 99.5% | Best stage |
| **Total** | **2500** | **250** | **250,000** | **0.010** | **10,000** | **96.54% (RTY)** | Process ≈ 3.9σ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DMAIC Flowchart — Six Sigma Project Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Customer Voice / Problem Statement] --> B[DEFINE Phase]
    B --> B1[Project Charter]
    B --> B2[SIPOC Diagram]
    B --> B3[CTQ Tree / VOC]
    B1 --> C[MEASURE Phase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Data Collection Plan]
    C --> C2[Measurement System Analysis MSA]
    C --> C3[Baseline DPMO Calculation]
    C1 --> D[ANALYZE Phase]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Ishikawa Fishbone]
    D --> D2[Hypothesis Testing]
    D --> D3[Regression / Pareto]
    D1 --> E[IMPROVE Phase]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Design of Experiments DOE]
    E --> E2[Pilot Run / Kaizen]
    E --> E3[Solution Validation]
    E1 --> F[CONTROL Phase]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Control Charts]
    F --> F2[Updated FMEA]
    F --> F3[Standard Operating Procedures]
    F1 --> G[Project Closure and Handover]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[End: Sustained Improvement]
```

## 4.2 Six Sigma Concept — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[INPUT: Raw Materials / Customer Demand] --> P2[PROCESS: Under Variation sigma]
    P2 --> P3{MONITOR: Statistical Process Control SPC}
    P3 --> P4[OUTPUT: Products / Services]
    P4 --> P5{COMPARISON with USL and LSL}
    P5 -->|Within Limits| P6[Good Unit Yield Y]
    P5 -->|Outside Limits| P7[Defect: DPU / DPMO counted]
    P7 --> P8[FEEDBACK: DMAIC Root Cause Loop]
    P8 --> P2
    P6 --> P9[Final Rolled Throughput Yield RTY]
    P9 --> P10[Sigma Level Determination]
    P10 -->|sigma less than 6| P8
    P10 -->|sigma equals 6| P11[World Class: 3.4 DPMO]
```

## 4.3 DPMO-to-Sigma Conversion Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Defect Data] --> R2[Compute DPU]
    R2 --> R3[Compute DPO]
    R3 --> R4[Compute DPMO]
    R4 --> R5{Lookup in Sigma Table}
    R5 --> R6[Sigma Level 6 = 3.4 DPMO]
    R5 --> R7[Sigma Level 5 = 233 DPMO]
    R5 --> R8[Sigma Level 4 = 6210 DPMO]
    R5 --> R9[Sigma Level 3 = 66807 DPMO]
    R6 --> R10[Apply Long Term 1.5 sigma Shift]
    R7 --> R10
    R8 --> R10
    R9 --> R10
    R10 --> R11[Final Process Capability Statement]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "Six Sigma." What does "3.4 DPMO" signify in the context of Six Sigma quality?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Six Sigma is a data-driven quality philosophy and methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It achieves this by ensuring that the process variation is so small that the specification limits lie at **±6σ** from the process mean.
>
> The figure **3.4 DPMO** represents the maximum acceptable defect rate at the world-class Six Sigma level, accounting for the **1.5σ long-term drift** observed in real processes. It signifies near-perfect quality where only **3.4 units out of one million** fail to meet specifications. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between DPU and DPMO with one numerical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **DPU (Defects Per Unit)** is the average number of defects per single unit, computed as Total Defects divided by Total Units. Example: 50 defects in 100 units → DPU = 0.5.
> - **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** normalizes defects to a per-million-opportunity basis, computed as DPO × 1,000,000. Example: 50 defects across 100 units with 5 opportunities each → DPO = 0.1, DPMO = 100,000.
>
> DPU ignores opportunity count; DPMO accounts for process complexity. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Provided

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **DMAIC methodology** in detail, listing the tools used in each phase. What is the role of the **Black Belt** in a Six Sigma project? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process has **3 stages**. The DPO values are **0.05, 0.02, and 0.03** respectively. Calculate the **First-Pass Yield at each stage**, the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**, and determine the **process sigma level** based on the overall DPMO. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — DMAIC Methodology (7 marks):**

| Phase | Activities | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Define (1.5 marks)** | Identify problem, customer (CTQ), project scope, and goals | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree |
| **Measure (1.5 marks)** | Quantify current process performance; collect baseline data | Data Plan, MSA, Process Mapping, Capability Indices |
| **Analyze (1.5 marks)** | Identify root causes of variation and defects | Ishikawa, 5-Whys, Pareto, Hypothesis Testing, Regression |
| **Improve (1.5 marks)** | Generate, test, and implement solutions | DOE, Pilot, Kaizen, FMEA, Poka-Yoke |
| **Control (1.0 mark)** | Sustain improvements through standardization | Control Charts, SOPs, Visual Management |

**Role of Black Belt (Bonus knowledge, +1 mark if mentioned):**
A Black Belt is a full-time Six Sigma project leader who is trained in advanced statistical tools. They lead DMAIC projects, mentor Green Belts, and report progress to the Master Black Belt/Champion. **(1 mark for Black Belt role)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Stage 1:**
$$Y_1 = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 \text{ (95\%)}$$

**Stage 2:**
$$Y_2 = 1 - 0.02 = 0.98 \text{ (98\%)}$$

**Stage 3:**
$$Y_3 = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97 \text{ (97\%)}$$

**Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY):**
$$RTY = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.97$$

$$RTY = 0.931 \times 0.97 = 0.9031 \text{ (90.31\%)}$$

**Overall DPMO Calculation:**
$$DPO_{\text{overall}} = 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.03 = 0.10 \text{ (assumed aggregate)}$$

$$DPMO = 0.10 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 100{,}000$$

**Sigma Level Determination:**
From the KTU reference table, DPMO of **100,000** corresponds to a process operating at approximately **2.9σ to 3σ level** (between industry average and good practice). The process requires major Six Sigma intervention to reach world-class quality. **(7 marks distributed across 7 logical steps above)**

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** What is **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**? Classify and explain its four categories with suitable engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital records **2,500 patients** per month. Each patient record has **8 critical data fields** (opportunities). Monthly audits reveal **120 defective entries**. Calculate DPU, DPO, DPMO, and the σ level. If the hospital wants to reach **5σ quality**, how many defects can it tolerate per month? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — COPQ Classification (7 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** COPQ is the total cost incurred by an organization due to defects, errors, and inefficiencies — i.e., all costs that would *disappear* if the process were perfect.

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Failure Costs (2 marks)** | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, Scrap, Re-inspection, Material wastage in a CNC shop |
| **External Failure Costs (2 marks)** | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty claims, Product recalls, Customer complaints, Lawsuits |
| **Appraisal Costs (1 mark)** | Costs of *detecting* defects | Inspection, Testing labs, Calibration, Quality audits |
| **Prevention Costs (1 mark)** | Costs of *preventing* defects | Training, Quality planning, Supplier capability programs, DOE |

**Part (b) — Hospital Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** 2,500 patients/month, 8 opportunities/patient, 120 defects.

**Step 1: DPU**
$$DPU = \frac{120}{2500} = 0.048 \text{ defects/patient}$$

**Step 2: DPO**
$$DPO = \frac{120}{2500 \times 8} = \frac{120}{20{,}000} = 0.006$$

**Step 3: DPMO**
$$DPMO = 0.006 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 6{,}000$$

**Step 4: Sigma Level**
From the KTU table, DPMO of **6,000** corresponds to approximately **4.05σ level**.

**Step 5: Defects Tolerable at 5σ (233 DPMO)**
$$\text{Max Defects} = \frac{233 \times (2500 \times 8)}{1{,}000{,}000} = \frac{233 \times 20{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{Max Defects} = \frac{4{,}660{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 4.66$$

$$\text{Max Defects} \approx 4 \text{ to } 5 \text{ defects per month}$$

**Conclusion:** To reach 5σ, the hospital must reduce defects from **120/month** to under **5/month** — a **96% reduction** requiring major process redesign.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing σ with Z:** The σ level ≠ the Z-score. Apply the **1.5σ shift** for long-term process behavior.
> 2. **Wrong denominator in DPMO:** DPMO requires **Opportunities per Unit**, not just Units. Missing this loses 2-3 marks immediately.
> 3. **Confusing Yield and RTY:** First-pass yield is per stage; RTY is the **product** of all stage yields.
> 4. **DMAIC sequence errors:** Writing phases out of order (e.g., Improve before Analyze) is treated as a conceptual error and loses **1-2 marks**.
> 5. **Missing units in numericals:** Always write DPMO, σ level, and Yield with their **units and percentage equivalents**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read Before Every KTU Exam!)**

- ✅ Six Sigma aims for **3.4 DPMO** (world-class) at **6σ capability**.
- ✅ The **1.5σ shift** is the bridge between short-term Z and long-term σ level.
- ✅ **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (in order!).
- ✅ **DPU** counts defects per unit; **DPMO** normalizes per million opportunities.
- ✅ **RTY** is the **product of stage yields**, not the sum or average.
- ✅ **COPQ** has 4 categories: Internal Failure, External Failure, Appraisal, Prevention.
- ✅ A **Black Belt** leads projects; **Green Belt** supports; **Master Black Belt** mentors.
- ✅ **6σ quality** is the goal, not a constraint — it represents near-perfection.
- ✅ Lean + Six Sigma = **Lean Six Sigma** (speed + quality).
- ✅ Higher σ level = exponentially lower DPMO (logarithmic improvement curve).
- ✅ Standard tools in **Analyze phase:** Ishikawa, Pareto, 5-Whys, Regression.
- ✅ Standard tools in **Control phase:** SPC Charts, FMEA, SOPs, Poka-Yoke.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always show **all 7 calculation steps** with units.
- ✅ Mermaid diagrams of DMAIC, COPQ, and DPMO flow are commonly drawn in **Module 4 long-answer questions**.
- ✅ **Key formula memory triggers:** DPU (defects/units), DPO (defects/total opps), DPMO (× 1,000,000), RTY (product of Yi).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of QFD and Case Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application of QFD and Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of QFD

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and product/service development methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — what the customer wants — into specific, measurable **technical/engineering characteristics** — how the organization will deliver it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Accepted Wording):**
> *"QFD is a systematic, team-based approach that uses a series of matrices (most notably the **House of Quality — HOQ**) to translate qualitative customer requirements into quantitative engineering targets, ensuring that the final design reflects the true priorities of the customer."*

QFD was originally developed in **1972** in Japan by **Dr. Akao Yoji** at the Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries and was first applied extensively by **Toyota** and its suppliers during the 1970s-80s. It is sometimes called the **"House of Quality"** methodology because its central matrix resembles the roof-and-walls structure of a house.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" Metaphor

Imagine you go to a tailor and say, *"I want a shirt that feels good, lasts long, and looks elegant."* The tailor must convert your fuzzy, emotional, qualitative wishes into:

- Thread count (80s cotton? 120s Egyptian?)
- Stitch density (stitches per inch)
- Color fastness rating
- Fabric GSM (grams per square meter)

The tailor essentially performs a **translation** from your "WHATs" (feel good, last long) to "HOWs" (thread count, GSM, stitch density). **QFD is exactly this translator — but for engineers designing a product, service, or process.** The "translator" is the **House of Quality matrix**, which captures the customer's voice, technical features, and competitive analysis all in one structured framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of QFD Philosophy:**
> 1. **Customer First** — Every technical decision is anchored to a real customer need.
> 2. **Cross-Functional Teamwork** — Marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality work together.
> 3. **Documented Reasoning** — Decisions are evidence-based, recorded in matrices, not in memory.

## 1.3 Applications of QFD in Industry

QFD is a **versatile tool** that extends well beyond traditional manufacturing:

| **Application Domain** | **Use of QFD** |
|---|---|
| Automotive Industry | Vehicle feature prioritization, supplier coordination |
| Software & IT | Mapping user requirements to software specifications |
| Healthcare | Patient satisfaction → hospital service standards |
| Education | Curriculum design based on stakeholder (student/employer) needs |
| Service Sector | Restaurant chains, banking — translating customer experience into SOPs |
| Construction | Green building design, client requirement to material specifications |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The four phases of QFD in product development are commonly referred to as the **"Four Houses"**:
> 1. **House of Quality** (Product Planning)
> 2. **House of Design** (Part Deployment)
> 3. **House of Process** (Process Planning)
> 4. **House of Production** (Production Planning)
>
> The first House is the most important for KTU exams — questions almost always focus on **HOQ**.

## 1.4 Why QFD Matters — The Strategic Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1-10-100 Rule (Why Early Translation Saves Money):**
> - A quality issue fixed at the **design stage** costs **$1**.
> - The same issue fixed at **production stage** costs **$10**.
> - The same issue fixed at **post-sale (warranty)** costs **$100**.
>
> QFD's biggest value is **front-loading quality** — catching mismatches between customer wants and engineering specs **before** a single bolt is turned.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Anatomy of the "House"

The HOQ is a single complex matrix made of **seven sub-matrices** arranged in a house shape. Each sub-matrix answers a different question.

| **Sub-Matrix (Room)** | **Location in House** | **What It Contains** | **What Question It Answers** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Customer Requirements (WHATs)** | Left Wall (vertical) | List of customer needs in their own language | *"What does the customer want?"* |
| **2. Planning Matrix** | Far Left (next to WHATs) | Importance rating, customer satisfaction, benchmark vs. competitors | *"How important is each need?"* |
| **3. Technical Requirements (HOWs)** | Ceiling (horizontal, top) | Measurable engineering characteristics | *"How will we measure it?"* |
| **4. Relationship Matrix (Central Room)** | Center of the house | Correlation strength between each WHAT and each HOW (symbols: ● = strong 9, ○ = medium 3, △ = weak 1, blank = 0) | *"How strongly does each technical feature affect each customer need?"* |
| **5. Technical Correlations (Roof)** | Triangular roof at the top | How the HOWs interact with each other (++ strong positive, + positive, – negative, – – strong negative, blank = none) | *"Do our technical features help or hurt each other?"* |
| **6. Technical Importance Ratings** | Basement (bottom) | Calculated weighted scores for each HOW | *"Which technical features matter most?"* |
| **7. Competitive / Technical Benchmarks** | Basement (right of #6) | Target values, units, current vs. competitor specs | *"What should our target spec be?"* |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — All QFD Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Formula Cheat Sheet — Memorize These:**

### Formula 1: Customer Importance Rating
Customer requirements are usually rated by customers on a **1 to 5 Likert scale**:
$$ I_j \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} $$
Where **5 = extremely important**, **1 = not at all important**.

### Formula 2: Raw Technical Importance (Absolute Weight)
For each technical requirement $i$, sum the products of its relationship strength with every customer requirement:

$$ W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \times I_j $$

Where:
- $W_i$ = raw (absolute) weight of the $i^{th}$ technical requirement
- $R_{ij}$ = relationship strength (commonly **9 = strong**, **3 = medium**, **1 = weak**, **0 = none**)
- $I_j$ = importance of the $j^{th}$ customer requirement
- $n$ = total number of customer requirements

### Formula 3: Normalized (Relative) Weight
$$ NW_i = \frac{W_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} W_i} \times 100 $$

Where $m$ = total number of technical requirements. The unit is **percentage (%)**.

### Formula 4: Sum of All Normalized Weights
$$ \sum_{i=1}^{m} NW_i = 100\% $$
This is a **mandatory check** — examiners often verify this sum in answers.

### Formula 5: Benchmark / Customer Satisfaction Gap
$$ \text{Gap}_j = C_j^{\text{target}} - C_j^{\text{current}} $$
Where $C_j$ is the customer satisfaction score (usually 1–5) for the $j^{th}$ requirement.

### Formula 6: Improved Importance (with Sales Point Weighting)
$$ II_j = I_j \times SP_j $$
Where $SP_j$ = sales point of the $j^{th}$ requirement (the % of customers who would buy the product *because* of this feature). Used in advanced HOQs.

### Formula 7: Improved Technical Importance (with Sales Points)
$$ W_i^{SP} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \times II_j = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \times I_j \times SP_j $$

### Formula 8: Target Value Justification (Direction Symbols)
| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Implication for Target** |
|---|---|---|
| ▲ | Higher is better | Maximize (e.g., brightness, speed) |
| ▼ | Lower is better | Minimize (e.g., weight, noise) |
| ◆ | Nominal / exact value | Hit target precisely (e.g., voltage = 230V) |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of HOQ

- **Marketing team** uses the left wall to confirm they're listening to the right customers.
- **Design engineering** uses the ceiling to set engineering specifications.
- **Quality assurance** uses the basement to set test priorities.
- **Manufacturing** uses the roof to detect feature conflicts early.
- **Procurement** uses the benchmark data to negotiate with suppliers.
- **Senior management** uses the normalized weights to allocate R&D budget.

> [!TIP]
> **Strategic Insight for KTU Answers:** Always mention in 14-mark answers that QFD is a **"living document"** — the HOQ is updated as customer preferences shift, technology evolves, and competitors launch new products. It is not a one-time exercise.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Building a House of Quality

1. **Identify the customer** (who is buying? — segmentation).
2. **Gather the Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — interviews, surveys, focus groups, complaint logs.
3. **Translate VOC into customer requirements (WHATs)** — in customer's own language; e.g., "easy to use" rather than "ergonomic grip angle of 12.4°".
4. **Rate customer importance** — typically on 1–5 scale from a customer survey.
5. **Benchmark competitors** — customer satisfaction ratings for our product and competitors.
6. **List technical requirements (HOWs)** — measurable, quantifiable engineering specs derived from the WHATs.
7. **Build the relationship matrix** — engineering team fills in ●, ○, △ symbols.
8. **Build the roof (technical correlations)** — identify positive/negative interactions.
9. **Calculate technical importance** — apply **Formula 2**.
10. **Normalize** — apply **Formula 3**.
11. **Set target values** — based on competitive benchmarks and importance.
12. **Document trade-offs** — the roof matrix shows which technical targets cannot be improved together.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study Setup — "Smart Fitness Band for Young Adults"

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Context:**
> A startup, **PulseTech Pvt. Ltd.**, wants to design a mid-range **smart fitness band** (price ₹2,500–4,000) for Indian young adults (age 18–30). The marketing team has surveyed 500 customers and identified **5 customer requirements (WHATs)**. The R&D team has identified **5 technical requirements (HOWs)**.

**Customer Requirements (WHATs) — from VOC survey:**

| **Code** | **Customer Need (in their own words)** |
|---|---|
| CR1 | Long battery life (doesn't need daily charging) |
| CR2 | Comfortable on wrist (can wear while sleeping) |
| CR3 | Accurate step and heart-rate tracking |
| CR4 | Affordable price |
| CR5 | Stylish look (modern, slim design) |

**Customer Importance Ratings $I_j$ (from 1–5 survey):**

| CR Code | Importance $I_j$ |
|---|---|
| CR1 | 5 |
| CR2 | 4 |
| CR3 | 5 |
| CR4 | 3 |
| CR5 | 3 |
| **Total** | **20** |

**Technical Requirements (HOWs):**

| **Code** | **Technical Requirement** | **Unit** | **Direction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| TR1 | Battery capacity | mAh | ▲ Higher is better |
| TR2 | Band weight | grams | ▼ Lower is better |
| TR3 | Sensor accuracy (step counter) | % deviation | ▼ Lower is better |
| TR4 | Manufacturing cost | ₹ | ▼ Lower is better |
| TR5 | Casing thickness | mm | ▼ Lower is better |

**Relationship Matrix $R_{ij}$ (filled by R&D team):**

| | **TR1 (mAh)** | **TR2 (g)** | **TR3 (% dev)** | **TR4 (₹)** | **TR5 (mm)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CR1 (Battery Life)** | ● = **9** | 0 | 0 | ○ = **3** | 0 |
| **CR2 (Comfort)** | 0 | ● = **9** | 0 | 0 | ○ = **3** |
| **CR3 (Accuracy)** | 0 | 0 | ● = **9** | 0 | 0 |
| **CR4 (Affordable)** | △ = **1** | △ = **1** | △ = **1** | ● = **9** | △ = **1** |
| **CR5 (Stylish)** | 0 | ○ = **3** | 0 | 0 | ● = **9** |

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Raw (Absolute) Technical Importance $W_i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Heart of QFD Calculation** — every KTU 14-mark question with an HOQ expects this step.

Using **Formula 2**: $\quad W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{5} R_{ij} \times I_j$

**Step 1: Calculate raw weight for TR1 (Battery capacity in mAh)**

$$W_1 = (R_{1,1} \times I_1) + (R_{1,2} \times I_2) + (R_{1,3} \times I_3) + (R_{1,4} \times I_4) + (R_{1,5} \times I_5)$$

$$W_1 = (9 \times 5) + (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (3 \times 3) + (0 \times 3)$$

$$W_1 = 45 + 0 + 0 + 9 + 0 = \mathbf{54}$$

**Step 2: Calculate raw weight for TR2 (Band weight in grams)**

$$W_2 = (0 \times 5) + (9 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (1 \times 3) + (3 \times 3)$$

$$W_2 = 0 + 36 + 0 + 3 + 9 = \mathbf{48}$$

**Step 3: Calculate raw weight for TR3 (Sensor accuracy)**

$$W_3 = (0 \times 5) + (0 \times 4) + (9 \times 5) + (1 \times 3) + (0 \times 3)$$

$$W_3 = 0 + 0 + 45 + 3 + 0 = \mathbf{48}$$

**Step 4: Calculate raw weight for TR4 (Manufacturing cost)**

$$W_4 = (1 \times 5) + (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 5) + (9 \times 3) + (1 \times 3)$$

$$W_4 = 5 + 4 + 5 + 27 + 3 = \mathbf{44}$$

**Step 5: Calculate raw weight for TR5 (Casing thickness)**

$$W_5 = (0 \times 5) + (3 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (1 \times 3) + (9 \times 3)$$

$$W_5 = 0 + 12 + 0 + 3 + 27 = \mathbf{42}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Normalized (Relative) Weight $NW_i$

Using **Formula 3**: $\quad NW_i = \dfrac{W_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} W_i} \times 100$

**Step 6: Sum the raw weights**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} W_i = 54 + 48 + 48 + 44 + 42 = \mathbf{236}$$

**Step 7: Compute each normalized weight**

$$NW_1 = \frac{54}{236} \times 100 = 22.88\%$$

$$NW_2 = \frac{48}{236} \times 100 = 20.34\%$$

$$NW_3 = \frac{48}{236} \times 100 = 20.34\%$$

$$NW_4 = \frac{44}{236} \times 100 = 18.64\%$$

$$NW_5 = \frac{42}{236} \times 100 = 17.80\%$$

**Step 8: Validation check (Formula 4)**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} NW_i = 22.88 + 20.34 + 20.34 + 18.64 + 17.80 = 100.00\% \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always perform this final sum check. If it does not equal 100% (allowing for ±0.05% rounding error), you have made an arithmetic mistake and **will lose 1 mark**.

## 3.4 Final Ranked Output — Engineering Priority for R&D Budget

Sorting in descending order of $NW_i$ gives the **R&D priority order**:

| **Rank** | **Technical Requirement** | **Raw Weight $W_i$** | **Normalized Weight $NW_i$** | **R&D Action** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TR1 — Battery capacity (mAh) | 54 | **22.88%** | Invest in higher-density Li-ion cells |
| 2 | TR2 — Band weight (g) | 48 | **20.34%** | Use lightweight polymer alloys |
| 3 | TR3 — Sensor accuracy (% dev) | 48 | **20.34%** | Source 9-axis MEMS sensors |
| 4 | TR4 — Manufacturing cost (₹) | 44 | **18.64%** | Negotiate with EMS suppliers in scale |
| 5 | TR5 — Casing thickness (mm) | 42 | **17.80%** | CAD redesign for ≤ 9 mm profile |

> [!TIP]
> **Management Interpretation:** Although CR4 (affordable price) has the *lowest customer importance rating (3/5)*, the technical requirement **TR4 (cost)** still scores 18.64% because it is a *fundamental enabler* of *every* customer need. This is the **hidden insight** QFD provides — it shows that "low cost" is a *cross-cutting requirement*, not a standalone feature.

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Technical Correlations (Roof Matrix) — Worked Walkthrough

The roof captures interactions **between** technical requirements. Suppose PulseTech's R&D completes the roof as follows:

| | **TR1** | **TR2** | **TR3** | **TR4** | **TR5** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TR1** | – | – | ++ | – – | – |
| **TR2** | | – | 0 | + | ++ |
| **TR3** | | | – | 0 | 0 |
| **TR4** | | | | – | + |
| **TR5** | | | | | – |

**Interpretation Table:**

| **Cell** | **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Design Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| TR1 vs TR3 | **++** (strong positive) | Higher battery capacity → better sensor power stability | Pursue both together — synergistic win |
| TR1 vs TR4 | **– –** (strong negative) | Bigger battery → more expensive | **Trade-off!** Either reduce mAh or accept higher cost |
| TR2 vs TR5 | **++** (strong positive) | Lighter weight correlates with thinner casing | Pursue both — they reinforce each other |
| TR3 vs TR4 | **0** (no relation) | Sensor accuracy is independent of cost | No trade-off |
| TR2 vs TR4 | **+** (mild positive) | Lighter band slightly increases cost (premium material) | Manageable trade-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The TR1–TR4 strong-negative correlation is the **single most important finding** of this QFD study. The cross-functional team must now make an executive decision: *Either* increase the retail price to absorb a higher mAh battery, *or* accept 2–3 days of battery life instead of 7. This is **decision-support in action** — exactly the value QFD adds over informal design discussions.

## 3.6 Summary Matrix — Final HOQ Snapshot (Numeric)

| **Customer Need** | **$I_j$** | **TR1** | **TR2** | **TR3** | **TR4** | **TR5** | **$W_i$** | **$NW_i$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 Battery life | 5 | 9 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 54 | **22.88%** |
| CR2 Comfortable | 4 | 0 | 9 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 48 | **20.34%** |
| CR3 Accurate | 5 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 48 | **20.34%** |
| CR4 Affordable | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 9 | 1 | 44 | **18.64%** |
| CR5 Stylish | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 9 | 42 | **17.80%** |
| **Total Weight $\sum W_i$** | | | | | | | **236** | **100.00%** |

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — House of Quality Block Architecture

The following diagram shows the **structural layout of the seven sub-matrices of the House of Quality** and the flow of information between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLANNING["PLANNING MATRIX - Far Left Wall"]
        IMPORT["Customer Importance Ratings 1 to 5"]
        BENCH["Competitive Benchmark 1 to 5"]

    end

    subgraph WHAT["CUSTOMER REQUIREMENTS - Left Wall - WHATs"]

        CR2["CR2 Comfortable Wrist"]
        CR3["CR3 Accurate Tracking"]
        CR4["CR4 Affordable Price"]
        CR5["CR5 Stylish Design"]
    end

    subgraph REL["RELATIONSHIP MATRIX - Center Room"]
        RELM["Strength Symbols Strong 9 Medium 3 Weak 1 None 0"]
    end

    subgraph HOW["TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS - Ceiling - HOWs"]
        TR1["TR1 Battery mAh"]
        TR2["TR2 Weight grams"]
        TR3["TR3 Sensor Accuracy"]
        TR4["TR4 Cost in Rupees"]
        TR5["TR5 Casing Thickness"]
    end

    subgraph ROOF["TECHNICAL CORRELATIONS - Triangular Roof"]
        ROOFM["Interactions plus plus plus minus minus blank None"]
    end

    subgraph BASE["TECHNICAL BASEMENT - Bottom Row"]
        WEIGHT["Raw Weight Wi"]
        NORMW["Normalized Weight NW in percent"]
        TARGET["Target Values with Direction Symbols"]
        UNIT["Engineering Units"]
    end

    IMPORT --> CR1
    IMPORT --> CR2
    IMPORT --> CR3
    IMPORT --> CR4
    IMPORT --> CR5

    BENCH --> CR1
    GAP --> CR1

    CR1 --> RELM
    CR2 --> RELM
    CR3 --> RELM
    CR4 --> RELM
    CR5 --> RELM

    RELM --> TR1
    RELM --> TR2
    RELM --> TR3
    RELM --> TR4
    RELM --> TR5

    TR1 --> ROOFM
    TR2 --> ROOFM
    TR3 --> ROOFM
    TR4 --> ROOFM
    TR5 --> ROOFM

    RELM --> WEIGHT
    WEIGHT --> NORMW
    NORMW --> TARGET
    TARGET --> UNIT
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — QFD Process Flow (4-Stage Deployment)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Stage 1\nVOICE OF CUSTOMER\nSurveys, Interviews,\nComplaints, Web Reviews"] --> B["Stage 2\nHOUSE OF QUALITY\nProduct Planning\nWHATs to HOWs"]
    B --> C["Stage 3\nHOUSE OF DESIGN\nPart Deployment\nCritical Parts to Specs"]
    C --> D["Stage 4\nHOUSE OF PROCESS\nProcess Planning\nParts to Process Steps"]
    D --> E["Stage 5\nHOUSE OF PRODUCTION\nProduction Planning\nProcess to Controls"]

    B --> B1["Output\nEngineering Targets\nand Priorities"]
    C --> C1["Output\nComponent Specifications\nand Tolerances"]
    D --> D1["Output\nProcess Flow and\nMachine Selection"]
    E --> E1["Output\nInspection Plans and\nWork Instructions"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Logic for Roof Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Two Technical Requirements show\nstrong negative correlation\nExample: TR1 vs TR4"] --> Q1["Is one requirement\nof HIGHER importance than\nthe other? Check NW percent"]
    Q1 -- "Yes - Higher NW wins" --> RES1["Allocate R and D budget\nto the higher weight feature\nCompromise on the lower weight"]
    Q1 -- "No - Roughly equal NW" --> Q2["Is one requirement\ntied to SAFETY or\nREGULATION? CRITICAL"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> RES2["Lock the safety feature\nAdjust the other to fit"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3["Can we DECOUPLE\nthe trade-off via\nnew technology?"]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> RES3["Invest in decoupling\ntechnology - Best outcome"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> RES4["Escalate to\nsteering committee\nfor business decision"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** In the exam, a labelled block diagram like the above (even hand-drawn) for the HOQ structure carries **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Examiners reward the *use* of visual aids. Always accompany HOQ calculations with a clearly drawn house-shaped figure.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Name the central tool used in QFD and list any FOUR sub-matrices of this tool."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, customer-driven planning methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific **technical/engineering requirements**, ensuring that the final product or service design reflects the priorities of the customer.
>
> **Central Tool (1 mark):** The central tool used in QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix that resembles the structure of a house, integrating customer needs, technical features, and competitive analysis.
>
> **Four Sub-Matrices (1 mark — list any 4):**
> 1. Customer Requirements (WHATs)
> 2. Technical Requirements (HOWs)
> 3. Relationship Matrix (Center of the house)
> 4. Technical Correlations (Roof)
> 5. Technical Importance Ratings (Basement)
> 6. Competitive Benchmarking (Planning matrix)
> 7. Target Values

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"What is the 'Voice of the Customer' (VOC) in QFD? State any THREE techniques used to gather VOC."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Voice of the Customer (VOC) refers to the **actual, expressed, and unexpressed needs, wants, expectations, and preferences** of the customer regarding a product or service. It is the raw qualitative input from which all customer requirements in the QFD are derived.
>
> **Three VOC Gathering Techniques (2 marks — 1 each):**
> 1. **Customer Surveys / Questionnaires** — structured questions with Likert-scale ratings (1–5) to assess importance.
> 2. **Focus Group Discussions** — moderated group interviews (8–12 participants) to explore opinions in depth.
> 3. **Customer Complaint Analysis** — mining warranty claims, service tickets, and online reviews.
> 4. **One-on-One Customer Interviews** — in-depth, semi-structured conversations.
> 5. **Observation / Ethnographic Studies** — watching customers use the product in real settings.
> 6. **Benchmarking against competitors** — reverse engineering competitor features.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain the concept of the **House of Quality (HOQ)** with a neat block diagram. List and briefly explain **all seven sub-matrices** of the HOQ. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company is developing a **domestic ceiling fan**. The marketing team has identified **5 customer requirements (CRs)** and the engineering team has identified **4 technical requirements (TRs)**. The customer importance ratings ($I_j$) and the relationship strengths ($R_{ij}$) are given in the following tables. Construct the House of Quality, calculate the **raw weight ($W_i$)** and the **normalized weight ($NW_i$)** for each technical requirement. Identify the **top two engineering priorities** and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Customer Requirements and Importance:**

| **CR Code** | **Customer Need** | **Importance $I_j$ (1–5)** |
|---|---|---|
| CR1 | Quiet operation | 5 |
| CR2 | Low power consumption | 4 |
| CR3 | Strong airflow | 5 |
| CR4 | Affordable price | 3 |
| CR5 | Attractive design | 2 |

**Relationship Matrix $R_{ij}$:**

| | **TR1: Motor Power (W)** | **TR2: Blade Angle (°)** | **TR3: Housing Material (cost ₹)** | **TR4: Noise Level (dB)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 | 0 | 3 | 0 | **9** |
| CR2 | **9** | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| CR3 | 0 | **9** | 0 | 1 |
| CR4 | 1 | 0 | **9** | 1 |
| CR5 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 0 |

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — HOQ Concept & Seven Sub-Matrices (7 marks)

> [!TIP]
> **HOQ Concept (2 marks):** The House of Quality is a **graphical and analytical matrix** developed by Akao Yoji (1972) and popularized by Hauser & Clausing (1988). It is the foundational matrix of the QFD methodology. The metaphor of a "house" is used because of its roof-and-walls layout. The matrix **simultaneously** displays: (1) what the customer wants, (2) how the company will deliver it, (3) how strongly they are linked, (4) how the technical features interact with each other, and (5) what competitors are doing. It is a *decision-support document* used by cross-functional teams during product planning.
>
> **Seven Sub-Matrices (5 marks — distribute 1 mark each for naming and explanation):**

| **#** | **Sub-Matrix** | **Location** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** | Left Wall | List of customer needs in the customer's own language, e.g., "fan should be quiet". |
| 2 | **Planning Matrix** | Far Left Column | Contains importance ratings, customer satisfaction scores, and competitive benchmark values. |
| 3 | **Technical Requirements (HOWs)** | Ceiling (top) | Measurable engineering characteristics, e.g., noise level in dB, motor power in W. |
| 4 | **Relationship Matrix** | Central Room | Cross-link between WHATs and HOWs with symbols: ● (strong = 9), ○ (medium = 3), △ (weak = 1). |
| 5 | **Technical Correlations (Roof)** | Triangular Roof | Shows how technical features interact with each other: ++ (strong positive), – – (strong negative), etc. |
| 6 | **Technical Importance Ratings** | Basement (left) | Calculated weighted scores of each technical feature — answers "what to focus on". |
| 7 | **Target Values / Units / Benchmarks** | Basement (right) | Engineering targets with direction symbols (▲ maximize, ▼ minimize, ◆ nominal) and units. |

**(Include a hand-drawn or printed house-shaped block diagram in the answer to earn full marks — 1 mark reserved for diagram.)**

#### Part (b) — HOQ Construction and Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply Formula 2 to calculate raw weights**

**For TR1 (Motor Power in W):**

$$W_1 = (0 \times 5) + (9 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (1 \times 3) + (0 \times 2)$$

$$W_1 = 0 + 36 + 0 + 3 + 0 = \mathbf{39}$$

**For TR2 (Blade Angle in degrees):**

$$W_2 = (3 \times 5) + (1 \times 4) + (9 \times 5) + (0 \times 3) + (3 \times 2)$$

$$W_2 = 15 + 4 + 45 + 0 + 6 = \mathbf{70}$$

**For TR3 (Housing Material cost in ₹):**

$$W_3 = (0 \times 5) + (3 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (9 \times 3) + (3 \times 2)$$

$$W_3 = 0 + 12 + 0 + 27 + 6 = \mathbf{45}$$

**For TR4 (Noise Level in dB):**

$$W_4 = (9 \times 5) + (0 \times 4) + (1 \times 5) + (1 \times 3) + (0 \times 2)$$

$$W_4 = 45 + 0 + 5 + 3 + 0 = \mathbf{53}$$

**Step 2: Sum the raw weights**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} W_i = 39 + 70 + 45 + 53 = \mathbf{207}$$

**Step 3: Apply Formula 3 to calculate normalized weights**

$$NW_1 = \frac{39}{207} \times 100 = \mathbf{18.84\%}$$

$$NW_2 = \frac{70}{207} \times 100 = \mathbf{33.82\%}$$

$$NW_3 = \frac{45}{207} \times 100 = \mathbf{21.74\%}$$

$$NW_4 = \frac{53}{207} \times 100 = \mathbf{25.60\%}$$

**Step 4: Validation**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} NW_i = 18.84 + 33.82 + 21.74 + 25.60 = 100.00\% \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5: Ranking & Top Two Engineering Priorities**

| **Rank** | **Technical Requirement** | **$W_i$** | **$NW_i$** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TR2 — Blade Angle** | 70 | **33.82%** | Highest weight; critical for both airflow (CR3) and noise (CR1). |
| 2 | **TR4 — Noise Level** | 53 | **25.60%** | Second highest; directly addresses the top-rated CR1 (Quiet operation). |

**Top Two Engineering Priorities (concluding statement, 1 mark):**
The R&D team should focus primarily on **optimizing the blade angle geometry** to balance airflow and noise, and secondarily on **reducing the noise level** through better motor mounts and aerodynamic blade design. Cost (TR3) and motor power (TR1) are secondary.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

### Question B (14 Marks)
**(a)** "QFD translates customer requirements into engineering specifications." Discuss this statement by explaining the **four phases (Four Houses)** of QFD, with a clear example of how a single customer need gets progressively translated into a manufacturing instruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital is using QFD to improve its **out-patient (OPD) service**. The marketing team has identified **4 patient requirements (PRs)** and the operations team has identified **4 service specifications (SSs)**. The importance and relationship data are given below. Calculate the **raw weight ($W_i$)** and **normalized weight ($NW_i$)** for each service specification. Also, suggest the **top two operational improvements** the hospital should make. **(7 marks)**

| **PR Code** | **Patient Need** | **$I_j$ (1–5)** |
|---|---|---|
| PR1 | Short waiting time | 5 |
| PR2 | Courteous staff behaviour | 4 |
| PR3 | Clear medical instructions at discharge | 5 |
| PR4 | Affordable consultation fee | 3 |

| | **SS1: Number of Doctors (per shift)** | **SS2: Staff Soft-Skill Training (hrs/month)** | **SS3: Discharge Counselling Time (min)** | **SS4: Consultation Fee (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PR1 | **9** | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| PR2 | 1 | **9** | 3 | 0 |
| PR3 | 0 | 3 | **9** | 0 |
| PR4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **9** |

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Four Phases of QFD (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **QFD Statement (1 mark):** Yes, QFD translates customer requirements (WHATs) into engineering specifications (HOWs), and the translation is **progressive, layered, and structured** across **four distinct phases** (often called the "Four Houses" of QFD).

**The Four Phases:**

| **Phase** | **House Name** | **Input (from previous house)** | **Output (translates into)** | **Example with Smartphone Battery** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **House of Quality** | Voice of Customer (VOC) — e.g., "Phone should last 2 days" | **Engineering Targets** — e.g., Battery capacity ≥ 5,000 mAh | "Long battery life" → "≥ 5,000 mAh Li-Po cell, 33W fast charge" |
| **2** | **House of Design** | Targets from HOQ | **Part / Component Specifications** — e.g., cell chemistry, BMS IC, connector | "5,000 mAh target" → "Lithium Polymer cell, 3.8V nominal, 4.45V max, BMS IC S-8261" |
| **3** | **House of Process** | Component specifications | **Process Plan** — assembly, welding, test, inspection steps | "BMS IC" → "SMT pick-and-place, reflow soldering at 245°C, AOI test, ICT test" |
| **4** | **House of Production** | Process plan | **Work Instructions and Controls** — operator-level SOPs | "SMT at 245°C" → "Operator SOP: set stencil printer to 0.1 mm, reflow profile stage 1: 150°C for 60s, stage 2: 245°C peak for 30s" |

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):** Thus, the single VOC statement *"phone should last 2 days"* cascades through four structured houses and ends as a precise, machine-level, operator-readable instruction. This is the **fundamental value proposition of QFD** — eliminating ambiguity from customer voice.

#### Part (b) — Hospital OPD HOQ Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Raw weight calculations (Formula 2)**

**SS1 (Number of Doctors per shift):**

$$W_1 = (9 \times 5) + (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (0 \times 3) = 45 + 4 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{49}$$

**SS2 (Staff Soft-Skill Training hours/month):**

$$W_2 = (1 \times 5) + (9 \times 4) + (3 \times 5) + (0 \times 3) = 5 + 36 + 15 + 0 = \mathbf{56}$$

**SS3 (Discharge Counselling Time in min):**

$$W_3 = (1 \times 5) + (3 \times 4) + (9 \times 5) + (0 \times 3) = 5 + 12 + 45 + 0 = \mathbf{62}$$

**SS4 (Consultation Fee in ₹):**

$$W_4 = (0 \times 5) + (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 5) + (9 \times 3) = 0 + 0 + 0 + 27 = \mathbf{27}$$

**Step 2: Sum of raw weights**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} W_i = 49 + 56 + 62 + 27 = \mathbf{194}$$

**Step 3: Normalized weights (Formula 3)**

$$NW_1 = \frac{49}{194} \times 100 = \mathbf{25.26\%}$$

$$NW_2 = \frac{56}{194} \times 100 = \mathbf{28.87\%}$$

$$NW_3 = \frac{62}{194} \times 100 = \mathbf{31.96\%}$$

$$NW_4 = \frac{27}{194} \times 100 = \mathbf{13.92\%}$$

**Step 4: Validation (sum = 100.01% ≈ 100% ✓)** — the 0.01% excess is rounding error, acceptable.

**Step 5: Top Two Operational Improvements**

| **Rank** | **Service Specification** | **$NW_i$** | **Operational Improvement** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SS3 — Discharge Counselling Time** | **31.96%** | Mandate a **minimum 5-minute discharge counselling** session for every patient, with a printed instruction leaflet and a follow-up SMS. |
| 2 | **SS2 — Staff Soft-Skill Training** | **28.87%** | Conduct **at least 8 hours/month of soft-skill training** for all front-line OPD staff, focusing on empathy, communication, and patient reassurance. |

**Insight (1 mark):** Even though PR1 (short waiting time) has the *highest individual importance rating (5)*, the cross-functional impact of **discharge counselling (SS3)** is the *highest weighted service specification* because it serves *two* high-importance patient needs (PR2 and PR3). This again demonstrates QFD's ability to reveal **hidden cross-cutting priorities**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QFD/HOS Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Wrong symbol-to-number mapping.** Examiners use ● = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1. Using 5, 3, 1 or any other scale will produce incorrect $W_i$ and will lose **2–3 marks** across the entire calculation chain.
> 2. **Forgetting the validation sum.** The normalized weights MUST sum to 100% (within ±0.05% rounding). If your sum is 99.50% or 100.50%, you have a calculation error. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for unverified totals.
> 3. **Confusing CRs with TRs.** Customer requirements (CRs) are in the customer's language; technical requirements (TRs) are in the engineer's language. Mixing them up means **0 marks** for the entire HOQ.
> 4. **Skipping the house-shaped diagram.** A 14-mark HOQ question without a diagram loses a **mandatory 1–2 marks** reserved for visual structure.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result.** Calculating $W_i$ and $NW_i$ is worth 4–5 marks, but the **last 2–3 marks** are reserved for **interpretation, ranking, and management recommendation**. Writing only the numbers without commenting on them is a major loss.
> 6. **Forgetting the direction symbols (▲ ▼ ◆).** In target value questions, examiners often ask "what should the target direction be?" Always specify whether the spec should be maximized, minimized, or held nominal.
> 7. **Using formulas without defining variables.** Always state what $W_i$, $R_{ij}$, $I_j$ represent *before* plugging in numbers. Examiners in KTU Kerala value **clarity of notation**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Application of QFD and Case Study**

- ✅ **QFD full form:** Quality Function Deployment. Originator: **Akao Yoji, Japan, 1972**. Core tool: **House of Quality (HOQ)**.
- ✅ **Purpose of QFD:** Translate **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **measurable engineering requirements** before product design is finalized.
- ✅ **Symbol Convention (Standard, KTU-Exam Accepted):** ● = **9** (strong), ○ = **3** (medium), △ = **1** (weak), blank = **0** (no relationship).
- ✅ **Three Core Formulas (must memorize):**
  1. Raw weight: $W_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{ij} \times I_j$
  2. Normalized weight: $NW_i = \dfrac{W_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} W_i} \times 100$
  3. Validation: $\sum_{i=1}^{m} NW_i = 100\%$
- ✅ **Seven sub-matrices of HOQ:** (1) Customer Requirements, (2) Planning Matrix, (3) Technical Requirements, (4) Relationship Matrix, (5) Technical Correlations (Roof), (6) Technical Importance Ratings, (7) Target Values.
- ✅ **Four Phases of QFD (Four Houses):** House of Quality → House of Design → House of Process → House of Production.
- ✅ **Direction Symbols in Targets:** ▲ maximize, ▼ minimize, ◆ nominal (exact).
- ✅ **Roof Symbols:** ++ strong positive, + positive, – – strong negative, – negative, blank = none. The roof reveals **trade-offs** between technical features.
- ✅ **1-10-100 Rule:** Fixing a quality issue costs $1 at design, $10 at production, $100 at post-sale — QFD catches issues at the **$1** stage.
- ✅ **Cross-cutting vs. Standalone TRs:** A TR with low individual importance but multiple weak (△) relationships across many CRs can still be a high-priority engineering target — always compute and rank, do not eyeball.
- ✅ **Validation Step:** Always perform the **100% sum check** for normalized weights.
- ✅ **Draw the House:** Always accompany HOQ answers with a **clearly labelled house-shaped diagram** — examiners allocate 1–2 marks purely for the visual.
- ✅ **Interpretation is mandatory:** A 14-mark HOQ calculation question typically awards 4–5 marks for calculation and **2–3 marks for interpretation / management recommendations** — never skip the conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Control - Its Introduction and Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Quality Control - Its Introduction and Benefits # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Quality Control (QC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):** > **Quality Control (QC)** is a systematic, planned set of operational techniques and activities employed at each stage of the production or service-delivery process to fulfil the requirements of quality as defined by design specifications and customer expectations. It is essentially the **operational, day-to-day, inspection-driven arm** of the broader Quality Management (QM) philosophy. In the context of KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework aligned with NEP 2020, Quality Control is classified as a **corrective and preventive operational discipline** that focuses on detecting defects in the finished product, identifying the causes of those defects, and removing the problems before the product reaches the customer. **Standardized Terminology Used by KTU Examiners:** | Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) | | :--- | :--- | | **Quality Control (QC)** | The operational techniques used to fulfil quality requirements | | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | The planned systematic activities implemented in a quality system to provide confidence that the product will satisfy quality requirements | | **Quality Management (QM)** | The comprehensive management system that integrates QA and QC across the organization | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):** > - **QC = Reactive / Detective** → "Find and fix the bad product" > - **QA = Proactive / Preventive** → "Build the system right so the bad product is never made" > - **QM = Strategic / Holistic** → "Lead the entire organization toward customer satisfaction" ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Bakery Analogy 🍞:** > Imagine you run a bakery.
