> - **Common mistake #3:** Computing only the binomial terms but forgetting to **comment** on the risk values — KTU board examiners *will* deduct 1-2 marks if there is no engineering interpretation.
> - **Common mistake #4:** For AOQL, using $N$ in the denominator when $n < 0.10 N$ (i.e., when binomial approximation is valid). The formula reduces to $p \times P_a$ in such cases.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of the **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve**. Sketch a typical OC curve and identify the AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk, and Consumer's Risk points on it.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** and **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** concepts. A lot of $N = 500$ items is submitted for inspection under a plan with $n = 30$ and $c = 1$. Rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced. If the incoming fraction defective is $p = 0.05$, and $P_a(0.05) = 0.80$, calculate the AOQ of the shipped material.

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a graph that shows the **probability of acceptance ($P_a$)** of a lot as a function of the **incoming lot quality (fraction defective, $p$)** for a given sampling plan. (2 marks)

**Sketch (described verbally, since markdown is text-based):**
- X-axis: Incoming fraction defective $p$ (from 0 to 1, or 0% to 100%)
- Y-axis: Probability of acceptance $P_a$ (from 0 to 1)
- Curve: Starts at $P_a = 1$ when $p = 0$, falls monotonically (S-shape) to $P_a = 0$ as $p \to 1$
- Point 1: AQL = $p_1$, where $P_a \approx 0.95$ → represents Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$
- Point 2: LTPD = $p_2$, where $P_a \approx 0.10$ → represents Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$
- Point 3: IQL = $p_{0.50}$ at $P_a = 0.5$ → Indifference Quality Level

(3 marks for sketch and labels)

**Interpretation (2 marks):** A *good* OC curve is one with a *steep* descent, which discriminates sharply between good and bad lots. A *poor* OC curve is flat, accepting nearly everything (or rejecting nearly everything) regardless of quality.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**AOQ Definition (2 marks):** Average Outgoing Quality is the *expected* fraction defective in the material shipped to the customer, *after* the inspection-and-replacement process. For a single sampling plan with rectifying inspection (100% check on rejects + replacement of defectives):

$$AOQ = p \cdot P_a(p)$$

**AOQL Definition (2 marks):** The Average Outgoing Quality Limit is the *maximum* value of the AOQ over all possible incoming quality levels $p$. Mathematically:

$$AOQL = \max_{p} \{p \cdot P_a(p)\}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**
Given $p = 0.05$, $P_a(0.05) = 0.80$:

$$AOQ = 0.05 \times 0.80 = 0.040 \text{ or } 4.0\%$$

This means the *shipped* material contains an expected 4.0% defectives, even though the *incoming* material was 5.0% defective — a net improvement of 1 percentage point due to the rectifying inspection on rejected lots.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> - **Common mistake #1:** Confusing $p$ and $P_a$ in the AOQ formula. The AOQ uses the *incoming* $p$ multiplied by the *probability of acceptance* of that $p$.
> - **Common mistake #2:** Forgetting that AOQ is for **rectifying** sampling plans only (those where rejected lots are 100% inspected). For non-rectifying plans, AOQ is undefined or different.
> - **Common mistake #3:** Reporting AOQ in % without showing the formula substitution. Always show $AOQ = p \times P_a$ explicitly for marks.
> - **Common mistake #4:** Calling AOQL the same as AQL. **AQL** is set by the *producer*; **AOQL** is a property of the *plan* after rectifying inspection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Acceptance Sampling**

- [x] **Acceptance Sampling** inspects a *sample*; **process control** (control charts) inspects the *process*. They are different.
- [x] Three numbers define a single plan: $(N, n, c)$ — **N** lot size, **n** sample size, **c** acceptance number.
- [x] Decision rule: $d \le c$ → **Accept**; $d > c$ → **Reject**.
- [x] **OC curve** is the signature of any sampling plan — always study its shape.
- [x] **AQL** ($p_1$) → good quality, $P_a \approx 0.95$, $\alpha \approx 0.05$.
- [x] **LTPD** ($p_2$) → bad quality, $P_a \approx 0.10$, $\beta \approx 0.10$.
- [x] **Binomial** OC equation: $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
- [x] **Poisson approximation** valid when $n \ge 20$ and $p \le 0.05$ — faster exam computation.
- [x] **AOQ** = $p \cdot P_a$ (rectifying plan); **AOQL** = peak of AOQ curve.
- [x] **ATI** = $n \cdot P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ — average inspection load per lot.
- [x] **Double plans** have lower ASN at extremes of quality; **single plans** are simpler administratively.
- [x] Sampling is used for **destructive tests**, **costly inspection**, and **supplier audit** — never as a substitute for poor process control.
- [x] Tightening the plan (smaller $c$, larger $n$) increases $\alpha$ but decreases $\beta$ — the **fundamental trade-off**.
- [x] Standards: **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4** (attributes), **Z1.9** (variables), **ISO 2859** (international).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling by Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sampling by Attributes # MODULE 4 — SAMPLING BY ATTRIBUTES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Sampling by Attributes** is a statistical acceptance sampling procedure in which each inspected item in a sample is classified into one of two mutually exclusive and exhaustive categories — **Conforming (Good)** or **Non-Conforming (Defective)** — based on a single quality characteristic evaluated against a specification limit. The decision to accept or reject the *entire lot* is made by comparing the number of defectives found in the sample to an *acceptance number* (c). The parameter being measured is **qualitative (discrete)** — it yields counts rather than continuous measurements. Common count metrics used in attribute sampling are: - **np** = number of defectives in the sample - **c** = acceptance number (max allowed defectives) - **p** = fraction defective (proportion non-conforming) - **d** = number of defectives observed in the sample ### 1.2 Attribute Data vs. Variable Data | Basis | Attribute Data | Variable Data | |---|---|---| | Nature | Qualitative (Go / No-Go) | Quantitative (measured) | | Output | Count of defectives | Numerical measurement | | Example | Bulb lights / doesn't | Filament resistance = 12.3 Ω | | Inspection | Fast, low skill required | Slow, requires calibrated tools | | Information | Less | More | | Plan | MIL-STD-105E, Dodge-Romig | MIL-STD-414 | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Quality Gatekeeper" > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Passport Officer Analogy:** Imagine a customs officer at an airport. He doesn't weigh every traveller (that would be *variable inspection*). Instead, he just checks whether the passport is valid (attribute — yes/no). If the number of travellers with invalid passports in a sample exceeds a threshold (the *acceptance number*), the entire flight (the *lot*) is sent back. This is exactly how attribute sampling works: a few sampled items act as the "representative face" of the entire lot. ### 1.4 Advantages and Limitations **Advantages:** - **Simplicity** — easier for shop-floor workers to apply. - **Cost-effective** — no expensive measuring instruments required. - **Each item classified** — works for subjective attributes (colour, finish). - **Applicable to destructive tests.** **Limitations:** - Provides **less information** per inspection than variable sampling. - Requires **larger sample sizes** for the same protection. - Assumes **binomial/Poisson** distribution, which may not always hold. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Statistical Foundations When sampling by attributes, the number of defectives in a sample of size *n* drawn from a lot with fraction defective *p* follows the **Binomial Distribution** (for finite correction negligible). The probability of finding exactly *d* defectives is: $$P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$ For large *n* and small *p*, this is approximated by the **Poisson Distribution**: $$P(d) = \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$$ ### 2.2 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve > [!IMPORTANT] > The **OC Curve** is the signature diagram of any acceptance sampling plan. It plots **P_a (Probability of Acceptance)** on the y-axis against **p (fraction defective)** on the x-axis. The curve shows the discriminating power of the plan — its ability to distinguish good lots from bad lots. The probability of acceptance for a single-sampling plan is the sum of probabilities of getting 0, 1, 2, ..., c defectives: $$P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$ ### 2.3 Key Risk Parameters on the OC Curve | Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Risk To | |---|---|---|---| | Acceptable Quality Level | **AQL** | Worst tolerable quality (low p) | **Producer** | | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | **LTPD / RQL** | Worst quality consumer will accept | **Consumer** | | Producer's Risk | **α** | P(reject good lot at p = AQL) | Producer | | Consumer's Risk | **β** | P(accept bad lot at p = LTPD) | Consumer | | Indifference Quality Level | **IQL** | P_a = 0.50 | Both equally | ### 2.4 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) and AOQL After a rejected lot is **rectified** (100% inspected and defectives replaced), the quality of outgoing product improves. $$AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$$ - *N* = lot size - *n* = sample size - (N − n) = uninspected items that go out as-is when lot is accepted. The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the maximum value of AOQ over all possible values of *p*: $$AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} (AOQ)$$ ### 2.5 Average Total Inspection (ATI) $$ATI = n \cdot P_a + (N - n) \cdot (1 - P_a)$$ This represents the **average number of items inspected per lot** — including both the sample and the 100% inspection of rejected lots. ### 2.6 ⚡ KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Probability of acceptance (Binomial) | | 2 | $P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Poisson approximation | | 3 | $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N}$ | Average outgoing quality | | 4 | $AOQL = \max(AOQ)$ | Worst-case outgoing quality | | 5 | $ATI = n \cdot P_a + (N-n)(1-P_a)$ | Average inspection effort | | 6 | $p = d/n$ | Sample fraction defective | | 7 | $\sigma_p = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ | Standard error of p | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Building the OC Curve > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A single sampling plan has *n* = 50 and *c* = 1. Calculate the probability of acceptance at *p* = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.08 and plot the OC curve. Also compute AOQL for *N* = 2000. --- #### Step 1 — Write the acceptance probability formula For *c* = 1, we accept if *d* = 0 or *d* = 1. $$P_a = P(d=0) + P(d=1)$$ Using the Binomial expression: $$P_a = (1-p)^n + n \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{n-1}$$ --- #### Step 2 — Substitute p = 0.01, n = 50 First compute (1 − p) = 0.99 and n·p = 0.50. $$P(d=0) = (0.99)^{50}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\ln(0.99) = -0.01005 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 50 \times (-0.01005) = -0.5025$$ $$P(d=0) = e^{-0.5025} = 0.6050$$ Next, P(d = 1): $$P(d=1) = 50 \times 0.01 \times (0.99)^{49}$$ $$\ln(0.99^{49}) = 49 \times (-0.01005) = -0.4925 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.6112$$ $$P(d=1) = 0.50 \times 0.6112 = 0.3056$$ So: $$P_a(0.01) = 0.6050 + 0.3056 = 0.9106$$ --- #### Step 3 — Substitute p = 0.03, n = 50 (1 − p) = 0.97, np = 1.50. $$P(d=0) = (0.97)^{50} = e^{50 \ln 0.97} = e^{50(-0.03046)} = e^{-1.523}$$ $$P(d=0) = 0.2182$$ $$P(d=1) = 50(0.03)(0.97)^{49} = 1.50 \times 0.2249 = 0.3374$$ $$P_a(0.03) = 0.2182 + 0.3374 = 0.5556$$ --- #### Step 4 — Substitute p = 0.05, n = 50 (1 − p) = 0.95, np = 2.50. $$P(d=0) = (0.95)^{50} = e^{50(-0.05129)} = e^{-2.565} = 0.0769$$ $$P(d=1) = 50(0.05)(0.95)^{49} = 2.50 \times 0.0810 = 0.2025$$ $$P_a(0.05) = 0.0769 + 0.2025 = 0.2794$$ --- #### Step 5 — Substitute p = 0.08, n = 50 (1 − p) = 0.92, np = 4.0. $$P(d=0) = (0.92)^{50} = e^{50(-0.08338)} = e^{-4.169} = 0.0155$$ $$P(d=1) = 50(0.08)(0.92)^{49} = 4.0 \times 0.0168 = 0.0673$$ $$P_a(0.08) = 0.0155 + 0.0673 = 0.0828$$ --- #### Step 6 — Tabulate and Plot the OC Curve | p | 0.01 | 0.03 | 0.05 | 0.08 | 0.10 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P_a | 0.911 | 0.556 | 0.279 | 0.083 | ≈ 0.034 | The OC curve descends steeply — a **good discriminating plan**. --- #### Step 7 — Compute AOQL for N = 2000 We use: $$AOQ(p) = P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot \frac{N-n}{N} = P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot \frac{1950}{2000} = 0.975 \cdot P_a(p) \cdot p$$ | p | P_a | p·P_a | AOQ | |---|---|---|---| | 0.01 | 0.911 | 0.00911 | 0.00888 | | 0.02 | 0.787 | 0.01574 | 0.01535 | | 0.03 | 0.556 | 0.01668 | 0.01626 | | 0.04 | 0.341 | 0.01364 | 0.01330 | | 0.05 | 0.279 | 0.01395 | 0.01360 | | 0.06 | 0.124 | 0.00744 | 0.00725 | Maximum AOQ occurs around p ≈ 0.025 – 0.030, giving: $$AOQL \approx 0.0163 \text{ (i.e., } 1.63\% \text{ defective outgoing)}$$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sampling Plan Types | Feature | Single Sampling | Double Sampling | Multiple Sampling | |---|---|---|---| | # of samples drawn | 1 | Up to 2 | Up to 7 | | Decision points | 1 | 2 | Several | | Avg. sample size | n₁ | Smaller | Smallest | | Administrative cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest | | Psychological impact | Strongest on supplier | Moderate | Weakest | | OC curve | Single | Combined | Combined | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flow Diagram — Single Sampling Plan by Attributes ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Lot of N items received] --> B[Draw random sample of size n] B --> C[Inspect each item: Conforming or Defective] C --> D[Count number of defectives d] D --> E{Is d ≤ c?} E -- Yes --> F[ACCEPT the lot] E -- No --> G[REJECT the lot] F --> H[Send lot to customer] G --> I[Rectify: 100% inspect and replace defectives] I --> H H --> J[End] ``` ### 4.2 OC Curve Architecture — Risk Regions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low p
Good Quality] -->|Region 1| B[High P_a
Acceptance Region] B -->|Region 2| C[P_a crosses 0.5
Indifference Zone] C -->|Region 3| D[Low P_a
Rejection Region] D --> E[High p
Bad Quality] F[AQL] -.->|alpha risk| B G[LTPD] -.->|beta risk| D H[IQL] -.-> C ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — AOQ System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD L[Incoming Lot of N items] --> S[Sample n items] S --> T{Decision based on d vs c} T -- d ≤ c --> AC[Lot Accepted] T -- d > c --> RJ[Lot Rejected] RJ --> RI[100 percent Rectifying Inspection] RI --> RP[Replace defectives] RP --> OUT[Outgoing Stream] AC --> OUT OUT --> AOQ[Compute AOQ = Pa × p × N minus n / N] AOQ --> AOQL[Identify Maximum as AOQL] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between sampling by attributes and sampling by variables. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Attribute | Variable | |---|---|---| | Data type | Qualitative (Go/No-Go) | Quantitative (measured) | | Decision basis | Number of defectives | Average / std deviation | | Examples: Bolt inspection (passes ring gauge / not), Bulb lights / does not | Bolt diameter = 8.02 mm, Tensile strength = 412 MPa | | | Plan standards | MIL-STD-105E, Dodge-Romig | MIL-STD-414 | Attribute sampling gives less information but is simpler and cheaper. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Define the following terms: (i) AQL, (ii) LTPD, (iii) Producer's Risk.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The maximum fraction defective that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered *satisfactory* as a process average. The probability of rejecting a lot at AQL is the producer's risk (α). Typical values: 0.1%, 0.65%, 1.0%, 4.0%. - **(ii) LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** Also called RQL (Rejectable Quality Level). The worst quality level that the consumer is willing to tolerate. The probability of accepting such a lot is the consumer's risk (β), usually set at 0.10. - **(iii) Producer's Risk (α):** The probability of rejecting a lot that has quality equal to AQL. Symmetrically, the probability that a good lot will be unnecessarily rejected. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern] **(a)** A single sampling plan has *n* = 80 and *c* = 2. Compute the probability of acceptance at p = 0.01, 0.04, 0.06, 0.10. Plot the OC curve. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For the same plan, calculate the AOQ at each value of p, and determine the AOQL. Take N = 1500. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) **Step 1 — Formula:** $$P_a = (1-p)^{80} + 80p(1-p)^{79} + \frac{80 \cdot 79}{2} p^2 (1-p)^{78}$$ **Step 2 — At p = 0.01:** np = 0.80, (1-p) = 0.99. - P(d=0) = (0.99)^80 = e^(80 × −0.01005) = e^(−0.804) = **0.4475** - P(d=1) = 0.80 × (0.99)^79 = 0.80 × 0.4520 = **0.3616** - P(d=2) = (80·79/2)(0.01)²(0.99)^78 = 3160 × 0.0001 × 0.4566 = **0.1443** $$P_a(0.01) = 0.4475 + 0.3616 + 0.1443 = 0.9534$$ **Step 3 — At p = 0.04:** np = 3.20, (1-p) = 0.96. - P(d=0) = (0.96)^80 = e^(80 × −0.04082) = e^(−3.266) = **0.0383** - P(d=1) = 3.20 × (0.96)^79 = 3.20 × 0.0399 = **0.1276** - P(d=2) = (5.12)²/2 × (0.96)^78 = 5.12 × 0.0415 = **0.2123** (Using Poisson approximation: e^(−3.2)(3.2)²/2 = 0.0408 × 5.12 = 0.2090) $$P_a(0.04) = 0.0383 + 0.1276 + 0.2090 = 0.3749$$ **Step 4 — At p = 0.06:** np = 4.80, (1-p) = 0.94. - P(d=0) ≈ e^(−4.8) = **0.0082** - P(d=1) = 4.80 × 0.0087 = **0.0418** - P(d=2) = (4.80)²/2 × 0.0092 = 11.52 × 0.0092 = **0.1060** $$P_a(0.06) = 0.0082 + 0.0418 + 0.1060 = 0.1560$$ **Step 5 — At p = 0.10:** np = 8.0, (1-p) = 0.90. - P(d=0) = (0.90)^80 = e^(80 × −0.10536) = e^(−8.429) = **0.00022** - P(d=1) = 8.0 × 0.00024 = **0.00192** - P(d=2) = 32 × 0.00027 = **0.00864** $$P_a(0.10) \approx 0.0108$$ **Step 6 — OC Table:** | p | 0.01 | 0.04 | 0.06 | 0.10 | |---|---|---|---|---| | P_a | 0.953 | 0.375 | 0.156 | 0.011 | **OC Curve Sketch:** A smooth, monotonically decreasing curve passing through these four points. #### Solution to (b) **Formula:** $$AOQ(p) = P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot \frac{N - n}{N} = P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot \frac{1420}{1500} = 0.9467 \cdot P_a \cdot p$$ | p | P_a | p · P_a | AOQ | |---|---|---|---| | 0.01 | 0.953 | 0.00953 | 0.00902 | | 0.02 | 0.886 | 0.01772 | 0.01677 | | 0.03 | 0.722 | 0.02166 | 0.02051 | | 0.04 | 0.375 | 0.01500 | 0.01420 | | 0.05 | 0.246 | 0.01230 | 0.01164 | | 0.06 | 0.156 | 0.00936 | 0.00886 | **Maximum AOQ occurs near p = 0.025 – 0.03:** $$\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.0205 \text{ or } 2.05\%}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern] (Internal Choice) **(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for a **Single Sampling Plan by Attributes** with a neat flowchart. Define AQL, LTPD, α, and β on the OC curve. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define and derive expressions for **AOQ** and **AOQL**. Explain the concept of **rectifying inspection**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to (a) **Single Sampling Plan — Procedure:** 1. A lot of N items is received. 2. A random sample of n items is drawn. 3. Each item is inspected and classified as good or defective. 4. The number of defectives d is counted. 5. Compare d with the acceptance number c: - If d ≤ c → **Accept the lot**. - If d > c → **Reject the lot**. 6. A rejected lot may be subjected to 100% rectifying inspection. **OC Curve and Risk Parameters:** The OC curve plots P_a vs p. Four key points are: - **AQL** — corresponds to high P_a (≈ 1 − α). Producer's risk α = P(reject a lot of AQL quality). - **LTPD** — corresponds to low P_a (= β). Consumer's risk β = P(accept a lot of LTPD quality). - **IQL** — point where P_a = 0.5; quality is "indifferent" to both parties. - The ideal OC curve is a vertical step at p = p₀, but in practice, we get a smooth S-curve. **Diagram description (to be drawn):** - X-axis: p (fraction defective), Y-axis: P_a (0 to 1). - Mark AQL near origin (low p) with P_a = 0.95. - Mark LTPD at high p with P_a = 0.10. - The curve is convex — sharply bending near IQL. #### Solution to (b) **Derivation of AOQ:** Suppose a lot of N items with fraction defective p is sent out, after sampling with probability P_a. Of the (N − n) uninspected items, a fraction p is defective. So defective items going out per lot, on average, are: $$\text{Defectives going out} = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)$$ The total items going out (whether accepted as-is or rectified) ≈ N. Therefore: $$AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$$ **AOQL:** $$AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} [AOQ(p)]$$ The maximum of the AOQ curve is finite because as p → 1, P_a → 0, so AOQ → 0. **Rectifying Inspection:** When a lot is rejected, instead of returning it to the supplier, the manufacturer may perform 100% inspection, replace all defectives, and then ship the lot. This is called **rectifying inspection**. The AOQ formula assumes this rectifying step. **Practical Insight:** The AOQ curve always lies below the OC curve. AOQL is the *guaranteed worst* quality leaving the system under rectifying inspection. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the (N − n)/N term in AOQ** — most students write AOQ = P_a · p. This is wrong when N is large but finite. Examiner will deduct **2 full marks**. > 2. **Confusing AQL with LTPD** — AQL is for the producer; LTPD is for the consumer. Mixing them up invites a 0. > 3. **Not labelling axes on OC curve** — you must write P_a vs p with units / ranges. Unlabelled diagrams get partial credit only. > 4. **Using wrong acceptance number c** — for c = 2, you must sum P(d=0) + P(d=1) + P(d=2). Forgetting the third term is a frequent error. > 5. **AOQL ≠ ATI** — do not interchange these. AOQL is a quality measure; ATI is an effort measure. > 6. **In double sampling**, the OC of the combined plan must be computed as P_a = P_a1 + P_a2 (probability of acceptance on 1st sample + on 2nd sample after going to 2nd). Students often forget the second term. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Sampling by Attributes** classifies items as Conforming / Non-Conforming — qualitative, count-based inspection. - **Single Sampling Plan** uses one sample of size *n* and acceptance number *c*. - **OC Curve** is the central tool — it plots P_a against p and is a monotonically decreasing S-curve. - **Producer's Risk (α)** = P(rejecting lot at AQL) ≈ 0.05 typically. - **Consumer's Risk (β)** = P(accepting lot at LTPD) ≈ 0.10 typically. - **AQL** = acceptable quality (low p) — protect producer. - **LTPD / RQL** = rejectable quality (high p) — protect consumer. - **AOQ** = P_a × p × (N − n)/N — average fraction defective going out under rectifying inspection. - **AOQL** = maximum of the AOQ curve — guaranteed worst-case outgoing quality. - **ATI** = n·P_a + (N − n)(1 − P_a) — average number of items inspected per lot. - **Double Sampling Plan** uses two stages (n₁, c₁, n₂, c₂) — smaller ASN at p near 0 or 1. - **Multiple Sampling Plan** uses up to 7 stages — smallest ASN, but administratively expensive. - **Rectifying Inspection** = 100% inspection of rejected lots + replacement of defectives. - **MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1** is the international standard for attribute single, double, and multiple plans indexed by AQL. - **Dodge-Romig Tables** provide attribute plans indexed by LTPD and AOQL for rectifying inspection. - **Binomial distribution** is the theoretical basis; **Poisson** is used as approximation for small p and large n. - **Two key equations to memorise:** $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ and $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)/N$.

topics.title: Single and Double Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single and Double Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — SINGLE AND DOUBLE SAMPLING PLANS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a **random sample** is taken from a production **lot**, and a decision to **accept or reject** the lot is made based on the number of defectives found in that sample. It is fundamentally different from process control (SPC), which monitors the *process* — acceptance sampling judges the *product lot*.

### 🔹 Single Sampling Plan (SSP)

A **Single Sampling Plan** is the most elementary acceptance sampling scheme. It is completely specified by two parameters:

- **n** = the sample size drawn from the lot
- **c** = the acceptance number (maximum allowable defectives)

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Lot is } 
\begin{cases}
\text{ACCEPTED} & \text{if } d \le c \\
\text{REJECTED} & \text{if } d > c
\end{cases}
$$

where *d* is the number of nonconforming (defective) items observed in the sample of size *n*.

### 🔹 Double Sampling Plan (DSP)

A **Double Sampling Plan** is a two-stage acceptance scheme. It is fully defined by four parameters:

- **n₁** = size of the *first* sample
- **c₁** = acceptance number for the first sample
- **n₂** = size of the *second* sample (taken only when the first sample is *inconclusive*)
- **c₂** = combined acceptance number (applied to defectives from both samples together, where c₂ > c₁)

**Decision Logic of DSP:**

| Stage | Observation | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| First | d₁ ≤ c₁ | **ACCEPT** the lot |
| First | d₁ > c₂ | **REJECT** the lot |
| First | c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂ | **DRAW** second sample of size n₂ |
| Second | (d₁ + d₂) ≤ c₂ | **ACCEPT** the lot |
| Second | (d₁ + d₂) > c₂ | **REJECT** the lot |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mango Vendor at the Railway Station**
> Imagine you are a quality-conscious buyer ordering a large basket (lot) of mangoes from a vendor. You cannot taste-test every mango (that would defeat the purpose of buying a basket), so you randomly pick a few (the sample).

🟢 **Single Sampling Plan** — "One-Chance Rule":
> You pull out **5 mangoes** (n = 5). You decide: *"If 1 or fewer are bad (c = 1), I will buy the basket. If 2 or more are bad, I will reject it."* You make your decision **immediately** based on that one pick.

🔵 **Double Sampling Plan** — "Two-Chance Rule":
> You first pull out **3 mangoes** (n₁ = 3). 
> - If **0 are bad** (c₁ = 0): you accept the basket outright.
> - If **2 or more are bad**: you reject the basket outright.
> - If **exactly 1 is bad**: you are *unsure*, so you take a **second look** at another 4 mangoes (n₂ = 4). Now you combine the bad mangoes from both picks. If the total is **2 or fewer** (c₂ = 2), you buy the basket; otherwise, you reject it.
> 
> The double plan saves effort on obviously *good* lots and avoids rejecting obviously *bad* lots, while giving a second chance to *borderline* lots.

This "second chance on borderline cases" is the **defining feature** of a double sampling plan, leading to a lower **Average Sample Number (ASN)** than its single-sampling equivalent for good-quality lots.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve of a SSP

The **OC Curve** plots the probability of acceptance (Pₐ) against the incoming lot quality (p = fraction defective). It is the most important descriptor of any sampling plan.

**Step-by-step logic behind the OC curve:**

1. The lot is accepted only if *d* defectives or fewer are found in *n* draws.
2. Each draw is treated as a **Bernoulli trial** (defective / non-defective).
3. The number of defectives *d* follows a **Binomial distribution** (when n/N ≤ 0.1, else Hypergeometric).
4. Therefore, the probability of acceptance is the cumulative Binomial:

$$
P_a = P(d \le c) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} \, p^{i} \, (1-p)^{n-i}
$$

5. As *p* → 0, Pₐ → 1 (good lots are accepted).
6. As *p* → 1, Pₐ → 0 (bad lots are rejected).
7. The OC curve is **monotonically decreasing**.

### 2.2 Key Performance Metrics of a SSP

**Average Sample Number (ASN):**
$$
ASN_{SSP} = n \quad \text{(fixed for every lot)}
$$

**Average Total Inspection (ATI)** — for plans where rejected lots are **100% inspected** and defectives replaced:
$$
ATI = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)
$$

**Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** — assuming *rectifying inspection* (defective items in accepted lots are replaced by good ones):

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** — the maximum of AOQ over all possible values of p:

$$
AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \left[ \frac{P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \right]
$$

### 2.3 Operating Characteristic of a DSP

For a Double Sampling Plan, define the conditional probabilities:

- Probability of acceptance on the **first** sample:

$$
P_{a1} = \sum_{i=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n_1-i}
$$

- Probability of rejection on the **first** sample:

$$
P_{r1} = \sum_{i=c_2+1}^{n_1} \binom{n_1}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n_1-i}
$$

- Probability of going to the **second** sample:

$$
P_2 = \sum_{i=c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n_1-i}
$$

- Probability of acceptance on the **second** sample (given the second sample was drawn):

$$
P_{a2} = \sum_{j=c_1+1}^{c_2} \left[ \binom{n_1}{j} p^{j} (1-p)^{n_1-j} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{c_2-j} \binom{n_2}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{n_2-k} \right]
$$

- **Total probability of acceptance:**

$$
P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2}
$$

### 2.4 Key Performance Metrics of a DSP

**Average Sample Number (ASN)** — note that this is *not* constant:

$$
ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ASN curve of a DSP is **bow-shaped**. For very good lots, ASN → n₁ (no second sample needed). For very bad lots, ASN → n₁ (rejected at first stage). The maximum ASN occurs at intermediate quality levels (around the indifference quality).

**Average Total Inspection (ATI)** for a DSP with rectifying inspection:

$$
ATI = (n_1 + n_2 P_2) P_a + N(1 - P_a) \quad \text{(for 100% screening of rejected lots)}
$$

**Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot \bigl(N - n_1 - n_2 P_2\bigr)}{N}
$$

---

## 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Parameter | Single Sampling Plan (SSP) | Double Sampling Plan (DSP) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Decision parameter | Compare *d* to *c* in one sample of size *n* | Compare *d₁* to c₁, c₂; combine with *d₂* if needed |
| 2 | Plan parameters | (n, c) | (n₁, c₁, n₂, c₂) |
| 3 | Pₐ formula | $P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$ | $P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2}$ |
| 4 | Probability of going to 2nd sample | Not applicable | $P_2 = \sum_{i=c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n_1-i}$ |
| 5 | ASN | $n$ (constant) | $n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_2$ (variable) |
| 6 | AOQ | $\frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | $\frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - ASN)}{N}$ |
| 7 | AOQL | $\max$ of AOQ over *p* | $\max$ of AOQ over *p* |
| 8 | ATI (rectifying) | $n P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ | $ASN \cdot P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ |
| 9 | OC curve shape | Smooth, monotonically decreasing | Two-stage (piecewise); comparable to SSP if matched at p₁, p₂ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Acceptance Sampling Plans**
> - Incoming raw-material inspection (steel, electronic components) in manufacturing.
> - Final product inspection in batch production (pharmaceuticals, automobiles).
> - Procurement quality control in supply-chain management (vendor qualification).
> - Military, defence, and aerospace standards (e.g., MIL-STD-105E, ISO 2859-1).
> - Quality audits where 100% testing is destructive (light bulbs, fuses, missile warheads).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the OC Function for a SSP

The number of defectives *d* in a random sample of size *n* drawn from a large lot (n ≤ 0.1 N) follows a Binomial distribution:

$$
P(d = i) = \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

The lot is accepted if d ≤ c, so we sum the probabilities of observing 0, 1, 2, …, c defectives:

$$
P_a = P(d = 0) + P(d = 1) + \cdots + P(d = c)
$$

$$
P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}
$$

**This is the operating characteristic (OC) function of a Single Sampling Plan.** The plot of Pₐ versus p is the OC curve.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the ASN for a DSP

In a double sampling plan, two mutually exclusive outcomes consume samples:

- **Case 1**: First sample is decisive (accept if d₁ ≤ c₁, reject if d₁ > c₂). Total items inspected = n₁. Probability of this case = (P_{a1} + P_{r1}).

- **Case 2**: First sample is inconclusive (c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂). A second sample of size n₂ is drawn. Total items inspected = n₁ + n₂. Probability of this case = P_2.

Expected (average) sample size:

$$
ASN = n_1 (P_{a1} + P_{r1}) + (n_1 + n_2) P_2
$$

Since probabilities sum to unity, P_{a1} + P_{r1} + P_2 = 1, so (P_{a1} + P_{r1}) = 1 − P_2:

$$
ASN = n_1 (1 - P_2) + (n_1 + n_2) P_2
$$

$$
ASN = n_1 - n_1 P_2 + n_1 P_2 + n_2 P_2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,ASN = n_1 + n_2 P_2\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 WORKED PROBLEM 1 — Single Sampling Plan

> **Problem:** A lot of N = 2000 items is inspected using a Single Sampling Plan with n = 60 and c = 2. The lot fraction defective is p = 0.04. Find (a) the probability of acceptance, (b) the AOQ, and (c) the AOQL (approximate from the data given).

**Given:** N = 2000, n = 60, c = 2, p = 0.04.

#### Part (a) — Probability of Acceptance (Pₐ)

By definition, Pₐ is the sum of binomial probabilities for i = 0, 1, 2 defectives.

$$
P_a = \binom{60}{0}(0.04)^0(0.96)^{60} + \binom{60}{1}(0.04)^1(0.96)^{59} + \binom{60}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{58}
$$

**Term 1 (i = 0):**
$$
P(d=0) = 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.96)^{60} = 0.0859
$$

**Term 2 (i = 1):**
$$
P(d=1) = 60 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.96)^{59}
$$
$$
(0.96)^{59} = \frac{(0.96)^{60}}{0.96} = \frac{0.0859}{0.96} = 0.0895
$$
$$
P(d=1) = 60 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 0.0895 = 2.4 \cdot 0.0895 = 0.2148
$$

**Term 3 (i = 2):**
$$
P(d=2) = \binom{60}{2} (0.04)^2 (0.96)^{58}
$$
$$
\binom{60}{2} = \frac{60 \cdot 59}{2} = 1770
$$
$$
(0.96)^{58} = \frac{0.0859}{(0.96)^2} = \frac{0.0859}{0.9216} = 0.0932
$$
$$
P(d=2) = 1770 \cdot 0.0016 \cdot 0.0932 = 2.832 \cdot 0.0932 = 0.2640
$$

**Summing up:**

$$
P_a = 0.0859 + 0.2148 + 0.2640 = 0.5647
$$

> The lot has a **56.47%** chance of being accepted when the incoming quality is 4% defective.

#### Part (b) — Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.5647 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (2000 - 60)}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.5647 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 1940}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.5647 \cdot 77.6}{2000} = \frac{43.82}{2000} = 0.02191
$$

> The average outgoing quality is **2.19% defective** after rectifying inspection.

#### Part (c) — AOQL (Approximate Indication)

The AOQL is the maximum of AOQ as p varies. We can verify the trend:

| p | Pₐ (approx.) | AOQ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.01 | ≈ 0.980 | 0.00951 |
| 0.04 | 0.565 | 0.02191 |
| 0.07 | ≈ 0.180 | 0.01224 |

The maximum AOQ lies in the vicinity of p ≈ 0.04. For precise AOQL in KTU exam, a finer tabular sweep is required, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\,AOQL \approx 0.022 \text{ (or about 2.2\% defective)}\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 WORKED PROBLEM 2 — Double Sampling Plan

> **Problem:** A Double Sampling Plan has parameters n₁ = 30, c₁ = 0, n₂ = 30, c₂ = 2. The incoming fraction defective is p = 0.05. Find (a) the probability of acceptance Pₐ, and (b) the ASN.

**Given:** n₁ = 30, c₁ = 0, n₂ = 30, c₂ = 2, p = 0.05.

#### Part (a) — Probability of Acceptance (Pₐ)

**Step 1: Probability of acceptance on first sample (P_{a1}):** since c₁ = 0, accept only if d₁ = 0.

$$
P_{a1} = (0.95)^{30}
$$

Computing: ln(0.95) = −0.05129, so 30 × (−0.05129) = −1.5387, e^(−1.5387) = 0.2146

$$
P_{a1} = 0.2146
$$

**Step 2: Probability of going to second sample (P_2):** need c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂, i.e., 1 ≤ d₁ ≤ 2.

$$
P_2 = P(d_1 = 1) + P(d_1 = 2)
$$

For d₁ = 1:
$$
P(d_1 = 1) = \binom{30}{1}(0.05)^1(0.95)^{29} = 30 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.95)^{29}
$$
$$
(0.95)^{29} = \frac{0.2146}{0.95} = 0.2259
$$
$$
P(d_1 = 1) = 1.5 \cdot 0.2259 = 0.3389
$$

For d₁ = 2:
$$
P(d_1 = 2) = \binom{30}{2}(0.05)^2(0.95)^{28} = 435 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot (0.95)^{28}
$$
$$
(0.95)^{28} = \frac{0.2146}{(0.95)^2} = \frac{0.2146}{0.9025} = 0.2378
$$
$$
P(d_1 = 2) = 1.0875 \cdot 0.2378 = 0.2587
$$

$$
P_2 = 0.3389 + 0.2587 = 0.5976
$$

**Step 3: Probability of acceptance on second sample (P_{a2}):** accept if (d₁ + d₂) ≤ c₂ = 2. This requires sub-cases:

- **Sub-case A:** d₁ = 1, d₂ ≤ 1 (i.e., 0 or 1 defective in second sample)
- **Sub-case B:** d₁ = 2, d₂ = 0

$$
P_{a2} = P(d_1=1) \cdot P(d_2 \le 1) + P(d_1=2) \cdot P(d_2 = 0)
$$

Compute P(d₂ ≤ 1):
$$
P(d_2 \le 1) = (0.95)^{30} + 30(0.05)(0.95)^{29} = 0.2146 + 0.3389 = 0.5535
$$

Compute P(d₂ = 0):
$$
P(d_2 = 0) = (0.95)^{30} = 0.2146
$$

Now:
$$
P_{a2} = 0.3389 \times 0.5535 + 0.2587 \times 0.2146
$$
$$
P_{a2} = 0.1876 + 0.0555 = 0.2431
$$

**Step 4: Total probability of acceptance:**
$$
P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 0.2146 + 0.2431 = 0.4577
$$

> The lot has a **45.77%** chance of acceptance under this DSP when p = 0.05.

#### Part (b) — Average Sample Number (ASN)

$$
ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_2
$$

$$
ASN = 30 + 30 \times 0.5976
$$

$$
ASN = 30 + 17.93 = 47.93
$$

> On average, **47.93 items** are inspected per lot under this DSP. (Note: This is less than the 60 items required in the equivalent SSP at the same AQL.)

---

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix — SSP vs. DSP

| Feature | Single Sampling Plan | Double Sampling Plan |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sample size | Fixed = n | Variable, between n₁ and (n₁+n₂) |
| ASN | Constant n | Variable, ≤ n₁ + n₂ |
| Decision stages | One | Up to two |
| Administrative simplicity | **High** | Moderate |
| Inspection cost on good lots | Higher (always inspects n) | **Lower** (often stops at n₁) |
| Psychological appeal to vendor | Less (all-or-nothing) | **Higher** (second chance) |
| Information gathered | Less | **More** (two opportunities) |
| OC curve construction | Direct (binomial sum) | Requires two-stage summation |
| Best use | Small lots, simple production | Large lots, expensive inspection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Decision-Flow Diagram — Single Sampling Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LotA[INCOMING LOT of N units] --> DrawA[Draw Random Sample of size n]
    DrawA --> CountA[Count Number of Defectives d in sample]
    CountA --> CheckA{Is d less than or equal to c}
    CheckA -->|YES yes d at most c| AcceptA[ACCEPT LOT Ship to Customer]
    CheckA -->|NO d greater than c| RejectA[REJECT LOT Return or Rectify]
    RejectA --> RectifyA[Optional: 100 percent Rectifying Inspection]
    RectifyA --> ShipA[Ship to Customer with AOQ]
```

### 4.2 Decision-Flow Diagram — Double Sampling Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LotB[INCOMING LOT of N units] --> Draw1[Draw First Sample of size n1]
    Draw1 --> Count1[Count Defectives d1]
    Count1 --> Decision1{d1 compared to thresholds}
    Decision1 -->|d1 at most c1| AcceptB[ACCEPT LOT After Stage 1]
    Decision1 -->|d1 greater than c2| RejectB[REJECT LOT After Stage 1]
    Decision1 -->|c1 less than d1 at most c2| Draw2[Draw Second Sample of size n2]
    Draw2 --> Count2[Count Defectives d2 in second sample]
    Count2 --> Total[Compute Combined Defectives T = d1 plus d2]
    Total --> Decision2{T compared to c2}
    Decision2 -->|T at most c2| AcceptB
    Decision2 -->|T greater than c2| RejectB
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Performance Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SSP[Single Sampling Plan]
      SSPin[Inputs n c p N] --> SSPpa[Pa from Binomial Sum]
      SSPpa --> SSPaon[AOQ from Pa and p and N]
      SSPpa --> SSPati[ATI from Pa and N and n]
      SSPaon --> SSPlimit[AOQL Maximum of AOQ over p]
    end
    subgraph DSP[Double Sampling Plan]
      DSPin[Inputs n1 c1 n2 c2 p N] --> DSPpa1[Pa1 from first sample]
      DSPin --> DSPp2[Prob go to second P2]
      DSPin --> DSPpa2[Pa2 from combined samples]
      DSPpa1 --> DSPpatot[Pa equals Pa1 plus Pa2]
      DSPpa2 --> DSPpatot
      DSPp2 --> DSPasn[ASN equals n1 plus n2 times P2]
      DSPpatot --> DSPaon[AOQ from Pa and ASN and p and N]
      DSPasn --> DSPaon
      DSPaon --> DSPlimit[AOQL Maximum of AOQ over p]
    end
```

### 4.4 OC Curve Comparison (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Plot Pa versus p] --> B[Smooth Monotonically Decreasing Curve for SSP]
    A --> C[Piecewise Curve for DSP with Two Branches]
    B --> D[Ideal OC crosses p1 and p2 at desired levels]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Plan matched to Producer Risk alpha and Consumer Risk beta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the OC Curve**
> - A point on the OC curve tells the probability of acceptance for a given lot quality *p*.
> - The plan is typically designed to satisfy two design points: the **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** with probability 1 − α, and the **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)** with probability β.
> - These points are jointly referred to as the (p₁, 1 − α) and (p₂, β) design requirements.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
**Define a Single Sampling Plan. List any two merits and two demerits of a single sampling plan.**

**Model Answer:**
A Single Sampling Plan is an acceptance sampling procedure in which a single random sample of size *n* is drawn from a lot, and the lot is accepted if the number of defectives *d* in the sample does not exceed a pre-determined acceptance number *c*. Otherwise, the lot is rejected.

*Merits:*
1. Conceptually the simplest plan; easy to design, administer, and explain to workers and management.
2. The OC curve and AOQ are straightforward to compute using the Binomial formula.

*Demerits:*
1. The sample size is fixed; there is no opportunity to make a "second-look" decision for borderline lots, leading to higher inspection cost on good lots.
2. The plan provides no second chance, which can be psychologically demotivating for vendors and may not use inspection information efficiently.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**Define Average Sample Number (ASN) for a Double Sampling Plan. Why is it not constant like in a single sampling plan?**

**Model Answer:**
The Average Sample Number (ASN) of a Double Sampling Plan is the expected number of items inspected per lot, averaged over all possible outcomes of the first and second samples. It is given by:

$$
ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_2
$$

where P_2 is the probability that the first sample is inconclusive (i.e., c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂) and a second sample must be drawn.

The ASN is *not* constant because a second sample is taken only for some lots. For good-quality lots, the first sample is decisive and ASN = n₁. For poor-quality lots, the first sample rejects the lot and ASN = n₁. The ASN exceeds n₁ only for borderline lots where the second sample is required, with the maximum occurring at intermediate values of *p*. Hence the ASN varies with the incoming lot quality, giving a characteristic "bow-shaped" ASN curve.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Single Sampling Plan Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A consumer receives lots of N = 3000 items. The acceptable quality level is AQL = 0.015 (1.5%) and the lot tolerance percent defective is LTPD = 0.06 (6%). A Single Sampling Plan with n = 90 and c = 3 is proposed.

**(a)** For p = 0.02 and p = 0.06, compute the probability of acceptance Pₐ and verify whether the plan satisfies the design constraints. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the same plan, compute the AOQ at p = 0.02 and estimate the AOQL by computing AOQ at p = 0.03 and p = 0.04 also. State the maximum outgoing quality. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** n = 90, c = 3.

*For p = 0.02:*
$$
P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{90}{i} (0.02)^i (0.98)^{90-i}
$$

Compute each term:
- i = 0: (0.98)⁹⁰ = 0.1626
- i = 1: 90 × 0.02 × (0.98)⁸⁹ = 1.8 × 0.1659 = 0.2986
- i = 2: 4005 × 0.0004 × (0.98)⁸⁸ = 1.602 × 0.1693 = 0.2712
- i = 3: 91,720 × 0.000008 × (0.98)⁸⁷ = 0.7338 × 0.1728 = 0.1268

$$
P_a (0.02) = 0.1626 + 0.2986 + 0.2712 + 0.1268 = 0.8592
$$

*For p = 0.06:*
$$
P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{3} \binom{90}{i} (0.06)^i (0.94)^{90-i}
$$

- i = 0: (0.94)⁹⁰ = 0.00365
- i = 1: 90 × 0.06 × (0.94)⁸⁹ = 5.4 × 0.00388 = 0.02095
- i = 2: 4005 × 0.0036 × (0.94)⁸⁸ = 14.418 × 0.00413 = 0.05955
- i = 3: 91,720 × 0.000216 × (0.94)⁸⁷ = 19.81 × 0.00439 = 0.08697

$$
P_a (0.06) = 0.00365 + 0.02095 + 0.05955 + 0.08697 = 0.1711
$$

**Verification of design constraints:**
- At p = AQL = 0.015, the plan should accept with high probability (≥ 0.90). The Pₐ at p = 0.02 is 0.86, which is close to the typical 0.95 target — the plan is reasonable but a slightly larger *n* may be required for tighter producer protection.
- At p = LTPD = 0.06, the consumer risk β = Pₐ = 0.1711, which is well below the typical 0.10 target. **The plan is acceptable.**

**Part (b):** AOQ at various values of p.

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} = P_a \cdot p \cdot \frac{2910}{3000} = 0.97 \cdot P_a \cdot p
$$

| p | Pₐ | AOQ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.02 | 0.8592 | 0.97 × 0.8592 × 0.02 = 0.01667 |
| 0.03 | (compute) ≈ 0.5800 | 0.97 × 0.58 × 0.03 = 0.01688 |
| 0.04 | (compute) ≈ 0.2990 | 0.97 × 0.299 × 0.04 = 0.01160 |
| 0.06 | 0.1711 | 0.97 × 0.1711 × 0.06 = 0.00996 |

The maximum AOQ occurs at approximately p = 0.03:

$$
\boxed{\,AOQL \approx 0.0169 \text{ (or about 1.69\% defective)}\,}
$$

This means that after rectifying inspection, no matter how poor the incoming quality, the **average outgoing quality will never exceed 1.69% defective.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Double Sampling Plan Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

A double sampling plan has the following parameters: n₁ = 50, c₁ = 1, n₂ = 50, c₂ = 4. The incoming fraction defective is p = 0.03.

**(a)** Compute the probability of acceptance Pₐ of the lot. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compute the Average Sample Number (ASN) and the AOQ for N = 2000. Comment on the inspection efficiency. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** n₁ = 50, c₁ = 1, c₂ = 4, p = 0.03.

*Step 1: P_{a1}* (accept on first sample; d₁ ≤ 1):

$$
P_{a1} = (0.97)^{50} + 50 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (0.97)^{49}
$$

(0.97)⁵⁰ = 0.2181; (0.97)⁴⁹ = 0.2181/0.97 = 0.2248

$$
P_{a1} = 0.2181 + 1.5 \cdot 0.2248 = 0.2181 + 0.3372 = 0.5553
$$

*Step 2: P_2* (1 < d₁ ≤ 4; need d₁ = 2, 3, 4):

$$
P_2 = P(d_1=2) + P(d_1=3) + P(d_1=4)
$$

- (0.97)⁴⁸ = 0.2248/0.97 = 0.2317
- (0.97)⁴⁷ = 0.2317/0.97 = 0.2389
- (0.97)⁴⁶ = 0.2389/0.97 = 0.2463

P(d₁=2) = C(50,2) × 0.03² × 0.97⁴⁸ = 1225 × 0.0009 × 0.2317 = 0.2555
P(d₁=3) = C(50,3) × 0.03³ × 0.97⁴⁷ = 19600 × 0.000027 × 0.2389 = 0.1264
P(d₁=4) = C(50,4) × 0.03⁴ × 0.97⁴⁶ = 230300 × 0.00000081 × 0.2463 = 0.0459

$$
P_2 = 0.2555 + 0.1264 + 0.0459 = 0.4278
$$

*Step 3: P_{a2}* (acceptance on second sample; d₁ + d₂ ≤ 4):

Since the calculation is exhaustive (multiple sub-cases), use the conditional probability formula:

$$
P_{a2} = \sum_{j=2}^{4} P(d_1 = j) \cdot P(d_2 \le 4 - j)
$$

Where P(d₂ ≤ k) for n₂ = 50, p = 0.03:

- P(d₂ = 0) = (0.97)⁵⁰ = 0.2181
- P(d₂ = 1) = 50 × 0.03 × (0.97)⁴⁹ = 0.3372
- P(d₂ = 2) = 0.2555 (computed above)
- P(d₂ = 3) = 0.1264
- P(d₂ = 4) = 0.0459

Cumulative:
- P(d₂ ≤ 2) = 0.2181 + 0.3372 + 0.2555 = 0.8108
- P(d₂ ≤ 1) = 0.2181 + 0.3372 = 0.5553
- P(d₂ ≤ 0) = 0.2181

Now:
$$
P_{a2} = P(d_1=2) P(d_2 \le 2) + P(d_1=3) P(d_2 \le 1) + P(d_1=4) P(d_2 \le 0)
$$
$$
P_{a2} = 0.2555 \times 0.8108 + 0.1264 \times 0.5553 + 0.0459 \times 0.2181
$$
$$
P_{a2} = 0.2071 + 0.0702 + 0.0100 = 0.2873
$$

*Step 4: Total Pₐ:*

$$
P_a = P_{a1} + P_{a2} = 0.5553 + 0.2873 = 0.8426
$$

> The probability of accepting the lot is **84.26%** under this DSP at p = 0.03.

**Part (b):** ASN and AOQ.

*ASN:*
$$
ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P_2 = 50 + 50 \times 0.4278 = 50 + 21.39 = 71.39
$$

On average, **71.39 items** are inspected per lot. (Compare: an equivalent SSP might require 80–90 items, so this DSP saves inspection effort on borderline lots.)

*AOQ:* (N = 2000)
$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - ASN)}{N} = \frac{0.8426 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (2000 - 71.39)}{2000}
$$
$$
AOQ = \frac{0.8426 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 1928.61}{2000} = \frac{48.75}{2000} = 0.02438
$$

The **average outgoing quality is 2.44% defective** after rectifying inspection.

**Comment on inspection efficiency:**
The DSP achieves a probability of acceptance (84.26%) comparable to or better than a similarly-sized SSP, while inspecting on average only **71.39 items instead of 100**. This represents a savings of nearly 30% in inspection effort for this borderline quality level, demonstrating the key advantage of double sampling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the c₁ threshold is strict:** Students often mistakenly sum d₁ = 0, 1, 2 for P_{a1} when c₁ = 1. Remember: P_{a1} = Σ from i = 0 to c₁ (i.e., *c₁ is the upper limit*, not the boundary of the "second sample zone").
> 2. **Confusing P_2 and P_{a2}:** P_2 is the *probability of drawing the second sample at all*, while P_{a2} is the *probability of accepting after the second sample*. They are different quantities and both must appear in the formula for total Pₐ.
> 3. **Ignoring the ASN multiplier P_2 in the AOQ:** In the AOQ formula for a DSP, the term (N − ASN) is used, not (N − n₁) or (N − n₁ − n₂).
> 4. **Skipping unit verification:** Always state the answer with units and a concluding remark, e.g., "The lot has an 84.26% probability of being accepted." Examiners reward contextual interpretation.
> 5. **Calculation error on Binomial coefficients:** For larger n, use C(n, 2) = n(n−1)/2 and C(n, 3) = n(n−1)(n−2)/6. A common error is using n²/2 instead of n(n−1)/2.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ A **Single Sampling Plan** is defined by **(n, c)**; lot is accepted iff **d ≤ c**.
- ✅ A **Double Sampling Plan** is defined by **(n₁, c₁, n₂, c₂)**; c₁ < c₂; a second sample is drawn only when **c₁ < d₁ ≤ c₂**.
- ✅ The **OC function** for SSP is the cumulative Binomial: Pₐ = Σᵢ₌₀ᶜ C(n,i) pⁱ (1−p)ⁿ⁻ⁱ.
- ✅ The **OC function** for DSP is the sum of two-stage probabilities: Pₐ = P_{a1} + P_{a2}.
- ✅ **ASN for SSP** is constant = n; **ASN for DSP** is variable = n₁ + n₂·P_2.
- ✅ **AOQ** measures the quality of lots *leaving* the inspection system (after rectifying). Formula: AOQ = Pₐ · p · (N − ASN)/N for DSP.
- ✅ **AOQL** is the **maximum value** of AOQ over all p; it represents the *worst-case* outgoing quality.
- ✅ The **OC curve** is monotonically decreasing; its shape depends on n and c (or n₁, c₁, n₂, c₂).
- ✅ The **ASN curve** of a DSP is **bow-shaped** — it dips to n₁ at extremes and peaks at intermediate p.
- ✅ **DSP advantage:** lower average inspection cost on good and bad lots, with a "second chance" for borderline lots.
- ✅ **SSP advantage:** administratively simple, easier to construct OC curve.
- ✅ **Rectifying inspection** (replacing defectives in accepted lots) is assumed in AOQ calculations.
- ✅ The **indifference quality** p* is the value of p at which Pₐ = 0.5 on the OC curve.
- ✅ KTU exams often test the **calculation of Pₐ, AOQ, and ASN** with given plan parameters — practice these exhaustively.
- ✅ Always show **step-by-step expansion** of binomial terms in the exam; do not just quote the final Pₐ.
- ✅ Mismatching the **c₁ boundary in P_{a1}** is the most common computational error in DSP problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspections by Samples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inspections by Samples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sampling Inspection** (also called *Acceptance Sampling*) is a statistical quality control procedure in which a randomly selected subset (sample) of items from a lot/batch is inspected, and based on the number of defectives found in that sample, a decision is made to either **accept** or **reject** the entire lot. This is performed when **100% inspection is either impractical, uneconomical, or destructive**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Sampling Inspection is the methodology of evaluating the quality of a lot by examining a small portion of items drawn randomly from it, and using the sample evidence to infer the quality of the whole lot under probabilistic risk.*

Key Parameter Definitions (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):

| Symbol | Parameter | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **N** | Lot Size | Total number of items in the batch submitted for inspection |
| **n** | Sample Size | Number of items randomly drawn from N |
| **c** | Acceptance Number | Maximum allowable defectives in the sample to accept the lot |
| **d** | Number of Defectives | Defective items actually observed in the sample |
| **p** | Incoming Quality | Fraction defective in the submitted lot (true proportion) |
| **p₁** | **AQL** (Acceptable Quality Level) | Worst tolerable quality with high probability of acceptance |
| **p₂** | **LTPD** (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) | Lowest quality considered unsatisfactory; rejection desired |
| **α** | Producer's Risk | P(rejecting good lot) — Type I error |
| **β** | Consumer's Risk | P(accepting bad lot) — Type II error |

## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fruit Market Analogy:** Imagine you are a wholesale buyer purchasing 1000 mangoes (the **lot**) from a farmer. You cannot taste all 1000 (destructive testing / time-consuming). So you randomly pick 5 mangoes (the **sample, n=5**) and cut them open. If **2 or fewer** are rotten (your **acceptance number, c=2**), you accept the entire lot; otherwise you reject it. This is exactly how industries like electronics, pharmaceuticals, and automobile parts conduct sampling inspections.

**Why use sampling instead of 100% inspection?**

1. **Destructive Testing** — testing itself damages the product (light bulbs, explosives, tensile testing)
2. **Cost Reduction** — inspecting every item is prohibitively expensive
3. **Time Efficiency** — inspection of large lots is time-consuming
4. **Fatigue & Error** — human inspectors lose concentration during prolonged 100% inspection
5. **Homogeneity Assumption** — under proper randomization, sample reflects the lot

## 1.3 Classification of Sampling Inspection Plans

```
Sampling Inspection
├── By Decision Type
│   ├── Attributes (Go/No-Go) → c,d based
│   └── Variables (measured) → mean,variance based
├── By Sample Size
│   ├── Single Sampling Plan
│   ├── Double Sampling Plan
│   └── Multiple/Sequential Sampling Plan
└── By Severity
    ├── Normal Inspection
    ├── Reduced Inspection
    └── Tightened Inspection
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Sampling Plan — Operational Logic

In a **Single Sampling Plan**, a single random sample of size **n** is drawn from a lot of size **N**. The number of defectives **d** is counted. If **d ≤ c**, the lot is accepted; if **d > c**, the lot is rejected.

**Operational Steps:**

1. Submit lot of size **N** to inspection station
2. Draw **n** items at random (without replacement if finite lot)
3. Inspect each of the **n** items for conformance
4. Count defectives **d** found in the sample
5. Apply decision rule:
   - If **d ≤ c** → **ACCEPT** the lot
   - If **d > c** → **REJECT** the lot
6. In rectifying inspection → rejected lots undergo 100% screening

## 2.2 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve

The **OC Curve** is the heart of sampling inspection theory. It is a plot of the **probability of acceptance (Pₐ)** versus the **incoming lot quality (p)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The OC curve answers the fundamental question: *"If I submit a lot with fraction defective p, what is the probability that my sampling plan will accept it?"*

### Why the OC Curve matters:
- A good sampling plan has an **S-shaped (sigmoid) OC curve**
- The curve slopes downward as p increases
- It visually quantifies both **producer's risk** and **consumer's risk**

## 2.3 Two-Point Risk Specification on OC Curve

Two critical points define a sampling plan on the OC curve:

| Point | p-value | Pₐ desired | Risk |
|-------|---------|------------|------|
| Point 1 (Producer's) | p₁ = AQL | High (≈ 1−α) | α = Producer's Risk |
| Point 2 (Consumer's) | p₂ = LTPD | Low (≈ β) | β = Consumer's Risk |

**KTU Standard Risk Values:**
- **α = 0.05** (5%) — Industry default
- **β = 0.10** (10%) — Industry default

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Application |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Binomial probability of acceptance | Standard for **n ≤ 50** |
| 2 | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Hypergeometric probability (finite lot) | When **n/N > 0.10** |
| 3 | $P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Poisson approximation | When **n ≥ 50, p ≤ 0.10** |
| 4 | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Average Outgoing Quality | Rectifying plan analysis |
| 5 | $AOQL = \max_{p} \{AOQ\}$ | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | Worst-case AOQ |
| 6 | $ATI = n \cdot P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ | Average Total Inspection | Resource planning |
| 7 | $ASN = n_1 + n_2(1-P_{a1})$ | Average Sample Number | Double sampling plans |
| 8 | $\sigma_{OC} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | OC curve steepness measure | Plan comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** Never use vertical pipe `|x|` inside formula table rows. All absolute value notations use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, e.g., $\vert p - p_1 \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Sampling Inspection |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Electronics | PCB component lot acceptance |
| Pharmaceuticals | Tablet batch quality verification |
| Automotive | Brake pad, tire batch testing |
| Textiles | Fabric defect rate acceptance |
| Defense/Aerospace | MIL-STD-105E / MIL-STD-414 compliance |
| Food Processing | Bottle/can integrity (destructive testing) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Acceptance under Binomial Model

When the lot is **large** (N → ∞) and sampling is with replacement, each item independently has probability **p** of being defective.

**Starting Point:** Each item is a Bernoulli trial:
- Success (defective) with probability **p**
- Failure (good) with probability **(1−p)**

The probability of obtaining exactly **d** defectives in **n** trials is given by the Binomial PMF:

$$
P(X = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

The lot is **accepted** if defectives found d ≤ c. Therefore, summing over d = 0 to c:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

### Example Numerical Computation (KTU Standard Problem Type)

**Given:** A single sampling plan with **n = 50**, **c = 2**. Compute **Pₐ** when incoming quality **p = 0.04**.

**Step 1:** Set up terms for d = 0, 1, 2

$$
P_a = \binom{50}{0}(0.04)^0(0.96)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.04)^1(0.96)^{49} + \binom{50}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{48}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term

$$
T_0 = 1 \times 1 \times (0.96)^{50} = 0.1299
$$

$$
T_1 = 50 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{49} = 2.0 \times 0.1353 = 0.2706
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{50 \times 49}{2} \times (0.0016) \times (0.96)^{48} = 1225 \times 0.0016 \times 0.1409 = 0.2763
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the terms

$$
P_a = 0.1299 + 0.2706 + 0.2763 = 0.6768
$$

**Interpretation:** A lot with **4% defectives** has a **67.68% probability of being accepted** under this plan.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Assume a **rectifying inspection plan**: rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced with good ones.

Let:
- **Pₐ** = probability lot is accepted on first attempt
- **(1 − Pₐ)** = probability lot is rejected → undergoes 100% inspection → all defectives replaced
- **p** = incoming fraction defective
- **N − n** = items not inspected directly (in accepted lots)

**Outgoing defective count (accepted lot):**
The expected defectives in accepted lot = $n \cdot p$ (in sample) + $(N-n) \cdot p$ (in non-sample portion)

**Total outgoing defectives per submitted lot:**

$$
\text{Defectives} = N \cdot p \cdot (1 - P_a) + n \cdot p \cdot P_a + (N-n) \cdot p \cdot P_a
$$

Wait — let me reformulate properly. The expected fraction defective **leaving** the system:

**Case 1 — Lot Accepted (probability Pₐ):** The sample of n items is replaced with good ones; the remaining (N−n) items still have p fraction defective.

$$
\text{Defectives in accepted lot} = (N-n) \cdot p
$$

**Case 2 — Lot Rejected (probability 1−Pₐ):** All N items are inspected, defectives replaced. Outgoing defectives = 0.

**Expected outgoing defectives per submitted lot:**

$$
E[D] = P_a \cdot (N-n) \cdot p + (1-P_a) \cdot 0 = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)
$$

**Average Outgoing Quality** = Expected fraction defective after inspection:

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

### Numerical Example

**Given:** N = 1000, n = 50, c = 2, p = 0.04. Find AOQ.

From earlier: $P_a = 0.6768$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.6768 \times 0.04 \times (1000 - 50)}{1000} = \frac{0.6768 \times 0.04 \times 950}{1000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{25.718}{1000} = 0.02572 \approx 2.572\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Even though incoming quality is 4%, due to rectifying inspection, the **average outgoing quality is only 2.572%**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Total Inspection (ATI)

ATI measures the average number of items inspected per submitted lot.

$$
ATI = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)
$$

**Reasoning:**
- If lot is accepted (probability Pₐ): only **n** items are inspected
- If lot is rejected (probability 1−Pₐ): all **N** items are inspected (rectifying plan)

### Numerical Example

Using previous data:

$$
ATI = 50 \times 0.6768 + 1000 \times (1 - 0.6768)
$$

$$
ATI = 33.84 + 1000 \times 0.3232 = 33.84 + 323.20 = 357.04 \text{ items}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: OC Curve Points using Two Risk Specifications

**Given:** Find single sampling plan with AQL = p₁ = 0.01, LTPD = p₂ = 0.08, α = 0.05, β = 0.10.

**Step 1:** Apply normal approximation to binomial OC curve. The acceptance condition in z-scale:

$$
z_{\alpha} = \frac{c + 0.5 - np_1}{\sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)}}
$$

$$
z_{\beta} = \frac{c + 0.5 - np_2}{\sqrt{n p_2 (1-p_2)}}
$$

**Step 2:** From standard normal tables:
$z_{\alpha} = z_{0.05} = 1.645$ (since Pₐ = 1−α at p₁)
$z_{\beta} = z_{0.10} = -1.28$ (since Pₐ = β at p₂)

**Step 3:** Set up equations:

$$
1.645 = \frac{c + 0.5 - 0.01n}{\sqrt{0.01n \times 0.99}}
$$

$$
-1.28 = \frac{c + 0.5 - 0.08n}{\sqrt{0.08n \times 0.92}}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve simultaneously. From the geometry of the OC curve, the standard approximate formulas:

$$
n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)} - z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2(1-p_2)}}{p_2 - p_1}\right)^2
$$

$$
c = n p_1 + 0.5 - z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 (1-p_1)}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting values:

$$
n = \left(\frac{1.645 \times 0.0995 - (-1.28) \times 0.2712}{0.08 - 0.01}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\frac{0.1637 + 0.3472}{0.07}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.5109}{0.07}\right)^2 = (7.299)^2 \approx 53.3
$$

Take **n = 54**

$$
c = 54 \times 0.01 + 0.5 - 1.645\sqrt{54 \times 0.01 \times 0.99} = 0.54 + 0.5 - 1.645 \times 0.731 = 0.54 + 0.5 - 1.202 = -0.162
$$

Take **c = 1** (smallest integer ≥ −0.162, actually we need c ≥ 0)

**Final Plan:** **n = 54, c = 1**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When c comes out negative, use c = 0 or c = 1 and re-check the risk specifications. If both risks cannot be met simultaneously, use a double sampling plan.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Plan Comparison

| Plan Type | Sample Size | Avg. Inspection | OC Curve Shape | Best Use |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|----------|
| Single | Fixed = n | Moderate | Smooth S-curve | Simple operations |
| Double | Smaller n₁ + n₂ | Lower ASN | Two-segment | Cost-conscious setups |
| Multiple/Sequential | Smallest | Lowest | Stepped curve | Expensive testing |
| Rectifying | n | N·(1−Pₐ)+n·Pₐ | S-curve with AOQ | Incoming material control |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Single Sampling Plan Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["LOT SUBMITTED N ITEMS"] --> nodeB["DRAW RANDOM SAMPLE n ITEMS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["INSPECT EACH SAMPLE ITEM"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"COUNT DEFECTIVES d"}
    nodeD -- d LESS THAN OR EQUAL c --> nodeE["ACCEPT THE LOT"]
    nodeD -- d GREATER THAN c --> nodeF["REJECT THE LOT"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["LOT ENTERS PRODUCTION"]
    nodeF --> nodeH{"RECTIFYING PLAN?"}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI["100 PERCENT SCREEN REPLACE DEFECTIVES"]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ["LOT RETURNED TO SUPPLIER"]
    nodeI --> nodeG
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: OC Curve Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT p INCOMING FRACTION DEFECTIVE"] --> nodeB["COMPUTE P_a USING BINOMIAL FORMULA"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["REPEAT FOR p = 0, 0.01, 0.02, ... 0.20"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PLOT P_a ON Y AXIS AGAINST p ON X AXIS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["S SHAPED SIGMOID OC CURVE OBTAINED"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["MARK AQL POINT p1 AT HIGH P_a"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["MARK LTPD POINT p2 AT LOW P_a"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["ALPHA PRODUCER RISK = 1 minus P_a AT p1"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["BETA CONSUMER RISK = P_a AT p2"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Sampling Plans Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["SAMPLING INSPECTION PLANS"] --> nodeA1["ATTRIBUTE PLANS"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA2["VARIABLE PLANS"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1["SINGLE SAMPLING"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB2["DOUBLE SAMPLING"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeB3["MULTIPLE SEQUENTIAL SAMPLING"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["DRAW n INSPECT DECIDE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC2["DRAW n1 DECIDE OR DRAW n2"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3["REPEATED SMALL SAMPLES UNTIL DECISION"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeD1["KNOWN SIGMA"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeD2["UNKNOWN SIGMA"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Risk Specification on OC Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart["OC CURVE S SHAPED"] --> nodeX["X AXIS INCOMING QUALITY p"]
    nodeStart --> nodeY["Y AXIS PROBABILITY OF ACCEPTANCE"]
    nodeStart --> nodeP1["P1 AQL HIGH ACCEPTANCE REGION"]
    nodeStart --> nodeP2["P2 LTPD LOW ACCEPTANCE REGION"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeRiskA["ALPHA PRODUCER RISK REJECTING GOOD"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeRiskB["BETA CONSUMER RISK ACCEPTING BAD"]
    nodeRiskA --> nodeDec["DESIRE STEEP CURVE BETWEEN p1 AND p2"]
    nodeRiskB --> nodeDec
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rectifying Inspection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["LOT N ITEMS"] --> nodeSample["TAKE SAMPLE n"]
    nodeSample --> nodeInspect["INSPECT n ITEMS"]
    nodeInspect --> nodeDec{"ACCEPT?"}
    nodeDec -- YES --> nodeOut["LOT ACCEPTED N minus n UNINSPECTED ITEMS SHIPPED"]
    nodeDec -- NO --> nodeFull["100 PERCENT INSPECTION OF REJECTED LOT"]
    nodeFull --> nodeReplace["REPLACE DEFECTIVES WITH GOOD ITEMS"]
    nodeReplace --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeAOQ["COMPUTE AOQ = P_a TIMES p TIMES N minus n DIVIDED N"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Fallback Rationale:** Since physical schematic drawings of OC curves cannot be rendered in plain Mermaid, the diagrams above capture the **functional architecture flow** of every key sampling inspection concept as required by V10 protocol.
