<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the following terms with reference to acceptance sampling inspection:**
(a) AQL
(b) LTPD
(c) Producer's Risk

**Model Answer:**

**(a) AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** It is the **maximum fraction defective** (p₁) in a lot that is considered tolerable as a process average. A sampling plan is designed to accept most lots at or below AQL with a high probability (typically 1 − α = 0.95).

**(b) LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** It is the **fraction defective** (p₂) in a lot that the consumer considers undesirable. The sampling plan is required to reject such lots with high probability (typically 1 − β = 0.90).

**(c) Producer's Risk (α):** It is the probability that a **good lot** (quality ≤ AQL) is **rejected** by the sampling plan. The complementary probability 1 − α = 0.95 is the probability of acceptance at AQL.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between 100% inspection and sampling inspection. State any two situations where sampling inspection is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 100% Inspection | Sampling Inspection |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Items Checked | All N items | Only n items (n < N) |
| Cost | High | Lower |
| Time | Long | Short |
| Fatigue | Inspector fatigue → errors | Less fatigue |
| Suitability | Non-destructive only | Destructive testing feasible |
| Lot Disposition | Item-by-item | Whole-lot based on sample |

**Two situations where sampling is preferred:**
1. **Destructive Testing** — e.g., tensile testing of steel rods, cartridge firing tests
2. **High-volume, low-cost items** — e.g., screws, resistors where 100% inspection cost exceeds defect cost

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** A single sampling plan has **n = 80** and **c = 3**. Calculate the probability of acceptance for an incoming lot with **p = 0.05**. Use the binomial distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to acceptance sampling, explain the concepts of **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve**, **AQL**, and **LTPD** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Solution:**

Given: n = 80, c = 3, p = 0.05, q = 0.95

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{3} \binom{80}{d} (0.05)^d (0.95)^{80-d}
$$

**Step 1:** Calculate each term:

$$
T_0 = \binom{80}{0}(0.05)^0(0.95)^{80} = 1 \times 1 \times 0.0171 = 0.0171
$$

$$
T_1 = 80 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{79} = 4.0 \times 0.0180 = 0.0720
$$

$$
T_2 = \frac{80 \times 79}{2} \times (0.05)^2 \times (0.95)^{78} = 3160 \times 0.0025 \times 0.0190 = 0.1501
$$

$$
T_3 = \frac{80 \times 79 \times 78}{6} \times (0.05)^3 \times (0.95)^{77} = 82160 \times 0.000125 \times 0.0200 = 0.2054
$$

**Step 2:** Sum:

$$
P_a = 0.0171 + 0.0720 + 0.1501 + 0.2054 = 0.4446
$$

**Answer:** $P_a \approx 0.4446$ or **44.46%**

**(b) Solution:**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a plot of the probability of lot acceptance (Pₐ) on the Y-axis versus the incoming fraction defective (p) on the X-axis. It is the single most important graphical tool for evaluating a sampling plan's performance.

**Key features:**
- The curve is **S-shaped** (sigmoidal)
- Starts near Pₐ = 1 for very good quality
- Drops to Pₐ ≈ 0 for very poor quality
- Slope between AQL and LTPD indicates **discriminating power**

**AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** This is the worst tolerable quality level p₁. At this point, the OC curve shows a high probability of acceptance (1 − α). It is a measure designed to protect the **producer**.

**LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** This is the unacceptable quality level p₂. At this point, the OC curve shows a low probability of acceptance (β). It is designed to protect the **consumer**.

**Sketch (ASCII representation for KTU answer sheet):**

```
  Pa |
 1.0 |*****
     |     ***
 0.9 |        ***  ← AQL point (p1, 1-α)
     |           ***
 0.5 |              ***
     |                 ****
  β  |-----------------------***--  ← LTPD point (p2, β)
 0.0 |__________________________*****
     0    p1   p2                  p →
         (AQL) (LTPD)
```

**Area Interpretation:**
- Region above curve near p₁ → Producer's risk α
- Region below curve near p₂ → Consumer's risk β

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the terms **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** and **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)**. A rectifying single sampling plan with **N = 2000, n = 100, c = 2** is applied. Calculate the **AOQ** and **AOQL** for an incoming quality level of **p = 0.03**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Single, Double, and Multiple Sampling Plans** with respect to sample size, OC curve shape, and administrative complexity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Solution:**

**Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):** It is the expected fraction defective in the product **after** inspection (assuming rectifying inspection — rejected lots are 100% screened and defectives replaced).

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):** It is the **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible incoming quality levels p. AOQL represents the **worst-case** average quality leaving the inspection system.

**Mathematical Formulas:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

$$
AOQL = \max_{p} \{AOQ\}
$$

**Calculation for p = 0.03:**

Step 1: Compute Pₐ using binomial

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{100}{d}(0.03)^d(0.97)^{100-d}
$$

Using Poisson approximation with λ = np = 3:

$$
P_a \approx e^{-3}\left[\frac{3^0}{0!} + \frac{3^1}{1!} + \frac{3^2}{2!}\right]
$$

$$
P_a \approx 0.0498 \times [1 + 3 + 4.5] = 0.0498 \times 8.5 = 0.4233
$$

Step 2: Apply AOQ formula

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.4233 \times 0.03 \times (2000 - 100)}{2000}
$$

$$
AOQ = \frac{0.4233 \times 0.03 \times 1900}{2000} = \frac{24.13}{2000} = 0.01207
$$

**Answer:** $AOQ \approx 0.01207$ or **1.207%**

**For AOQL** — we would need to compute AOQ for many values of p and find the maximum. Typically AOQL occurs near p ≈ c/n = 0.02.

**(b) Solution:**

| Parameter | Single Sampling | Double Sampling | Multiple Sampling |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Sample Size** | Fixed = n | n₁ then n₂ (if needed) | Several small samples |
| **Number of Stages** | One | Two | Up to seven |
| **OC Curve Shape** | Smooth S-curve | Smooth S-curve | Stepped S-curve |
| **Average Sample Number (ASN)** | n | Lower than n (typically 0.6n to 0.8n) | Lowest (≈ 0.5n) |
| **Administrative Complexity** | Simplest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Decision Speed** | Slowest (full sample needed) | Faster for good/bad lots | Fastest for good/bad lots |
| **Documentation** | Simple | More forms required | Complex record keeping |
| **Best Used When** | Simple operations, low cost per inspection | Moderate cost, need ASN reduction | High cost per inspection |

**KTU Key Insight:** As the number of stages increases from single → double → multiple, the **ASN decreases** but **administrative complexity increases**. There is a trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Sampling Inspection Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "+0.5" continuity correction** when using normal approximation → loses 2 marks per instance
> 
> 2. **Mixing up AQL and LTPD definitions** — AQL is associated with **producer's risk (α)**, LTPD with **consumer's risk (β)**. Confusing these costs 3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Not distinguishing n from N** — sample size **n** vs. lot size **N**. Many students interchange them in AOQ formula.
> 
> 4. **Using binomial when hypergeometric is required** — when n/N > 0.10, hypergeometric must be used; otherwise approximation error is penalized.
> 
> 5. **Failing to draw OC curve** in Part B questions — examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for the sketch; omit = −2 marks
> 
> 6. **Wrong summation limits in Pₐ** — summing from d=1 instead of d=0 loses full 7 marks on computation sub-question
> 
> 7. **AOQ computed without the (N−n)/N correction** — students often write AOQ = Pₐ · p only, missing the factor (N−n)/N = 1.0 when N is large but exact otherwise

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inspections by Samples**

### Core Concept Bullets
- ☐ Sampling inspection inspects only **n items** out of **N** to decide lot acceptance
- ☐ Acceptance number **c** is the maximum tolerable defectives in sample
- ☐ **Pₐ** decreases as incoming quality **p** increases
- ☐ OC curve is the graph of Pₐ vs p — always **S-shaped**
- ☐ **AQL** = producer's protection point; **LTPD** = consumer's protection point
- ☐ **α** = P(rejecting good lot) = 1 − Pₐ(AQL)
- ☐ **β** = P(accepting bad lot) = Pₐ(LTPD)
- ☐ Steeper OC curve = better discriminating plan
- ☐ **AOQ** = average quality after rectifying inspection
- ☐ **AOQL** = worst-case AOQ across all p values
- ☐ **ATI** = expected number of items inspected per submitted lot
- ☐ **ASN** = expected sample size (relevant for double/multiple plans)

### Standard KTU Formula Quick-Fire
- ☐ $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ ← **MASTER THIS**
- ☐ $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$
- ☐ $ATI = nP_a + N(1-P_a)$
- ☐ $ASN = n_1 + n_2(1-P_{a1})$

### Common Numerical Values to Memorize
- ☐ $z_{0.05} = 1.645$
- ☐ $z_{0.10} = 1.28$
- ☐ $z_{0.025} = 1.96$
- ☐ $\alpha = 0.05$ (industry default producer risk)
- ☐ $\beta = 0.10$ (industry default consumer risk)

### KTU Plan Selection Decision Heuristic
- ☐ Destructive testing → Sampling **mandatory**
- ☐ High inspection cost → Use **multiple/sequential** plan
- ☐ Low inspection cost + simple process → Use **single** plan
- ☐ Need to balance two risks (α, β) → Use **two-point** specification method
- ☐ Large lot + continuous flow → Use **rectifying** plan with AOQL
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LTPD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LTPD ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Quality Engineering and Management* (OESFT615), **LTPD** stands for **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective** (often written as **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective**, $p_1$). It is formally defined as the **level of quality in an individual lot that is considered the poorest tolerable** by the consumer, and which the consumer wishes the acceptance sampling plan to reject most of the time. Formally: > **LTPD ($p_1$)**: The percent defective in a submitted lot for which the probability of acceptance is at a specified low value $\beta$, called the **Consumer's Risk**. By KTU convention, $\beta = 0.10$ is used as the standard consumer's risk level. Thus, for a given acceptance sampling plan, if a submitted lot has fraction defective equal to $p_1 = \text{LTPD}$, then: $$P_a(p_1) = \beta = 0.10$$ This means there is only a **10% chance** that such a "bad" lot will be accepted by the sampling plan. The plan is therefore designed to protect the consumer against accepting a lot of this poor quality more than 10% of the time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** LTPD is one of the four pillar parameters of Acceptance Sampling design (the others being **AQL** = Acceptable Quality Level $p_2$, **$\alpha$** = Producer's Risk, and **$\beta$** = Consumer's Risk). KTU examiners specifically test the ability to **distinguish LTPD from AQL** and to **design single sampling plans from LTPD–$\beta$ specifications**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **wholesale buyer of mangoes** for a supermarket chain. You are willing to accept a lot of mangoes if at most **3% are rotten** (this is your AQL — acceptable quality). But you absolutely cannot tolerate a lot where **10% are rotten**, because at that level, your customers will complain and your brand reputation will suffer. So you set: - **AQL ($p_2$)** = 3% — the level above which the supplier's reputation suffers if rejected. - **LTPD ($p_1$)** = 10% — the level above which your business starts hurting badly if accepted. You tell your quality inspector: *"If I send you a lot with 10% rotten mangoes, I want you to reject it 90% of the time — I will only accept a 10% chance (consumer's risk $\beta = 0.10$) that such a bad lot slips through."* That 10% rotten threshold you just set is exactly the **LTPD**. The inspection plan is then designed so that its **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** passes through the point $(p_1, 0.10)$. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick:** **LTPD = "Low Tolerance, Poor Defect level"**. It is the **bad quality** boundary you want to catch. **AQL = "Acceptable"** — it is the **good quality** boundary the producer defends. ### 1.3 The Two Risks — Visual Anchor | Symbol | Name | Typical Value | Whose Risk? | At What Quality Level? | |--------|------|---------------|-------------|------------------------| | $\alpha$ | Producer's Risk | 0.05 | Supplier | At AQL ($p_2$) | | $\beta$ | Consumer's Risk | 0.10 | Buyer | At LTPD ($p_1$) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU values used in Board Examinations:** > - $\alpha = 0.05$ → $P_a(p_2) = 0.95$ (supplier wants 95% acceptance at AQL). > - $\beta = 0.10$ → $P_a(p_1) = 0.10$ (buyer tolerates only 10% acceptance at LTPD). --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Relationship of LTPD with the OC Curve Every acceptance sampling plan has an Operating Characteristic (OC) curve, which plots $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) against $p$ (incoming lot fraction defective). The KTU board explicitly tests the ability to read two points on this curve: 1. The **AQL point**: $(p_2, 1 - \alpha) = (p_2, 0.95)$ — desirable lot, should mostly be accepted. 2. The **LTPD point**: $(p_1, \beta) = (p_1, 0.10)$ — undesirable lot, should mostly be rejected. The OC curve is a smooth, monotonically decreasing function. As $p$ increases from $0$ to $1$, $P_a$ drops from $1$ to $0$. It passes *through* the AQL and LTPD points. > **Why this matters for LTPD:** The LTPD is **not** a probability; it is a **fraction defective level**. The "10% probability" you keep hearing about is **not** the LTPD itself — it is the consumer's risk $\beta$ evaluated *at* the LTPD. A common student error is to confuse $p_1 = 0.10$ with $\beta = 0.10$. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table is the **canonical reference** for all LTPD-related numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. | # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / When Used | |---|---------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | LTPD | $p_1$ | Lot tolerance percent defective (bad quality threshold) | | 2 | Consumer's Risk at LTPD | $P_a(p_1) = \beta$ | Prob. of accepting a lot of LTPD quality | | 3 | Standard Consumer's Risk | $\beta = 0.10$ | KTU default | | 4 | Operating Ratio (OR) | $\text{OR} = \dfrac{p_1}{p_2} = \dfrac{\text{LTPD}}{\text{AQL}}$ | Ratio measuring discrimination of the plan | | 5 | Binomial $P_a$ | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Exact probability for attribute sampling | | 6 | Poisson Approximation | $P_a \approx \displaystyle\sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 30$ | | 7 | **LTPD Design Rule (Poisson)** | $np_1 = \text{Tabulated value of } (n, c) \text{ for } P_a = 0.10$ | Used to find $(n, c)$ for given LTPD | | 8 | AOQ | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Average outgoing quality after rectifying inspection | | 9 | AOQL | $\text{AOQL} = \max_p \text{AOQ}(p)$ | Maximum average outgoing quality | | 10 | ATI | $\text{ATI} = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a)$ | Average Total Inspection | | 11 | ATI (rectifying) | $\text{ATI} = n + (N-n)(1-P_a)$ | Equivalent form for rectifying plans | | 12 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_2)$ | Prob. of rejecting a good (AQL) lot | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals:** > - LTPD is given in **percent defective** (e.g., LTPD = 4%) but used as a **decimal fraction** in formulas (e.g., $p_1 = 0.04$). > - Sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$ must both be **integers**, with $c \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, ...\}$ and $c < n$. > - Always validate that $n \cdot p_1 \le 10$ before applying the Poisson approximation. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility LTPD-based plans are the backbone of **military and aerospace inspection** (e.g., **MIL-STD-105E**, **MIL-STD-414**, **DOD-STD-105D**), **pharmaceutical batch release**, and **automotive component acceptance** (e.g., Tata Motors, Mahindra vendor quality control). When you buy a critical safety bolt for an aircraft, the buyer does not accept probabilistic AQL reasoning alone — they want a guarantee: *"If this batch contains more than LTPD% defective bolts, the plan must reject it with 90% confidence."* This contractual certainty is what LTPD provides. ### 2.4 Designing a Single Sampling Plan from LTPD Given LTPD = $p_1$ and consumer's risk $\beta$, the design procedure (KTU Board Standard): 1. Pick an integer acceptance number $c$ (usually 0, 1, 2, or 3 — the smaller the $c$, the sharper the discrimination). 2. From the Poisson $P_a$ table for $\beta = 0.10$, read the value of $np_1$ corresponding to the chosen $c$. Call it $c_1$ (KTU notation). 3. Compute the sample size: $n = \dfrac{c_1}{p_1}$. 4. Verify Producer's Risk: ensure that $P_a(p_2) \ge 0.95$ (i.e., $\alpha \le 0.05$). If not, choose a different $c$ or relax the LTPD. > **Tabulated $c_1$ values for $\beta = 0.10$ (Poisson):** > - $c = 0 \Rightarrow np_1 = 2.303$ > - $c = 1 \Rightarrow np_1 = 3.890$ > - $c = 2 \Rightarrow np_1 = 5.322$ > - $c = 3 \Rightarrow np_1 = 6.681$ > - $c = 4 \Rightarrow np_1 = 7.994$ > - $c = 5 \Rightarrow np_1 = 9.275$ --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why $np_1$ Determines $(n, c)$ **Step 1 — Start with the Poisson approximation to the Binomial:** $$P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^d}{d!}$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate at LTPD ($p = p_1$):** By definition of LTPD, $P_a(p_1) = \beta$. Let $\lambda_1 = n p_1$. Then: $$\sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^d}{d!} = \beta$$ **Step 3 — Tabulate this equation for fixed $\beta$:** For each integer $c$, the equation $\sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda_1} \lambda_1^d}{d!} = \beta$ has a unique positive solution $\lambda_1$. By KTU convention with $\beta = 0.10$, this solution is tabulated as $c_1$. **Step 4 — Solve for sample size $n$:** Since $\lambda_1 = n p_1$, we get: $$n = \frac{c_1}{p_1}$$ > **Logic Conversion:** We are inverting the Poisson CDF: instead of asking *"given $n, c$, what is $P_a$?"* we ask *"given $P_a = 0.10$, what combination of $n$ and $c$ makes this true?"* The tabulated $c_1$ values encode the $n$ component implicitly via the product $n p_1$. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Single Plan Design (KTU Typical 14-Mark Format) **Problem Statement (KTU Past Year Style):** Design a single sampling plan for attribute inspection such that **LTPD = 4%** and **consumer's risk $\beta = 0.10$**. The lot size is $N = 1500$. The acceptable quality level is AQL = 0.5%. Verify the producer's risk. Compute AOQL. **Step 1 — Choose acceptance number $c$:** Try $c = 1$. From the LTPD table, $np_1 = c_1 = 3.890$ for $c=1$. **Step 2 — Compute sample size $n$:** $$n = \frac{c_1}{p_1} = \frac{3.890}{0.04} = 97.25$$ Round **up** to ensure $P_a(p_1) \le 0.10$: $$n = 98$$ **Step 3 — Verify Consumer's Risk (recompute Poisson $P_a$):** $$\lambda_1 = n p_1 = 98 \times 0.04 = 3.92$$ $$P_a(0.04) = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \frac{e^{-3.92} (3.92)^d}{d!}$$ $$= e^{-3.92} \left[ 1 + 3.92 \right]$$ $$= 0.01991 \times 4.92 = 0.0979 \approx 0.10 \;\; \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Verify Producer's Risk at AQL = 0.005:** $$\lambda_2 = n p_2 = 98 \times 0.005 = 0.49$$ $$P_a(0.005) = \sum_{d=0}^{1} \frac{e^{-0.49} (0.49)^d}{d!} = e^{-0.49}(1 + 0.49)$$ $$= 0.6126 \times 1.49 = 0.9128$$ Producer's risk $\alpha = 1 - 0.9128 = 0.0872$. This is **greater than 0.05**, so the plan rejects good lots too often. **Step 5 — Adjust $c$ upward. Try $c = 2$:** Tabulated $c_1 = 5.322$ for $c = 2$. $$n = \frac{5.322}{0.04} = 133.05 \Rightarrow n = 134$$ Verify producer's risk at AQL: $$\lambda_2 = 134 \times 0.005 = 0.670$$ $$P_a(0.005) = e^{-0.670} \left[ 1 + 0.670 + \frac{0.670^2}{2} \right]$$ $$= 0.5117 \times \left[ 1 + 0.670 + 0.2245 \right] = 0.5117 \times 1.8945 = 0.9694$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.9694 = 0.0306 < 0.05 \;\; \checkmark$$ **Plan adopted: $n = 134$, $c = 2$.** **Step 6 — Compute AOQL (using AOQ formula):** $$\text{AOQ}(p) = \frac{p \cdot P_a(p) \cdot (N - n)}{N}$$ We evaluate AOQ at several values of $p$ to find the maximum. At $p = 0.04$: $$\text{AOQ}(0.04) = \frac{0.04 \times 0.10 \times (1500 - 134)}{1500} = \frac{0.04 \times 0.10 \times 1366}{1500}$$ $$= \frac{5.464}{1500} = 0.00364 = 0.364\%$$ Check nearby points. The maximum typically occurs near $p_1$. Reporting **AOQL ≈ 0.36% to 0.40%** is acceptable for KTU valuation. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing LTPD Plans Across $c$ Values For fixed $p_1 = 0.04$, the table below shows how plan parameters evolve: | $c$ | $c_1$ (at $\beta = 0.10$) | $n = c_1 / p_1$ | $P_a(p_2)$ at $p_2 = 0.005$ | $\alpha$ | Discrimination | |-----|---------------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|----------|----------------| | 0 | 2.303 | 58 | $e^{-0.29}(1+0.29) = 0.836$ | 0.164 | Sharpest (rejects bad fast, accepts good poorly) | | 1 | 3.890 | 98 | 0.913 | 0.087 | Better balance | | 2 | 5.322 | 134 | 0.969 | 0.031 | KTU-preferred balance | | 3 | 6.681 | 168 | 0.985 | 0.015 | Lenient — accepts most lots | > **Engineering Insight:** As $c$ increases, the **OC curve becomes less steep** (flatter), the operating ratio $p_1/p_2$ changes, and discrimination weakens. KTU Board examiners often ask students to **justify their choice of $c$** by simultaneously satisfying both producer's and consumer's risk constraints. ### 3.4 LTPD and the Operating Ratio (OR) — Full Derivation The **Operating Ratio** is defined as: $$\text{OR} = \frac{p_1}{p_2}$$ The classical KTU theorem states: > **If an attribute single sampling plan satisfies $P_a(p_1) = 0.10$ and $P_a(p_2) = 0.95$, then the operating ratio $p_1 / p_2$ must lie in a specific narrow range that depends only on $c$, and is tabulated as the "Discrimination Ratio".** For $c = 0$: OR $\approx 6.50$ For $c = 1$: OR $\approx 3.93$ For $c = 2$: OR $\approx 2.97$ For $c = 3$: OR $\approx 2.50$ For $c = 5$: OR $\approx 2.06$ **Derivation Sketch:** Starting from two Poisson equations: $$\sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-n p_2} (n p_2)^d}{d!} = 0.95$$ $$\sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-n p_1} (n p_1)^d}{d!} = 0.10$$ Divide the two product equations and eliminate $n$ via the tabulated $c_1, c_2$ values: $$p_1 = \frac{c_1}{n}, \quad p_2 = \frac{c_2}{n}$$ $$\text{OR} = \frac{p_1}{p_2} = \frac{c_1}{c_2}$$ > **Logic Conversion:** Since $c_1$ and $c_2$ are tabulated constants for a given $c$, the OR is itself a tabulated constant. This is why LTPD plans are easy to standardize — given a desired OR, you look up the right $c$. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OC Curve with LTPD and AQL Anchored The diagram below shows the canonical Operating Characteristic curve with LTPD ($p_1$) and AQL ($p_2$) marked, illustrating the consumer's risk and producer's risk regions. ```mermaid graph LR axisA["P_a Axis
Probability of Acceptance"] --> originA["0.0"] originA --> pt1["0.10
Consumer Risk Level"] pt1 --> pt2["0.50
Indifference Point"] pt2 --> pt3["0.90
High Acceptance Zone"] pt3 --> pt4["0.95
Producer Risk Level"] pt4 --> pt5["1.00
Certain Acceptance"] axisB["p Axis
Fraction Defective"] --> originB["0.00"] originB --> q1["p_2 = AQL
Acceptable Quality"] q1 --> q2["p = 0.50"] q2 --> q3["p_1 = LTPD
Lot Tolerance"] q3 --> q4["p = 1.00"] pt1 -.Consumer Risk Beta.-> q3 pt4 -.Producer Risk alpha.-> q1 pt2 -.OCI.-> q2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The horizontal dashed line at $P_a = 0.10$ intersects the OC curve at the LTPD point. The horizontal line at $P_a = 0.95$ intersects it at the AQL point. The vertical band between $p_2$ and $p_1$ is the **Indifference Zone** — KTU boards frequently ask students to shade this region. ### 4.2 LTPD Plan Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Start: Given LTPD p_1 and Beta = 0.10"] pickC["Step 1: Choose acceptance number c
typically 0, 1, 2, or 3"] lookup["Step 2: Look up c_1 from Poisson table
for chosen c at P_a = 0.10"] computeN["Step 3: Compute sample size
n = c_1 divided by p_1"] roundN["Step 4: Round n upward to integer"] checkA["Step 5: Verify Producer risk
P_a at p_2 must be at least 0.95"] decideA{"Step 6: Is alpha less than 0.05?"} finalPlan["Step 7: Adopt plan n, c
Report AOQ and AOQL"] retry["Step 8: Increase c by 1 and go back to Step 2"] startA --> pickC --> lookup --> computeN --> roundN --> checkA --> decideA decideA -- Yes --> finalPlan decideA -- No --> retry --> pickC ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Map — LTPD within Acceptance Sampling ```mermaid graph TD QSE["Quality Engineering and Management"] --> AS["Acceptance Sampling"] AS --> ASP["Attribute Sampling Plans"] ASP --> SSP["Single Sampling Plan
n, c"] ASP --> DSP["Double Sampling Plan
n1, n2, c1, c2"] ASP --> RECT["Rectifying Inspection Plans"] SSP --> KP1["Key Parameter: AQL p_2"] SSP --> KP2["Key Parameter: LTPD p_1"] SSP --> KP3["Key Parameter: Producer Risk alpha"] SSP --> KP4["Key Parameter: Consumer Risk beta"] RECT --> AOQ1["AOQ Formula uses n from LTPD design"] RECT --> AOQL1["AOQL is maximum of AOQ curve"] RECT --> ATI1["ATI depends on n and P_a"] KP2 -.Derives.-> AOQ1 KP2 -.Derives.-> AOQL1 ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD). State its relationship with the consumer's risk.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for risk link, 1 for numerical example):** Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD), denoted $p_1$, is the level of quality in an individual submitted lot which is considered the poorest tolerable by the consumer. It is the fraction defective for which the probability of acceptance is at the consumer's risk level $\beta$. By KTU convention, $\beta = 0.10$ is used. Mathematically: $P_a(p_1) = \beta = 0.10$. For example, if LTPD = 4%, then the sampling plan is designed such that lots containing 4% defective items are accepted only 10% of the time, providing a 90% probability of rejection for such poor-quality lots. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Distinguish between AQL and LTPD with respect to the stakeholder they protect and the typical numerical value of associated risk.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | AQL ($p_2$) | LTPD ($p_1$) | |--------|-------------|--------------| | Stakeholder protected | Producer / Supplier | Consumer / Buyer | | Risk parameter | $\alpha$ (Producer's Risk) | $\beta$ (Consumer's Risk) | | Typical KTU value | $\alpha = 0.05$ | $\beta = 0.10$ | | Interpretation | Acceptable quality level — should be accepted | Lot tolerance level — should mostly be rejected | | OC curve point | $(p_2, 0.95)$ | $(p_1, 0.10)$ | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **(a)** Define LTPD and Consumer's Risk. Explain the significance of the LTPD in the design of a single sampling plan. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a single sampling plan with lot size $N = 2000$, the following specifications are given: - LTPD = 5% with $\beta = 0.10$ - AQL = 0.8% with $\alpha = 0.05$ Design the plan (find $n$ and $c$) and compute the AOQL. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks — 2 for definitions, 3 for design significance, 2 for OC curve role]** LTPD ($p_1$) is the maximum fraction defective that the consumer is willing to tolerate in an individual lot. The consumer's risk $\beta$ is the probability that a lot of LTPD quality will be accepted by the sampling plan. Significance in design: - LTPD fixes the **point on the OC curve** at $P_a(p_1) = \beta = 0.10$. - This anchor point, together with AQL at $P_a(p_2) = 0.95$, defines the **two reference points** the OC curve must satisfy. - From the LTPD equation, the sample size $n$ is uniquely determined once $c$ is chosen: $n = c_1 / p_1$. - The LTPD thus acts as the **design pivot** for consumer-side quality protection. **(b) [7 marks — 1 for table reference, 2 for $n$ calculation, 2 for verification, 2 for AOQL]** Given: $p_1 = 0.05$, $\beta = 0.10$, $p_2 = 0.008$, $\alpha = 0.05$. Try $c = 1$: Tabulated $c_1 = 3.890$. $$n = \frac{3.890}{0.05} = 77.8 \Rightarrow n = 78$$ Verify producer's risk: $$\lambda_2 = n p_2 = 78 \times 0.008 = 0.624$$ $$P_a(0.008) = e^{-0.624} \left[ 1 + 0.624 \right] = 0.5358 \times 1.624 = 0.870$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.870 = 0.13 > 0.05 \;\; \text{(FAIL)}$$ Try $c = 2$: $c_1 = 5.322$. $$n = \frac{5.322}{0.05} = 106.44 \Rightarrow n = 107$$ Verify: $$\lambda_2 = 107 \times 0.008 = 0.856$$ $$P_a(0.008) = e^{-0.856} \left[ 1 + 0.856 + \frac{0.856^2}{2} \right]$$ $$= 0.4248 \times \left[ 1 + 0.856 + 0.366 \right] = 0.4248 \times 2.222 = 0.944$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.944 = 0.056 \approx 0.05 \;\; \text{(ACCEPTABLE)}$$ **Plan adopted: $n = 107$, $c = 2$.** Compute AOQL (approximate at $p = p_1 = 0.05$): $$\text{AOQL} \approx \frac{p_1 \cdot P_a(p_1) \cdot (N-n)}{N} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.10 \times (2000 - 107)}{2000}$$ $$= \frac{0.005 \times 1893}{2000} = \frac{9.465}{2000} = 0.004733 = 0.4733\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{AOQL} \approx 0.47\%}$$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Alternative Choice] **(a)** What is the Operating Ratio of an acceptance sampling plan? How is it related to LTPD and AQL? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A company wants to design a single sampling plan such that the LTPD is 3% and the consumer's risk is 0.10. The AQL is 0.5% and the producer's risk is 0.05. Determine the sample size and acceptance number. Use the tabulated values: - For $c = 1$: $c_1 = 3.890$, $c_2 = 0.355$ (at $P_a = 0.95$) - For $c = 2$: $c_1 = 5.322$, $c_2 = 1.020$ - For $c = 3$: $c_1 = 6.681$, $c_2 = 1.820$ **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** The Operating Ratio (OR) of an acceptance sampling plan is defined as the ratio of the lot tolerance percent defective to the acceptable quality level: $$\text{OR} = \frac{p_1}{p_2} = \frac{\text{LTPD}}{\text{AQL}}$$ It measures the **discriminating ability** of the plan. A small OR indicates a sharp plan (steep OC curve) — it can clearly distinguish between acceptable and unacceptable quality. A large OR indicates a lenient plan (gradual OC curve). For an LTPD plan with $\beta = 0.10$ and $\alpha = 0.05$, the OR depends only on $c$ and equals $c_1 / c_2$ where $c_1, c_2$ are tabulated Poisson values. **(b) [7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 per attempt, 2 for final choice]** Compute $n = c_1 / p_1$ and verify $P_a(p_2) \ge 0.95$. **Try $c = 1$:** $n = 3.890 / 0.03 = 129.67 \Rightarrow n = 130$ $n \cdot p_2 = 130 \times 0.005 = 0.650$ $P_a(0.005) = e^{-0.65}(1 + 0.65) = 0.522 \times 1.65 = 0.861 < 0.95$ (FAIL) **Try $c = 2$:** $n = 5.322 / 0.03 = 177.4 \Rightarrow n = 178$ $n \cdot p_2 = 178 \times 0.005 = 0.890$ $P_a(0.005) = e^{-0.89}\left[1 + 0.89 + \frac{0.89^2}{2}\right] = 0.4107 \times [1 + 0.89 + 0.396] = 0.4107 \times 2.286 = 0.939 < 0.95$ (FAIL) **Try $c = 3$:** $n = 6.681 / 0.03 = 222.7 \Rightarrow n = 223$ $n \cdot p_2 = 223 \times 0.005 = 1.115$ $P_a(0.005) = e^{-1.115}\left[1 + 1.115 + \frac{1.115^2}{2} + \frac{1.115^3}{6}\right]$ $= 0.3278 \times [1 + 1.115 + 0.6216 + 0.2311]$ $= 0.3278 \times 2.968 = 0.973 > 0.95 \;\; \checkmark$ **Plan adopted: $n = 223$, $c = 3$.** Operating Ratio: $$\text{OR} = \frac{c_1}{c_2} = \frac{6.681}{1.820} = 3.67$$ (Verify: $p_1 / p_2 = 0.03 / 0.005 = 6.0$ — discrepancy arises because $c_2$ value of 1.820 corresponds to $P_a = 0.95$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ exactly; the OR tabulated in design tables is the *theoretical* ratio assuming both Poisson equations hold exactly with the tabulated constants.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most common deduction (-2 marks):** Students often forget to **round $n$ upward** to satisfy $\beta \le 0.10$. If $n = c_1 / p_1 = 97.25$, using $n = 97$ instead of $98$ violates the LTPD constraint. > 2. **Common confusion (-1 mark):** Writing "$p_1 = 0.10$" because $\beta = 0.10$. The LTPD is **a quality level** (e.g., 4%), and $\beta$ is **a probability** (e.g., 0.10). They are *both* numbers around 0.04 to 0.10 but play completely different roles. > 3. **Verification omitted (-1 mark):** After designing $n, c$ from LTPD, the plan must always be **checked against the AQL constraint**. Designing without verification is incomplete. > 4. **Unit error (-1 mark):** Forgetting to convert LTPD from **percent** (4%) to **decimal fraction** (0.04) before substituting into $n = c_1 / p_1$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **LTPD ($p_1$)** = Lot Tolerance Percent Defective, the "poor quality" boundary the consumer wishes to reject most of the time. - The defining equation is **$P_a(p_1) = \beta$**, with KTU standard $\beta = 0.10$. - LTPD protects the **consumer**; AQL protects the **producer**. They form the **two anchor points** of the OC curve: $(p_2, 0.95)$ and $(p_1, 0.10)$. - The **design formula** for a single sampling plan from LTPD is: $n = c_1 / p_1$, where $c_1$ is the tabulated Poisson constant for the chosen $c$ at $P_a = 0.10$. - Standard $c_1$ values: $c=0 \Rightarrow 2.303$; $c=1 \Rightarrow 3.890$; $c=2 \Rightarrow 5.322$; $c=3 \Rightarrow 6.681$. - The **Operating Ratio** $\text{OR} = p_1 / p_2 = c_1 / c_2$ is a measure of plan discrimination and depends only on $c$. - After designing for LTPD, **always verify** the plan at AQL to ensure $\alpha \le 0.05$. If violated, increase $c$ by 1 and redo. - LTPD design is essential for **rectifying inspection plans**, where AOQ and AOQL are computed using $n$ obtained from LTPD. - The Poisson approximation to the binomial is valid when $p \le 0.10$ and $n p \le 10$. - **AOQ** = $\dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ — average outgoing quality after rectifying inspection. - **AOQL** = maximum value of AOQ over all $p$, typically reported at or near $p = p_1$. - **ATI** = $n + (N-n)(1 - P_a)$ — average total inspection in a rectifying plan. - Common student error: confusing $p_1$ (a fraction defective) with $\beta$ (a probability of acceptance). They are **dimensionally and conceptually different**. - Always **round up** sample size $n$ to the next integer to guarantee $P_a(p_1) \le 0.10$. ---

topics.title: Consumer's and Producer's Risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Consumer's and Producer's Risk # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Acceptance Sampling by Attributes**, every sampling decision is taken under uncertainty. The two fundamental risks that govern the performance of any single sampling plan are: **Producer's Risk (α — Alpha Risk)** It is the probability of **rejecting a lot** that is actually of **acceptable quality** (i.e., conforming to the AQL). Mathematically, it is the probability of a *Type I Error* in sampling inspection. $$\boxed{\alpha = P(\text{Reject lot} \mid p = p_1 = \text{AQL}) = 1 - P_a(p_1)}$$ **Consumer's Risk (β — Beta Risk)** It is the probability of **accepting a lot** that is actually of **poor/unacceptable quality** (i.e., conforming to the LTPD). Mathematically, it is the probability of a *Type II Error* in sampling inspection. $$\boxed{\beta = P(\text{Accept lot} \mid p = p_2 = \text{LTPD}) = P_a(p_2)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two terms are *not symmetric*. The producer's risk applies at the **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** and the consumer's risk applies at the **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)**. These risks are visualised directly on the **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** of the sampling plan. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blood Test" Parallel Imagine a doctor running a screening test for a disease: | Medical Scenario | Quality Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Patient is healthy, but test reports "diseased" | Producer's Risk (α) — Good lot wrongly rejected | | Patient is diseased, but test reports "healthy" | Consumer's Risk (β) — Bad lot wrongly accepted | | Probability of correctly detecting disease | Power of the test = 1 − β | | Strict threshold of the test | Acceptance Number **c** | | Number of patients sampled | Sample Size **n** | > A tighter test (more samples, lower c) reduces both risks — but increases inspection cost. The art of **Quality Engineering** lies in balancing these two risks against the cost of inspection. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Industrial Convention:** Typical KTU-recommended values are **α = 0.05** (5%) and **β = 0.10** (10%). The corresponding lot quality points are the **Producer's Risk Point (PRP)** and the **Consumer's Risk Point (CRP)** on the OC curve. --- ## 1.3 Critical Terminology — AQL vs LTPD > [!NOTE] > **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level), denoted p₁:** > The **maximum** fraction defective that can be tolerated as a *process average*. Lots at or below AQL should be accepted most of the time (1 − α probability). > [!NOTE] > **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective), denoted p₂:** > The **worst** fraction defective that the consumer is willing to accept in an *individual* lot. Lots at LTPD should be rejected most of the time (1 − β probability). Also called the **LQL (Limiting Quality Level)**. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Industry-Standard Metrics The following are the **standardised benchmark values** universally used in KTU problems: | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Producer's Risk | **α** | **0.05** (5%) | Rejecting a good lot | | Consumer's Risk | **β** | **0.10** (10%) | Accepting a bad lot | | Operating Ratio | **p₂ / p₁** | 2 to 10 | Sharpness of discrimination | | Sample Size | **n** | Variable | Number of items inspected | | Acceptance Number | **c** | Non-negative integer | Maximum defects allowed | --- ## 1.5 Engineering Significance In real industries (automotive, pharmaceutical, electronics), it is **physically impossible** to inspect 100% of items (destructive testing, time, cost). Hence we sample. The OC curve and the twin risks of **α** and **β** mathematically guarantee that the *long-run* percentage of bad lots accepted is bounded, even though an *individual* lot decision may be wrong. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Conceptual Foundation The OC curve is the **graphical fingerprint** of any single sampling plan. It plots the **probability of acceptance, Pₐ(p)** as a function of the **incoming lot fraction defective, p**. $$P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$ For large n and small p, the **Poisson approximation** is used (this is the form heavily tested in KTU): $$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^x}{x!}$$ ### Why Each Step Works — The Logic Breakdown 1. **Sample of size n is drawn** at random from the lot. 2. The number of defectives in the sample, **X**, follows a **Binomial distribution** B(n, p). 3. The lot is accepted **if and only if** the number of defectives in the sample is **≤ c** (the acceptance number). 4. Therefore, Pₐ(p) = P(X ≤ c) — a cumulative probability calculation. 5. The OC curve is the locus of (p, Pₐ(p)) for all p ∈ [0, 1]. --- ## 2.2 Shape and Properties of the OC Curve | Property | Description | |---|---| | Monotonic | Pₐ(p) is a *strictly decreasing* function of p | | Boundaries | Pₐ(0) = 1, Pₐ(1) = 0 (for c finite) | | Inflection | The curve is **convex (bulging)** for a "good" plan — sharp drop between p₁ and p₂ | | Ideal OC | Vertical line at p = p₁ — perfect discrimination (impossible in practice) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **steeper (more vertical)** the OC curve in the region between AQL and LTPD, the **better** the sampling plan — it discriminates sharply between good and bad lots. --- ## 2.3 The Two Risk Points on the OC Curve ``` P_a(p) 1.0 |●(0,1) | \ 1-α |●\___ <-- Producer's Risk Point: (p₁, 1−α) | \___ | \___ β |●___________\___ | \___ <-- Consumer's Risk Point: (p₂, β) 0.0 |________________●(1,0) 0 p₁ p₂ 1 AQL LTPD Fraction Defective (p) ``` - At **p = p₁ (AQL)** → Pₐ = 1 − α ⇒ probability of rejection = α (Producer's Risk) - At **p = p₂ (LTPD)** → Pₐ = β ⇒ probability of acceptance = β (Consumer's Risk) --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **master reference** for all numerical problems on this topic in KTU ESE 2024. | # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Boundary / Remark | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Acceptance Probability (Binomial) | $P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | Exact form | | 2 | Acceptance Probability (Poisson) | $P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^x}{x!}$ | Used when np ≤ 5 or n large, p small | | 3 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | Risk at AQL | | 4 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | Risk at LTPD | | 5 | Mean Number of Defects | $\lambda = np$ | Poisson parameter | | 6 | Operating Ratio | $R = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ | Where $d_1 = np_1$, $d_2 = np_2$ | | 7 | Design Equation — Sample Size | $n = \dfrac{d_1}{p_1} = \dfrac{d_2}{p_2}$ | d₁, d₂ from operating ratio tables | | 8 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | After rectifying inspection | | 9 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ | With rectifying plan | | 10 | Indifference Quality (p*) | $P_a(p^*) = 0.50$ | Median of OC curve | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the α–β Framework This dual-risk framework is **universal** in engineering decision-making: - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** α = risk of rejecting a valid drug batch; β = risk of releasing a contaminated batch. - **Aerospace (NDT):** α = false alarm on a crack-free component; β = missed detection of a critical flaw. - **Software QA:** α = rejecting a bug-free release; β = shipping a release with critical bugs. - **Automotive Supplier Chain:** α = rejected shipments from a *good* supplier (cost); β = accepted shipments from a *bad* supplier (recall cost). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 General Derivation of Pₐ(p) Under Poisson Approximation Let X be the number of defectives in a sample of size n drawn from a lot with fraction defective p. Then X ~ Binomial(n, p). For the KTU convention (small p, moderate n), we invoke the Poisson limit theorem: $$X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda), \quad \lambda = np$$ Hence, the probability of exactly *x* defectives in the sample is: $$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ The lot is accepted if X ≤ c. Therefore: $$P_a(p) = P(X \leq c) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^x}{x!}$$ This is the **master equation** of acceptance sampling by attributes. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Verifying α and β for a Given Plan > **Given:** n = 100, c = 2, AQL (p₁) = 0.02, LTPD (p₂) = 0.08 > **Find:** Producer's risk (α) and Consumer's risk (β) **Step 1 — Compute the Poisson parameters at AQL and LTPD** $$\lambda_1 = n \cdot p_1 = 100 \times 0.02 = 2.0$$ $$\lambda_2 = n \cdot p_2 = 100 \times 0.08 = 8.0$$ **Step 2 — Compute Pₐ at AQL using Poisson(λ₁ = 2.0)** $$P_a(p_1) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-2}(2)^x}{x!}$$ Expanding term-by-term: $$P(X=0) = e^{-2} \cdot \frac{2^0}{0!} = 0.1353 \cdot 1.0000 = 0.1353$$ $$P(X=1) = e^{-2} \cdot \frac{2^1}{1!} = 0.1353 \cdot 2.0000 = 0.2707$$ $$P(X=2) = e^{-2} \cdot \frac{2^2}{2!} = 0.1353 \cdot 2.0000 = 0.2707$$ $$P_a(p_1) = 0.1353 + 0.2707 + 0.2707 = 0.6767$$ **Step 3 — Producer's Risk (α)** $$\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.6767 = 0.3233 \quad (\approx 32.33\%)$$ **Step 4 — Compute Pₐ at LTPD using Poisson(λ₂ = 8.0)** $$P_a(p_2) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-8}(8)^x}{x!}$$ $$P(X=0) = e^{-8} \cdot 1 = 0.000335$$ $$P(X=1) = e^{-8} \cdot 8 = 0.002684$$ $$P(X=2) = e^{-8} \cdot 32 = 0.010735$$ $$P_a(p_2) = 0.000335 + 0.002684 + 0.010735 = 0.01375$$ **Step 5 — Consumer's Risk (β)** $$\beta = P_a(p_2) = 0.01375 \quad (\approx 1.38\%)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** This plan (n = 100, c = 2) has **too high a producer's risk (32.33%)**. Although consumer's risk is low (1.38%), the producer suffers massive false rejections. Hence this plan does **not** meet the standard α = 0.05 requirement. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Designing a Sampling Plan > **Given:** AQL (p₁) = 0.01, LTPD (p₂) = 0.05, α = 0.05, β = 0.10 > **Find:** Sample size (n) and acceptance number (c) **Step 1 — Compute the Operating Ratio** $$R = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{0.05}{0.01} = 5.0$$ **Step 2 — Enter the Standard α = 0.05, β = 0.10 Design Table** From the standard operating-ratio design table for α = 0.05 and β = 0.10: | c | d₁ | d₂ | d₂/d₁ (≈ p₂/p₁) | |---|---|---|---| | 3 | 1.37 | 6.78 | 4.95 | | 4 | 1.97 | 7.98 | 4.05 | | 5 | 2.61 | 9.13 | 3.50 | For R = 5.0, the closest match is **c = 3** (gives 4.95 ≈ 5.0). So we select: **c = 3, d₁ = 1.37, d₂ = 6.78** **Step 3 — Compute the Sample Size** $$n = \frac{d_1}{p_1} = \frac{1.37}{0.01} = 137$$ $$n = \frac{d_2}{p_2} = \frac{6.78}{0.05} = 135.6 \approx 136$$ We use the more conservative (larger) value: **n = 137** **Step 4 — Verification of α at AQL** $$\lambda_1 = n p_1 = 137 \times 0.01 = 1.37$$ $$P_a(p_1) = \sum_{x=0}^{3} \frac{e^{-1.37}(1.37)^x}{x!}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-1.37} \cdot 1 = 0.2542 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-1.37} \cdot 1.37 = 0.3482 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-1.37} \cdot \frac{1.37^2}{2} = 0.2385 \\ P(X=3) &= e^{-1.37} \cdot \frac{1.37^3}{6} = 0.1089 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(p_1) = 0.2542 + 0.3482 + 0.2385 + 0.1089 = 0.9498 \approx 0.95$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Verification of β at LTPD** $$\lambda_2 = n p_2 = 137 \times 0.05 = 6.85$$ $$P_a(p_2) = \sum_{x=0}^{3} \frac{e^{-6.85}(6.85)^x}{x!}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-6.85} \cdot 1 = 0.00106 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-6.85} \cdot 6.85 = 0.00726 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-6.85} \cdot \frac{6.85^2}{2} = 0.02487 \\ P(X=3) &= e^{-6.85} \cdot \frac{6.85^3}{6} = 0.05678 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(p_2) = 0.00106 + 0.00726 + 0.02487 + 0.05678 = 0.08997 \approx 0.09$$ $$\beta \approx 0.09 \approx 0.10 \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Designed Plan: n = 137, c = 3** > This plan simultaneously meets α = 0.05 at AQL = 1% and β = 0.10 at LTPD = 5%. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — α-Risk vs β-Risk | Dimension | Producer's Risk (α) | Consumer's Risk (β) | |---|---|---| | **Quality point on OC** | AQL (p₁) | LTPD (p₂) | | **Decision at point** | Reject a good lot | Accept a bad lot | | **Prob. of acceptance Pₐ** | 1 − α (high, typically 0.95) | β (low, typically 0.10) | | **Prob. of rejection** | α (typically 0.05) | 1 − β (typically 0.90) | | **Party suffering** | Producer / Seller | Consumer / Buyer | | **Cost consequence** | Lost sale, goodwill | Warranty, recall, brand damage | | **Statistical analogy** | Type I Error | Type II Error | | **Power of plan** | Increases as α decreases | Increases as β decreases | | **Effect of ↑ n** | α decreases | β decreases | | **Effect of ↑ c** | α increases | β decreases | --- ## 3.5 Limiting-Case Behaviour (Asymptotic Analysis) | Case | Behaviour | Pₐ(p₁) | Pₐ(p₂) | |---|---|---|---| | c = 0, n → ∞ | Perfect inspection (zero-acceptance) | → 0 if p₁ > 0 | → 0 | | c → ∞, n fixed | No rejection — all lots accepted | → 1 | → 1 | | n → ∞ (fixed c/n ratio) | Approximates 100% inspection | → 0 (if p₁ > 0) | → 0 | | p₁ = p₂ (single point) | Cannot satisfy both α and β | Indeterminate | Indeterminate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Acceptance Sampling Decision Process The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete decision flow** of a single sampling plan — from lot arrival to disposition — and highlights the **exact branches where α and β risks materialise**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INCOMING LOT
of size N, fraction defective p"] --> B["DRAW RANDOM SAMPLE
of size n"] B --> C["INSPECT SAMPLE
Count defectives D"] C --> D{"D LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO c ?"} D -- "YES D LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO c" --> E["ACCEPT LOT
Ship to consumer"] D -- "NO D GREATER THAN c" --> F["REJECT LOT
Return to producer"] E --> G{"TRUE QUALITY p ?"} F --> H{"TRUE QUALITY p ?"} G -- "p LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO p1 GOOD" --> I["CORRECT DECISION
Probability equals 1 minus alpha"] G -- "p GREATER THAN p2 BAD" --> J["WRONG DECISION
Consumer's risk beta"] H -- "p LESS THAN OR EQUAL TO p1 GOOD" --> K["WRONG DECISION
Producer's risk alpha"] H -- "p GREATER THAN p2 BAD" --> L["CORRECT DECISION
Probability equals 1 minus beta"] classDef correct fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#1a7a1a,color:#000 classDef wrong fill:#f7d4d4,stroke:#a11a1a,color:#000 classDef start fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#0a3a8a,color:#000 classDef neutral fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#8a6a00,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H start class I,L correct class J,K wrong ``` --- ## 4.2 OC-Curve Architecture — Mapping the Two Risk Zones The block diagram below is a *functional abstraction* of the OC curve, partitioning the (p, Pₐ) plane into the four regions corresponding to correct decisions and the two risk zones. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INSP["ACCEPTANCE SAMPLING PLAN n, c"] A0["SAMPLE INSPECTION
Binomial or Poisson model"] end A0 --> P1["P of a evaluated at p1 AQL"] A0 --> P2["P of a evaluated at p2 LTPD"] P1 --> R1["Producer's Point Pa equals 1 minus alpha HIGH acceptance"] P2 --> R2["Consumer's Point Pa equals beta LOW acceptance"] R1 --> Z1["ZONE 1 Producer's Risk alpha
Rejecting a good lot"] R2 --> Z2["ZONE 2 Consumer's Risk beta
Accepting a bad lot"] R1 --> OK1["CORRECT Accepting good lot 1 minus alpha"] R2 --> OK2["CORRECT Rejecting bad lot 1 minus beta"] classDef risk fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,color:#000 classDef ok fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#060,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#06a,color:#000 class Z1,Z2 risk class OK1,OK2 ok class A0,P1,P2,R1,R2 calc ``` --- ## 4.3 Risk-Parameter Interaction Map The following diagram shows how the four key design parameters (n, c, p₁, p₂) interact to determine α and β. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["INPUT AQL equals p1"] --> OR["OPERATING RATIO R equals p2 divided by p1"] P2["INPUT LTPD equals p2"] --> OR OR --> TBL["LOOK UP TABLE alpha equals 0.05 beta equals 0.10"] TBL --> CVAL["OUTPUT c, d1, d2"] CVAL --> NSIZE["n equals d1 divided by p1"] NSIZE --> PLAN["FINAL PLAN n, c"] PLAN --> VAL1["VERIFY Pa at p1 equals 0.95"] PLAN --> VAL2["VERIFY Pa at p2 equals 0.10"] VAL1 --> END["APPROVED SAMPLING PLAN"] VAL2 --> END classDef inp fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a07a00,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#06a,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#060,color:#000 class P1,P2 inp class OR,TBL,CVAL,NSIZE,VAL1,VAL2 calc class PLAN,END out ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define producer's risk and consumer's risk in the context of an acceptance sampling plan. State the typical numerical values adopted for these risks in industrial practice.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Producer's Risk (α):** It is the probability that a *good* lot (with quality at or better than AQL, p₁) is **rejected** by the sampling plan. It is the probability of a *Type I* error. **(1 Mark)** - **Consumer's Risk (β):** It is the probability that a *poor* lot (with quality at or worse than LTPD, p₂) is **accepted** by the sampling plan. It is the probability of a *Type II* error. **(1 Mark)** - **Typical industrial values:** α = 0.05 (5%) and β = 0.10 (10%). These are the KTU/industry-standard benchmark values used in design tables. **(1 Mark)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between AQL and LTPD. Why is AQL associated with the producer and LTPD with the consumer?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) | LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) | |---|---|---| | Symbol | p₁ | p₂ | | Nature | Maximum tolerable fraction defective as a *process average* | Worst fraction defective acceptable in an *individual* lot | | Probability of acceptance | High (1 − α) | Low (β) | | Associated party | Producer (seller) | Consumer (buyer) | | Statistical point on OC | Producer's Risk Point | Consumer's Risk Point | AQL is associated with the **producer** because the producer claims their process can sustain this level of defects on average (1 Mark). LTPD is associated with the **consumer** because the buyer states the *worst* level they can tolerate in a single lot (1 Mark). The two points are located on opposite ends of the OC curve (1 Mark). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE 2024 Internal-Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks (typically split 7 + 7). --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch of the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve, explain the terms: AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk (α), Consumer's Risk (β), and Indifference Quality. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Sketching the OC Curve** The OC curve is a plot of *Probability of Acceptance* Pₐ on the y-axis against the *Incoming Lot Fraction Defective* p on the x-axis. It is a monotonically decreasing curve from Pₐ = 1 at p = 0 to Pₐ = 0 at p = 1. **(1 Mark)** **Step 2 — Identifying AQL (p₁)** AQL is the *Acceptable Quality Level*, denoted p₁. It is the maximum percent defective that, for purposes of acceptance sampling, can be considered satisfactory as a process average. On the OC curve, AQL is the quality level at which the producer's risk α is controlled. **(1 Mark)** **Step 3 — Identifying LTPD (p₂)** LTPD is the *Lot Tolerance Percent Defective*, denoted p₂. It represents the poorest quality level that the consumer is willing to tolerate in an individual lot. On the OC curve, LTPD is the quality level at which the consumer's risk β is evaluated. **(1 Mark)** **Step 4 — Producer's Risk α** At p = p₁, the curve lies at a height of (1 − α). Therefore, α = 1 − Pₐ(p₁). This represents the probability that a good lot (at AQL) is *wrongly rejected*. **(1 Mark)** **Step 5 — Consumer's Risk β** At p = p₂, the curve lies at a height of β. Therefore, β = Pₐ(p₂). This represents the probability that a bad lot (at LTPD) is *wrongly accepted*. **(1 Mark)** **Step 6 — Indifference Quality (p*)** The indifference quality is the lot quality for which Pₐ = 0.50. It is the median of the OC curve — the lot has a 50% chance of being accepted or rejected. **(1 Mark)** **Step 7 — Summary Diagram (Textual Representation)** ``` P_a 1.0 ● │ ● ← Indifference point? No — explained 1-α │ ●─── Producer's Risk Point at p equals p1 │ ● │ ● 0.50 │─ ─ ─ ─●─ ─ ─ Indifference Quality p star │ ● β │─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─●─ Consumer's Risk Point at p equals p2 │ ● 0.0 │─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─●── 0 p1 p* p2 1 ``` **(1 Mark for correct identification of all points)** --- **(b)** A single sampling plan has n = 75 and c = 2. Calculate the probability of acceptance for p = 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07. If AQL is 1% and LTPD is 5%, determine producer's and consumer's risks using the Poisson approximation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up Poisson parameters** For each p, compute λ = np = 75p and use $P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 2 — Calculate for p = 0.01** $$\lambda_1 = 75 \times 0.01 = 0.75$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-0.75} = 0.4724 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-0.75} \times 0.75 = 0.3543 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-0.75} \times \frac{0.75^2}{2} = 0.1329 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(0.01) = 0.4724 + 0.3543 + 0.1329 = 0.9596$$ **Step 3 — Calculate for p = 0.03** $$\lambda_2 = 75 \times 0.03 = 2.25$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-2.25} = 0.1054 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-2.25} \times 2.25 = 0.2371 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-2.25} \times \frac{2.25^2}{2} = 0.2668 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(0.03) = 0.1054 + 0.2371 + 0.2668 = 0.6093$$ **Step 4 — Calculate for p = 0.05** $$\lambda_3 = 75 \times 0.05 = 3.75$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-3.75} = 0.0235 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-3.75} \times 3.75 = 0.0882 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-3.75} \times \frac{3.75^2}{2} = 0.1655 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(0.05) = 0.0235 + 0.0882 + 0.1655 = 0.2772$$ **Step 5 — Calculate for p = 0.07** $$\lambda_4 = 75 \times 0.07 = 5.25$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-5.25} = 0.00525 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-5.25} \times 5.25 = 0.02758 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-5.25} \times \frac{5.25^2}{2} = 0.07239 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(0.07) = 0.00525 + 0.02758 + 0.07239 = 0.1052$$ **Step 6 — Tabulate results (1 Mark)** | p | λ = 75p | Pₐ(p) | |---|---|---| | 0.01 | 0.75 | 0.9596 | | 0.03 | 2.25 | 0.6093 | | 0.05 | 3.75 | 0.2772 | | 0.07 | 5.25 | 0.1052 | **Step 7 — Producer's and Consumer's Risk** **(1 Mark)** At AQL = p₁ = 0.01: Pₐ = 0.9596 ⇒ **α = 1 − 0.9596 = 0.0404 (≈ 4.04%)** At LTPD = p₂ = 0.05: Pₐ = 0.2772 ⇒ **β = 0.2772 (≈ 27.72%)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5A(b):** > 1. **Forgetting to use Poisson approximation correctly**: Many students incorrectly use the Binomial formula and waste time. For p ≤ 0.10 and n ≥ 50, the Poisson approximation is the KTU-accepted shortcut. > 2. **Skipping the verification that α and β are interpreted at the correct p₁ and p₂**: A common error is reporting α at LTPD or β at AQL. Always read the question carefully. > 3. **Computational slip in the λ²/2 term**: Do not forget to divide by 2! (i.e., 2) in the P(X=2) term. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for designing a single sampling plan for attributes, when AQL (p₁), LTPD (p₂), producer's risk (α) and consumer's risk (β) are specified. Use α = 0.05, β = 0.10 in your explanation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the Operating Ratio** (1 Mark) $$R = \frac{p_2}{p_1}$$ This single number captures the "sharpness of discrimination" required of the plan. Larger R → fewer sample items needed, but coarser discrimination. **Step 2 — Enter the Standard Design Table** (2 Marks) For the standard values α = 0.05 and β = 0.10, refer to the published operating-ratio design table. The table provides, for each c (0, 1, 2, ..., 10), the corresponding values of d₁ and d₂, and the operating ratio d₂/d₁. The table excerpts are: | c | d₁ | d₂ | d₂/d₁ | |---|---|---|---| | 2 | 0.82 | 5.43 | 6.5 | | 3 | 1.37 | 6.78 | 4.95 | | 4 | 1.97 | 7.98 | 4.05 | | 5 | 2.61 | 9.13 | 3.50 | **Step 3 — Select c from the Table** (1 Mark) Find the row whose d₂/d₁ value most closely matches the required R. Read off the corresponding c, d₁, d₂. **Step 4 — Compute the Sample Size** (1 Mark) $$n = \frac{d_1}{p_1} = \frac{d_2}{p_2}$$ In practice, the larger of the two computed values is adopted (conservative). **Step 5 — Verify the Plan** (1 Mark) Re-evaluate Pₐ(p₁) and Pₐ(p₂) using the Poisson approximation and confirm that 1 − Pₐ(p₁) ≤ α and Pₐ(p₂) ≤ β. **Step 6 — Document the Plan** (1 Mark) The final plan is reported as (n, c). For implementation, an operating-procedure document is prepared with sampling frequency, rectification rules, and AOQ curves. --- **(b)** Design a single sampling plan for the following requirements: AQL (p₁) = 0.015, LTPD (p₂) = 0.06, α = 0.05, β = 0.10. Verify the designed plan. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the Operating Ratio** (1 Mark) $$R = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{0.06}{0.015} = 4.0$$ **Step 2 — Select c, d₁, d₂ from the Design Table** (1 Mark) From the α = 0.05, β = 0.10 design table, the row with d₂/d₁ ≈ 4.0 gives: $$c = 4, \quad d_1 = 1.97, \quad d_2 = 7.98$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Sample Size** (1 Mark) $$n = \frac{d_1}{p_1} = \frac{1.97}{0.015} = 131.33 \approx 132$$ Check: $n = \dfrac{d_2}{p_2} = \dfrac{7.98}{0.06} = 133.0$ Adopt the larger value: **n = 133, c = 4** (rounded up for conservatism) **Step 4 — Verification of α at AQL** (2 Marks) $$\lambda_1 = 133 \times 0.015 = 1.995 \approx 2.00$$ $$P_a(p_1) = \sum_{x=0}^{4} \frac{e^{-2}(2)^x}{x!}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-2} = 0.1353 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-2} \times 2 = 0.2707 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-2} \times 2 = 0.2707 \\ P(X=3) &= e^{-2} \times \frac{8}{6} = 0.1804 \\ P(X=4) &= e^{-2} \times \frac{16}{24} = 0.0902 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(p_1) = 0.1353 + 0.2707 + 0.2707 + 0.1804 + 0.0902 = 0.9473$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.9473 = 0.0527 \approx 0.05 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Verification of β at LTPD** (2 Marks) $$\lambda_2 = 133 \times 0.06 = 7.98$$ $$P_a(p_2) = \sum_{x=0}^{4} \frac{e^{-7.98}(7.98)^x}{x!}$$ $$\begin{aligned} P(X=0) &= e^{-7.98} = 0.000342 \\ P(X=1) &= e^{-7.98} \times 7.98 = 0.002728 \\ P(X=2) &= e^{-7.98} \times \frac{7.98^2}{2} = 0.010885 \\ P(X=3) &= e^{-7.98} \times \frac{7.98^3}{6} = 0.028949 \\ P(X=4) &= e^{-7.98} \times \frac{7.98^4}{24} = 0.057753 \end{aligned}$$ $$P_a(p_2) = 0.000342 + 0.002728 + 0.010885 + 0.028949 + 0.057753 = 0.100657$$ $$\beta \approx 0.10 \quad \checkmark$$ > **Final Designed Plan: n = 133, c = 4** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q5B(b):** > 1. **Failing to round up n**: Always round up to the next integer. A plan with n = 132 instead of 133 fails the α and β specifications. > 2. **Selecting the wrong row in the design table**: If operating ratio is 4.0, do not select the c = 3 row (which gives ratio 4.95) — the resulting β will be too low (over-inspection) and α will exceed 0.05. > 3. **Forgetting to verify both α and β**: A design is incomplete without verification. Showing the Poisson summation explicitly fetches the full 4 marks allocated to Steps 4 and 5. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Checklist for KTU ESE 2024 — Consumer's & Producer's Risk** - ☐ **Producer's Risk (α) = 1 − Pₐ(AQL)** — risk of *rejecting a good lot*. Apply at p = p₁. - ☐ **Consumer's Risk (β) = Pₐ(LTPD)** — risk of *accepting a bad lot*. Apply at p = p₂. - ☐ **Standard industrial values:** α = 0.05, β = 0.10. **Memorise these.** - ☐ **Master Poisson equation:** $P_a(p) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^x}{x!}$ - ☐ **Operating Ratio:** $R = p_2 / p_1 = d_2 / d_1$. Drives the design-table lookup. - ☐ **Design procedure:** (1) Compute R → (2) Read (c, d₁, d₂) from α = 0.05, β = 0.10 table → (3) Compute n = d₁/p₁ → (4) Verify both risks. - ☐ **Always round n up** to the next integer; verify with Poisson that both α and β constraints are met (or nearly met). - ☐ **OC curve is monotonically decreasing** and convex in the operative region. Steeper = better plan. - ☐ **AQL** is a *process average* concept; **LTPD** is a *single-lot* concept. Do not confuse. - ☐ **Type I and Type II error analogy** is the universal test-memory hook. - ☐ For problems stating *only n and c*, *compute* α and β. For problems stating *only α, β, p₁, p₂*, *design* n and c. - ☐ **Indifference Quality** is at Pₐ = 0.50 — sometimes asked as a 2-mark sub-part. - ☐ **Do NOT confuse** "probability of acceptance" with "probability of rejection" when applying α and β. - ☐ **Common error to avoid:** Reporting β at AQL or α at LTPD — read the question carefully.

topics.title: Construction and Use of Operating Characteristic Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction and Use of Operating Characteristic Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** is a graphical representation that depicts the discriminating power of an acceptance sampling plan. It plots the **probability of accepting a lot ($P_a$)** as a function of the **incoming process quality (fraction defective, $p$)**, while the sampling plan parameters — sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$ — are held constant.

Mathematically, the OC curve is defined as:

$$
P_a \;=\; P(\text{accept} \mid p,\; n,\; c)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** The OC curve shows the relationship between the fraction defective $p$ in a submitted lot and the probability that the lot will be accepted by the chosen sampling plan $(n, c)$. It is the single most important measure of the **discriminating ability** of any acceptance sampling scheme.

The two principal classifications of OC curves used in the **KTU Quality Engineering syllabus** are:

| OC Curve Type | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Type A OC Curve** | Assumes the lot is *infinite* (or very large); uses the **binomial distribution** | Theoretical / mathematical construction |
| **Type B OC Curve** | Assumes the lot is *finite* of size $N$; uses the **hypergeometric distribution** | Real industrial inspection scenarios |

In most engineering practice and in KTU examination numericals, the **binomial approximation** is used because it provides sufficiently accurate results when $N/n \geq 10$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a customs officer at a Kerala border checkpoint inspecting imported rice bags. Instead of opening every single bag (which is expensive and time-consuming), the officer randomly checks **20 bags** and accepts the consignment if **at most 2 bags** are found substandard. This is a sampling plan $(n=20, c=2)$.

Now consider three scenarios:
- If the supplier is sending **good quality rice** (very few defective bags, say $p = 0.01$), the officer almost *always* accepts the lot.
- If the supplier is sending **mediocre rice** ($p = 0.05$), the officer accepts *sometimes* and rejects *sometimes*.
- If the supplier is sending **bad rice** ($p = 0.25$), the officer almost *always* rejects the lot.

Plotting the probability of acceptance against the fraction defective $p$ gives an **S-shaped curve** that drops from $1.0$ on the left to $0.0$ on the right — this is the **OC curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A *good* OC curve is steep, hugging the upper-left corner at low $p$ and dropping sharply to the lower-right corner at high $p$. A *poor* OC curve slopes gradually, indicating weak discrimination between good and bad lots.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Industry-Standard Terminology)

The following metrics are **standardized quality engineering terms** and are heavily tested in the KTU 2024 Scheme:

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** — the maximum fraction defective that is considered *tolerable as a process average*. Often associated with the **producer's risk point**.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** — the fraction defective at which the consumer wants rejection with high probability. Associated with the **consumer's risk point**.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)** — the worst possible average quality that reaches the customer after rectifying inspection.
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)** — the probability of rejecting a lot of AQL quality.
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)** — the probability of accepting a lot of LTPD quality.
- **Operating Ratio (OR)** — the ratio $p_2 / p_1$, typically equal to the LTPD/AQL ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perfect discrimination** ($P_a = 1$ for good lots and $P_a = 0$ for bad lots) is *theoretically* impossible with a finite sample. This is precisely why the OC curve never becomes a step function — it is always a smooth S-shape.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How the OC Curve Is Constructed

The OC curve construction proceeds through the following ordered logic:

1. **Fix the sampling plan** $(n, c)$: Decide on a sample size and maximum allowable defectives in the sample.
2. **Select a value of incoming fraction defective $p$.**
3. **Calculate the probability of finding exactly $d$ defectives** in the sample using the **binomial probability mass function**:

$$
P(d) \;=\; \binom{n}{d}\, p^{d}\, (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

4. **Sum the probabilities** for $d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, c$ to obtain the probability of *accepting* the lot:

$$
P_a \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d}\, p^{d}\, (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

5. **Plot the point** $(p,\; P_a)$ on the graph.
6. **Repeat for multiple values of $p$** (typically $p = 0.00, 0.01, 0.02, \ldots, 0.30$) and connect the points to obtain the smooth OC curve.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examinations, students are expected to compute **at least 5 points** on the OC curve and present a tabular plot. The **binomial distribution** is the default unless the question explicitly states a finite lot, in which case the hypergeometric distribution is used.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Binomial probability of acceptance | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Core OC curve equation (Type A) |
| 2 | Hypergeometric probability | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | Exact probability for finite lot of size $N$ with $D$ defectives |
| 3 | Poisson approximation | $P_a \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$ | Used when $p \le 0.10$ and $n \ge 20$ |
| 4 | Producer's risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | Probability of rejecting an AQL lot |
| 5 | Consumer's risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | Probability of accepting an LTPD lot |
| 6 | Operating ratio | $OR = p_2 / p_1 = LTPD/AQL$ | Indicator of curve steepness |
| 7 | AOQL (Rectifying Inspection) | $AOQL = \max_{p} \; p \cdot P_a$ | Worst average quality reaching customer |
| 8 | Average Total Inspection | $ATI = n + (N-n)(1-P_a)$ | Expected items inspected per lot |
| 9 | Rectifying OC relationship | $AOQ = \dfrac{p \cdot P_a \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Average outgoing quality after replacement of defectives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In the AOQ formula, the denominator is the **original lot size $N$**, NOT $(N-n)$. The factor $(N-n)/N$ accounts for the fact that the unsampled items remain in the lot. Confusing these two terms is a **favourite KTU valuation trap**.

### 2.3 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Step 1 (Fix $n, c$)** — Without a fixed plan, the curve cannot be defined. Different $(n, c)$ pairs produce different OC curves.
- **Step 2–4 (Compute $P_a$)** — This is the mathematical heart. Each $p$ value gives a single point; together, the points trace the curve.
- **Step 5 (Plot)** — Reveals the shape. A steeper curve near $p_1$ indicates a *more discriminating* plan.
- **Step 6 (Multiple $p$ values)** — Required for smoothness. KTU typically expects plots at $p = 0.00, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, OC curves are used for:
- **Vendor selection and certification** (deciding whether to keep a supplier).
- **Setting incoming inspection intensity** in a manufacturing plant.
- **Reducing inspection cost** by rejecting obviously defective lots without 100% inspection.
- **Compliance with ISO 2859 / MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4** standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Note:** Modern industries often use **standard tables** (e.g., MIL-STD-105E) where the OC curve is pre-computed for standard AQL values. However, the KTU syllabus expects students to **derive** the curve using binomial mathematics from first principles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Binomial OC Curve for Plan $(n=20, c=2)$

**Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Pattern):**  
Construct the OC curve for the sampling plan $n = 20$, $c = 2$, using the binomial distribution. Compute $P_a$ for $p = 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25$.

#### Step 1: Write the governing equation

$$
P_a \;=\; \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d}\, p^{d}\, (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

For our plan $(n=20, c=2)$, expand the summation:

$$
P_a \;=\; \binom{20}{0} p^{0} (1-p)^{20} \;+\; \binom{20}{1} p^{1} (1-p)^{19} \;+\; \binom{20}{2} p^{2} (1-p)^{18}
$$

Since $\binom{20}{0}=1$, $\binom{20}{1}=20$, and $\binom{20}{2}=190$, this simplifies to:

$$
P_a \;=\; (1-p)^{20} \;+\; 20p(1-p)^{19} \;+\; 190p^{2}(1-p)^{18}
$$

#### Step 2: Evaluate at $p = 0.01$

$$
P_a(0.01) \;=\; (0.99)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.01)(0.99)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.01)^{2}(0.99)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.99)^{20} \;=\; 0.8179
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.01 \cdot (0.99)^{19} \;=\; 0.20 \cdot 0.8262 \;=\; 0.1652
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.0001 \cdot (0.99)^{18} \;=\; 0.0190 \cdot 0.8345 \;=\; 0.01586
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.01) \;=\; 0.8179 + 0.1652 + 0.01586 \;=\; 0.9989
$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate at $p = 0.05$

$$
P_a(0.05) \;=\; (0.95)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.05)(0.95)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.05)^{2}(0.95)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.95)^{20} \;=\; 0.3585
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.95)^{19} \;=\; 1.0 \cdot 0.3774 \;=\; 0.3774
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot (0.95)^{18} \;=\; 0.4750 \cdot 0.3972 \;=\; 0.1887
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.05) \;=\; 0.3585 + 0.3774 + 0.1887 \;=\; 0.9246
$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate at $p = 0.10$

$$
P_a(0.10) \;=\; (0.90)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.10)(0.90)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.10)^{2}(0.90)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.90)^{20} \;=\; 0.1216
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.10 \cdot (0.90)^{19} \;=\; 2.0 \cdot 0.1351 \;=\; 0.2702
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.01 \cdot (0.90)^{18} \;=\; 1.9 \cdot 0.1502 \;=\; 0.2854
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.10) \;=\; 0.1216 + 0.2702 + 0.2854 \;=\; 0.6772
$$

#### Step 5: Evaluate at $p = 0.15$

$$
P_a(0.15) \;=\; (0.85)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.15)(0.85)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.15)^{2}(0.85)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.85)^{20} \;=\; 0.0388
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.15 \cdot (0.85)^{19} \;=\; 3.0 \cdot 0.0456 \;=\; 0.1368
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.0225 \cdot (0.85)^{18} \;=\; 4.275 \cdot 0.0536 \;=\; 0.2294
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.15) \;=\; 0.0388 + 0.1368 + 0.2294 \;=\; 0.4050
$$

#### Step 6: Evaluate at $p = 0.20$

$$
P_a(0.20) \;=\; (0.80)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.20)(0.80)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.20)^{2}(0.80)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.80)^{20} \;=\; 0.0115
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.20 \cdot (0.80)^{19} \;=\; 4.0 \cdot 0.0144 \;=\; 0.0576
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.80)^{18} \;=\; 7.6 \cdot 0.0180 \;=\; 0.1368
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.20) \;=\; 0.0115 + 0.0576 + 0.1368 \;=\; 0.2059
$$

#### Step 7: Evaluate at $p = 0.25$

$$
P_a(0.25) \;=\; (0.75)^{20} \;+\; 20(0.25)(0.75)^{19} \;+\; 190(0.25)^{2}(0.75)^{18}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
(0.75)^{20} \;=\; 0.00317
$$

$$
20 \cdot 0.25 \cdot (0.75)^{19} \;=\; 5.0 \cdot 0.00423 \;=\; 0.02115
$$

$$
190 \cdot 0.0625 \cdot (0.75)^{18} \;=\; 11.875 \cdot 0.00564 \;=\; 0.06697
$$

Summing:

$$
P_a(0.25) \;=\; 0.00317 + 0.02115 + 0.06697 \;=\; 0.0913
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Computed Values

| Fraction Defective $p$ | Probability of Acceptance $P_a$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 0.9989 | Almost certain acceptance — excellent quality |
| 0.05 | 0.9246 | High acceptance — acceptable quality |
| 0.10 | 0.6772 | Moderate acceptance — borderline |
| 0.15 | 0.4050 | Roughly 50/50 — discriminating region |
| 0.20 | 0.2059 | Low acceptance — mostly rejected |
| 0.25 | 0.0913 | Very low acceptance — clear rejection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminating Region of the OC curve** lies in the range where $P_a$ transitions from approximately **0.90 to 0.10**. For this plan, that region is roughly $p \in [0.05, 0.20]$. Plans with smaller $c$ have a *steeper* but *more leftward* curve.

### 3.3 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) Derivation

Under **rectifying inspection**, when a lot is rejected, it is subjected to 100% screening and all defectives are replaced. The AOQ is computed as:

$$
AOQ \;=\; \frac{p \, P_a \, (N-n)}{N}
$$

where $N$ is the lot size, $n$ is the sample size, $p$ is the incoming fraction defective, and $P_a$ is the probability of acceptance.

The **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the maximum value of AOQ over all possible values of $p$:

$$
AOQL \;=\; \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \left[ \frac{p \, P_a \, (N-n)}{N} \right]
$$

For the plan $(n=20, c=2)$ with $N=500$:

$$
AOQ \;=\; \frac{p \, P_a \, (480)}{500} \;=\; 0.96 \, p \, P_a
$$

The maximum typically occurs in the steepest portion of the OC curve (around $p \approx 0.10$ for this plan), and the AOQL is a key KTU parameter.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OC Curve — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fix sampling plan n, c] --> B[Choose fraction defective p value]
    B --> C[Compute binomial probability for each d]
    C --> D["Calculate P_d = nCd * p^d * 1-p^n-d"]
    D --> E["Sum P_d for d = 0 to c"]
    E --> F["Record point (p, P_a)"]
    F --> G{More p values to test?}
    G -- Yes --> B
    G -- No --> H[Plot all points on graph]
    H --> I[Connect points to form smooth S-shaped OC curve]
    I --> J[Identify AQL point p1 and LTPD point p2]
    J --> K[Read off alpha = 1 - P_a p1 and beta = P_a p2]
```

### 4.2 OC Curve Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Lot Quality p] --> B[OC Curve as Discriminating Filter]
    B --> C{Acceptance Decision}
    C -- "P_a is high (good lot)" --> D[Accept Lot]
    C -- "P_a is low (bad lot)" --> E[Reject Lot]
    D --> F[Send to Customer]
    E --> G[Rectifying Inspection 100 percent Screen]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Outgoing Quality AOQ approx AOQL]
```

### 4.3 OC Curve Shape — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compare two plans n1 c1 and n2 c2] --> B{Same n different c?}
    B -- "Yes, c smaller" --> C[Steeper curve, shifted left]
    B -- "Yes, c larger" --> D[Flatter curve, shifted right]
    A --> E{Same c different n?}
    E -- "Yes, n larger" --> F[Steeper curve, sharper discrimination]
    E -- "Yes, n smaller" --> G[Flatter curve, weaker discrimination]
    C --> H[Better for consumer, worse for producer]
    D --> I[Better for producer, worse for consumer]
    F --> J[Better discrimination overall]
    G --> K[Weaker discrimination overall]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
Define an Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. What does it represent in the context of acceptance sampling?

**Model Answer:**

An Operating Characteristic (OC) curve is a graph that shows the **probability of accepting a lot ($P_a$)** as a function of the **incoming fraction defective ($p$)** for a given acceptance sampling plan $(n, c)$. It represents the **discriminating ability** of the sampling plan — i.e., how well the plan separates good lots from bad lots. A steep OC curve indicates strong discrimination, while a flat curve indicates weak discrimination. The curve is S-shaped and is constructed using the binomial (Type A) or hypergeometric (Type B) distribution.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
Differentiate between **Type A** and **Type B** OC curves. State one situation where each is used.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Type A OC Curve | Type B OC Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Lot size assumption | Infinite (or $N \gg n$) | Finite lot of size $N$ |
| Distribution used | Binomial | Hypergeometric |
| When used | Theoretical analysis, large production runs | Small lots, isolated shipments |
| Accuracy | Approximate (very accurate when $N/n \ge 10$) | Exact |
