E[\text{sampling run length in inspected units}] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (1-p)^k = \frac{1}{1 - (1-p)} = \frac{1}{p}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert from inspected units to total units**

Since only every *n*-th unit is sampled:

$$
ASN_{\text{total units}} = \frac{1}{f \cdot p}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the screening contribution (inspected units in screening phase)**

The expected number of inspections during screening until *i* consecutive good units is found is given by the negative binomial expectation for the *i*-th success in a sequence:

$$
ASN_{\text{screening}} = \frac{i}{p}
$$

**Step 5 — Total ASN per cycle**

$$
\boxed{\; ASN \;=\; \frac{1}{f \cdot p} + \frac{i}{p} \;=\; \frac{1 + i f}{f p} \;}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A CSP-1 plan is operated with $f = 1/5$ and $i = 2$. The incoming defect rate is $p = 0.04$.

**Find (i) AOQ, (ii) AOQL (using small-*i* approximation), (iii) ASN per 1000 units.**

**(i) AOQ:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{p(1-f)}{1 - fp} = \frac{0.04 \times (1 - 0.2)}{1 - 0.2 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.04 \times 0.8}{1 - 0.008} = \frac{0.032}{0.992}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQ = 0.03226 \; \text{(or } 3.226\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(ii) AOQL (approximate):**

$$
AOQL \approx \frac{i \cdot f}{i+1} = \frac{2 \times 0.2}{3} = \frac{0.4}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQL \approx 0.1333 \; \text{(or } 13.33\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(iii) ASN per 1000 units:**

$$
ASN_{\text{per unit}} = \frac{1 + if}{fp} = \frac{1 + 2(0.2)}{0.2 \times 0.04} = \frac{1.4}{0.008} = 175
$$

For 1000 units:

$$
\boxed{\; ASN_{1000} = 175{,}000 \text{ inspections expected} \;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note**: The per-1000-unit ASN appears large because the screening-phase inspections are very expensive; once a defect occurs, the next 2 units (and all intervening) are inspected. This shows why CSP-1 is *not* a low-inspection plan — it is a *low-average-inspection-on-good-quality* plan.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CSP-1 Operational State Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the state transitions of a CSP-1 plan. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SamplingMode
    SamplingMode --> SamplingMode : Sampled Unit Good (1 minus p)
    SamplingMode --> ScreeningMode : Sampled Unit Defective (p)
    ScreeningMode --> ScreeningMode : Inspected Unit Defective (replace it)
    ScreeningMode --> CountClearance : Inspected Unit Good (counter increments)
    CountClearance --> ScreeningMode : Counter less than i
    CountClearance --> SamplingMode : Counter reaches i consecutive goods
    SamplingMode --> [*] : Process Terminated
    ScreeningMode --> [*] : Process Terminated
```

### 4.2 CSP-1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the functional flow of inspection, rectification, and decision-making in CSP-1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production Line Entry] --> B{Inspection Mode Selector}
    B -->|Mode equals Sampling| C[Sampling Counter plus 1]
    C --> D{Unit equals nth Unit}
    D -->|Yes| E[Inspect Selected Unit]
    D -->|No| F[Pass Unit Without Inspection]
    E --> G{Unit Defective}
    F --> H[Production Line Exit]
    G -->|No| B
    G -->|Yes| I[Switch to Screening Mode]
    I --> J[Inspect Every Unit]
    J --> K{Unit Defective}
    K -->|Yes| L[Replace Defective with Good]
    L --> J
    K -->|No| M[Clearance Counter plus 1]
    M --> N{Clearance Counter equals i}
    N -->|No| J
    N -->|Yes| O[Reset Clearance Counter]
    O --> B
    H --> P[Outgoing Quality Stream]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Diagram — CSP-1 vs CSP-2 vs CSP-3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CSP1[CSP-1 Standard Plan]
        A1[Sampling Mode] -->|Defective Found| B1[Screening 100 percent]
        B1 -->|i Consecutive Goods| A1
    end
    subgraph CSP2[CSP-2 Repeated Inspection]
        A2[Sampling Mode] -->|Defective Found| B2[Screening 100 percent]
        B2 -->|Defective Found| C2[Reinspect Defective Repeatedly]
        C2 -->|Unit Good| B2
        B2 -->|i Consecutive Goods| A2
    end
    subgraph CSP3[CSP-3 Short Burst Screening]
        A3[Sampling Mode] -->|Defective Found| B3[Screen next k Units]
        B3 -->|k Units Completed| A3
    end
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Which CSP Plan to Choose

| Situation | Recommended Plan | Reason |
|-----------|------------------|--------|
| Single-flow continuous production, balanced cost-quality | **CSP-1** | Simplest, well-tabulated, lowest computational overhead. |
| High-value items where defective must not pass at all | **CSP-2** | Re-inspection of every defective eliminates risk of bad units escaping. |
| Quality is generally good, only occasional defects expected | **CSP-3** | Short screening bursts save cost. |
| Process is unstable, may produce runs of defectives | **CSP-F** | Can discontinue the plan entirely if quality is bad. |
| Process quality fluctuates widely over time | **CSP-V** | Adapts *f* dynamically to observed quality. |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Continuous Sampling Plan (CSP). State any two situations where it is preferred over lot-by-lot acceptance sampling.*

**Model Answer:**
A Continuous Sampling Plan is a rectifying inspection procedure applied to a continuous flow of material in which a fraction *f* of units is inspected during a sampling mode, and 100% inspection is triggered upon finding a defect until *i* consecutive defect-free units are observed.

CSP is preferred over lot-by-lot acceptance sampling in the following situations:
1. When production is in a continuous flow rather than discrete lots (e.g., items on a conveyor belt).
2. When inspection cost per unit is significant, making 100% inspection uneconomical for all units.
3. When the item is durable and defectives can be replaced or reworked cheaply during screening.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for situations)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a rectifying and a non-rectifying sampling plan, with one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rectifying Plan | Non-Rectifying Plan |
|---------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Defective units found | Replaced with good ones (or rejected) | Simply counted; not replaced |
| Outgoing quality | Improved relative to incoming | Equals incoming quality (in terms of defects passing) |
| Example | **CSP-1 (Continuous Sampling Plan)** | **MIL-STD-105 Single Sampling Plan** (without rectification) |

CSP-1, MIL-STD-1235 (ORD), and Dodge's CSP are rectifying because screened defectives are replaced. Lot acceptance plans like MIL-STD-105 are typically non-rectifying unless explicitly applied in a rectifying framework.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for comparison + 1.5 for examples)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students frequently lose marks on three issues: (1) forgetting that CSP is *rectifying*, (2) using the wrong denominator in the AOQ formula (must include the rectification factor), and (3) confusing AOQ with AOQL. Remember: **AOQ is a function of *p*; AOQL is its maximum.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` / Model Paper

**(a)** *Explain the operational procedure of the Dodge CSP-1 plan with a neat flowchart. State the role of the parameters $i$ and $f$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

CSP-1 was proposed by H.F. Dodge in 1943 for the inspection of a continuous flow of material.

**Operational Procedure:**
1. **Sampling Mode (Initial):** Begin with inspection of a fraction $f$ of the items. Practically, every $n = 1/f$-th unit is inspected; all others pass unexamined.
2. **Detection Event:** If an inspected unit is **good**, the sampling mode continues without interruption.
3. **Switch to Screening Mode:** If a **defective** unit is detected during sampling mode, the procedure immediately switches to **100% inspection** of all units (screening mode). The defective is replaced with a good unit.
4. **Clearance Counting:** During screening, consecutive defect-free units are counted. Until **i** such consecutive good units are observed, screening continues.
5. **Return to Sampling Mode:** Once the clearance number *i* is reached, the system reverts to sampling mode and the cycle restarts.

**Role of Parameters:**
- **$f$ (Sampling Fraction):** Determines the inspection effort during normal operation. Higher $f$ means more units inspected per cycle, higher cost, but better defect detection.
- **$i$ (Clearance Number):** Determines how quickly the system returns to sampling mode after a defect. A larger *i* means longer screening (more rectification, fewer defectives escape), but higher cost. A smaller *i* means faster return to cheap sampling but more defectives may slip through.

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**(7 marks: 2 for procedure steps + 2 for parameter roles + 2 for accuracy/terminology + 1 for flowchart)**

---

**(b)** *For a CSP-1 plan with $f = 1/4$ and $i = 3$, calculate (i) AOQ at $p = 0.05$, (ii) the approximate AOQL, and (iii) ASN per 1000 units.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $f = 1/4 = 0.25$, $i = 3$, $p = 0.05$.

**(i) AOQ at p = 0.05:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{p(1-f)}{1 - fp} = \frac{0.05 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1 - 0.25 \times 0.05}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.05 \times 0.75}{1 - 0.0125} = \frac{0.0375}{0.9875} = 0.03797
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQ \approx 0.038 \; \text{(or } 3.80\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(ii) Approximate AOQL:**

Using the small-*i* approximation:

$$
AOQL \approx \frac{i \cdot f}{i + 1} = \frac{3 \times 0.25}{3 + 1} = \frac{0.75}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQL \approx 0.1875 \; \text{(or } 18.75\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(iii) ASN per 1000 units:**

$$
ASN_{\text{per unit}} = \frac{1 + if}{fp} = \frac{1 + 3 \times 0.25}{0.25 \times 0.05} = \frac{1 + 0.75}{0.0125} = \frac{1.75}{0.0125} = 140
$$

For 1000 units produced:

$$
ASN_{1000} = 140 \times 1000 = 140{,}000 \text{ inspections expected}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; ASN_{1000} = 140{,}000 \;}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 for AOQ setup and answer, 2 for AOQL formula and answer, 3 for ASN derivation and answer)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** *Describe the MIL-STD-1235 (ORD) Continuous Sampling Plans. Compare CSP-1, CSP-2, and CSP-3 in a tabular form.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MIL-STD-1235 (ORD)** is the U.S. military standard that defines five Continuous Sampling Plans — CSP-1, CSP-2, CSP-3, CSP-F, and CSP-V — for the inspection of continuous flows of ordnance material. Each plan is indexed by the pair $(i, f)$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | CSP-1 | CSP-2 | CSP-3 |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Sampling mode** | Every *n*-th unit | Every *n*-th unit | Every *n*-th unit |
| **Action on defect** | Switch to 100% inspection | Switch to 100% inspection | Inspect next *k* units at 100% |
| **Treatment of defectives in screening** | Replace with good | Replace, then **re-inspect** the same unit until it passes | Replace with good |
| **Return criterion** | *i* consecutive good units | *i* consecutive good units | After *k* units inspected in burst |
| **Strictness** | Moderate | Stricter (defectives re-inspected) | Less strict (short burst only) |
| **Typical use** | General production | High-reliability items | Quality generally good |

**(7 marks: 2 for MIL-STD introduction + 3 for tabular comparison + 2 for distinguishing features)**

---

**(b)** *A manufacturer uses CSP-1 with $f = 1/5$ and $i = 4$. The incoming process fraction defective is $p = 0.03$. Compute the AOQ, the approximate AOQL, and the cycle efficiency.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $f = 1/5 = 0.2$, $i = 4$, $p = 0.03$.

**(i) AOQ:**

$$
AOQ = \frac{p(1-f)}{1 - fp} = \frac{0.03 \times 0.8}{1 - 0.2 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.024}{0.994}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQ \approx 0.02414 \; \text{(or } 2.414\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(ii) Approximate AOQL:**

$$
AOQL \approx \frac{i \cdot f}{i+1} = \frac{4 \times 0.2}{5} = \frac{0.8}{5} = 0.16
$$

$$
\boxed{\; AOQL \approx 0.16 \; \text{(or } 16\%\text{)} \;}
$$

**(iii) Cycle Efficiency (CE):**

Cycle Efficiency is the fraction of total cycle time spent in the (cheaper) sampling mode rather than the screening mode. For CSP-1:

$$
CE = \frac{L_{\text{sampling}}}{L_{\text{sampling}} + L_{\text{screening}}}
$$

Where:
- $L_{\text{sampling}} = \dfrac{1}{fp} = \dfrac{1}{0.2 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{1}{0.006} \approx 166.67$ units
- $L_{\text{screening}} = \dfrac{1 - (1-p)^i}{p} = \dfrac{1 - (0.97)^4}{0.03}$

Compute $(0.97)^4$:

$$
(0.97)^2 = 0.9409 \quad ; \quad (0.97)^4 = (0.9409)^2 = 0.88529
$$

So:

$$
L_{\text{screening}} = \frac{1 - 0.88529}{0.03} = \frac{0.11471}{0.03} \approx 3.82 \text{ units}
$$

Therefore:

$$
CE = \frac{166.67}{166.67 + 3.82} = \frac{166.67}{170.49} \approx 0.9776
$$

$$
\boxed{\; CE \approx 97.76\% \;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 97.76% of the production cycle time is spent in the cheaper sampling mode — confirming the cost-effectiveness of CSP-1 at this quality level.

**(7 marks: 2 for AOQ + 2 for AOQL + 3 for cycle efficiency derivation and computation)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Continuous Sampling Plan**

- **CSP** = Continuous Sampling Plan; designed for **continuous flow** of items, not discrete lots.
- **CSP is RECTIFYING** — defective units found are replaced with good ones. (Most commonly missed KTU point!)
- **Two parameters**: $f$ (sampling fraction, $0 < f \le 1$) and $i$ (clearance number, positive integer).
- **CSP-1**: Original Dodge plan. On defect → 100% inspection → after *i* good units → back to sampling.
- **CSP-2**: Defective in screening mode is **re-inspected** until it passes.
- **CSP-3**: After defect, only the next *k* units are screened at 100%.
- **CSP-F**: Allows discontinuing the plan if quality is bad.
- **CSP-V**: Variable sampling rate; *f* changes with observed quality.
- **MIL-STD-1235 (ORD)** governs all these five plans.
- **AOQ formula**: $\; AOQ = \dfrac{p(1-f)}{1 - fp} \;$ (CSP-1 steady state).
- **AOQL formula** (approximate): $\; AOQL \approx \dfrac{i \cdot f}{i+1} \;$ for small *i*.
- **AOQL formula** (Dodge exact): $\; AOQL = \dfrac{\sqrt{f}}{i+1} \sqrt{\dfrac{(i-1)^{i+1}}{(i+1)^{i-1}}} \;$.
- **ASN formula**: $\; ASN = \dfrac{1 + if}{fp} \;$ (per unit of production).
- **Cycle Efficiency** = fraction of time spent in sampling mode = $\dfrac{L_{\text{sampling}}}{L_{\text{sampling}} + L_{\text{screening}}}$.
- **Probability of *i* consecutive good units** = $(1-p)^i$.
- **Expected screening length** = $\dfrac{1 - (1-p)^i}{p}$.
- **Expected sampling length (in produced units)** = $\dfrac{1}{fp}$.
- **Higher $f$** = more inspection cost, fewer defectives escape.
- **Higher $i$** = slower return to sampling, better rectification.
- **AOQ < p** always (because of rectification); $AOQ \to p$ as $f \to 1$.
- **CSP is preferred** when production is continuous, inspection cost is non-trivial, and defective items can be replaced cheaply.
- **Real-world examples**: Bottling lines, rolled steel sheets, conveyorized manufacturing, electrical component assembly, bulk material handling.
- **Common KTU trap**: Confusing CSP (continuous flow) with MIL-STD-105 (lot-by-lot). They are NOT the same.
- **Common KTU trap 2**: Forgetting to add the screening-phase contribution when computing total ASN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vendor Ratings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT615 - QUALITY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vendor Ratings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vendor Rating** is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation methodology used by an organization to assess, score, and rank its suppliers (vendors) based on a defined set of performance criteria over a specified period, with the objective of making informed sourcing, negotiation, and partnership decisions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, vendor rating is a **Supplier Performance Measurement System** that converts qualitative supplier attributes into a **single composite numerical score**, enabling objective comparison among heterogeneous vendors supplying similar materials or services.

The American Society for Quality (ASQ) defines it as: *"A process used to evaluate and rate the performance of suppliers with the intent of improving supplier quality, delivery, and overall value."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Vendor Rating like a Student Report Card.**
> 
> Just as a school does not judge a student by marks in one subject alone, a company does not judge a vendor only on price. The school prepares a report card with subjects (Maths, Science, English) — each carrying a different **weightage** — and gives a final aggregate percentage. Similarly, vendor rating aggregates multiple performance parameters (Quality, Delivery, Cost, Service) — each with a **weight** — to produce a **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)**.
> 
> If a vendor has 90% quality but only 30% on-time delivery, their aggregate score pulls down, just like a student scoring 95 in Maths but 35 in English pulls down their CGPA.

## 1.3 Why Vendor Rating is Important in Engineering Industries

- **Risk Mitigation:** Prevents supply chain disruptions by identifying weak vendors.
- **Cost Optimization:** Helps in Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) decisions.
- **Quality Assurance:** Ensures incoming material conforms to specifications (reduces Incoming Inspection cost).
- **Strategic Partnerships:** Supports decisions on single sourcing vs. multi-sourcing.
- **Continuous Improvement:** Vendors receive feedback and benchmark targets.
- **Just-in-Time (JIT) Compatibility:** Certified vendors enable lean inventory systems.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Defect Rate (DR)** — defects per million opportunities (DPMO) or percentage rejection
> - **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** — percentage of deliveries arriving on or before due date
> - **Price Index** — vendor price compared to market benchmark
> - **Lead Time Compliance** — actual vs. promised lead time
> - **Responsiveness / Rejection Resolution Time**
> - **Technical Capability Score**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vendor Rating Methods

| Method Type | Core Logic | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Point Method (WPM)** | Multi-criteria weighted scoring | Multi-vendor comparison with diverse parameters |
| **Categorical / Ranking Method** | Ordinal ranking (A, B, C, D) | Quick tactical decisions |
| **Cost Ratio Method** | Vendor price ÷ Market lowest price | Pure cost-driven sourcing |
| **Matrix / Two-Factor Method** | Plotting Quality vs. Delivery on 2-D grid | Visual decision-making |
| **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)** | Geometric mean of normalized indices | Comprehensive single-number rating |
| **Total Cost Approach (TCO)** | Price + Quality Cost + Delay Cost | Strategic sourcing |

## 2.2 Weighted Point Method — Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1:** Identify evaluation criteria (e.g., Quality, Delivery, Price, Service).

**Step 2:** Assign **weight** $W_i$ to each criterion such that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i = 100 \quad \text{(or } 1.0 \text{ if normalized)}$$

**Step 3:** Score each vendor on each criterion on a uniform scale, typically **0 to 10** or **0 to 100**.

**Step 4:** Compute weighted score:

$$S_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i}$$

Where:
- $S_j$ = composite score of vendor $j$
- $W_i$ = weight of criterion $i$
- $R_{ij}$ = raw score of vendor $j$ on criterion $i$
- $n$ = number of criteria

**Step 5:** Rank vendors in descending order of $S_j$.

## 2.3 Vendor Performance Index (VPI) Method

> [!NOTE]
> **VPI is a Geometric Mean approach** — penalizes poor performance on any single dimension heavily (because a low score drags the product down). This makes it stricter than a simple weighted average.

The classical formula:

$$VPI = \sqrt[3]{Q \times D \times P}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Quality Rating (percentage acceptance)
- $D$ = Delivery Rating (percentage on-time delivery)
- $P$ = Price Rating (price competitiveness ratio)

A vendor with $Q = 100\%$, $D = 100\%$, $P = 100\%$ gets $VPI = 100$ (ideal).
A vendor with $Q = 80\%$, $D = 80\%$, $P = 80\%$ gets $VPI = 80$.
A vendor with $Q = 100\%$, $D = 100\%$, $P = 20\%$ gets $VPI \approx 58.5$ (heavy penalty).

## 2.4 Cost Ratio Method

$$CR = \frac{P_v}{P_{\min}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $P_v$ = Price quoted by vendor
- $P_{\min}$ = Lowest price in the market (or benchmark)

A ratio of 100 means the vendor is cheapest; values above 100 indicate overpricing.

## 2.5 Two-Factor Matrix Method

Quality rating $Q$ is plotted on X-axis and Delivery rating $D$ on Y-axis (each 0–100). Vendors are classified into four quadrants:

| Quadrant | Q | D | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1 — Preferred** | High | High | Increase share, long-term contract |
| **Q2 — Quality Issue** | Low | High | Development program |
| **Q3 — Worst** | Low | Low | Phase out / replace |
| **Q4 — Delivery Issue** | High | Low | Logistics review |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas to Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| Sl. No. | Formula | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_j = \dfrac{\sum W_i R_{ij}}{\sum W_i}$ | Weighted composite score | Dimensionless (0–100) |
| 2 | $VPI = \sqrt[n]{\prod_{i=1}^{n} X_i}$ | Geometric mean rating | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $VPI = \sqrt[3]{Q \times D \times P}$ | 3-factor performance index | Percentage |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{\min}} \times 100$ | Cost competitiveness | Percentage |
| 5 | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100$ | Incoming quality | Percentage / DPMO |
| 6 | $\text{OTD} = \dfrac{\text{On-time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100$ | Delivery reliability | Percentage |
| 7 | $\text{Expected Loss} = p \times C_d$ | Quality cost forecasting | Currency |

> **Notation key:** $W_i$ = weight, $R_{ij}$ = raw score, $Q$ = quality, $D$ = delivery, $P$ = price, $p$ = probability of defect, $C_d$ = cost of defect.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Weighted Point Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical KTU Board Question):**
> 
> A company procures a critical electronic component from four vendors — A, B, C, and D. The company uses three criteria with the following weights: Quality = **0.5**, Delivery = **0.3**, Price = **0.2**. The scores awarded (out of 10) are:
> 
> | Vendor | Quality | Delivery | Price |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | A | 8 | 7 | 6 |
> | B | 9 | 6 | 8 |
> | C | 7 | 9 | 7 |
> | D | 8 | 8 | 9 |
> 
> Compute the **composite weighted score** for each vendor and rank them.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Verify that weights sum to unity (or 100%).

$$\sum W_i = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Apply the weighted point formula to each vendor.

**For Vendor A:**

$$S_A = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 6)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= 4.0 + 2.1 + 1.2 \\
S_A &= 7.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vendor B:**

$$S_B = (0.5 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 6) + (0.2 \times 8)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= 4.5 + 1.8 + 1.6 \\
S_B &= 7.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vendor C:**

$$S_C = (0.5 \times 7) + (0.3 \times 9) + (0.2 \times 7)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= 3.5 + 2.7 + 1.4 \\
S_C &= 7.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vendor D:**

$$S_D = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 8) + (0.2 \times 9)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= 4.0 + 2.4 + 1.8 \\
S_D &= 8.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Rank vendors in descending order of $S_j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= 8.2 \rightarrow \text{Rank 1 (Best)} \\
S_B &= 7.9 \rightarrow \text{Rank 2} \\
S_C &= 7.6 \rightarrow \text{Rank 3} \\
S_A &= 7.3 \rightarrow \text{Rank 4 (Worst)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision. Vendor D is awarded the largest share of orders; Vendor A is placed on a watchlist for quality improvement.

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Vendor Performance Index (VPI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:**
> 
> Vendor X has Quality Rating $Q = 95\%$, Delivery Rating $D = 90\%$, and Price Rating $P = 80\%$. Vendor Y has $Q = 90\%$, $D = 95\%$, $P = 95\%$. Compare using the VPI method.

**For Vendor X:**

$$VPI_X = \sqrt[3]{95 \times 90 \times 80}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
VPI_X &= \sqrt[3]{684{,}000} \\
VPI_X &\approx 88.07
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vendor Y:**

$$VPI_Y = \sqrt[3]{90 \times 95 \times 95}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
VPI_Y &= \sqrt[3]{812{,}250} \\
VPI_Y &\approx 93.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Vendor Y has a higher VPI (93.28 > 88.07) and is preferred, despite Vendor X having a marginally higher Quality rating. This demonstrates the **balancing power** of the geometric mean — it punishes imbalanced profiles.

## 3.3 Conversion Logic Explanation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Geometric Mean for VPI?**
> 
> The geometric mean $\sqrt[n]{\prod X_i}$ is always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean $\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$. This means a single weak dimension pulls the VPI down disproportionately. This aligns with engineering reality: a vendor with perfect quality but terrible delivery is essentially **useless** in a JIT system. The arithmetic mean would overestimate such a vendor; the geometric mean exposes the true weakness.

Mathematically, this is the **AM-GM Inequality**:

$$\frac{X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n}{n} \geq \sqrt[n]{X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdots X_n}$$

with equality only if all $X_i$ are equal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vendor Rating Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Define Sourcing Need] --> A1[Identify Evaluation Criteria]
    A1 --> A2[Assign Weights Wi to Each Criterion]
    A2 --> A3[Collect Performance Data from Vendors]
    A3 --> A4{Quality Records}
    A3 --> A5{Delivery Records}
    A3 --> A6{Price Quotations}
    A3 --> A7{Service Records}
    A4 --> A8[Normalize Scores on Uniform Scale 0 to 100]
    A5 --> A8
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Apply Weighted Point Method]
    A8 --> A10[Compute VPI Geometric Mean]
    A8 --> A11[Plot Two-Factor Matrix Q vs D]
    A9 --> A12[Compute Composite Score Sj]
    A10 --> A13[Compute VPI Value]
    A11 --> A14[Classify Vendor in Quadrant]
    A12 --> A15[Rank Vendors Descending]
    A13 --> A15
    A14 --> A15
    A15 --> A16[Decision: Award / Develop / Phase Out]
    A16 --> A17[Communicate Feedback to Vendors]
    A17 --> A18[Periodic Re-Evaluation]
    A18 --> A3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Vendor Rating. List any three commonly used methods of vendor rating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Vendor rating is a systematic evaluation methodology used to assess and rank suppliers based on predefined performance criteria such as quality, delivery, price, and service, in order to support objective sourcing decisions.
> 
> **Three Methods (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Weighted Point Method** — assigns weights to multiple criteria and computes a composite score.
> 2. **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)** — uses geometric mean of Quality, Delivery, and Price ratings.
> 3. **Categorical Method** — ranks vendors as A, B, C, D based on overall judgment.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Weighted Point Method and Vendor Performance Index (VPI) method of vendor rating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Weighted Point Method | VPI Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Aggregation Type** | Weighted Arithmetic Mean | Geometric Mean |
| **Compensation for Weakness** | High (a weak score can be offset by a strong score elsewhere) | Low (any weak dimension severely drags the index down) |
| **Number of Criteria** | Flexible (any number) | Typically 3 (Q, D, P) |
| **Strictness** | Lenient | Strict |

*(Examiner awards 1.5 marks for differences; 0.5 mark each for the three distinguishing features.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Weighted Point Method** of vendor rating in detail. What are the steps involved? Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm sources ball bearings from three suppliers X, Y, and Z. The performance data over six months is given below. Compute the **composite vendor score** using the Weighted Point Method and rank the suppliers. **(7 marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | X | Y | Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (acceptance %) | 0.4 | 95 | 90 | 98 |
| Delivery (OTD %) | 0.35 | 88 | 95 | 85 |
| Price competitiveness | 0.25 | 80 | 85 | 90 |

(Scores on a 0–100 scale; weights sum to 1.0)

---

### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Weighted Point Method is a multi-criteria decision-making tool in which each evaluation criterion is assigned a relative weight based on its strategic importance, and each vendor is scored on each criterion. The weighted scores are summed to produce a single composite rating.
> 
> **Steps (3 marks):**
> 1. Identify evaluation criteria (e.g., Quality, Delivery, Price, Service, Technical capability).
> 2. Assign weights $W_i$ such that $\sum W_i = 1.0$ or 100.
> 3. Score each vendor on each criterion on a uniform scale (typically 0–10 or 0–100).
> 4. Compute composite score: $S_j = \sum (W_i \times R_{ij})$.
> 5. Rank vendors in descending order of $S_j$.
> 
> **Advantages (1 mark):** Quantitative, objective, accommodates multiple criteria, easy to communicate.
> 
> **Limitations (1 mark):** Subjectivity in weight assignment; equal scaling assumes linear trade-offs; does not penalize imbalanced vendor profiles.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify weights:**

$$\sum W_i = 0.4 + 0.35 + 0.25 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute composite score for Vendor X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_X &= (0.4 \times 95) + (0.35 \times 88) + (0.25 \times 80) \\
S_X &= 38.0 + 30.8 + 20.0 \\
S_X &= 88.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite score for Vendor Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Y &= (0.4 \times 90) + (0.35 \times 95) + (0.25 \times 85) \\
S_Y &= 36.0 + 33.25 + 21.25 \\
S_Y &= 90.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute composite score for Vendor Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= (0.4 \times 98) + (0.35 \times 85) + (0.25 \times 90) \\
S_Z &= 39.2 + 29.75 + 22.5 \\
S_Z &= 91.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Ranking:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Z &= 91.45 \rightarrow \text{Rank 1 (Best Supplier)} \\
S_Y &= 90.50 \rightarrow \text{Rank 2} \\
S_X &= 88.80 \rightarrow \text{Rank 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Supplier Z has the highest composite score (91.45) and should receive the largest order share. Although Supplier Z is the costliest, its superior quality compensates the higher price (a typical TCO argument).

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

**(a)** What is the **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)**? Derive and explain the formula. Why is the geometric mean preferred over the arithmetic mean in VPI? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two vendors, P and Q, are being evaluated. The ratings (on a 0–100 scale) are as follows:

| Vendor | Quality | Delivery | Price |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | 92 | 85 | 70 |
| Q | 85 | 92 | 92 |

Compute the VPI for each vendor. Which vendor would you recommend and why? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Vendor Performance Index is a single composite metric that aggregates Quality, Delivery, and Price ratings of a vendor into one number using the geometric mean. It provides a balanced view of supplier performance.
> 
> **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from the requirement that the composite index should be:
> - Scale-invariant (a vendor with $Q = D = P = x$ must yield VPI = $x$).
> - Symmetric in all three dimensions (no criterion dominates).
> - Strict (penalizes imbalance).
> 
> The multiplicative form satisfies these requirements:
> 
> $$VPI = (Q \times D \times P)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{Q \times D \times P}$$
> 
> **Why geometric mean (2 marks):** By the AM-GM inequality, the geometric mean is always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean, with equality only when all values are equal. This means that if a vendor is excellent in Quality and Delivery but poor in Price (like Vendor P in part b), the VPI drops significantly, reflecting real engineering risk — a vendor with poor cost control cannot be sustained in a competitive supply chain.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**For Vendor P:**

$$VPI_P = \sqrt[3]{92 \times 85 \times 70}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
VPI_P &= \sqrt[3]{547{,}400} \\
VPI_P &\approx 81.71
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Vendor Q:**

$$VPI_Q = \sqrt[3]{85 \times 92 \times 92}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
VPI_Q &= \sqrt[3]{719{,}840} \\
VPI_Q &\approx 89.61
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$VPI_Q (89.61) > VPI_P (81.71)$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Vendor Q is recommended. Although Vendor P has slightly higher Quality (92 vs 85), Vendor Q offers a far more **balanced** profile with strong scores in all three dimensions. The geometric mean penalizes Vendor P's weak Price score (70) heavily, correctly identifying Vendor Q as the lower-risk choice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\sum W_i = 1.0$** — if the question gives unnormalized weights, you MUST normalize first or your arithmetic mean will be wrong. Examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing arithmetic mean and geometric mean in VPI** — the VPI formula is $\sqrt[3]{Q \times D \times P}$, NOT $(Q + D + P)/3$. This is the most common error.
> 3. **Writing score out of 10 but adding weights for 100** — always cross-check the units of the raw score and weight system.
> 4. **Not stating the decision/action** — a numerical answer without a final sourcing decision loses the "application" marks.
> 5. **In VPI problems, forgetting to show cube-root calculation** — show the cube-root step explicitly: $\sqrt[3]{x}$ is NOT optional in KTU scripts.
> 6. **Mixing up "Vendor Rating" with "Vendor Selection"** — Rating evaluates past performance; Selection is the decision process. Examiners may deduct marks if definitions are conflated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- Vendor rating = **systematic, quantitative evaluation** of suppliers.
- Three primary methods: **Weighted Point Method, VPI, Matrix Method**.
- **Weighted Point Formula:** $S_j = \sum (W_i \times R_{ij})$.
- **VPI Formula:** $VPI = \sqrt[3]{Q \times D \times P}$ — geometric mean.
- **Weights must sum to 1.0** (or 100%); always verify before calculation.
- **VPI penalizes imbalance** — geometric mean < arithmetic mean (AM-GM).
- **Cost Ratio:** $CR = (P_v / P_{\min}) \times 100$ — value 100 = cheapest.
- **Two-Factor Matrix:** 4 quadrants — Preferred, Quality Issue, Delivery Issue, Phase Out.
- **Common criteria:** Quality, Delivery, Price, Service, Technical capability.
- **Total Cost Approach** includes price + defect cost + delay cost.
- Always conclude with a **decision**: award / develop / phase out.
- VPI is **stricter** than Weighted Point Method — remember this for comparison questions.
- $\sqrt[3]{}$ calculation must be explicitly shown in scripts.
- Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value in LaTeX — never the bare pipe `|` in tables.
